title: Complete Cyclic Subtask Graphs for Tool-Using LLM Agents: Flexibility, Cost, and Bottlenecks in Multi-Agent Workflows
summary: Long-horizon tool-using tasks sometimes benefit from revisiting earlier subtasks for recovery and exploration, but added multi-agent workflow flexibility can also introduce coordination overhead and substantial inference cost. We study complete cyclic subtask graphs, a deliberately maximally flexible multi-agent architecture in which executable subtask nodes are fully connected and a unified state-analysis-and-routing agent selects transitions using natural-language criteria. This makes unrestricted revisitation explicit and directly analyzable at the subtask level. We evaluate task-specific (Spec-Cyc) and benchmark-generic (Gen-Cyc) graphs on TextCraft, ALFWorld, and Finance-Agent, with ablations over planner/executor/router strength, tool exposure (generalist vs specialized), $n$-shot successful trajectory summaries, and fault-injected random subtask perturbations. The benchmarks expose three distinct regimes. ALFWorld highlights a setting where explicit revisitation supports recovery and exploration; TextCraft, a largely prerequisite-chain domain, often favors the efficiency of simpler forward execution; and Finance-Agent remains bottlenecked by retrieval, grounding, and evidence synthesis more than by workflow flexibility alone. Shared-win token comparisons further show that the added flexibility can be substantially more expensive than a single ReAct agent. Overall, we use complete cyclic subtask graphs as a maximally flexible experimental lens for measuring when multi-agent revisitation helps, when it mainly adds coordination cost, and when external task bottlenecks dominate.

AI.tldr: Complete cyclic subtask graphs as a maximally flexible multi-agent architecture enable unrestricted revisitation, showing varied benefits across domains: helpful for recovery/exploration in ALFWorld, inefficient for linear TextCraft tasks, and limited by retrieval bottlenecks in Finance-Agent.
AI.motivation: To systematically study when revisiting earlier subtasks in long-horizon tool-using tasks is beneficial versus when it mainly adds coordination overhead and inference cost, using a deliberately maximally flexible architecture.
AI.method: Use complete cyclic subtask graphs where all executable subtask nodes are fully connected, with a unified state-analysis-and-routing agent selecting transitions via natural-language criteria. Evaluate on TextCraft, ALFWorld, and Finance-Agent with task-specific (Spec-Cyc) and benchmark-generic (Gen-Cyc) graphs, plus ablations over agent strength, tool exposure, trajectory summaries, and fault-injected perturbations.
AI.result: Three distinct regimes emerge: 1) ALFWorld benefits from revisitation for recovery/exploration, 2) TextCraft (largely prerequisite-chain domain) often favors simpler forward execution efficiency, and 3) Finance-Agent remains bottlenecked by retrieval/grounding/synthesis more than workflow flexibility. Added flexibility can be substantially more expensive than single ReAct agents.
AI.conclusion: Complete cyclic subtask graphs serve as an experimental lens showing that multi-agent revisitation helps in some domains (e.g., recovery/exploration), mainly adds coordination cost in others (e.g., linear tasks), and is often dominated by external task bottlenecks; flexibility must be balanced with inference cost.

title: Beyond Single-Agent Alignment: Preventing Context-Fragmented Violations in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: We identify and formalize a novel security risk: Context-Fragmented Violations (CFVs) - a class of policy breaches where individual agent actions appear locally safe and reasonable, yet collectively violate organizational policies because critical policy facts are siloed in different departments private contexts. Existing prompt-based alignment mechanisms and monolithic interceptors are poorly matched to violations that span contextual islands. We propose Distributed Sentinel, a distributed zero-trust enforcement architecture that introduces the Semantic Taint Token (STT) Protocol. Through lightweight sidecar proxies, our system propagates security state across organizational boundaries without exposing raw cross-domain data, enabling Counterfactual Graph Simulation for cross-domain policy verification. We construct PhantomEcosystem, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 9 categories of realistic cross-agent violation scenarios with adversarially balanced safe controls. On this benchmark, Distributed Sentinel achieves F1 = 0.95 with 106ms end-to-end latency (16ms verification + 90ms entity extraction on A100), compared to 0.85 F1 for prompt-based filtering and 0.65 for rule-based DLP. To empirically validate the need for external enforcement, we evaluate eight frontier LLMs in execution-oriented multi-agent workflows with per-agent domain world models. All models exhibit substantial violation rates (14-98%), with cross-domain data flows showing systematically higher violation rates than same-domain flows. These results indicate that self-avoidance is unreliable and that multi-agent security benefits from a centralized enforcement layer operating above individual agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Context-Fragmented Violations, a security risk where multi-agent actions appear locally safe but collectively violate policies across contextual islands, and proposes Distributed Sentinel architecture with Semantic Taint Token Protocol for cross-domain policy verification.
AI.motivation: Existing security approaches (prompt-based alignment, monolithic interceptors) fail to detect Context-Fragmented Violations where policy violations span multiple contextual islands across departments, creating a significant security gap in multi-agent systems.
AI.method: Proposes Distributed Sentinel: a distributed zero-trust enforcement architecture using Semantic Taint Token Protocol with lightweight sidecar proxies that propagate security state across organizational boundaries without exposing raw data, enabling Counterfactual Graph Simulation for cross-domain policy verification.
AI.result: On the PhantomEcosystem benchmark (9 categories of cross-agent violations), Distributed Sentinel achieves F1=0.95 with 106ms end-to-end latency, outperforming prompt-based filtering (F1=0.85) and rule-based DLP (F1=0.65). Evaluation of 8 frontier LLMs shows substantial violation rates (14-98%), with cross-domain flows having systematically higher violations.
AI.conclusion: Self-avoidance of policy violations is unreliable in multi-agent systems; Context-Fragmented Violations represent a real threat that requires external enforcement like Distributed Sentinel's architecture for effective security across organizational boundaries.

title: Usable Agent Discovery for Decentralized AI Systems
summary: Large-scale agentic systems run on distributed infrastructures where many software agents share physical hosts and are discovered via peer-to-peer mechanisms. Discovery must handle node-level churn from failures and host departures and agent-level churn from demand-driven activation, deactivation, and state changes. Their interaction reshapes classic trade-offs between structured and unstructured overlays. We study decentralized agent discovery under this two-level churn, assuming nodes host multiple agents, overlays are structured or gossip-based, and agents switch between warm and cold states. Using Kademlia as a structured and Cyclon+Vicinity as a gossip baseline, we compare stable, node-churn-only, agent-cooling-only, and combined regimes to see when routing efficiency, resilience, and service readiness align or favor different designs. Structured overlays are more robust and efficient in stable and node-churn regimes, while gossip-based overlays remain competitive and can be faster when readiness dominates.

AI.tldr: Two-level churn (node-level + agent-level) in large-scale agentic systems requires re-evaluating structured vs gossip-based overlays for agent discovery; structured overlays excel in stable/node-churn regimes while gossip remains competitive when readiness is prioritized.
AI.motivation: Large-scale agentic systems operate on distributed infrastructures where many software agents share physical hosts and use peer-to-peer discovery. Discovery must handle two types of churn: node-level (failures, host departures) and agent-level (demand-driven activation, deactivation, state changes). This interaction between the two churn types reshapes the classic trade-offs between structured and unstructured overlays, requiring new evaluation of decentralized agent discovery approaches.
AI.method: The study examines decentralized agent discovery under two-level churn, assuming: nodes host multiple agents, overlays are either structured or gossip-based, and agents switch between warm and cold states. The researchers use Kademlia as a structured overlay baseline and Cyclon+Vicinity as a gossip-based baseline. They compare four regimes: stable (no churn), node-churn-only, agent-cooling-only, and combined regimes. The analysis evaluates routing efficiency, resilience, and service readiness alignment.
AI.result: Structured overlays (Kademlia) demonstrate superior robustness and efficiency in stable and node-churn-only regimes. Gossip-based overlays (Cyclon+Vicinity) remain competitive and can achieve faster performance when service readiness is the dominant concern. The two-level churn reveals different strengths for each overlay type depending on the operational regime and primary system requirements.
AI.conclusion: The choice between structured and gossip-based overlays for agent discovery in large-scale agentic systems depends on the dominant churn regime and prioritization criteria. Structured overlays are better for stable environments and node-level churn, while gossip-based overlays are preferable when service readiness is paramount. The two-level churn analysis provides a more nuanced understanding of overlay trade-offs in modern distributed agentic systems.

title: Architecture Matters for Multi-Agent Security
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS), composed of networks of two or more autonomous AI agents, have become increasingly popular in production deployments, yet introduce security risks that do not arise in single-agent settings. Even if individual agents exhibit robust security, architectural decisions governing their coordination can create attack surfaces that have not been systematically characterized. In this work, we present an empirical study of how MAS design decisions shape the tradeoff between task performance and attack resistance. Across three agentic environments (browser, desktop, and code) and 13 architectural configurations, we use stagewise evaluations that distinguish planning refusal, execution-stage interception, partial harmful execution, and successful attack completion to study three key design choices: (i) agent roles, which determine how authority and responsibility are allocated; (ii) communication topology, which shapes how and when agents interact; and (iii) memory, which determines the context and state visibility accessible to each agent. We find that multi-agent architectures are more vulnerable than standalone agents in the majority of configurations, with attack success rates varying by up to 3.8x at comparable or higher benign accuracy, and that no single design is universally safer. These results motivate the development of further evaluations that move beyond the security properties of a single agent.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent systems introduce unique security vulnerabilities beyond single-agent settings, with architectural decisions significantly impacting attack resistance vs. task performance tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems (MAS) are increasingly deployed in production but introduce security risks not present in single-agent settings, as architectural decisions governing agent coordination create new attack surfaces that haven't been systematically characterized.
AI.method: Conducted empirical study across three agentic environments (browser, desktop, and code) and 13 architectural configurations using stagewise evaluations to distinguish planning refusal, execution-stage interception, partial harmful execution, and successful attack completion. Focused on three key design choices: agent roles (authority allocation), communication topology (interaction patterns), and memory (context visibility).
AI.result: Multi-agent architectures are more vulnerable than standalone agents in most configurations, with attack success rates varying by up to 3.8x at comparable or higher benign accuracy. No single design is universally safer across different environments and configurations.
AI.conclusion: The findings demonstrate that MAS security cannot be evaluated solely based on individual agent security properties, motivating the need for new evaluation frameworks that specifically address multi-agent system vulnerabilities and attack surfaces arising from coordination architectures.

title: The Spectral Lifecycle of Transformer Training: Transient Compression Waves, Persistent Spectral Gradients, and the Q/K--V Asymmetry
summary: We present the first systematic study of weight matrix singular value spectra \emph{during} transformer pretraining, tracking full SVD decompositions of every weight matrix at 25-step intervals across three model scales (30M--285M parameters). We discover three phenomena: \textbf{(1)~Transient Compression Waves:} stable rank compression propagates as a traveling wave from early to late layers, creating a dramatic gradient that peaks early then \emph{reverses} -- late layers eventually over-compress past early layers. \textbf{(2)~Persistent Spectral Gradients:} the power-law exponent~$α$ develops a permanent depth gradient forming a non-monotonic inverted-U in deeper models, with peaks shifting toward earlier layers as depth increases. \textbf{(3)~Q/K--V Functional Asymmetry:} value/output projections compress uniformly while query/key projections carry the full depth-dependent dynamics. The dissociation between transient compression and persistent spectral shape reveals that \emph{rank and spectral shape encode fundamentally different information about training}. We formalize this as a two-timescale dynamical model and derive scaling laws ($Δα\propto L^{0.26}$, $R^2{=}0.99$). We validate on nine models across three families (custom, GPT-2, Pythia; 30M--1B parameters; 8--36 layers), demonstrate that $α$ predicts layer importance ($ρ{=}0.69$--$0.84$, $p{<}0.02$), and show that spectral-guided pruning outperforms Last-N heuristics by $1.1{\times}$--$3.6{\times}$ across seven models in two families (GPT-2 124M--774M, Pythia 160M--1B), with worst-vs-best gaps up to $23.7{\times}$ confirming the causal role of spectral structure.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies weight matrix singular value spectra during transformer pretraining, discovering three key phenomena about compression waves, spectral gradients, and functional asymmetry, which reveals that rank and spectral shape encode different training information, leading to practical applications in pruning.
AI.motivation: To understand how weight matrices evolve during transformer pretraining through systematic analysis of singular value spectra across different model scales and layers, which has not been studied in depth before.
AI.method: Track full SVD decompositions of every weight matrix at 25-step intervals across three model scales (30M-285M parameters) during pretraining. Analyze transient compression waves, persistent spectral gradients, and functional asymmetry between different attention projections.
AI.result: Three main discoveries: (1) Transient compression waves that propagate from early to late layers, (2) Permanent depth gradients in power-law exponent α forming inverted-U patterns in deeper models, (3) Functional asymmetry where value/output projections compress uniformly while query/key projections show depth-dependent dynamics. Also derived scaling laws (Δα∝L^0.26, R²=0.99) and validated on nine models across three families (30M-1B parameters).
AI.conclusion: Rank and spectral shape encode fundamentally different information about training, revealed through their dissociation in transient vs. persistent dynamics. The spectral structure has causal significance, demonstrated by spectral-guided pruning outperforming heuristic methods by 1.1×-3.6×, with α predicting layer importance (ρ=0.69-0.84).

title: An Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Method for General Aviation Aircraft Based on Multi-Fidelity Digital Twin and FMEA Knowledge Enhancement
summary: Fault diagnosis of general aviation aircraft faces challenges including scarce real fault data, diverse fault types, and weak fault signatures. This paper proposes an intelligent fault diagnosis framework based on multi-fidelity digital twin, integrating four modules: high-fidelity flight dynamics simulation, FMEA-driven fault injection, multi-fidelity residual feature extraction, and large language model (LLM)-enhanced interpretable report generation. A digital twin is constructed using the JSBSim six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) flight dynamics engine, generating 23-channel engine health monitoring data via semi-empirical sensor synthesis equations. A three-layer fault injection engine based on failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA) models the physical causal propagation of 19 engine fault types. A multi-fidelity residual computation framework comprising paired-mirror residuals and GRU surrogate prediction residuals is proposed: the high-fidelity path obtains clean fault deviation signals using nominal mirror trajectories with identical initial conditions, while the low-fidelity path achieves online real-time residual computation through a multi-step prediction GRU surrogate model. A 1D-CNN classifier performs end-to-end diagnosis of 20 fault classes. An LLM diagnostic report engine enhanced with FMEA knowledge fuses classification results, residual evidence, and domain causal knowledge to generate interpretable natural language reports. Experiments show the paired-mirror residual scheme achieves a Macro-F1 of 96.2% on the 20-class task, while the GRU surrogate scheme achieves 4.3x inference acceleration at only 0.6% performance cost. Comparison across 24 schemes reveals that residual feature quality contributes approximately 5x more to diagnostic performance than classifier architecture, establishing the "residual quality first" design principle.

AI.tldr: A multi-fidelity digital twin framework for aircraft engine fault diagnosis using flight simulation, fault injection, residual extraction, and LLM-enhanced interpretable reporting.
AI.motivation: General aviation aircraft fault diagnosis faces challenges: scarce real fault data, diverse fault types, and weak fault signatures, requiring innovative solutions for reliable fault detection and interpretation.
AI.method: 1) High-fidelity flight dynamics simulation using JSBSim 6-DoF engine with sensor synthesis; 2) FMEA-driven fault injection for 19 engine fault types; 3) Multi-fidelity residual computation (paired-mirror residuals & GRU surrogate prediction); 4) 1D-CNN classifier for 20 fault classes; 5) LLM-enhanced diagnostic report generation with FMEA knowledge.
AI.result: Paired-mirror residual achieves 96.2% Macro-F1 on 20-class task; GRU surrogate achieves 4.3x inference acceleration with only 0.6% performance loss; Residual feature quality contributes 5x more to performance than classifier architecture.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-fidelity digital twin framework effectively addresses aircraft fault diagnosis challenges, establishes "residual quality first" design principle, and demonstrates strong performance with interpretable reporting via LLM integration.

title: DLM: Unified Decision Language Models for Offline Multi-Agent Sequential Decision Making
summary: Building scalable and reusable multi-agent decision policies from offline datasets remains a challenge in offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), as existing methods often rely on fixed observation formats and action spaces that limit generalization. In contrast, large language models (LLMs) offer a flexible modeling interface that can naturally accommodate heterogeneous observations and actions. Motivated by this, we propose the Decision Language Model (DLM), which formulates multi-agent decision making as a dialogue-style sequence prediction problem under the centralized training with decentralized execution paradigm. DLM is trained in two stages: a supervised fine-tuning phase, which leverages dialogue-style datasets for centralized training with inter-agent context and generates executable actions from offline trajectories, followed by a group relative policy optimization phase to enhance robustness to out-of-distribution actions through lightweight reward functions. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that a unified DLM outperforms strong offline MARL baselines and LLM-based conversational decision-making methods, while demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization to unseen scenarios across tasks.

AI.tldr: DLM treats multi-agent decision-making as a dialogue sequence prediction problem using LLMs, enabling flexible policy learning from offline data with strong generalization to unseen scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing offline MARL methods have limited generalization due to fixed observation/action formats, while LLMs offer flexible modeling of heterogeneous observations and actions.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: 1) Supervised fine-tuning using dialogue-style datasets for centralized training, generating executable actions from offline trajectories; 2) Group relative policy optimization with lightweight reward functions for robustness.
AI.result: DLM outperforms strong offline MARL baselines and LLM-based conversational decision methods across multiple benchmarks, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization to unseen scenarios.
AI.conclusion: DLM successfully leverages LLM flexibility for scalable, reusable multi-agent decision policies from offline data, enabling effective generalization across diverse scenarios.

title: KARL: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLMs via Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reinforcement Learning
summary: Enabling large language models (LLMs) to appropriately abstain from answering questions beyond their knowledge is crucial for mitigating hallucinations. While existing reinforcement learning methods foster autonomous abstention, they often compromise answer accuracy because their static reward mechanisms, agnostic to models' knowledge boundaries, drive models toward excessive caution. In this work, we propose KARL, a novel framework that continuously aligns an LLM's abstention behavior with its evolving knowledge boundary. KARL introduces two core innovations: a Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward that performs online knowledge boundary estimation using within-group response statistics, dynamically rewarding correct answers or guided abstention; and a Two-Stage RL Training Strategy that first explores the knowledge boundary and bypasses the "abstention trap", and subsequently converts incorrect answers beyond the knowledge boundary into abstentions without sacrificing accuracy. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that KARL achieves a superior accuracy-hallucination trade-off, effectively suppressing hallucinations while maintaining high accuracy across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.

AI.tldr: KARL is a framework that aligns LLMs' abstention behavior with their evolving knowledge boundaries using knowledge-boundary-aware rewards and two-stage RL training to reduce hallucinations while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for enabling LLMs to abstain from unknown questions have limitations: their static reward mechanisms are agnostic to models' knowledge boundaries, leading to excessive caution and compromised answer accuracy. There's a need for a method that dynamically aligns abstention behavior with evolving knowledge boundaries.
AI.method: KARL introduces two core innovations: 1) Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward that performs online knowledge boundary estimation using within-group response statistics, dynamically rewarding correct answers or guided abstention, and 2) Two-Stage RL Training Strategy that first explores the knowledge boundary and bypasses the "abstention trap", then converts incorrect answers beyond the knowledge boundary into abstentions without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that KARL achieves superior accuracy-hallucination trade-off, effectively suppressing hallucinations while maintaining high accuracy across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.conclusion: KARL represents a significant advancement in enabling LLMs to appropriately abstain from questions beyond their knowledge, addressing the limitations of existing RL methods by dynamically aligning abstention behavior with evolving knowledge boundaries, resulting in better performance across diverse scenarios.

title: PExA: Parallel Exploration Agent for Complex Text-to-SQL
summary: LLM-based agents for text-to-SQL often struggle with latency-performance trade-off, where performance improvements come at the cost of latency or vice versa. We reformulate text-to-SQL generation within the lens of software test coverage where the original query is prepared with a suite of test cases with simpler, atomic SQLs that are executed in parallel and together ensure semantic coverage of the original query. After iterating on test case coverage, the final SQL is generated only when enough information is gathered, leveraging the explored test case SQLs to ground the final generation. We validated our framework on a state-of-the-art benchmark for text-to-SQL, Spider 2.0, achieving a new state-of-the-art with 70.2% execution accuracy.

AI.tldr: A novel text-to-SQL framework reformulates SQL generation as software test coverage, using parallel test cases to gather information before final SQL generation, achieving SOTA 70.2% execution accuracy on Spider 2.0 benchmark.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents for text-to-SQL face a latency-performance trade-off where improvements in one aspect typically come at the cost of the other. Current approaches struggle to balance these competing requirements effectively.
AI.method: Reformulates text-to-SQL generation through software test coverage lens. The approach prepares the original query with a suite of simpler, atomic SQL test cases that execute in parallel to ensure semantic coverage. Iterates on test case coverage, generating the final SQL only after gathering sufficient information, using explored test case SQLs to ground the final generation.
AI.result: Achieved new state-of-the-art performance on Spider 2.0 benchmark with 70.2% execution accuracy, demonstrating superior performance while addressing latency concerns through parallel test execution.
AI.conclusion: The test coverage approach effectively addresses the latency-performance trade-off in text-to-SQL by using parallel test execution and iterative information gathering, resulting in improved accuracy while maintaining reasonable latency through parallelization.

title: EndoGov: A knowledge-governed multi-agent expert system for endometrial cancer risk stratification
summary: Multimodal artificial intelligence models for endometrial cancer (EC) risk stratification typically optimize aggregate predictive performance but provide limited mechanisms for enforcing mandatory guideline overrides, such as assigning POLE-mutated tumors to the low-risk group despite high-grade morphology. We present EndoGov, a two-tier multi-agent expert system that factorizes the decision process as D(x) = G(P(x), R), where specialist agents P extract structured evidence and a governance agent G applies an executable rule set R. Tier 1 comprises pathology, molecular, and clinical agents that independently generate schema-constrained reports from frozen foundation-model features or structured records. Tier 2 queries an evidence-level-weighted Guideline Knowledge Graph, using deterministic hard-path rules for high-priority overrides and constrained soft-path reasoning for ambiguous cases. In TCGA-UCEC (n=541), EndoGov achieved 0.943 accuracy, 0.973 macro AUC, and a conditional logic-violation rate (C-LVR) of 0.93% among trigger-exposed cases. In CPTAC-UCEC (n=95), where reference labels are guideline-derived, EndoGov reached 0.842 accuracy compared with < 0.31 for locked-transfer neural baselines, supporting governance-pathway transfer under distribution shift rather than validation against independent clinical truth. End-to-end safety decomposition localized residual failures primarily to upstream molecular detection rather than downstream governance. Backend-swap experiments further showed that hard-path compliance is invariant to the LLM backend. These findings indicate that explicit clinical-rule governance can provide guideline-compliant, auditable EC risk assignment while preserving competitive discrimination.

AI.tldr: EndoGov is a two-tier multi-agent expert system for endometrial cancer risk stratification that separates evidence extraction from governance rules to ensure mandatory guideline compliance while maintaining competitive predictive performance.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal AI models for endometrial cancer risk stratification focus on optimizing aggregate predictive performance but lack mechanisms to enforce mandatory clinical guideline overrides, such as correctly assigning POLE-mutated tumors to low-risk groups despite contradictory high-grade morphology.
AI.method: EndoGov uses a two-tier multi-agent system with decision factorization D(x) = G(P(x), R). Tier 1 has specialist agents (pathology, molecular, clinical) that extract structured evidence from foundation-model features or structured records. Tier 2 uses a governance agent that queries an evidence-level-weighted Guideline Knowledge Graph, applying deterministic hard-path rules for high-priority overrides and constrained soft-path reasoning for ambiguous cases.
AI.result: In TCGA-UCEC (n=541), EndoGov achieved 0.943 accuracy, 0.973 macro AUC, and only 0.93% conditional logic-violation rate among trigger-exposed cases. In CPTAC-UCEC (n=95) with guideline-derived labels, it reached 0.842 accuracy vs < 0.31 for locked-transfer neural baselines. The system localized residual failures to upstream molecular detection rather than downstream governance, and hard-path compliance was invariant to LLM backend.
AI.conclusion: Explicit clinical-rule governance through EndoGov provides guideline-compliant, auditable endometrial cancer risk assignment while preserving competitive discrimination, addressing the critical need for AI systems that can enforce mandatory clinical overrides in risk stratification.

title: BiTA: Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit-Transformer Aggregator in a Temporal Graph Network Framework for Alert Prediction in Computer Networks
summary: Proactive alert prediction in computer networks is critical for mitigating evolving cyber threats and enabling timely defensive actions. Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) provide a principled framework for modeling time-evolving interactions; however, existing TGN-based methods predominantly rely on unidirectional or single-mechanism temporal aggregation, which limits their ability to capture recursive, multi-scale temporal patterns commonly observed in real-world attack behaviors. In this paper, we propose BiTA, a Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit-Transformer Aggregator for temporal graph learning. Rather than introducing a deeper or higher-capacity model, BiTA redesigns the temporal aggregation function within the TGN framework by jointly encoding bidirectional sequential dependencies and long-range contextual relations over each node's temporal neighborhood. This aggregation strategy enables complementary temporal reasoning at different scales while preserving the original TGN memory and message-passing structure. We evaluate BiTA on real-world alert datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in key performance metrics such as area under the curve, average precision, mean reciprocal rank, and per-category prediction accuracy when compared to state-of-the-art temporal graph models. BiTA outperforms baseline methods under both transductive and inductive settings, highlighting its robustness and generalization capabilities in dynamic network environments. BiTA is a scalable and interpretable framework for real-time cyber threat anticipation, paving the way toward more intelligent and adaptive intrusion detection systems.

AI.tldr: BiTA is a Bidirectional GRU-Transformer Aggregator that enhances temporal graph neural networks for proactive alert prediction by capturing both forward and backward temporal dependencies with multi-scale reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNs) for alert prediction rely on unidirectional or single-mechanism temporal aggregation, which limits their ability to capture recursive, multi-scale temporal patterns observed in real-world attack behaviors.
AI.method: BiTA redesigns the temporal aggregation function within TGN framework by jointly encoding bidirectional sequential dependencies (using GRU) and long-range contextual relations (using Transformer) over each node's temporal neighborhood, while preserving original TGN memory and message-passing structure.
AI.result: BiTA demonstrates significant improvements in key metrics (AUC, average precision, mean reciprocal rank, per-category accuracy) on real-world alert datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art temporal graph models in both transductive and inductive settings.
AI.conclusion: BiTA is a scalable and interpretable framework for real-time cyber threat anticipation that enables more intelligent and adaptive intrusion detection systems by capturing complementary temporal reasoning at different scales.

title: The Power of Power Law: Asymmetry Enables Compositional Reasoning
summary: Natural language data follows a power-law distribution, with most knowledge and skills appearing at very low frequency. While a common intuition suggests that reweighting or curating data towards a uniform distribution may help models better learn these long-tail skills, we find a counterintuitive result: across a wide range of compositional reasoning tasks, such as state tracking and multi-step arithmetic, training under power-law distributions consistently outperforms training under uniform distributions. To understand this advantage, we introduce a minimalist skill-composition task and show that learning under a power-law distribution provably requires significantly less training data. Our theoretical analysis reveals that power law sampling induces a beneficial asymmetry that improves the pathological loss landscape, which enables models to first acquire high-frequency skill compositions with low data complexity, which in turn serves as a stepping stone to efficiently learn rare long-tailed skills. Our results offer an alternative perspective on what constitutes an effective data distribution for training models.

AI.tldr: Training models on power-law distributed data (natural distribution) outperforms uniform data distributions for compositional reasoning tasks, contrary to common intuition about long-tail learning.
AI.motivation: Natural language data follows a power-law distribution where most knowledge and skills appear at very low frequency. While conventional wisdom suggests reweighting or curating data toward uniform distributions might help models learn long-tail skills better, the authors investigate whether this intuition holds for compositional reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The researchers introduce a minimalist skill-composition task and conduct extensive experiments across various compositional reasoning tasks including state tracking and multi-step arithmetic. They compare training under power-law distributions versus uniform distributions and provide theoretical analysis to understand the observed advantages.
AI.result: Contrary to common intuition, training under power-law distributions consistently outperforms training under uniform distributions across a wide range of compositional reasoning tasks. Theoretical analysis shows that power-law sampling requires significantly less training data and induces a beneficial asymmetry that improves the pathological loss landscape. This enables models to first acquire high-frequency skill compositions with low data complexity, which then serves as a stepping stone to efficiently learn rare long-tailed skills.
AI.conclusion: The findings offer an alternative perspective on what constitutes an effective data distribution for training models. Rather than uniform distributions being superior for long-tail learning, natural power-law distributions actually provide better training dynamics for compositional reasoning tasks through their inherent structural properties that facilitate more efficient skill acquisition.

title: GSAR: Typed Grounding for Hallucination Detection and Recovery in Multi-Agent LLMs
summary:
Autonomous multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly deployed to investigate operational incidents and produce structured diagnostic reports. Their trustworthiness hinges on whether each claim is grounded in observed evidence rather than model-internal inference. Existing groundedness evaluators (binary classifiers, LLM-as-judge scalars, self-correction loops) treat supporting evidence as interchangeable and emit a single signal that offers no principled control over downstream action.
  We present GSAR, a grounding-evaluation and replanning framework that (i) partitions claims into a four-way typology (grounded, ungrounded, contradicted, complementary), giving first-class standing to non-redundant alternative perspectives; (ii) assigns evidence-type-specific weights reflecting epistemic strength; (iii) computes an asymmetric contradiction-penalised weighted groundedness score; and (iv) couples that score to a three-tier decision function (proceed, regenerate, replan) driving a bounded-iteration outer loop under an explicit compute budget.
  We formalise the algorithm, prove six structural properties, and evaluate five design claims on FEVER with gold Wikipedia evidence under four independently-trained LLM judges (gpt-5.4, claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4-7, gemini-2.5-pro). Every ablation reproduces in the same direction on every judge: bootstrap 95% CIs on the rho=0 effect exclude 0 on all four; the no-complementary ablation under Opus 4.7 has CI [-96,-68] of 200; at n=1000 three independent judges converge to DeltaS(rho=0)=+0.058. A head-to-head against Vectara HHEM-2.1-Open is included. To our knowledge, GSAR is the first published groundedness framework coupling evidence-typed scoring with tiered recovery under an explicit compute budget.

AI.tldr: GSAR introduces a framework for evaluating claim grounding in multi-agent LLM systems with a four-way claim typology, evidence-weighted scoring, and tiered decision-making for recovery under compute budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing groundedness evaluators treat supporting evidence as interchangeable and emit single signals without principled control over downstream actions, lacking support for non-redundant alternative perspectives and proper handling of contradiction and complementary evidence.
AI.method: GSAR partitions claims into four types (grounded, ungrounded, contradicted, complementary), assigns evidence-type-specific weights reflecting epistemic strength, computes asymmetric contradiction-penalised weighted groundedness scores, and couples scores to a three-tier decision function (proceed, regenerate, replan) with bounded-iteration outer loop under compute budget.
AI.result: Evaluation on FEVER with gold Wikipedia evidence using four LLM judges shows consistent directional results across ablations with statistically significant effects, achieving DeltaS(rho=0)=+0.058 with three independent judges converging at n=1000, outperforming baseline Vectara HHEM-2.1-Open.
AI.conclusion: GSAR is the first groundedness framework coupling evidence-typed scoring with tiered recovery under explicit compute budget, providing principled control over claim verification in autonomous multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Stochastic KV Routing: Enabling Adaptive Depth-Wise Cache Sharing
summary: Serving transformer language models with high throughput requires caching Key-Values (KVs) to avoid redundant computation during autoregressive generation. The memory footprint of KV caching is significant and heavily impacts serving costs. This work proposes to lessen these memory requirements. While recent work has largely addressed KV cache reduction via compression and eviction along the temporal axis, we argue that the \emph{depth} dimension offers an orthogonal and robust avenue for optimization. Although prior research suggests that a full cache for every layer is redundant, implementing cross-layer cache sharing remains a practical challenge; existing methods typically suffer from reduced throughput or increased time-to-first-token. In this paper, we demonstrate that dropping a layer's cache offers efficient optimization without information loss. We propose a simple training approach: random cross-layer attention. During training, layers randomly choose to attend either to their own KV states or those of a preceding layer. This stochastic process adapts the model to be robust to various depth-wise cache sharing strategies, ensuring flexibility for unknown hardware constraints at deployment time. Our evaluations show that applying this scheme during pre-training or fine-tuning enables depth-wise cache sharing for various model families. Furthermore, for larger models in data-constrained settings, this approach is suggestive of a regularization-like effect, frequently preserving or improving performance while significantly reducing the cache's memory footprint.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces random cross-layer attention training to enable depth-wise KV cache sharing, significantly reducing memory footprint without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: KV caching for transformer language models has significant memory costs impacting serving costs. While temporal compression has been explored, depth dimension optimization is largely unexplored, and existing cross-layer cache sharing methods suffer from reduced throughput or increased latency.
AI.method: Proposes random cross-layer attention training where layers randomly attend to their own KV states or those of preceding layers. This stochastic adaptation makes models robust to various depth-wise cache sharing strategies for flexible deployment.
AI.result: The approach enables effective depth-wise cache sharing across various model families. For larger models in data-constrained settings, it often preserves or improves performance while significantly reducing KV cache memory footprint.
AI.conclusion: Depth-wise optimization via random cross-layer attention training offers a practical solution for KV cache reduction, addressing memory constraints without compromising throughput or latency, with potential regularization benefits.

title: On the Existence of an Inverse Solution for Preference-Based Reductions in Argumentation
summary: Preference-based argumentation frameworks (PAFs) extend Dung's approach to abstract argumentation (AAFs) by encoding preferences over arguments. Such preferences control the transformation of attacks into defeats, and different approaches to doing so result in different reductions from a PAF to an AAF. In this paper we consider a PAF inverse problem which takes an argumentation graph, a labelling and a semantics as an input, and outputs a ``yes" or ``no" as to whether there is a preference relation between the arguments which can yield the desired labelling. This inverse problem has applications in areas including preference elicitation and explainability. We consider this problem in the context of the four most widely-used preference based reductions under the complete semantics. We show that in most cases, the problem can be answered in polynomial time.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses an inverse problem for Preference-based Argumentation Frameworks (PAFs), determining if a preference relation exists that yields a given labelling under complete semantics.
AI.motivation: Preference-based argumentation frameworks extend Dung's abstract argumentation by incorporating preferences over arguments. There's a need to solve the inverse problem: given an argumentation graph, labelling, and semantics, determine if preferences exist that produce that labelling. This has applications in preference elicitation and explainability.
AI.method: The authors consider the PAF inverse problem in the context of four widely-used preference-based reductions under the complete semantics. They analyze the computational complexity of determining whether a preference relation exists that can yield a desired labelling.
AI.result: The research shows that in most cases, the inverse problem can be answered in polynomial time, indicating efficient computational tractability for most preference-based reductions.
AI.conclusion: The polynomial-time solvability of the PAF inverse problem for most cases under complete semantics is an important theoretical result that enables practical applications in preference elicitation and explainable argumentation systems.

title: Information-Theoretic Measures in AI: A Practical Decision Guide
comment: 25 pages, 2 tables, 1 figure. Submitted to Applied Intelligence (Springer)
summary:
Information-theoretic (IT) measures are ubiquitous in artificial intelligence: entropy drives decision-tree splits and uncertainty quantification, cross-entropy is the default classification loss, mutual information underpins representation learning and feature selection, and transfer entropy reveals directed influence in dynamical systems.
  A second, less consolidated family of measures, integrated information (Phi), effective information (EI), and autonomy, has emerged for characterizing agent complexity. Despite wide adoption, measure selection is often decoupled from estimator assumptions, failure modes, and safe inferential claims.
  This paper provides a practical decision framework for all seven measures, organized around three prescriptive questions for each: (i) what question does the measure answer and in which AI context; (ii) which estimator is appropriate for the data type and dimensionality; and (iii) what is the most dangerous misuse. The framework is operationalized in two complementary artifacts: a measure-selection flowchart and a master decision table. We cover both AI/ML and decision-making agent application domains per measure, with standardized Bridge Boxes linking IT quantities to cognitive constructs. Three worked examples illustrate the framework on concrete practitioner scenarios spanning representation learning, temporal influence analysis, and evolved agent complexity.

AI.tldr: A practical framework for selecting and properly using 7 information-theoretic measures in AI applications, with decision tools and examples.
AI.motivation: Information-theoretic measures are widely used in AI (entropy, cross-entropy, mutual information, transfer entropy, integrated information, effective information, autonomy) but measure selection is often disconnected from estimator assumptions, failure modes, and safe inferential claims. There's a need for practical guidance on proper usage.
AI.method: The paper provides a decision framework organized around three questions for each of the 7 measures: (1) what question does it answer and in which AI context, (2) which estimator is appropriate for data type/dimensionality, (3) what is the most dangerous misuse. The framework is operationalized through a measure-selection flowchart and a master decision table. It includes standardized Bridge Boxes linking IT quantities to cognitive constructs and provides three worked examples.
AI.result: The paper delivers a comprehensive practical framework for selecting and applying 7 IT measures in AI contexts, covering AI/ML and decision-making agent domains. It provides concrete tools (flowchart, decision table) and demonstrates the framework through three examples spanning representation learning, temporal influence analysis, and evolved agent complexity.
AI.conclusion: The paper addresses the gap between measure selection and proper usage in AI, providing practitioners with a structured approach to choose and apply information-theoretic measures appropriately while avoiding common pitfalls and misuse.

title: Parameter Efficiency Is Not Memory Efficiency: Rethinking Fine-Tuning for On-Device LLM Adaptation
summary: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become the standard for adapting large language models (LLMs). In this work we challenge the wide-spread assumption that parameter efficiency equates memory efficiency and on-device adaptability. We show that this is not true - while methods like LoRA and IA3 significantly reduce trainable parameters, they remain bound by intermediate tensors that scale linearly with sequence length, often triggering out-of-memory errors on-device. In this work, we introduce LARS (Low-memory Activation-Rank Subspace), a novel adaptation framework that decouples memory consumption from sequence length. While prior PEFT methods apply low-rank constraints to model parameters, LARS instead constrains the activation subspace used during training, directly targeting the dominant source of memory consumption and fundamentally flattening the memory growth rate. LARS reduces the memory footprint by an average of 33.54% on GPUs and 51.95% on CPUs in comparison to LoRA across reasoning, understanding and long-context datasets using different models while maintaining competitive accuracy and throughput. Besides GPUs, we deploy on Raspberry Pi and consumer-grade CPUs to demonstrate that LARS provides a scalable path for sophisticated LLM personalization on resource-constrained hardware and edge devices.

AI.tldr: LARS is a novel PEFT method that decouples memory consumption from sequence length by constraining activation subspaces instead of model parameters, achieving significantly lower memory usage on both GPUs and CPUs while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the widespread assumption that parameter efficiency equates to memory efficiency in PEFT methods. While methods like LoRA and IA3 reduce trainable parameters, they remain limited by intermediate tensors that scale linearly with sequence length, causing out-of-memory errors on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: LARS (Low-memory Activation-Rank Subspace) introduces a novel adaptation framework that decouples memory consumption from sequence length. Instead of applying low-rank constraints to model parameters like prior PEFT methods, LARS constrains the activation subspace used during training, directly targeting the dominant source of memory consumption and flattening the memory growth rate.
AI.result: LARS reduces memory footprint by an average of 33.54% on GPUs and 51.95% on CPUs compared to LoRA across reasoning, understanding, and long-context datasets using different models. It maintains competitive accuracy and throughput, and demonstrates successful deployment on resource-constrained hardware like Raspberry Pi and consumer-grade CPUs.
AI.conclusion: LARS provides a scalable path for sophisticated LLM personalization on resource-constrained hardware and edge devices by fundamentally addressing the memory bottleneck in PEFT methods, enabling efficient adaptation without the limitations of sequence-length-dependent memory consumption.

title: Towards Causally Interpretable Wi-Fi CSI-Based Human Activity Recognition with Discrete Latent Compression and LTL Rule Extraction
summary: We address Human Activity Recognition (HAR) utilizing Wi-Fi Channel State Information (CSI) under the joint requirements of causal interpretability, symbolic controllability, and direct operation on high-dimensional raw signals. Deep neural models achieve strong predictive performance on CSI-based HAR (CHAR), yet rely on continuous latent representations that are opaque and difficult to modify; purely symbolic approaches, in contrast, cannot process raw CSI streams. We propose a fully automatic and strictly decoupled pipeline in which CSI magnitude windows are compressed by a categorical variational autoencoder with Gumbel-Softmax latent variables under a capacity-controlled objective, yielding a compact discrete representation. The encoder is then frozen and used as a deterministic mapping to one-hot latent trajectories. Causal discovery is performed on these trajectories to estimate class-conditional temporal dependency graphs. Statistically supported lagged dependencies are translated into Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) rules, producing a fully symbolic and deterministic classifier based solely on rule evaluation and aggregation, without any learned discriminative head. Because rules are defined over discrete latent variables, antenna-specific rule sets can in principle be combined at the symbolic level, enabling structured multi-antenna fusion without retraining the encoder. Results from CHAR Latent Temporal Rule Extraction (CHARL-TRE) indicate competitive performance while preserving explicit temporal and causal structure, showing that deterministic symbolic classification grounded in unsupervised discrete latent representations constitutes a viable alternative to end-to-end black-box models for wireless HAR.

AI.tldr: A novel HAR system using Wi-Fi CSI that automatically extracts interpretable symbolic rules from discrete latent representations, enabling causal understanding without black-box models.
AI.motivation: Deep neural models for CSI-based HAR are opaque and difficult to modify, while purely symbolic approaches cannot process raw CSI streams. There's a need for systems that combine interpretability, controllability, and direct operation on high-dimensional signals.
AI.method: A decoupled pipeline where: 1) CSI magnitude windows are compressed using a categorical variational autoencoder with Gumbel-Softmax latent variables, 2) The frozen encoder produces deterministic one-hot latent trajectories, 3) Causal discovery creates class-conditional temporal dependency graphs, 4) Statistically supported dependencies are translated into Linear Temporal Logic rules, forming a symbolic classifier without learned discriminative heads.
AI.result: The proposed CHARL-TRE method achieves competitive performance while preserving explicit temporal and causal structure, demonstrating that symbolic classification based on unsupervised discrete latent representations can rival end-to-end black-box models for wireless HAR.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic symbolic classification grounded in unsupervised discrete latent representations constitutes a viable alternative to end-to-end black-box models for wireless HAR, offering interpretability, controllability, and enabling structured multi-antenna fusion without retraining.

title: LLM-Guided Agentic Floor Plan Parsing for Accessible Indoor Navigation of Blind and Low-Vision People
summary: Indoor navigation remains a critical accessibility challenge for the blind and low-vision (BLV) individuals, as existing solutions rely on costly per-building infrastructure. We present an agentic framework that converts a single floor plan image into a structured, retrievable knowledge base to generate safe, accessible navigation instructions with lightweight infrastructure. The system has two phases: a multi-agent module that parses the floor plan into a spatial knowledge graph through a self-correcting pipeline with iterative retry loops and corrective feedback; and a Path Planner that generates accessible navigation instructions, with a Safety Evaluator agent assessing potential hazards along each route. We evaluate the system on the real-world UMBC Math and Psychology building (floors MP-1 and MP-3) and on the CVC-FP benchmark. On MP-1, we achieve success rates of 92.31%, 76.92%, and 61.54% for short, medium, and long routes, outperforming the strongest single-call baseline (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) at 84.62%, 69.23%, and 53.85%. On MP-3, we reach 76.92%, 61.54%, and 38.46%, compared to the best baseline at 61.54%, 46.15%, and 23.08%. These results show consistent gains over single-call LLM baselines and demonstrate that our workflow is a scalable solution for accessible indoor navigation for BLV individuals.

AI.tldr: An agentic framework that converts floor plan images into spatial knowledge graphs to generate accessible indoor navigation instructions for blind/low-vision individuals with minimal infrastructure.
AI.motivation: Existing indoor navigation solutions for blind/low-vision individuals rely on costly per-building infrastructure, creating a critical accessibility challenge that needs scalable, low-cost alternatives.
AI.method: Two-phase system: 1) Multi-agent module that parses floor plan images into spatial knowledge graphs using self-correcting pipeline with iterative retry loops and corrective feedback; 2) Path Planner generating accessible navigation instructions with Safety Evaluator agent assessing route hazards.
AI.result: On UMBC MP-1: 92.31% (short), 76.92% (medium), 61.54% (long routes) success rates, outperforming Claude 3.7 Sonnet baseline (84.62%, 69.23%, 53.85%). On MP-3: 76.92%, 61.54%, 38.46% vs baseline 61.54%, 46.15%, 23.08%. Consistent gains over single-call LLM baselines.
AI.conclusion: The agentic workflow provides a scalable solution for accessible indoor navigation for BLV individuals, demonstrating consistent performance improvements over single-call LLM baselines and requiring only lightweight infrastructure.

title: Learning Without Adversarial Training: A Physics-Informed Neural Network for Secure Power System State Estimation under False Data Injection Attacks
summary: State estimation is a cornerstone of power system control-center operations, and its robust operation is increasingly a cyber-physical security concern as modern grids become more digitalized and communication-intensive. Neural network-based approaches have gained attention as alternatives to conventional model-based state estimation methods. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), which embed power-flow consistency into the learning objective, have shown improved accuracy over existing approaches. This work proposes a PINN-based model for Power System State Estimation (PSSE) that protects the estimation process against the stealth-constrained AC False Data Injection Attacks (FDIAs) considered in this study. The model is developed without adversarial training. Instead, a dynamic loss-weighting formulation based on homoscedastic uncertainty learns the relative scaling of supervised data-fit and physics-residual terms during training, reducing sensitivity to manual weight tuning. Robustness is evaluated on the IEEE 118-bus system using representative stealthy-FDIA families including state distortion, load redistribution, line overloading, and residual-constrained stealth corruption. Performance is measured using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on voltage magnitudes and phase angles. Results demonstrate higher accuracy and stability than existing fixed-weight PINN variants.

AI.tldr: A PINN-based power system state estimation model with dynamic loss weighting that provides robust defense against stealthy false data injection attacks without adversarial training.
AI.motivation: Power system state estimation is critical for grid operations but vulnerable to cyber-physical attacks as grids become more digitalized. Neural network approaches need robustness against stealthy false data injection attacks while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) for state estimation with dynamic loss-weighting formulation based on homoscedastic uncertainty that learns relative scaling of supervised data-fit and physics-residual terms automatically, eliminating manual weight tuning. Developed without adversarial training.
AI.result: Higher accuracy and stability than existing fixed-weight PINN variants when evaluated on IEEE 118-bus system against multiple stealthy-FDIA families (state distortion, load redistribution, line overloading, residual-constrained stealth corruption). Measured using Mean Absolute Error on voltage magnitudes and phase angles.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dynamic loss-weighting PINN approach provides an effective defense against stealth-constrained AC false data injection attacks in power system state estimation, achieving improved robustness and accuracy without the need for adversarial training or manual parameter tuning.

title: FormalScience: Scalable Human-in-the-Loop Autoformalisation of Science with Agentic Code Generation in Lean
summary: Formalising informal mathematical reasoning into formally verifiable code is a significant challenge for large language models. In scientific fields such as physics, domain-specific machinery (\textit{e.g.} Dirac notation, vector calculus) imposes additional formalisation challenges that modern LLMs and agentic approaches have yet to tackle. To aid autoformalisation in scientific domains, we present FormalScience; a domain-agnostic human-in-the-loop agentic pipeline that enables a single domain expert (without deep formal language experience) to produce \textit{syntactically correct} and \textit{semantically aligned} formal proofs of informal reasoning for low economic cost. Applying FormalScience to physics, we construct FormalPhysics, a dataset of 200 university-level (LaTeX) physics problems and solutions (primarily quantum mechanics and electromagnetism), along with their Lean4 formal representations. Compared to existing formal math benchmarks, FormalPhysics achieves perfect formal validity and exhibits greater statement complexity. We evaluate open-source models and proprietary systems on a statement autoformalisation task on our dataset via zero-shot prompting, self-refinement with error feedback, and a novel multi-stage agentic approach, and explore autoformalisation limitations in modern LLM-based approaches. We provide the first systematic characterisation of semantic drift in physics autoformalisation in terms of concepts such as notational collapse and abstraction elevation which reveals what formal language verifies when full semantic preservation is unattainable. We release the codebase together with an interactive UI-based FormalScience system which facilitates autoformalisation and theorem proving in scientific domains beyond physics.https://github.com/jmeadows17/formal-science

AI.tldr: FormalScience is a domain-agnostic human-in-the-loop agentic pipeline that enables domain experts to produce syntactically correct and semantically aligned formal proofs of informal reasoning, applied to physics to create FormalPhysics dataset with 200 university-level physics problems and their Lean4 formal representations.
AI.motivation: Formalizing informal mathematical reasoning into verifiable code is challenging for LLMs, especially in scientific fields like physics where domain-specific notation (Dirac notation, vector calculus) adds complexity. Existing LLMs and agentic approaches haven't effectively tackled these domain-specific formalization challenges.
AI.method: Presents FormalScience, a domain-agnostic human-in-the-loop agentic pipeline that allows single domain experts (without deep formal language experience) to produce formal proofs. Applied to physics as FormalPhysics, creating dataset of 200 university-level physics problems with Lean4 formal representations. Evaluated models via zero-shot prompting, self-refinement with error feedback, and novel multi-stage agentic approach.
AI.result: FormalPhysics achieves perfect formal validity and greater statement complexity compared to existing formal math benchmarks. Provides first systematic characterization of semantic drift in physics autoformalization, identifying concepts like notational collapse and abstraction elevation. Released codebase with interactive UI-based system for scientific domains beyond physics.
AI.conclusion: FormalScience enables efficient production of formally verifiable proofs in scientific domains, reveals limitations in current LLM-based autoformalization approaches, and provides framework for understanding what formal language verifies when full semantic preservation is unattainable.

title: FastOMOP: A Foundational Architecture for Reliable Agentic Real-World Evidence Generation on OMOP CDM data
summary: The Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership Common Data Model (OMOP CDM), maintained by the Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI) collaboration, enabled the harmonisation of electronic health records data of nearly one billion patients in 83 countries. Yet generating real-world evidence (RWE) from these repositories remains a manual process requiring clinical, epidemiological and technical expertise. LLMs and multi-agent systems have shown promise for clinical tasks, but RWE automation exposes a fundamental challenge: agentic systems introduce emergent behaviours, coordination failures and safety risks that existing approaches fail to govern. No infrastructure exists to ensure agentic RWE generation is flexible, safe and auditable across the lifecycle. We introduce FastOMOP, an open-source multi-agent architecture that addresses this gap by separating three infrastructure layers, governance, observability and orchestration, from pluggable agent-teams. Governance is enforced at the process boundary through deterministic validation independent of agent reasoning, ensuring no compromised or hallucinating agent can bypass safety controls. Agent teams for phenotyping, study design and statistical analysis inherit these guarantees through controlled tool exposure. We validated FastOMOP using a natural-language-to-SQL agent team across three OMOP CDM datasets: synthetic data from Synthea, MIMIC-IV and a real-world NHS dataset from Lancashire Teaching Hospitals (IDRIL). FastOMOP achieved reliability scores of 0.84-0.94 with perfect adversarial and out-of-scope block rates, demonstrating process-boundary governance delivers safety guarantees independent of model choice. These results indicate that the reliability gap in RWE deployment is architectural rather than model capability, and establish FastOMOP as a governed architecture for progressive RWE automation.

AI.tldr: FastOMOP introduces an open-source multi-agent architecture with governance, observability, and orchestration layers to enable safe and auditable automation of real-world evidence generation from OMOP CDM data, addressing reliability gaps through process-boundary governance rather than model improvements.
AI.motivation: While OMOP CDM has harmonized EHR data for nearly a billion patients, generating real-world evidence from these repositories remains manual and requires multiple expertise domains. Current LLM and multi-agent approaches for clinical tasks face fundamental challenges including emergent behaviors, coordination failures, and safety risks that existing governance approaches cannot address.
AI.method: FastOMOP separates infrastructure into three layers (governance, observability, orchestration) from pluggable agent-teams. Governance is enforced at process boundaries through deterministic validation independent of agent reasoning. Agent teams for phenotyping, study design, and statistical analysis inherit safety guarantees through controlled tool exposure. The system was validated using natural-language-to-SQL agent teams across three OMOP datasets: Synthea synthetic data, MIMIC-IV, and a real-world NHS dataset.
AI.result: FastOMOP achieved reliability scores of 0.84-0.94 with perfect adversarial and out-of-scope block rates across all three datasets. This demonstrates that process-boundary governance delivers safety guarantees independent of model choice and that compromised or hallucinating agents cannot bypass safety controls.
AI.conclusion: The reliability gap in real-world evidence deployment is architectural rather than a model capability limitation. FastOMOP establishes a governed architecture for progressive RWE automation that ensures flexible, safe, and auditable agentic systems across their lifecycle, independent of the underlying language models used.

title: CoFi-PGMA: Counterfactual Policy Gradients under Filtered Feedback for Multi-Agent LLMs
summary: Large language model (LLM) deployments increasingly rely on multi-agent architectures in which multiple models either compete through routing mechanisms or collaborate to produce a final answer. In both settings, the learning signal received by each agent is filtered by the system mechanism. Routing produces selection-gated feedback where only the chosen response is evaluated, while collaboration produces shared rewards that obscure the individual contribution of each agent. As a result, standard RLHF objectives designed for a single deployed policy become misspecified. We introduce CoFi-PGMA (Counterfactual Policy Gradients under Filtered Feedback for Multi-Agent LLMs), a unified framework for learning under filtered feedback in multi-agent LLM systems. Our approach derives a counterfactual per-agent training objective based on marginal contribution, which corrects the learning signal under both routing and collaborative mechanisms. For routing systems, the objective corresponds to off-policy corrections for selection-gated feedback, while for collaborative systems it reduces to leave-one-out difference rewards for credit assignment. We further analyze how softmax routing induces risk-sensitive incentives and provide practical training algorithms that integrate counterfactual estimators, multiturn-aware rewards, and policy optimization methods, and demonstrate the approach on a real-world reasoning dataset.

AI.tldr: CoFi-PGMA is a framework for training multi-agent LLM systems under filtered feedback (routing selection or collaboration rewards), using counterfactual objectives based on marginal contribution to correct learning signals.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent LLM systems (routing and collaboration) suffer from filtered feedback - routing provides selection-gated feedback where only chosen responses are evaluated, and collaboration produces shared rewards that obscure individual contributions. Standard RLHF objectives designed for single policies become misspecified in these settings.
AI.method: CoFi-PGMA introduces a unified framework using counterfactual policy gradients. It derives per-agent training objectives based on marginal contribution: for routing systems, this provides off-policy corrections for selection-gated feedback; for collaborative systems, it reduces to leave-one-out difference rewards for credit assignment. Includes analysis of softmax routing incentives and practical algorithms integrating counterfactual estimators, multiturn-aware rewards, and policy optimization methods.
AI.result: Demonstrated on a real-world reasoning dataset. The framework addresses filtered feedback challenges in both routing (corrects selection bias) and collaboration (enables credit assignment) systems.
AI.conclusion: CoFi-PGMA provides a principled solution to learning under filtered feedback in multi-agent LLM systems, unifying treatment of routing and collaboration mechanisms through counterfactual objectives based on marginal contribution, enabling effective training in real-world deployments.

title: A Systematic Approach for Large Language Models Debugging
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become central to modern AI workflows, powering applications from open-ended text generation to complex agent-based reasoning. However, debugging these models remains a persistent challenge due to their opaque and probabilistic nature and the difficulty of diagnosing errors across diverse tasks and settings. This paper introduces a systematic approach for LLM debugging that treats models as observable systems, providing structured, model-agnostic methods from issue detection to model refinement. By unifying evaluation, interpretability, and error-analysis practices, our approach enables practitioners to iteratively diagnose model weaknesses, refine prompts and model parameters, and adapt data for fine-tuning or assessment, while remaining effective in contexts where standardized benchmarks and evaluation criteria are lacking. We argue that such a structured methodology not only accelerates troubleshooting but also fosters reproducibility, transparency, and scalability in the deployment of LLM-based systems.

AI.tldr: A systematic methodology for debugging large language models by treating them as observable systems with structured, model-agnostic approaches that unify evaluation, interpretability, and error analysis.
AI.motivation: LLMs have become central to AI workflows but remain difficult to debug due to their opaque, probabilistic nature and the challenge of diagnosing errors across diverse tasks and settings. Current debugging lacks systematic approaches, especially in contexts without standardized benchmarks.
AI.method: Introduces a systematic approach that treats LLMs as observable systems, providing structured, model-agnostic methods spanning from issue detection to model refinement. Unifies evaluation, interpretability, and error-analysis practices to enable iterative diagnosis.
AI.result: Enables practitioners to iteratively diagnose model weaknesses, refine prompts and parameters, and adapt data for fine-tuning or assessment. The approach remains effective even in contexts lacking standardized benchmarks and evaluation criteria.
AI.conclusion: A structured LLM debugging methodology accelerates troubleshooting and fosters reproducibility, transparency, and scalability in deploying LLM-based systems, addressing a critical gap in current AI workflows.

title: AutoCompress: Critical Layer Isolation for Efficient Transformer Compression
summary: We present AutoCompress, a transformer compression method motivated by an empirical finding: in small transformers, Layer 0 carries disproportionately high task-critical information, with an NTK-based importance score of 3.6 compared to a maximum of 0.054 for all other layers -- a gap of over 60x. Based on this finding, we propose Critical Layer Isolation (CLI), an architecture that protects Layer 0 at full dimensionality, compresses all intermediate layers through a learned bottleneck, and restores the full dimension at the final layer. Applied to GPT-2 Medium (354.8M parameters), CLI-GPT2 achieves 204.5 perplexity on WikiText-103 with only 143.8M parameters -- a 2.47x compression ratio and 59.5% parameter reduction. Crucially, an ablation study demonstrates that a uniform bottleneck baseline of comparable size achieves only 571.8 perplexity under identical training conditions, confirming that the architectural decision to protect Layer 0 -- rather than simply reducing model size -- is the primary driver of performance. Code and checkpoints are publicly available.

AI.tldr: AutoCompress identifies Layer 0 in small transformers carries critical information (60x more important than other layers), proposes Critical Layer Isolation architecture that protects Layer 0 while compressing intermediate layers, achieving 2.47x compression on GPT-2 Medium with much better perplexity than uniform compression.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by an empirical discovery that in small transformers, Layer 0 carries disproportionately high task-critical information compared to other layers - with an importance score gap of over 60x. This observation suggests that naively compressing all layers uniformly would likely degrade performance significantly.
AI.method: The method proposes Critical Layer Isolation (CLI), which protects Layer 0 at full dimensionality, compresses all intermediate layers through a learned bottleneck (reducing their dimensions), and restores the full dimension at the final layer. This architecture selectively preserves the most critical information while compressing less important layers.
AI.result: Applied to GPT-2 Medium (354.8M parameters), CLI-GPT2 achieves 204.5 perplexity on WikiText-103 with only 143.8M parameters - a 2.47x compression ratio and 59.5% parameter reduction. An ablation study shows a uniform bottleneck baseline of comparable size achieves only 571.8 perplexity, confirming the importance of protecting Layer 0.
AI.conclusion: The architectural decision to protect Layer 0 - rather than simply reducing model size uniformly - is the primary driver of performance in transformer compression. The method demonstrates that understanding layer importance distribution enables more effective compression strategies.

title: A Decoupled Human-in-the-Loop System for Controlled Autonomy in Agentic Workflows
summary:
AI agents are increasingly deployed to execute tasks and make decisions within agentic workflows, introducing new requirements for safe and controlled autonomy. Prior work has established the importance of human oversight for ensuring transparency, accountability, and trustworthiness in such systems. However, existing implementations of Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) mechanisms are typically embedded within application logic, limiting reuse, consistency, and scalability across multi-agent environments.
  This paper presents a decoupled HITL system architecture that treats human oversight as an independent system component within the agent operating environment. The proposed design separates human interaction management from application workflows through explicit interfaces and a structured execution model. In addition, a design framework is introduced to formalize HITL integration along four dimensions: intervention conditions, role resolution, interaction semantics, and communication channel. This framework enables selective and context-aware human involvement while maintaining system-level consistency.
  The approach supports alignment with emerging agent communication protocols, allowing HITL to be implemented as a protocol-level concern. By externalizing HITL and structuring its integration, the system provides a foundation for scalable governance and progressive autonomy in agentic workflows.

AI.tldr: A decoupled Human-in-the-Loop system architecture for AI agent workflows that treats human oversight as an independent component, separating it from application logic through explicit interfaces and structured execution models.
AI.motivation: Current HITL implementations are embedded within application logic, limiting reuse, consistency, and scalability across multi-agent environments. There's a need for better human oversight mechanisms in AI agent workflows to ensure safety, transparency, accountability, and trustworthiness.
AI.method: Proposes a decoupled HITL system architecture with explicit interfaces and structured execution model. Introduces a design framework formalizing HITL integration along four dimensions: intervention conditions, role resolution, interaction semantics, and communication channel.
