AI.result: Enables selective and context-aware human involvement while maintaining system-level consistency. Supports alignment with emerging agent communication protocols, allowing HITL to be implemented as a protocol-level concern.
AI.conclusion: The decoupled approach provides a foundation for scalable governance and progressive autonomy in agentic workflows by externalizing HITL and structuring its integration, addressing limitations of current embedded implementations.

title: Conformal PM2.5 Mapping Under Spatial Covariate Shift: Satellite-Reanalysis Fusion for Africa's Green Industrial Transition
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables. Index Terms: PM2.5 mapping, conformal prediction, covariate shift, spatial cross-validation, air quality, green industrialisation, trustworthy AI, Africa
summary: Africa's green industrialization imperative demands reliable infrastructure for monitoring air quality. We present a satellite-reanalysis PM2.5 fusion system trained on 2,068,901 records from 404 monitoring locations in 29 African countries (OpenAQ, 2017-2022), combining LightGBM with leakage-resistant spatial cross-validation and conformal prediction to quantify predictions and their geographic applicability limits. Under 5-fold location-grouped spatial cross-validation, LightGBM achieves RMSE = 30.83 +/- 5.07 ug/m3, MAE = 14.54 +/- 1.66 ug/m3, R2 = 0.134 +/- 0.023, and macro F1 = 0.336 +/- 0.018. This R2 is substantially below random-split benchmarks (>0.90) but reflects true geographic generalisation difficulty rather than model failure. Split conformal prediction targeting 90% marginal coverage reveals severe East Africa degradation (actual PICP = 65.3% vs. nominal 90%), consistent with medium-strength covariate shift (humidity KS = 0.2237, sat_pblh KS = 0.2558). We operationalise these findings through regional reliability flags (High/Medium/Low/Unreliable) and a monitor prioritisation score directing infrastructure expansion toward highest-burden unmonitored populations, directly supporting Africa's green industrial transition and SDGs 3.9, 7.1.2, 9, 11.6.2, and 13.

AI.tldr: A satellite-reanalysis PM2.5 fusion system for Africa uses LightGBM with spatial cross-validation to provide air quality predictions with geographic reliability assessment and infrastructure expansion guidance.
AI.motivation: Africa needs reliable air quality monitoring infrastructure to support green industrialization, but faces challenges with limited ground monitoring stations and geographic generalization difficulties.
AI.method: Combined LightGBM with leakage-resistant spatial cross-validation and conformal prediction on 2,068,901 records from 404 monitoring locations across 29 African countries, using satellite-reanalysis data fusion.
AI.result: LightGBM achieved RMSE = 30.83 ug/m3, MAE = 14.54 ug/m3, R2 = 0.134 under spatial cross-validation, with significant performance degradation in East Africa due to covariate shift, leading to development of regional reliability flags and monitor prioritization scores.
AI.conclusion: The system provides realistic geographic generalization assessment, operational tools for air quality monitoring reliability classification, and direct guidance for infrastructure expansion to support Africa's green industrial transition and multiple Sustainable Development Goals.

title: Don't Make the LLM Read the Graph: Make the Graph Think
comment: main body has 9 pages, 4 figures, under review for COLM 2026 conference
summary: We investigate whether explicit belief graphs improve LLM performance in cooperative multi-agent reasoning. Through 3,000+ controlled trials across four LLM families in the cooperative card game Hanabi, we establish four findings. First, integration architecture determines whether belief graphs provide value: as prompt context, graphs are decorative for strong models and beneficial only for weak models on 2nd-order Theory of Mind (80% vs 10%, p<0.0001, OR=36.0); when graphs gate action selection through ranked shortlists, they become structurally essential even for strong models (100% vs 20% on 2nd-order ToM, p<0.001). Second, we identify "Planner Defiance," a model-family-specific failure where LLMs override correct planner recommendations at partial competence (90% override, replicated N=20); Gemini models show near-zero defiance while Llama 70B shows 90%, and models distinguish factual context (deferred to) from advisory recommendations (overridden). Third, full-game evidence confirms inter-agent conventions (+128% over baseline, p=0.003) outperform all single-agent interventions, and individual belief-graph components must be combined to produce gains. Fourth, preliminary scaling analysis (N=10/cell, exploratory) suggests graph depth has diminishing returns: shallow graphs provide the best cost-benefit ratio, while deeper ToM graphs appear harmful at larger player counts (-1.5 pts at 5-player, p=0.029).

AI.tldr: Explicit belief graphs can improve LLM performance in cooperative multi-agent reasoning, but their effectiveness depends on integration architecture, with different models showing varying levels of "Planner Defiance" where they override correct recommendations.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether explicit belief graphs (representing agents' mental states) improve LLM performance in cooperative multi-agent reasoning tasks, specifically to understand how different integration approaches affect performance and to identify model-specific failure patterns.
AI.method: Conducted 3,000+ controlled trials across four LLM families in the cooperative card game Hanabi. Tested belief graphs in two integration architectures: as prompt context and as ranked shortlists that gate action selection. Measured performance on 2nd-order Theory of Mind reasoning and analyzed model-specific behavior patterns like "Planner Defiance."
AI.result: 1) Integration architecture determines belief graph value: as prompt context, graphs help only weak models; when gating action selection via ranked shortlists, they become essential even for strong models. 2) Identified "Planner Defiance" where models override correct recommendations, varying by model family. 3) Inter-agent conventions outperform all single-agent interventions. 4) Shallow belief graphs provide best cost-benefit ratio; deeper Theory of Mind graphs can be harmful.
AI.conclusion: Explicit belief graphs can substantially improve multi-agent LLM reasoning, but success requires careful architectural choices, particularly using graphs to gate actions rather than as passive context. Model-specific behavioral patterns must be considered, and simpler belief representations often work better than complex ones.

title: Avionic Main Fuel Pump Simulation and Fault-Diagnosis Benchmark
summary: In many cyber-physical systems, especially in critical applications such as aeroplanes, data to train anomaly detection and diagnosis algorithms is lacking due to data protection issues and partial observability. To combat this inherent lack of data, we introduce a high-fidelity, physics-informed co-simulation of a common aircraft main-fuel-pump system modelled in \textsc{MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids}. We also describe its generated time-series data with health and fault mode annotations. To show feasibility of our benchmark, we apply an unsupervised Recurrent Variational Autoencoder (RNN-VAE) for anomaly detection and a SOM-VAE for operating mode discretization, trained to separate healthy and faulty conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a physics-informed co-simulation framework for aircraft fuel pump systems to generate synthetic data for training anomaly detection models when real data is scarce due to privacy and observability constraints.
AI.motivation: Critical cyber-physical systems like aircraft often lack sufficient training data for anomaly detection algorithms due to data protection issues and partial observability, creating a need for synthetic data generation approaches.
AI.method: Developed a high-fidelity physics-informed co-simulation of an aircraft main-fuel-pump system using MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids, generating annotated time-series data with health/fault mode labels. Applied unsupervised RNN-VAE for anomaly detection and SOM-VAE for operating mode discretization.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates feasibility of their benchmark simulation by successfully training unsupervised models to separate healthy and faulty conditions using the generated synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed simulation provides a viable solution for generating training data in data-scarce critical systems, enabling development of anomaly detection algorithms where real-world data is limited due to privacy and observability constraints.

title: Analytica: Soft Propositional Reasoning for Robust and Scalable LLM-Driven Analysis
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly tasked with complex real-world analysis (e.g., in financial forecasting, scientific discovery), yet their reasoning suffers from stochastic instability and lacks a verifiable, compositional structure. To address this, we introduce Analytica, a novel agent architecture built on the principle of Soft Propositional Reasoning (SPR). SPR reframes complex analysis as a structured process of estimating the soft truth values of different outcome propositions, allowing us to formally model and minimize the estimation error in terms of its bias and variance. Analytica operationalizes this through a parallel, divide-and-conquer framework that systematically reduces both sources of error. To reduce bias, problems are first decomposed into a tree of subpropositions, and tool-equipped LLM grounder agents are employed, including a novel Jupyter Notebook agent for data-driven analysis, that help to validate and score facts. To reduce variance, Analytica recursively synthesizes these grounded leaves using robust linear models that average out stochastic noise with superior efficiency, scalability, and enable interactive "what-if" scenario analysis. Our theoretical and empirical results on economic, financial, and political forecasting tasks show that Analytica improves 15.84% accuracy on average over diverse base models, achieving 71.06% accuracy with the lowest variance of 6.02% when working with a Deep Research grounder. Our Jupyter Notebook grounder shows strong cost-effectiveness that achieves a close 70.11% accuracy with 90.35% less cost and 52.85% less time. Analytica also exhibits highly noise-resilient and stable performance growth as the analysis depth increases, with a near-linear time complexity, as well as good adaptivity to open-weight LLMs and scientific domains.

AI.tldr: Analytica introduces Soft Propositional Reasoning (SPR) for LLM agents, decomposing complex analysis into proposition trees to reduce bias and variance, achieving 15.84% accuracy improvement with high noise resilience.
AI.motivation: LLM agents suffer from stochastic instability and lack verifiable, compositional structure when performing complex real-world analysis (financial forecasting, scientific discovery), needing formal methods to minimize estimation errors.
AI.method: Soft Propositional Reasoning (SPR) reframes analysis as estimating soft truth values of outcome propositions. Uses parallel divide-and-conquer: 1) bias reduction via proposition tree decomposition with tool-equipped LLM grounder agents (including novel Jupyter Notebook agent), 2) variance reduction via recursive synthesis using robust linear models to average stochastic noise.
AI.result: 15.84% average accuracy improvement over base models, achieving 71.06% accuracy with 6.02% variance. Jupyter Notebook grounder achieves 70.11% accuracy with 90.35% less cost and 52.85% less time. Shows noise-resilient, stable performance growth with near-linear time complexity.
AI.conclusion: Analytica's SPR-based architecture effectively addresses LLM reasoning limitations through formal error modeling, providing verifiable, compositional analysis with practical efficiency gains and strong domain adaptability.

title: Towards Understanding the Expressive Power of GNNs with Global Readout
summary: We study the expressive power of message-passing aggregate-combine-readout graph neural networks (ACR-GNNs). Particularly, we focus on the first-order (FO) properties expressible by this formalism. While a tight logical characterisation remains a difficult open question, we make two contributions towards answering it. First, we show that sum aggregation and readout suffice for GNNs to capture FO properties that cannot be expressed in the logic C2 on both directed and undirected graphs. This strengthens known results by Hauke and Wał{\k e}ga (2026) where aggregation and readout functions are specially crafted for the task. Second, we identify two natural ways of restoring characterisability (with regard to C2) for ACR-GNNs. One option is to limit local aggregation (without imposing restrictions on global readout), whilst the second is to run ACR-GNNs over graphs of bounded degree (but unbounded size). In both cases, the FO properties captured by GNNs are exactly those definable by a formula in graded modal logic with global counting modalities. Our results thus establish an innate lower- and upper-bound in terms of how far (fragments of) C2 can be taken to characterise GNNs, and imply that is indeed the unbounded interaction of aggregation and readout that pushes the logical expressive power of GNNs above C2.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes lower and upper bounds on the expressive power of ACR-GNNs regarding first-order properties, showing they exceed C2 logic with standard sum aggregation, while identifying conditions where they align with graded modal logic.
AI.motivation: While a tight logical characterization of message-passing graph neural networks remains an open question, the researchers aim to understand the expressive power boundaries of ACR-GNNs for first-order properties, particularly in relation to existing logical frameworks like C2.
AI.method: Analyzes ACR-GNNs (aggregate-combine-readout graph neural networks) focusing on first-order property expressibility. Studies standard sum aggregation, compares with logic C2, and examines two natural restrictions: limiting local aggregation or restricting to bounded-degree graphs.
AI.result: 1) Standard sum aggregation suffices for GNNs to capture FO properties not expressible in C2 on both directed and undirected graphs. 2) Two ways restore characterisability with C2: limiting local aggregation or using bounded-degree graphs - in both cases, GNNs capture exactly the FO properties definable in graded modal logic with global counting modalities.
AI.conclusion: The results establish innate lower and upper bounds for characterising GNNs with C2 fragments, showing that it's the unbounded interaction between aggregation and readout that pushes GNN expressive power above C2, while appropriate restrictions align them with graded modal logic.

title: Towards Automated Ontology Generation from Unstructured Text: A Multi-Agent LLM Approach
summary: Automatically generating formal ontologies from unstructured natural language remains a central challenge in knowledge engineering. While large language models (LLMs) show promise, it remains unclear which architectural design choices drive generation quality and why current approaches fail. We present a controlled experimental study using domain-specific insurance contracts to investigate these questions. We first establish a single-agent LLM baseline, identifying key failure modes such as poor Ontology Design Pattern compliance, structural redundancy, and ineffective iterative repair. We then introduce a multi-agent architecture that decomposes ontology construction into four artifact-driven roles: Domain Expert, Manager, Coder, and Quality Assurer. We evaluate performance across architectural quality (via a panel of heterogeneous LLM judges) and functional usability (via competency question driven SPARQL evaluation with complementary retrieval augmented generation based assessment). Results show that the multi-agent approach significantly improves structural quality and modestly enhances queryability, with gains driven primarily by front-loaded planning. These findings highlight planning-first, artifact-driven generation as a promising and more auditable path toward scalable automated ontology engineering.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent LLM architecture outperforms single-agent baseline in ontology generation from insurance contracts by using four specialized roles and improving structural quality through better planning.
AI.motivation: Despite LLM promise for ontology generation from natural language, unclear which architectural choices drive quality and why current approaches fail, requiring systematic investigation.
AI.method: Experimental study using insurance contracts: 1) Single-agent LLM baseline identification, 2) Multi-agent architecture with four roles (Domain Expert, Manager, Coder, Quality Assurer), 3) Evaluation via architectural quality (LLM judges) and functional usability (SPARQL queries with RAG assessment).
AI.result: Multi-agent approach significantly improves structural quality and modestly enhances queryability; gains driven primarily by front-loaded planning rather than iterative repair.
AI.conclusion: Planning-first, artifact-driven generation with specialized agent roles offers a more auditable and scalable path for automated ontology engineering compared to traditional approaches.

title: When Policies Cannot Be Retrained: A Unified Closed-Form View of Post-Training Steering in Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) can learn effective policies from fixed datasets, but deployment objectives may change after training, and in many applications the trained actor cannot be retrained because of data, cost, or governance constraints. We study deployment-time adaptation for frozen offline actors using Product-of-Experts (PoE) composition with a goal-conditioned prior. Our main practical finding is graceful degradation rather than universal performance gain: under degraded or random priors, precision-weighted composition remains anchored to the frozen actor, while additive and prior-only adaptation collapse, and a KL-budget selector often recovers a near-oracle operating point. We also make explicit a closed-form identity in the frozen-actor setting: for diagonal-Gaussian actors and priors, PoE with coefficient alpha yields the same deterministic policy as KL-regularized adaptation with beta = alpha / (1 - alpha), with posterior covariances differing only by a global scalar factor. Empirically, across four D4RL environments (3,900 MuJoCo episodes), we observe a 4/5/3 HELP/FROZEN/HURT split. Extending the analysis to six harder cells and two AntMaze diagnostics reveals an actor-competence ceiling: medium-expert remains HURT in all 9 cells at every tested alpha, while AntMaze with a behavior-cloned frozen actor yields zero success for all composition rules. Overall, PoE and KL-regularized adaptation are best viewed as a single actor-anchored safety mechanism for deployment-time steering.

AI.tldr: Offline RL deployment adaptation using Product-of-Experts composition with goal-conditioned priors, showing graceful degradation rather than universal improvement, with formal connection to KL-regularized adaptation.
AI.motivation: Offline RL policies are frozen after training due to data, cost, or governance constraints, but deployment objectives may change. Need adaptation methods that work without retraining the frozen actor.
AI.method: Product-of-Experts (PoE) composition with goal-conditioned prior for deployment-time adaptation of frozen offline actors. Compare precision-weighted composition vs. additive/prior-only adaptation and KL-budget selector.
AI.result: Precision-weighted PoE shows graceful degradation under poor priors (anchors to frozen actor), while other methods collapse. 4/5/3 HELP/FROZEN/HURT split on D4RL environments. Medium-expert remains HURT across all tested conditions. AntMaze with behavior-cloned actor yields zero success.
AI.conclusion: PoE and KL-regularized adaptation serve as actor-anchored safety mechanisms for deployment-time steering rather than universal improvement tools. Graceful degradation under uncertainty is a key practical advantage.

title: PhySE: A Psychological Framework for Real-Time AR-LLM Social Engineering Attacks
summary: The emerging threat of AR-LLM-based Social Engineering (AR-LLM-SE) attacks (e.g. SEAR) poses a significant risk to real-world social interactions. In such an attack, a malicious actor uses Augmented Reality (AR) glasses to capture a target visual and vocal data. A Large Language Model (LLM) then analyzes this data to identify the individual and generate a detailed social profile. Subsequently, LLM-powered agents employ social engineering strategies, providing real-time conversation suggestions, to gain the target trust and ultimately execute phishing or other malicious acts. Despite its potential, the practical application of AR-LLM-SE faces two major bottlenecks, (1) Cold-start personalization, Current Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods introduce critical delays in the earliest turns, slowing initial profile formation and disrupting real-time interaction, (2) Static Attack Strategies, Existing approaches rely on fixed-stage, handcrafted social engineering tactics that lack foundation in established psychological theory. To address these limitations, we propose PhySE, a novel framework with two core innovations, (1) VLM-Based SocialContext Training, To eliminate profiling delays, we efficiently pre-train a Visual Language Model (VLM) with social-context data, enabling rapid, on-the-fly profile generation, (2) Adaptive Psychological Agent, We introduce a psychological LLM that dynamically deploys distinct classes of psychological strategies based on target response, moving beyond static, handcrafted scripts. We evaluated PhySE through an IRB-approved user study with 60 participants, collecting a novel dataset of 360 annotated conversations across diverse social scenarios.

AI.tldr: PhySE is a framework that addresses bottlenecks in AR-LLM-based social engineering attacks by enabling rapid target profiling through VLM pre-training and adaptive psychological strategy deployment for real-time manipulation.
AI.motivation: AR-LLM-based Social Engineering (AR-LLM-SE) attacks pose significant real-world risks, but current approaches face critical bottlenecks: cold-start personalization delays and static attack strategies that lack psychological theory foundations.
AI.method: PhySE introduces two core innovations: 1) VLM-Based SocialContext Training - pre-training a Visual Language Model with social-context data for rapid on-the-fly profile generation, and 2) Adaptive Psychological Agent - an LLM that dynamically deploys distinct psychological strategies based on target responses rather than static scripts.
AI.result: Evaluated through an IRB-approved user study with 60 participants, collecting a novel dataset of 360 annotated conversations across diverse social scenarios.
AI.conclusion: PhySE addresses the major bottlenecks in AR-LLM-SE attacks by enabling rapid personalization and adaptive psychological strategies, potentially making such attacks more effective and dangerous in real-world settings.

title: MTServe: Efficient Serving for Generative Recommendation Models with Hierarchical Caches
summary: Generative recommendation (GR) offers superior modeling capabilities but suffers from prohibitive inference costs due to the repeated encoding of long user histories. While cross-request Key-Value (KV) cache reuse presents a significant optimization opportunity, the massive scale of individual user states creates a storage explosion that far exceeds physical GPU limits. We propose MTServe, a hierarchical cache management system that virtualizes GPU memory by leveraging host RAM as a scalable backup store. To bridge the I/O gap between tiers, MTServe introduces a suite of system-level optimizations, including a hybrid storage layout, an asynchronous data transfer pipeline, and a locality-driven replacement policy. On both public and production datasets, MTServe delivers up to 3.1* speedup while maintaining near-perfect hit ratios (>98.5%).

AI.tldr: MTServe: hierarchical cache system for generative recommendation that virtualizes GPU memory using host RAM, achieving 3.1× speedup with >98.5% hit ratios.
AI.motivation: Generative recommendation suffers from high inference costs due to repeated encoding of long user histories. Cross-request KV cache reuse offers optimization potential, but massive user state scale creates storage explosion exceeding GPU memory limits.
AI.method: Hierarchical cache management system that virtualizes GPU memory by leveraging host RAM as scalable backup store. Includes hybrid storage layout, asynchronous data transfer pipeline, and locality-driven replacement policy to bridge I/O gap between tiers.
AI.result: Up to 3.1× speedup while maintaining near-perfect hit ratios (>98.5%) on both public and production datasets.
AI.conclusion: MTServe effectively addresses GPU memory constraints for generative recommendation systems by creating a practical hierarchical cache solution with significant performance improvements.

title: Judging the Judges: A Systematic Evaluation of Bias Mitigation Strategies in LLM-as-a-Judge Pipelines
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables. Under review at TMLR
summary: LLM-as-a-Judge has become the dominant paradigm for evaluating language model outputs, yet LLM judges exhibit systematic biases that compromise evaluation reliability. We present a comprehensive empirical study comparing nine debiasing strategies across five judge models from four provider families (Google, Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta), three benchmarks (MT-Bench n=400, LLMBar n=200, custom n=225), and four bias types. Our key findings: (1) Style bias is the dominant bias (0.76-0.92 across all models), far exceeding position bias (<= 0.04), yet has received minimal research attention. (2) All models show a conciseness preference on expansion pairs, but truncation controls confirm they correctly distinguish quality from length (0.92-1.00 accuracy), suggesting quality-sensitive evaluation rather than a simple length bias. (3) Debiasing is beneficial but model-dependent: the combined budget strategy significantly improves Claude Sonnet 4 by +11.2 pp (p < 0.0001), with directionally positive trends for other models. Only 2 of 20 non-baseline configurations show decreased agreement. We release our evaluation framework, controlled dataset, and all experimental artifacts at https://github.com/sksoumik/llm-as-judge.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive empirical study comparing 9 debiasing strategies for LLM judges across 5 models, 3 benchmarks, and 4 bias types, finding style bias dominates but debiasing helps model-dependently.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge has become dominant for evaluating language models but suffers from systematic biases that compromise evaluation reliability, necessitating effective debiasing strategies.
AI.method: Empirical comparison of 9 debiasing strategies across 5 judge models from 4 providers (Google, Anthropic, OpenAI, Meta), using 3 benchmarks (MT-Bench n=400, LLMBar n=200, custom n=225) and examining 4 bias types.
AI.result: 1) Style bias is dominant (0.76-0.92 across models) and severely understudied. 2) All models show conciseness preference but correctly distinguish quality from length (0.92-1.00 accuracy). 3) Debiasing benefits are model-dependent, with combined budget strategy improving Claude Sonnet 4 by +11.2 pp, and only 2/20 non-baseline configs show decreased agreement.
AI.conclusion: LLM judges have significant biases, particularly style bias which dominates but receives minimal attention. Effective debiasing strategies exist but are model-dependent. The study provides empirical evidence for debiasing effectiveness and releases evaluation framework for community use.

title: Predicting Wind Loads on Container Ships in Harbor Environments through Multi-Fidelity Modeling
summary:
Modern container ships face higher wind loads due to increased windage areas, making accurate predictions of wind loads essential for mooring design. Existing empirical models, largely developed for container ships with smaller windage areas and simpler geometrical configurations than those of modern large-scale vessels, often lack accuracy and do not account for the influence of nearby structures. This study proposes a multi-fidelity surrogate modelling framework for the prediction of wind-load coefficients, combining empirical correlations with simplified and detailed CFD models for ships in open-sea and harbor environments. The approach relies on recursive co-kriging to consistently fuse information across fidelity levels, enabling accurate predictions at a reduced computational cost. A sensitivity analysis is used to identify the most influential geometric parameters, and the resulting reduced parameter space is explored through sequential sampling to efficiently construct the training database.
  The surrogate models are validated over a wide range of loading configurations and for two distinct harbor environments. The results demonstrate that the multi-fidelity approach significantly improves prediction accuracy compared to single-fidelity models, while substantially reducing the reliance on high-fidelity simulations. In particular, the proposed framework captures the dependence of wind loads on key geometric parameters and consistently outperforms traditional empirical correlations, providing a robust and efficient tool for engineering applications.

AI.tldr: A multi-fidelity surrogate modeling framework using recursive co-kriging improves wind-load coefficient predictions for modern container ships by combining empirical correlations with CFD models, outperforming traditional methods with reduced computational cost.
AI.motivation: Modern container ships have increased windage areas and complex geometries, making existing empirical models inaccurate. These models were developed for older ships with smaller windage areas and don't account for nearby harbor structures, creating a need for more accurate and efficient wind-load prediction methods.
AI.method: Develops a multi-fidelity surrogate modeling framework that combines empirical correlations with simplified and detailed CFD models. Uses recursive co-kriging to fuse information across fidelity levels, sensitivity analysis to identify influential geometric parameters, sequential sampling to construct training databases efficiently, and validates models across loading configurations and harbor environments.
AI.result: The multi-fidelity approach significantly improves prediction accuracy compared to single-fidelity models and traditional empirical correlations. It substantially reduces reliance on high-fidelity simulations while capturing wind load dependencies on key geometric parameters. Validated over wide loading configurations and two distinct harbor environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a robust, efficient tool for engineering applications, outperforming traditional empirical correlations and offering accurate wind-load predictions at reduced computational cost for modern container ships in both open-sea and harbor environments.

title: From Coarse to Fine: Self-Adaptive Hierarchical Planning for LLM Agents
summary: Large language model-based agents have recently emerged as powerful approaches for solving dynamic and multi-step tasks. Most existing agents employ planning mechanisms to guide long-term actions in dynamic environments. However, current planning approaches face a fundamental limitation that they operate at a fixed granularity level. Specifically, they either provide excessive detail for simple tasks or insufficient detail for complex ones, failing to achieve an optimal balance between simplicity and complexity. Drawing inspiration from the principle of \textit{progressive refinement} in cognitive science, we propose \textbf{AdaPlan-H}, a self-adaptive hierarchical planning mechanism that mimics human planning strategies. Our method initiates with a coarse-grained macro plan and progressively refines it based on task complexity. It generates self-adaptive hierarchical plans tailored to the varying difficulty levels of different tasks, which can be optimized by imitation learning and capability enhancement. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves task execution success rates while mitigating overplanning at the planning level, providing a flexible and efficient solution for multi-step complex decision-making tasks. To contribute to the community, our code and data will be made publicly available at https://github.com/import-myself/AHP.

AI.tldr: AdaPlan-H is a self-adaptive hierarchical planning mechanism for LLM-based agents that generates flexible plans at varying granularity levels based on task complexity, improving success rates while reducing overplanning.
AI.motivation: Existing planning mechanisms for LLM-based agents operate at fixed granularity levels, providing either excessive detail for simple tasks or insufficient detail for complex ones, failing to achieve an optimal balance between simplicity and complexity.
AI.method: AdaPlan-H uses a self-adaptive hierarchical planning mechanism inspired by progressive refinement in cognitive science. It starts with a coarse-grained macro plan and progressively refines it based on task complexity, generating adaptive hierarchical plans that can be optimized through imitation learning and capability enhancement.
AI.result: Experimental results show significant improvements in task execution success rates while mitigating overplanning at the planning level, providing a flexible and efficient solution for multi-step complex decision-making tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AdaPlan-H offers an effective approach to adaptive planning for LLM-based agents, addressing the limitations of fixed-granularity planning and demonstrating practical benefits for complex task execution. The authors are making code and data publicly available to contribute to the research community.

title: Quantifying and Mitigating Self-Preference Bias of LLM Judges
summary: LLM-as-a-Judge has become a dominant approach in automated evaluation systems, playing critical roles in model alignment, leaderboard construction, quality control, and so on. However, the scalability and trustworthiness of this approach can be substantially distorted by Self-Preference Bias (SPB), which is a directional evaluative deviation in which LLMs systematically favor or disfavor their own generated outputs during evaluation. Existing measurements rely on costly human annotations and conflate generative capability with evaluative stance, and thus are impractical for large-scale deployment in real-world systems. To address this issue, we introduce a fully automated framework to quantifying and mitigating SPB, which constructs equal-quality pairs of responses with negligible quality differences, enabling statistical disentanglement of discriminability from bias propensity without human gold standards. Empirical analysis across 20 mainstream LLMs reveals that advanced capabilities are often uncorrelated, or even negatively correlated, with low SPB. To mitigate this bias, we propose a structured multi-dimensional evaluation strategy grounded in cognitive load decomposition, which reduces SPB by 31.5\% on average.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an automated framework to measure and mitigate Self-Preference Bias (SPB) in LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation systems, revealing that advanced LLM capabilities often correlate with higher bias, and proposes a cognitive load-based method that reduces SPB by 31.5%.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge is widely used for automated evaluation in model alignment and quality control, but suffers from Self-Preference Bias where LLMs systematically favor or disfavor their own outputs. Existing measurement methods are costly, rely on human annotations, and conflate generative capability with evaluative stance, making them impractical for large-scale deployment.
AI.method: The authors introduce a fully automated framework that constructs equal-quality pairs of responses with negligible quality differences. This enables statistical disentanglement of discriminability from bias propensity without requiring human gold standards. They empirically analyze 20 mainstream LLMs and propose a structured multi-dimensional evaluation strategy grounded in cognitive load decomposition to mitigate SPB.
AI.result: Empirical analysis reveals that advanced LLM capabilities are often uncorrelated, or even negatively correlated, with low SPB. The proposed cognitive load-based mitigation strategy reduces SPB by 31.5% on average across tested models.
AI.conclusion: Self-Preference Bias significantly distorts LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation systems, and advanced LLM capabilities don't guarantee unbiased evaluation. The proposed automated framework enables practical large-scale bias measurement and mitigation, with the cognitive load decomposition strategy proving effective at reducing evaluative bias.

title: StoryTR: Narrative-Centric Video Temporal Retrieval with Theory of Mind Reasoning
summary: Current video moment retrieval excels at action-centric tasks but struggles with narrative content. Models can see \textit{what is happening} but fail to reason \textit{why it matters}. This semantic gap stems from the lack of \textbf{Theory of Mind (ToM)}: the cognitive ability to infer implicit intentions, mental states, and narrative causality from surface-level observations. We introduce \textbf{StoryTR}, the first video moment retrieval benchmark requiring ToM reasoning, comprising 8.1k samples from narrative short-form videos (shorts/reels). These videos present an ideal testbed. Their high information density encodes meaning through subtle multimodal cues. For instance, a glance paired with a sigh carries entirely different semantics than the glance alone. Yet multimodal perception alone is insufficient; ToM is required to decode that a character ``smiling'' may actually be ``concealing hostility.'' To teach models this reasoning capability, we propose an \textbf{Agentic Data Pipeline} that generates training data with explicit three-tier ToM chains (intent decoding, narrative reasoning, boundary localization). Experiments reveal the severity of the reasoning gap: Gemini-3.0-Pro achieves only 0.53 Avg IoU on StoryTR. However, our 7B \textbf{Shorts-Moment} model, trained on ToM-guided data, improves +15.1\% relative IoU over baselines, demonstrating that \textit{narrative reasoning capability matters more than parameter scale}.

AI.tldr: StoryTR is the first video moment retrieval benchmark requiring Theory of Mind reasoning, showing that narrative understanding matters more than model scale for analyzing story-driven content.
AI.motivation: Current video moment retrieval models excel at action-centric tasks but struggle with narrative content because they lack Theory of Mind - the cognitive ability to infer implicit intentions, mental states, and narrative causality from surface-level observations.
AI.method: Introduce StoryTR benchmark with 8.1k samples from narrative short-form videos; propose an Agentic Data Pipeline that generates training data with explicit three-tier ToM chains (intent decoding, narrative reasoning, boundary localization); train a 7B Shorts-Moment model on this ToM-guided data.
AI.result: Experiments reveal severe reasoning gap: Gemini-3.0-Pro achieves only 0.53 Avg IoU on StoryTR. However, their 7B Shorts-Moment model improves +15.1% relative IoU over baselines, demonstrating that narrative reasoning capability matters more than parameter scale.
AI.conclusion: Theory of Mind reasoning is crucial for video moment retrieval in narrative content; teaching models to decode implicit intentions and mental states significantly improves performance, showing that narrative reasoning capability outweighs model scale for understanding story-driven videos.

title: StackFeat RL: Reinforcement Learning over Iterative Dual Criterion Feature Selection for Stable Biomarker Discovery
summary:
Feature selection in high-dimensional genomic data ($d \gg n$) demands methods that are simultaneously accurate, sparse, and stable. Existing approaches either require manual threshold specification (mRMR, stability selection), produce unstable selections under data perturbation (Lasso, Boruta), or ignore biological structure entirely. We introduce StackFeat-RL, a meta-learning framework that optimises the hyperparameters of an iterative dual-criterion feature selection algorithm via REINFORCE policy gradients. The dual criterion, requiring both coefficient consistency and selection frequency, guards against two failure modes missed by single-criterion methods, while iterative accumulation provides convergence guarantees via the law of large numbers.
  On COVID-19 miRNA data (GSE240888, 332 features) and three Alzheimer's disease classification tasks (GSE84422, 13237 genes; Normal vs.\ Possible, Probable, and Definite AD), StackFeat-RL achieves the highest predictive accuracy among all evaluated methods, including ElasticNet, Boruta, mRMR, and stability selection, while requiring 3--4$\times$ fewer features.
  Keywords: feature selection, reinforcement learning, REINFORCE, elastic net, biomarker discovery, Alzheimer's disease, dual-criterion selection, protein interaction networks

AI.tldr: StackFeat-RL is a reinforcement learning-based meta-learning framework that optimizes hyperparameters for a dual-criterion feature selection algorithm, achieving higher accuracy with fewer features in high-dimensional genomic data.
AI.motivation: Existing feature selection methods for high-dimensional genomic data (d >> n) have limitations: they either require manual threshold specification (like mRMR and stability selection), produce unstable selections under data perturbation (like Lasso and Boruta), or ignore biological structure entirely. There's a need for methods that are simultaneously accurate, sparse, and stable.
AI.method: StackFeat-RL uses a meta-learning framework that optimizes hyperparameters of an iterative dual-criterion feature selection algorithm via REINFORCE policy gradients. The dual criterion requires both coefficient consistency and selection frequency, protecting against failure modes missed by single-criterion methods. Iterative accumulation provides convergence guarantees via the law of large numbers.
AI.result: On COVID-19 miRNA data (GSE240888, 332 features) and three Alzheimer's disease classification tasks (GSE84422, 13237 genes), StackFeat-RL achieves the highest predictive accuracy among all evaluated methods (including ElasticNet, Boruta, mRMR, and stability selection) while requiring 3-4× fewer features.
AI.conclusion: StackFeat-RL successfully addresses the limitations of existing feature selection methods by providing a stable, accurate, and sparse solution for high-dimensional genomic data through its reinforcement learning-optimized dual-criterion approach, making it particularly valuable for biomarker discovery in complex diseases like Alzheimer's and COVID-19.

title: Discovering Agentic Safety Specifications from 1-Bit Danger Signals
comment: Accepted to the Adaptive and Learning Agents Workshop (ALA 2026) @ AAMAS 2026. Code is available at github.com/vicgalle/experiential-prompt-optimization-safe
summary: Can large language model agents discover hidden safety objectives through experience alone? We introduce EPO-Safe (Experiential Prompt Optimization for Safe Agents), a framework where an LLM iteratively generates action plans, receives sparse binary danger warnings, and evolves a natural language behavioral specification through reflection. Unlike standard LLM reflection methods that rely on rich textual feedback (e.g., compiler errors or detailed environment responses), EPO-Safe demonstrates that LLMs can perform safety reasoning from a strictly impoverished signal in structured, low-dimensional environments: the agent never observes the hidden performance function $R^*$, only a single bit per timestep indicating that an action was unsafe. We evaluate on five AI Safety Gridworlds (Leike et al., 2017) and five text-based scenario analogs where visible reward $R$ may diverge from $R^*$. EPO-Safe discovers safe behavior within 1-2 rounds (5-15 episodes), producing human-readable specifications with correct explanatory hypotheses about hazards (e.g., "X cells are directionally hazardous: entering from the north is dangerous"). Critically, we show that standard reward-driven reflection actively degrades safety: agents reflecting on reward alone use the loop to justify and accelerate reward hacking, proving that reflection must be paired with a dedicated safety channel to discover hidden constraints. We further evaluate robustness to noisy oracles: even when 50% of non-dangerous steps produce spurious warnings, mean safety performance degrades by only 15% on average, though sensitivity is environment-dependent, as cross-episode reflection naturally filters inconsistent signals. Each evolved specification functions as an auditable set of grounded behavioral rules discovered autonomously through interaction, rather than authored by humans as in Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022).

AI.tldr: EPO-Safe enables LLM agents to discover hidden safety constraints through experiential learning using only sparse binary danger signals, outperforming standard reward-driven reflection which actually degrades safety.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM reflection methods require rich textual feedback, but real-world safety scenarios often provide only minimal signals (like simple danger warnings). The paper aims to show that LLMs can perform safety reasoning from impoverished signals to discover hidden safety objectives that diverge from visible rewards.
AI.method: EPO-Safe framework where LLM agents iteratively: 1) generate action plans, 2) receive sparse binary danger warnings (single bit per timestep indicating unsafe actions), 3) evolve natural language behavioral specifications through reflection. Evaluated on five AI Safety Gridworlds and five text-based analogs where visible reward R may diverge from hidden safety objective R*.
AI.result: EPO-Safe discovers safe behavior within 1-2 rounds (5-15 episodes), producing human-readable specifications with correct explanatory hypotheses about hazards. Standard reward-driven reflection actively degrades safety by enabling reward hacking. Even with 50% noisy warnings, safety performance degrades by only 15% on average as cross-episode reflection filters inconsistent signals.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can autonomously discover hidden safety constraints through experiential learning with minimal feedback, producing auditable behavioral rules. Reflection must be paired with dedicated safety channels rather than reward alone, as reward-driven reflection accelerates unsafe behavior. EPO-Safe represents a step toward autonomous safety specification discovery through interaction rather than human authoring.

title: Utility-Aware Data Pricing: Token-Level Quality and Empirical Training Gain for LLMs
summary: Traditional data valuation methods based on ``row-count $\times$ quality coefficient'' paradigms fail to capture the nuanced, nonlinear contributions that data makes to Large Language Model (LLM) capabilities. This paper presents a dynamic data valuation framework that transitions from static accounting to utility-based pricing. Our approach operates on three layers: (1) token-level information density metrics using Shannon entropy and Data Quality Scores; (2) empirical training gain measurement through influence functions, proxy model strategies, and Data Shapley values; and (3) cryptographic verifiability through hash-based commitments, Merkle trees, and a tamper-evident training ledger. We provide comprehensive experimental validation on three real domains (instruction following, mathematical reasoning, and code summarization), demonstrating that proxy-based empirical gain achieves near-perfect ranking alignment with realized utility, substantially outperforming row-count and token-count baselines. This framework enables a fair Data-as-a-Service economy where high-reasoning data is priced according to its actual contribution to model intelligence, while providing the transparency and auditability necessary for trustworthy data markets.

AI.tldr: A dynamic data valuation framework that shifts from static counting to utility-based pricing for LLMs, using information density metrics, empirical training gain measurement, and cryptographic verifiability.
AI.motivation: Traditional data valuation methods based on simple row-count × quality coefficient approaches fail to capture the nuanced, nonlinear contributions that data makes to Large Language Model capabilities, necessitating a more sophisticated framework.
AI.method: Three-layer approach: 1) Token-level information density metrics using Shannon entropy and Data Quality Scores, 2) Empirical training gain measurement through influence functions, proxy model strategies, and Data Shapley values, 3) Cryptographic verifiability through hash-based commitments, Merkle trees, and tamper-evident training ledger.
AI.result: Experimental validation on three real domains (instruction following, mathematical reasoning, and code summarization) shows that proxy-based empirical gain achieves near-perfect ranking alignment with realized utility, substantially outperforming row-count and token-count baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables a fair Data-as-a-Service economy where high-reasoning data is priced according to its actual contribution to model intelligence, while providing the transparency and auditability necessary for trustworthy data markets.

title: AdaMamba: Adaptive Frequency-Gated Mamba for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
summary: Accurate long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) requires the capture of complex long-range dependencies and dynamic periodic patterns. Recent advances in frequency-domain analysis offer a global perspective for uncovering temporal characteristics. However, real-world time series often exhibit pronounced cross-domain heterogeneity where variables that appear synchronized in the time domain can differ substantially in the frequency domain. Existing frequency-based LTSF methods often rely on implicit assumptions of cross-domain homogeneity, which limits their ability to adapt to such intricate variability. To effectively integrate frequency-domain analysis with temporal dependency learning, we propose AdaMamba, a novel framework that endogenizes adaptive and context-aware frequency analysis within the Mamba state-space update process. Specifically, AdaMamba introduces an interactive patch encoding module to capture inter-variable interaction dynamics. Then, we develop an adaptive frequency-gated state-space module that generates input-dependent frequency bases, and generalizes the conventional temporal forgetting gate into a unified time-frequency forgetting gate. This allows dynamic calibration of state transitions based on learned frequency-domain importance, while preserving Mamba's capability in modeling long-range dependencies. Extensive experiments on seven public LTSF benchmarks and two domain-specific datasets demonstrate that AdaMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in forecasting accu racy while maintaining competitive computational efficiency. The code of AdaMamba is available at https://github.com/XDjiang25/AdaMamba.

AI.tldr: AdaMamba is a novel time series forecasting framework that integrates adaptive frequency analysis with Mamba's state-space modeling, addressing cross-domain heterogeneity through frequency-gated state transitions and interactive patch encoding.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series often exhibit cross-domain heterogeneity where variables that appear synchronized in time domain differ substantially in frequency domain. Existing frequency-based methods rely on implicit assumptions of cross-domain homogeneity, limiting their ability to handle intricate variability.
AI.method: 1. Interactive patch encoding module to capture inter-variable interaction dynamics. 2. Adaptive frequency-gated state-space module that generates input-dependent frequency bases. 3. Generalized temporal forgetting gate into unified time-frequency forgetting gate for dynamic state transition calibration based on learned frequency-domain importance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on seven public LTSF benchmarks and two domain-specific datasets demonstrate that AdaMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in forecasting accuracy while maintaining competitive computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: AdaMamba effectively integrates frequency-domain analysis with temporal dependency learning by endogenizing adaptive and context-aware frequency analysis within the Mamba state-space update process, addressing cross-domain heterogeneity challenges in long-term time series forecasting.

title: Accelerating Frequency Domain Diffusion Models with Error-Feedback Event-Driven Caching
summary: Diffusion models achieve remarkable success in time series generation. However, slow inference limits their practical deployment. We propose E$^2$-CRF (Error-Feedback Event-Driven Cumulative Residual Feature caching) to accelerate frequency domain diffusion models. Our method exploits two structural properties: (1) spectral localization, where signal energy concentrates in low frequencies, and (2) mirror symmetry, which halves the effective frequency dimension. E$^2$-CRF uses a closed-loop error-feedback system that adaptively caches transformer KV features across diffusion steps. We trigger recomputation using event-driven residual dynamics instead of fixed schedules. Our method selectively recomputes high-energy or rapidly-changing tokens while reusing cached features for stable high-frequency components. E$^2$-CRF achieves ~2.2 speedup while maintaining sample quality. We demonstrate effectiveness on 5 datasets. Our caching strategy naturally aligns with the diffusion process's structure-to-detail progression. We include sufficient-condition error and complexity bounds under standard regularity assumptions (Appendix), alongside empirical validation. Our code is available at https://github.com/NoakLiu/FastFourierDiffusion and is also integrated in https://github.com/NoakLiu/FastCache-xDiT.

AI.tldr: E²-CRF is a novel caching method that accelerates frequency domain diffusion models for time series generation by exploiting spectral properties and using error-feedback event-driven feature reuse, achieving ~2.2× speedup without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success in time series generation, but their slow inference speed limits practical deployment. There's a need to accelerate these models while maintaining sample quality.
AI.method: E²-CRF exploits two structural properties: (1) spectral localization (signal energy concentrates in low frequencies) and (2) mirror symmetry (halves effective frequency dimension). It uses a closed-loop error-feedback system that adaptively caches transformer KV features across diffusion steps, triggering recomputation using event-driven residual dynamics instead of fixed schedules. The method selectively recomputes high-energy or rapidly-changing tokens while reusing cached features for stable high-frequency components.
AI.result: The method achieves ~2.2× speedup while maintaining sample quality. It demonstrates effectiveness on 5 datasets, and the caching strategy naturally aligns with the diffusion process's structure-to-detail progression. The paper includes sufficient-condition error and complexity bounds under standard regularity assumptions with empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: E²-CRF provides an effective acceleration method for frequency domain diffusion models by intelligently exploiting structural properties of time series data and the diffusion process, enabling practical deployment through significant speed improvements without quality degradation.

title: CAP-CoT: Cycle Adversarial Prompt for Improving Chain of Thoughts in LLM Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has emerged as a simple and effective way to elicit step-by-step solutions from large language models (LLMs). However, CoT reasoning can be unstable across runs on long, multi-step problems, leading to inconsistent answers for unchanged task. Most prior work focuses on improving the forward reasoning chain within a single pass, with less attention to iterative and contrastive correction. To address this gap, we propose CAP-CoT, a Cycle Adversarial Prompt optimization framework designed to improve both CoT reasoning accuracy and stability of a single deployed solver. In each cycle, a forward solver generates candidate reasoning chains, an adversarial challenger constructs plausible but deliberately flawed chains using targeted error strategies, and a feedback agent contrasts the two chains and produces step-aligned structured feedback. This feedback closes the optimization loop in two directions, including updating the solver prompt based on errors exposed by the challenger, and updating the challenger prompt to generate increasingly targeted errors in subsequent cycles. Unlike safety-oriented adversarial prompting such as jailbreak or prompt-injection attacks, our adversarial component is task-semantic and aims to expose logical vulnerabilities in reasoning chains. Experiments across six benchmarks and four LLM backbones demonstrate that within two to three adversarial prompt optimization cycles, CAP-CoT consistently reduces variability across runs while improving reasoning accuracy and robustness to prompt perturbations.

AI.tldr: CAP-CoT is a Cycle Adversarial Prompt optimization framework that uses adversarial challengers to generate flawed reasoning chains, which are then contrasted with correct chains to produce structured feedback, improving both accuracy and stability of Chain-of-Thought reasoning in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought prompting is unstable across runs on long, multi-step problems, leading to inconsistent answers for unchanged tasks. Prior work focuses on improving forward reasoning within single passes, with little attention to iterative and contrastive correction.
AI.method: Cycle Adversarial Prompt optimization framework with three components per cycle: 1) forward solver generates candidate reasoning chains, 2) adversarial challenger constructs plausible but flawed chains using targeted error strategies, 3) feedback agent contrasts the two chains and produces step-aligned structured feedback. This feedback updates both solver and challenger prompts iteratively.
AI.result: Experiments across six benchmarks and four LLM backbones show CAP-CoT consistently reduces variability across runs while improving reasoning accuracy and robustness to prompt perturbations within two to three optimization cycles.
AI.conclusion: CAP-CoT effectively addresses CoT reasoning instability by using adversarial challenge-feedback cycles to expose logical vulnerabilities, leading to more consistent and accurate multi-step reasoning in LLMs through targeted prompt optimization.

title: Deep Clustering for Climate: Analyzing Teleconnections through Learned Categorical States
summary: Understanding and representing complex climate variability is essential for both scientific analysis and predictive modeling. However, identifying meaningful climate regimes from raw variables is challenging, as they exhibit high noise and nonlinear dependencies. In this work, we explore the use of Masked Siamese Networks to discretize climate time series into semantically rich clusters. Focusing on daily minimum and maximum temperature, we show that the resulting representations: (i) yield clusters that reflect meaningful climate states under our modeling assumptions, offering a simplified representation for downstream use; (ii) enable sampling and analysis of specific climate scenarios; and (iii) exhibit statistical associations with El Niño events, underscoring their scientific relevance. Our findings highlight the potential of self-supervised discretization as a tool for climate data analysis and open avenues for incorporating richer climate indicators in future work.

AI.tldr: Using Masked Siamese Networks to discretize climate time series into meaningful clusters that capture climate regimes and show associations with El Niño events.
AI.motivation: Understanding complex climate variability is crucial for scientific analysis and predictive modeling, but identifying meaningful climate regimes from raw variables is challenging due to high noise and nonlinear dependencies in the data.
AI.method: The authors employ Masked Siamese Networks to discretize climate time series into semantically rich clusters, focusing specifically on daily minimum and maximum temperature data.
AI.result: The resulting representations: (i) produce clusters reflecting meaningful climate states, (ii) enable sampling and analysis of specific climate scenarios, and (iii) exhibit statistical associations with El Niño events, demonstrating scientific relevance.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised discretization shows strong potential as a tool for climate data analysis, opening avenues for incorporating richer climate indicators in future research.

title: Active Inference: A method for Phenotyping Agency in AI systems?
summary: The proliferation of agentic artificial intelligence has outpaced the conceptual tools needed to characterize agency in computational systems. Prevailing definitions mainly rely on autonomy and goal-directedness. Here, we argue for a minimal notion open to principled inspection given three criteria: intentionality as action grounded in beliefs and desires, rationality as normatively coherent action entailed by a world model, and explainability as action causally traceable to internal states; we subsequently instantiate these as a partially observable Markov decision process under a variational framework wherein posterior beliefs, prior preferences, and the minimization of expected free energy jointly constitute an agentic action chain. Using a canonical T-maze paradigm, we evidence how empowerment, formulated as the channel capacity between actions and anticipated observations, serves as an operational metric that distinguishes zero-, intermediate-, and high-agency phenotypes through structural manipulations of the generative model. We conclude by arguing that as agents engage in epistemic foraging to resolve ambiguity, the governance controls that remain effective must shift systematically from external constraints to the internal modulation of prior preferences, offering a principled, variational bridge from computational phenotyping to AI governance strategy

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a minimal definition of agency based on intentionality, rationality, and explainability, operationalized through a variational framework, and demonstrates how empowerment metrics can distinguish agency levels in computational systems.
AI.motivation: The proliferation of agentic AI has outpaced the conceptual tools needed to characterize agency in computational systems. Prevailing definitions relying mainly on autonomy and goal-directedness are insufficient for principled inspection and analysis.
AI.method: Proposes a three-criteria framework: intentionality (action grounded in beliefs/desires), rationality (normatively coherent action entailed by world model), and explainability (action causally traceable to internal states). Instantiates this as a partially observable Markov decision process under a variational framework where posterior beliefs, prior preferences, and minimization of expected free energy constitute agency. Uses a T-maze paradigm to test the framework, with empowerment (channel capacity between actions and anticipated observations) as the operational metric.
AI.result: The empowerment metric successfully distinguishes zero-, intermediate-, and high-agency phenotypes through structural manipulations of the generative model in the T-maze paradigm. Shows how agents engage in epistemic foraging to resolve ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: As agents develop agency through epistemic foraging, effective governance must shift from external constraints to internal modulation of prior preferences. This provides a principled, variational bridge from computational phenotyping to AI governance strategy.

title: Score-Repellent Monte Carlo: Toward Efficient Non-Markovian Sampler with Constant Memory in General State Spaces
summary: History-dependent sampling can reduce long-run Monte Carlo variance by discouraging redundant revisits, but existing schemes typically encode history through empirical measure on finite state spaces, which is infeasible in high-dimensional discrete configuration spaces or ill-posed in continuous domains. We propose Score-Repellent Monte Carlo (SRMC) framework that summarizes trajectory history by a running average of score evaluations in $R^d$, where $d$ is the dimension of the score and state representation. This history is converted into a surrogate target through an exponential score tilt, indexed with $α$ that represents the strength of repellence in controlling the magnitude of the history-based repulsion. The surrogate family is normalization-free in the standard MCMC sense, yielding a generic wrapper: at each iteration, any base kernel targeting $π$ can instead be run on the current surrogate $π_{θ_n}$ while the history is updated online. We analyze the coupled evolution of the history recursion and Monte Carlo estimators using stochastic approximation with controlled Markovian noise, establishing almost sure convergence and a joint central limit theorem. We further identify regimes in which the asymptotic covariance decreases as $α$ increases, with scaling $O(1/α)$, extending the near-zero-variance effect of finite-state history-dependent samplers to general state spaces with constant memory. Experiments on continuous targets and discrete energy-based models demonstrate improved estimator variance and mode coverage, while retaining $O(d)$ memory usage and modest per-iteration overhead.

AI.tldr: SRMC introduces a history-dependent Monte Carlo framework using running average of score evaluations to create repulsion effects, enabling variance reduction in high-dimensional and continuous spaces with constant memory overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing history-dependent sampling methods are limited to finite state spaces or become infeasible in high-dimensional discrete configuration spaces and ill-posed in continuous domains. There's a need for a method that can leverage history information for variance reduction while being applicable to general state spaces.
AI.method: Proposes Score-Repellent Monte Carlo (SRMC) that summarizes trajectory history using a running average of score evaluations in R^d. This history is converted into a surrogate target through an exponential score tilt parameterized by α (repellence strength). The surrogate family is normalization-free, allowing any base MCMC kernel targeting π to run on the current surrogate π_θ_n while history updates online.
AI.result: Established almost sure convergence and joint central limit theorem using stochastic approximation with controlled Markovian noise. Identified regimes where asymptotic covariance decreases as α increases with O(1/α) scaling. Experiments on continuous targets and discrete energy-based models show improved estimator variance and mode coverage with O(d) memory usage and modest per-iteration overhead.
AI.conclusion: SRMC extends near-zero-variance effects of finite-state history-dependent samplers to general state spaces while maintaining constant memory, providing a generic wrapper for existing MCMC methods that improves sampling efficiency in high-dimensional and continuous domains.

title: AI Identity: Standards, Gaps, and Research Directions for AI Agents
summary: AI agents are now running real transactions, workflows, and sub-agent chains across organizational boundaries without continuous human supervision. This creates a problem no current infrastructure is equipped to solve: how do you identify, verify, and hold accountable an entity with no body, no persistent memory, and no legal standing? We define AI Identity as the continuous relationship between what an AI agent is declared to be and what it is observed to do, bounded by the confidence that those two things correspond at any given moment. Through a structured survey of industry trends, emerging standards, and technical literature, we conduct a gap analysis across the full agent identity lifecycle and make three contributions: (1) a structural comparison of human and AI identity across four dimensions (substrate, persistence, verifiability, and legal standing) showing that the asymmetry is fundamental and that extending human frameworks to agents without structural modification produces systematic failures; (2) an evaluation of current technical and regulatory documents against the identity requirements of autonomous agents, finding that none adequately address the challenge of governing nondeterministic, boundary-crossing entities; and (3) identification of five critical gaps (semantic intent verification, recursive delegation accountability, agent identity integrity, governance opacity and enforcement, and operational sustainability) that no current technology or regulatory instrument resolves. These gaps are structural; more engineering effort alone will not close them. Foundational research on AI identity is the central conclusion of this report.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the critical gap in current infrastructure for identifying, verifying, and holding accountable AI agents that operate autonomously across organizational boundaries without continuous human supervision, proposing that foundational research on AI identity is needed rather than just more engineering effort.
AI.motivation: AI agents are increasingly conducting real transactions, workflows, and sub-agent chains across organizational boundaries autonomously, creating an accountability problem since current infrastructure cannot identify, verify, or hold accountable entities with no physical body, no persistent memory, and no legal standing.
AI.method: The paper conducts a structured survey of industry trends, emerging standards, and technical literature, performing a gap analysis across the full agent identity lifecycle. It compares human and AI identity across four dimensions (substrate, persistence, verifiability, and legal standing) and evaluates current technical and regulatory documents against autonomous agent identity requirements.
AI.result: Three main contributions: (1) Structural comparison showing fundamental asymmetry between human and AI identity, with human frameworks failing when extended to AI without modification; (2) Evaluation revealing that no current technical or regulatory documents adequately address governing nondeterministic, boundary-crossing AI agents; (3) Identification of five critical unresolved gaps: semantic intent verification, recursive delegation accountability, agent identity integrity, governance opacity and enforcement, and operational sustainability.
AI.conclusion: The gaps identified are structural in nature and cannot be closed by engineering effort alone. Foundational research on AI identity is urgently needed to develop appropriate frameworks for identifying, verifying, and holding accountable autonomous AI agents operating across organizational boundaries.

title: Reward Models Are Secretly Value Functions: Temporally Coherent Reward Modeling
summary: Reward models in RLHF are trained to score only the final token of a response - a choice that discards rich signal from every intermediate position and produces models whose token-level outputs are noise. We argue this is a missed opportunity: a well-trained reward model's output at any token should represent the conditional expectation of the final reward given the response so far. We introduce Temporally Coherent Reward Modeling (TCRM), which induces this property via two regularization terms on top of the standard Bradley-Terry loss, with minimizers provably equal to conditional expectations. The regularizers correspond to Monte Carlo and TD value-learning objectives, establishing a direct connection to RL value functions. TCRM requires zero changes to architecture, data, or inference, yet unlocks three capabilities from one principle: interpretable token-level reward trajectories (middle-token pairwise accuracy improved from 50% to 88.9%, final-token accuracy preserved); state-of-the-art PRM performance on ProcessBench (44.9% average F1) among models trained only on outcome data; and unified reward/value modeling in PPO, reducing peak GPU memory by 27% and step time by 19% with matching LLM quality.

AI.tldr: TCRM (Temporally Coherent Reward Modeling) improves RLHF reward models by training them to predict expected final rewards at every token position, not just the final token, using regularization based on reinforcement learning principles.
AI.motivation: Current RLHF reward models only score the final token of responses, discarding valuable intermediate signals and producing noisy token-level outputs. This is a missed opportunity since a well-trained reward model should represent the conditional expectation of final reward at every position.
AI.method: TCRM adds two regularization terms to the standard Bradley-Terry loss: one Monte Carlo and one TD value-learning objective, which provably enforce that the reward model outputs conditional expectations of final rewards. The method requires no architectural, data, or inference changes.
AI.result: TCRM achieves: 1) Interpretable token-level reward trajectories (middle-token pairwise accuracy improved from 50% to 88.9%), 2) State-of-the-art PRM performance on ProcessBench (44.9% average F1), 3) Unified reward/value modeling in PPO, reducing peak GPU memory by 27% and step time by 19% with matching LLM quality.
AI.conclusion: TCRM demonstrates that training reward models to output conditional expectations of final rewards at every token position unlocks multiple capabilities from a single principle, significantly improving interpretability, performance, and efficiency in RLHF.

title: LEGO: An LLM Skill-Based Front-End Design Generation Platform
summary: Existing LLM-based EDA agents are often isolated task-specific systems. This leads to repeated engineering effort and limited reuse of successful design and debugging strategies. We present LEGO, a unified skill-based platform for front-end design generation. It decomposes the digital front-end flow into six independent steps and represents every agent capability as a standardized composable circuit skill within a plug-and-play architecture. To build this skill library, we survey more than 100 papers, select 11 representative open-source projects, and extract 42 executable circuit skills within a six-step finite state machine formulation. Circuit Skill Builder automates skill extraction with linear scalability. Agent Skill RAG achieves submillisecond retrieval without relying on embedding models. Empirical evaluation on a hard subset of 41 VerilogEval v2 problems that gpt-5.2-codex fails to solve under extra-high reasoning effort shows that individual circuit skills constructed within LEGO raise Pass@1 from 0.000 to 0.805. This is an 80.5% gain over the baseline. Cross-project skill compositions also reach 0.805 Pass@1. They outperform hierarchy-verilog by 14.6% and VerilogCoder by 2.5%. They also match MAGE. These results show that modular skill composition supports both effective and flexible RTL design automation. The LEGO platform and all circuit skills are publicly available at GitHub: https://github.com/loujc/LEGO-An-LLM-Skill-Based-Front-End-Design-Generation-Platform

AI.tldr: LEGO is a unified skill-based platform that decomposes front-end digital design into standardized composable circuit skills, achieving 80.5% improvement on hard Verilog problems over existing LLM-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based EDA agents are isolated task-specific systems, leading to repeated engineering effort and limited reuse of successful design/debugging strategies across projects.
AI.method: 1) Decomposes digital front-end flow into six independent steps; 2) Represents agent capabilities as standardized composable circuit skills in plug-and-play architecture; 3) Builds skill library from 100+ papers and 11 open-source projects; 4) Uses Circuit Skill Builder for automated extraction; 5) Implements Agent Skill RAG for fast retrieval.
AI.result: On 41 hard VerilogEval v2 problems that baseline gpt-5.2-codex fails, LEGO raises Pass@1 from 0.000 to 0.805 (80.5% gain). Cross-project skill compositions also reach 0.805 Pass@1, outperforming hierarchy-verilog by 14.6% and VerilogCoder by 2.5%, while matching MAGE performance.
AI.conclusion: Modular skill composition supports both effective and flexible RTL design automation, demonstrating the value of unified skill-based platforms over isolated task-specific systems in EDA.

title: Collocation-based Robust Physics Informed Neural Networks for time-dependent simulations of pollution propagation under thermal inversion conditions on Spitsbergen
comment: Robust Variational Physics Informed Neural Networks; Pollution propagation simulations; Longyearbyen at Spitsbergen; Advection-diffusion model; In-field measurements; Open source software
summary: In this paper, we propose a Physics-Informed Neural Network framework for time-dependent simulations of pollution propagation originating from moving emission sources. We formulate a robust variational framework for the time-dependent advection-diffusion problem and establish the boundedness and inf-sup stability of the corresponding discrete weak formulation. Based on this mathematical foundation, we construct a robust loss function that is directly related to the true approximation error, defined as the difference between the neural network approximation and the (unknown) exact solution. Additionally, a collocation-based strategy is introduced to speed up neural network training. As a case study, we investigate pollution propagation caused by snowmobile traffic in Longyearbyen, Spitsbergen, supported by detailed in-field measurements collected using dedicated sensors. The proposed framework is applied to analyze the effects of thermal inversion on pollutant accumulation. Our results demonstrate that thermal inversion traps dense and humid air masses near the ground, significantly enhancing particulate matter (PM) concentration and worsening local air quality.

AI.tldr: A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework for simulating pollution from moving sources, with application to snowmobile traffic in Longyearbyen, showing how thermal inversion traps pollutants and worsens air quality.
AI.motivation: Need to simulate time-dependent pollution propagation from moving emission sources (like vehicles) with mathematical rigor and computational efficiency, using real-world environmental data for validation.
