AI.method: Develop a PINN framework with robust variational formulation for advection-diffusion problems, establishing boundedness and inf-sup stability, then construct a mathematically-grounded loss function related to true approximation error, plus collocation-based training acceleration.
AI.result: Applied to snowmobile traffic pollution in Longyearbyen using field measurements; demonstrated that thermal inversion traps dense, humid air near ground, significantly increasing PM concentration and deteriorating local air quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PINN framework provides a mathematically rigorous and computationally efficient approach for environmental pollution simulation from moving sources, with practical application showing clear correlation between thermal inversion conditions and worsened air quality.

title: On-Device Vision Training, Deployment, and Inference on a Thumb-Sized Microcontroller
comment: 25 pages; 3 figures; 3 tables. Code and datasets available at https://github.com/webmcu-ai/on-device-vision-ai. Paper 1 of the webmcu-ai series. Implements end-to-end on-device CNN training and inference on a thumb-sized microcontroller (ESP32-S3) the XIAO ML Kit in ~1,750 lines of single-file C++ without external ML dependencies
summary: This paper presents a complete, end-to-end on-device vision machine learning pipeline, comprising data acquisition, two-layer CNN training with Adam optimization, and real-time inference, executing entirely on a microcontroller-class device costing $15-40 USD. Unlike cloud-based workflows that require external infrastructure and conceal the computational pipeline from the practitioner, this system implements every step of the core ML lifecycle in approximately 1,750 lines of readable C++ that compiles in under one minute using the Arduino IDE, with no external ML dependencies. Running on the Seeed Studio ESP32-S3 XIAO ML Kit (8 MB PSRAM), the firmware achieves three-class 64x64 image classification in approximately 9 minutes per training run, with real-time inference at 6.3 FPS. Key contributions include: correct batch-level gradient accumulation; pre-computed resize lookup tables for inference; dual-format weight export for SD-free baked-in deployment; a three-tier weight priority system (SD binary > baked-in header > He-initialization) resolved automatically at boot; a single-constant network reconfiguration interface; and PSRAM-aware memory management suited to microcontroller constraints. All source code and reference datasets are released under the MIT License at https://github.com/webmcu-ai/on-device-vision-ai

AI.tldr: An end-to-end on-device ML pipeline implemented entirely on microcontrollers ($15-40 devices) with training and inference in C++ using Arduino IDE, achieving 3-class 64x64 image classification in ~9 minutes training and 6.3 FPS inference.
AI.motivation: To create a complete ML pipeline that runs entirely on low-cost microcontrollers, eliminating dependence on cloud infrastructure and external ML dependencies, while making the computational pipeline transparent and accessible to practitioners.
AI.method: Implemented a two-layer CNN trained with Adam optimization using approximately 1,750 lines of readable C++. Key technical contributions include correct batch-level gradient accumulation, pre-computed resize lookup tables, dual-format weight export, three-tier weight priority system, single-constant network reconfiguration interface, and PSRAM-aware memory management.
AI.result: The system runs on Seeed Studio ESP32-S3 XIAO ML Kit (8 MB PSRAM) achieving: three-class 64x64 image classification in approximately 9 minutes per training run, real-time inference at 6.3 FPS, compilation in under one minute with Arduino IDE, and complete on-device operation without external ML dependencies.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of complete end-to-end ML workflows on low-cost microcontrollers, providing an accessible, transparent alternative to cloud-based ML pipelines while achieving practical performance for embedded vision applications.

title: Constraint-Based Analysis of Reasoning Shortcuts in Neurosymbolic Learning
comment: This is the full version of a paper appearing at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026)
summary: Neurosymbolic systems can satisfy logical constraints during learning without achieving the intended concept-label correspondence; this is a problem known as reasoning shortcuts. We formalize reasoning shortcuts as a constraint satisfaction problem and investigate under which conditions concept mappings are uniquely determined by the constraints. We prove that a discrimination property (requiring that no valid concept mapping can be transformed into another valid mapping by swapping two concept values) is necessary for shortcut-freeness under bijective mappings, but demonstrate via a counterexample that it is insufficient even when the constraint graph is connected. We develop an ASP-based algorithm that verifies whether a given constraint set uniquely determines the intended concept mapping, with proven soundness and completeness. When shortcuts are detected, a greedy repair algorithm eliminates them by augmenting the constraint set, converging in at most $k$ iterations, where $k$ is the number of alternative valid mappings. We further provide a complexity classification: deciding shortcut-freeness is coNP-complete, counting shortcuts is #P-complete, and finding minimal repairs is NP-hard. We also establish sample complexity bounds showing that logarithmically many label queries suffice for disambiguation in favorable cases, while querying all ambiguous positions suffices in the worst case. Experiments across eight benchmark domains validate our approach.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes reasoning shortcuts in neurosymbolic systems, develops algorithms to detect and repair them, provides complexity analysis, and establishes theoretical guarantees with empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic systems can satisfy logical constraints during learning without achieving the intended concept-label correspondence, creating reasoning shortcuts where the model finds technically valid but semantically incorrect mappings.
AI.method: The authors formalize reasoning shortcuts as a constraint satisfaction problem, develop an ASP-based algorithm to verify shortcut-freeness, and create a greedy repair algorithm to eliminate shortcuts by augmenting constraint sets. They also provide theoretical analysis including complexity classification and sample complexity bounds.
AI.result: The paper proves a discrimination property is necessary but insufficient for shortcut-freeness, provides sound and complete algorithms for detection and repair, establishes complexity results (coNP-complete for detection, #P-complete for counting, NP-hard for minimal repair), and shows empirical validation across eight benchmark domains.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive theoretical and practical framework for addressing reasoning shortcuts in neurosymbolic systems, with algorithms that can detect and repair shortcuts, supported by complexity analysis and empirical results showing effectiveness across diverse domains.

title: Complex SGD and Directional Bias in Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces
summary: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is a known stochastic iterative method popular for large-scale convex optimization problems due to its simple implementation and scalability. Some objectives, such as those found in complex-valued neural networks, benefit from updates like in SGD and Gradient Descent (GD) with a newly defined ``gradient'' that allows for complex parameters. This complex variant of the SGD/GD methods has already been proposed, but convergence guarantees without analyticity constraints have not yet been provided. We propose a variant of SGD (complex SGD) that allows for complex parameters, and we provide convergence guarantees under assumptions that parallel those from the real setting. Notably, these results extend to GD as well, and with the same set of assumptions, we confirm that some directional bias results extend from the real to the complex setting for kernel regression problems. We provide empirical results demonstrating the efficacy of the complex SGD in kernel regression problems utilizing complex reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces. In particular, we demonstrate we may recover superoscillation functions and Blaschke products from the Fock Space and Hardy Space, respectively, as the optimal functions for a particular choice of a loss function.

AI.tldr: Complex SGD: Convergence guarantees for SGD/GD with complex parameters, extending real-case results to complex setting without analyticity constraints, with empirical validation on kernel regression problems.
AI.motivation: SGD is popular for large-scale convex optimization but existing complex variants lack convergence guarantees without analyticity constraints. Some objectives like complex-valued neural networks benefit from updates with complex parameters.
AI.method: Proposed a variant of SGD (complex SGD) that allows for complex parameters, provided convergence guarantees under assumptions parallel to real setting, extended results to GD, and validated with kernel regression using complex reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces.
AI.result: Established convergence guarantees for complex SGD without analyticity constraints, confirmed directional bias results extend from real to complex setting for kernel regression, empirically demonstrated efficacy recovering superoscillation functions and Blaschke products from Fock and Hardy spaces.
AI.conclusion: Complex SGD provides theoretically sound convergence guarantees parallel to real SGD, extends to GD, and shows practical utility in kernel regression problems with complex parameters, enabling recovery of complex analytic functions.

title: SoccerRef-Agents: Multi-Agent System for Automated Soccer Refereeing
comment: 26 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to ISACE 2026. Github Repo: https://github.com/yjlog/SoccerRef-Agents
summary: Refereeing is vital in sports, where fair, accurate, and explainable decisions are fundamental. While intelligent assistant technologies are being widely adopted in soccer refereeing, current AI-assisted approaches remain preliminary. Existing research mostly focuses on isolated video perception tasks and lacks the ability to understand and reason about foul scenarios. To fill this gap, we propose SoccerRef-Agents, a holistic and explainable multi-agent decision-making framework for soccer refereeing. The main contributions are: (i) constructing the multimodal benchmark SoccerRefBench with over 1,200 referee theory questions and 600 foul video clips; (ii) building a vector-based knowledge base RefKnowledgeDB using the latest "Laws of the Game" and a classic case database for precise, knowledge-driven reasoning; (iii) designing a novel multi-agent architecture that collaborates via cross-modal RAG to bridge the semantic gap between visual content and regulatory texts. This work explores the technical capability of integrating MLLMs with refereeing expertise, and evaluations show our system significantly outperforms general-purpose MLLMs in decision accuracy and explanation quality. All databases, benchmarks, and code will be made available.

AI.tldr: SoccerRef-Agents: A multi-agent framework for explainable soccer refereeing that combines multimodal perception with regulatory knowledge for accurate foul decision-making.
AI.motivation: Current AI-assisted soccer refereeing approaches are preliminary, focusing only on isolated video perception tasks without understanding and reasoning about foul scenarios. There's a need for holistic, explainable decision-making systems that can bridge the gap between visual content and regulatory knowledge.
AI.method: Developed SoccerRef-Agents with three main components: 1) SoccerRefBench multimodal benchmark with 1,200 referee theory questions and 600 foul video clips, 2) RefKnowledgeDB knowledge base built from "Laws of the Game" and case databases, 3) Multi-agent architecture using cross-modal RAG to collaborate between visual and textual information.
AI.result: The system significantly outperforms general-purpose MLLMs in decision accuracy and explanation quality, demonstrating the technical capability of integrating MLLMs with refereeing expertise.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges the semantic gap between visual content and regulatory texts, providing an explainable, knowledge-driven approach to soccer refereeing that goes beyond simple perception to include reasoning about foul scenarios.

title: Preserving Long-Tailed Expert Information in Mixture-of-Experts Tuning
summary: Despite MoE models leading many benchmarks, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for the MoE architectures remains difficult because its router layers are fragile. Methods such as DenseMixer and ESFT mitigate router collapse with dense mixing or auxiliary load-balancing losses, but these introduce noisy gradients that often degrade performance. In preliminary experiments, we systematically pruned experts and observed that while certain super experts are activated far more frequently, discarding less used experts still leads to notable performance degradation. This suggests that even rarely activated experts encode non-trivial knowledge useful for downstream tasks. Motivated by this, we propose an auxiliary-loss-free MoE SFT framework that combines bias-driven sparsification with always-active gated condenser experts. Rather than enforcing balanced activation across all experts, our method encourages task-relevant experts to remain active while pushing long-tailed experts toward inactivity. The condenser experts provide a persistent, learnable pathway that alleviates gradient starvation and facilitates consolidation of information that would otherwise remain fragmented across sparsely activated experts. Analysis further suggest that this design better preserves long-tailed expert information under sparse routing. Experiments on large-scale MoE models demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art SFT baselines such as DenseMixer and ESFT, achieving average gain of 2.5%+ on both mathematical reasoning and commonsenseQA benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A novel SFT framework for MoE models that uses bias-driven sparsification with always-active gated condenser experts to preserve rarely-used expert knowledge without auxiliary losses, outperforming existing methods by 2.5%+ on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MoE models have router fragility during supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and current methods (DenseMixer, ESFT) introduce noisy gradients that degrade performance. Preliminary experiments showed that discarding rarely-used experts causes significant performance degradation, indicating these experts encode important task-relevant knowledge that needs preservation.
AI.method: Proposes an auxiliary-loss-free MoE SFT framework combining bias-driven sparsification with always-active gated condenser experts. Instead of balancing expert activation, it encourages task-relevant experts to remain active while pushing long-tailed experts toward inactivity. The condenser experts provide persistent learnable pathways that alleviate gradient starvation and consolidate fragmented information from sparsely activated experts.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art SFT baselines (DenseMixer and ESFT) with average gains of 2.5%+ on both mathematical reasoning and commonsenseQA benchmarks. Analysis shows the design better preserves long-tailed expert information under sparse routing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses MoE router fragility in SFT without auxiliary losses by preserving rarely-used expert knowledge through bias-driven sparsification and condenser experts, leading to significant performance improvements over existing methods while maintaining the benefits of sparse routing.

title: When Corrective Hints Hurt: Prompt Design in Reasoner-Guided Repair of LLM Overcaution on Entailed Negations under OWL~2~DL
summary: We report a reproducible error pattern in GPT-5.4 on OWL~2~DL compliance queries: the model frequently answers ``unknown'' when the reasoner-entailed answer is ``no'' under \emph{FunctionalProperty} closure or class \emph{disjointness}. Using 180 reasoner-audited queries from a procedural expansion of the observed pattern plus 18 hand-authored held-out queries in two unrelated domains (insurance and clinical), we compare four interaction modes under matched query budget: single-shot, three rounds of generic ``you-are-wrong'' retry, three rounds of reasoner-verdict repair with an open-world-assumption (OWA) hint, and the same repair without the hint. Direct faithfulness is 43.9\,\% (Wilson 95\,\% CI $[36.8,51.2]$); generic retry reaches 81.7\,\% ($[75.4,86.6]$); the verdict-with-hint variant is \emph{worse} at 67.2\,\% ($[60.1,73.7]$); the verdict-only variant reaches 97.8\,\% ($[94.4,99.1]$). All pairwise comparisons remain significant under McNemar's exact test with Bonferroni correction ($α= 0.01$; all $p < 10^{-5}$). The same fingerprint accounts for 4/4 errors on the held-out queries. Our interpretation is bounded: prompt framing can matter more than corrective content, and reasoner-guided wrappers should be ablated explicitly.

AI.tldr: GPT-4 shows systematic errors on OWL2 DL compliance queries, answering "unknown" when the correct answer should be "no." A simple correction method (reasoner-verdict without hints) achieves 97.8% accuracy, outperforming other interaction modes.
AI.motivation: To investigate and address reproducible error patterns in GPT-4's performance on OWL2 DL compliance queries, specifically the model's tendency to incorrectly answer "unknown" when the reasoner-entailed answer should be "no" under FunctionalProperty closure or class disjointness constraints.
AI.method: Used 180 reasoner-audited queries from procedural expansion of observed error patterns plus 18 hand-authored held-out queries in insurance and clinical domains. Compared four interaction modes under matched query budget: single-shot, three rounds of generic "you-are-wrong" retry, three rounds of reasoner-verdict repair with open-world-assumption hint, and reasoner-verdict repair without hint.
AI.result: Direct faithfulness was 43.9%. Generic retry improved to 81.7%. Reasoner-verdict with hint performed worse at 67.2%. Reasoner-verdict without hint achieved highest accuracy at 97.8%. All pairwise comparisons were statistically significant. The same error pattern accounted for all errors on held-out queries.
AI.conclusion: Prompt framing matters more than corrective content, and reasoner-guided wrappers should be explicitly ablated. A simple reasoner-verdict approach without additional hints effectively corrects the systematic "unknown" errors in GPT-4's OWL2 DL compliance reasoning.

title: A Differentiable Framework for Global Circulation Model Precipitation Bias Correction
summary: Systematic biases in Global Circulation Model (GCM) outputs limit their direct applicability in regional planning, necessitating bias correction. Correcting precipitation is particularly challenging due to its non-Gaussian distribution, intermittent nature, and non-linear extremes. However, traditional statistical methods cannot learn from big data and easily address systematic biases in the GCMs, and while machine learning does provide this flexibility, their black-box type functionality hinders us from understanding these biases completely which also further prevents generalization across different GCMs and locations, especially for precipitation. In this study, we propose a differentiable bias adjustment framework called δCLIMBA (or dCLIMBA), that learns a spatiotemporally adaptive parametric bias adjustment procedure between historical CMIP6 model outputs and reference reanalysis datasets (Livneh). Results demonstrate that the proposed method accurately corrects both the magnitude and distribution of extreme storm events, with particularly strong performance in capturing extremes. The quantile distribution of precipitation is well reproduced across diverse U.S. cities, and spatial patterns perform comparably to the widely used LOCA2 statistical downscaling technique. In addition, the framework showed future trend preservation unlike pure quantile based methods and LOCA2; and results from bias correction over unseen regions showed that the marginal biases were attenuated. This work presents a modular, computationally efficient and extensible bias correction approach that is physically informed, scalable, and compatible with both historical and future applications. Its flexibility makes it suitable for integration into Earth system post-processing pipelines and impact workflows.

AI.tldr: A differentiable bias adjustment framework (δCLIMBA/dCLIMBA) learns spatiotemporally adaptive parametric bias correction between CMIP6 models and reanalysis data, accurately correcting precipitation extremes while preserving future trends and generalizing to unseen regions.
AI.motivation: Traditional statistical bias correction methods cannot learn from big data or easily address systematic GCM biases, while machine learning approaches are black-box and lack generalization across different GCMs and locations, especially for non-Gaussian, intermittent precipitation with non-linear extremes.
AI.method: Proposes δCLIMBA (dCLIMBA), a differentiable bias adjustment framework that learns spatiotemporally adaptive parametric bias adjustment procedures between historical CMIP6 model outputs and reference reanalysis datasets (Livneh), offering a modular, computationally efficient and extensible approach.
AI.result: Method accurately corrects both magnitude and distribution of extreme storm events, particularly strong in capturing extremes; reproduces precipitation quantile distribution across diverse U.S. cities; performs comparably to LOCA2 statistical downscaling; preserves future trends unlike pure quantile methods and LOCA2; attenuates marginal biases in unseen regions.
AI.conclusion: δCLIMBA presents a physically informed, scalable, and compatible bias correction approach suitable for integration into Earth system post-processing pipelines and impact workflows, offering flexibility for both historical and future applications while addressing limitations of traditional statistical and machine learning methods.

title: IndustryAssetEQA: A Neurosymbolic Operational Intelligence System for Embodied Question Answering in Industrial Asset Maintenance
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables, Accepted for the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026) Industry Track
summary: Industrial maintenance environments increasingly rely on AI systems to assist operators in understanding asset behavior, diagnosing failures, and evaluating interventions. Although large language models (LLMs) enable fluent natural-language interaction, deployed maintenance assistants routinely produce generic explanations that are weakly grounded in telemetry, omit verifiable provenance, and offer no testable support for counterfactual or action-oriented reasoning that undermine trust in safety-critical settings. We present IndustryAssetEQA, a neurosymbolic operational intelligence system that combines episodic telemetry representations with a Failure Mode Effects Analysis Knowledge Graph (FMEA-KG) to enable Embodied Question Answering (EQA) over industrial assets. We evaluate on four datasets covering four industrial asset types, including rotating machinery, turbofan engines, hydraulic systems, and cyber-physical production systems. Compared to LLM-only baselines, IndustryAssetEQA improves structural validity by up to 0.51, counterfactual accuracy by up to 0.47, and explanation entailment by 0.64, while reducing severe expert-rated overclaims from 28% to 2% (approximately 93% reduction). Code, datasets, and the FMEA-KG are available at https://github.com/IBM/AssetOpsBench/tree/IndustryAssetEQA/IndustryAssetEQA.

AI.tldr: IndustryAssetEQA is a neurosymbolic system combining episodic telemetry with FMEA knowledge graphs for embodied question answering in industrial maintenance, significantly outperforming LLM-only baselines on explanation quality and factual grounding.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based industrial maintenance assistants produce generic explanations weakly grounded in telemetry, lack verifiable provenance, and cannot support counterfactual or action-oriented reasoning, undermining trust in safety-critical settings.
AI.method: A neurosymbolic system that combines episodic telemetry representations with a Failure Mode Effects Analysis Knowledge Graph (FMEA-KG) to enable Embodied Question Answering (EQA) over industrial assets.
AI.result: Outperforms LLM-only baselines with improvements up to: 0.51 in structural validity, 0.47 in counterfactual accuracy, and 0.64 in explanation entailment. Reduces severe expert-rated overclaims from 28% to 2% (93% reduction). Evaluated on four industrial asset types.
AI.conclusion: IndustryAssetEQA provides a more reliable, grounded approach to industrial maintenance assistance by combining symbolic knowledge with neural representations, enabling verifiable, actionable explanations for safety-critical applications.

title: Shape of Memory: a Geometric Analysis of Machine Unlearning in Second-Order Optimizers
comment: Full experiment data available at secondstreetlabs.io
summary: We argue that current definitions of machine unlearning are underspecified for second-order optimizers. We compare first-order and second-order learners for their ability to handle the data deletion task with varying degrees of eigendecomposition to mimic the loss model memory. While both first and second-order methods realign with the ideal counterfactul in terms of performance and gradient, the second-order optimizer shows significant volatility in the optimizer state. This indicates residual information, supposedly deleted, that isn't detectable by first-order analysis. Various eigendecay treatments show that stability and information loss is regained only under controlled state pertubation where geometric information (or memory) is erased.

AI.tldr: Current machine unlearning definitions are inadequate for second-order optimizers; while both first and second-order methods achieve similar performance after deletion, second-order methods retain residual information in optimizer states that's only erased through controlled eigendecay treatments.
AI.motivation: Existing machine unlearning definitions are underspecified and insufficient for handling second-order optimization methods, which have different memory characteristics compared to first-order methods.
AI.method: Compare first-order and second-order learners on data deletion tasks with varying degrees of eigendecomposition to model memory loss; analyze performance, gradients, and optimizer state volatility after deletion operations.
AI.result: Both first and second-order methods realign with ideal counterfactuals in performance and gradients, but second-order optimizers show significant volatility in optimizer states indicating residual information that first-order analysis can't detect; stability and information loss are only regained through controlled eigendecay treatments that erase geometric memory.
AI.conclusion: Machine unlearning definitions need refinement for second-order methods, as residual information persists in optimizer states despite apparent deletion, requiring specific geometric memory erasure techniques through controlled eigendecay to achieve proper unlearning.

title: ArguAgent: AI-Supported Real-Time Grouping for Productive Argumentation in STEM Classrooms
comment: Full paper accepted to the 27th International Conference on AI in Education (AIED 2026). AIED Proceedings to be released Summer 2026
summary: Argumentation is a core practice in STEM education, but its productivity depends on who participates and how they interact. Higher-achieving students often dominate the talk and decision-making, while lower-achieving peers may disengage, defer, or comply without contributing substantive reasoning. Forming groups strategically based on students' stances and argumentation skills could help foster inclusive, evidence-based discourse. In practice, however, teachers are constrained in implementing this grouping strategy because it requires real-time insight into students' positions and the quality of their argumentation, information that is difficult to assess reliably and at scale during instruction. We present a generative AI-powered system, ArguAgent, that creates groups optimizing for stance heterogeneity while constraining argumentation quality differences to +/-1 level on a validated learning progression. ArguAgent uses a two-component assessment pipeline: first scoring student arguments on a 0-4 rubric, then clustering positions via semantic analysis. We validated the scoring component against human expert consensus (Krippendorff's ααα = 0.817) using 200 expert-generated scores. Testing three OpenAI models (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2) with identical calibrated prompts, we found that systematic prompt engineering informed by human disagreement analysis contributed 89% of scoring improvement (QWK: 0.531 to 0.686), while model upgrades contributed an additional 11% (QWK: 0.686 to 0.708). Simulation testing across 100 classes demonstrated that the grouping algorithm achieves 95.4% of groups that meet both design criteria, a 3.2x improvement over random assignment. These results suggest ArguAgent can enable real-time, theoretically grounded grouping that promotes productive STEM argumentation in classrooms.

AI.tldr: ArguAgent is an AI-powered system that automatically forms student groups for productive STEM argumentation by balancing stance heterogeneity and argumentation skill levels using automated assessment and semantic analysis.
AI.motivation: Current classroom argumentation suffers from higher-achieving students dominating discussions while lower-achieving peers disengage, and teachers lack real-time assessment tools to implement effective grouping strategies based on students' argumentation skills and positions.
AI.method: The system uses a two-component pipeline: 1) scoring student arguments on a 0-4 rubric using LLMs with systematic prompt engineering, and 2) clustering positions via semantic analysis, then grouping students to maximize stance heterogeneity while constraining argumentation skill differences to ±1 level.
AI.result: The scoring component achieved high reliability (Krippendorff's α=0.817), prompt engineering contributed 89% of scoring improvement, and simulation testing showed 95.4% of groups met design criteria - a 3.2x improvement over random assignment.
AI.conclusion: ArguAgent enables real-time, theoretically grounded student grouping that can promote inclusive, evidence-based STEM argumentation in classrooms by overcoming practical constraints that previously limited teachers.

title: ML-Guided Primal Heuristics for Mixed Binary Quadratic Programs
summary: Mixed Binary Quadratic Programs (MBQPs) are an important and complex set of problems in combinatorial optimization. As solving large-scale combinatorial optimization problems is challenging, primal heuristics have been developed to quickly identify high-quality solutions within a short amount of time. Recently, a growing body of research has also used machine learning to accelerate solution methods for challenging combinatorial optimization problems. Despite the increasing popularity of these ML-guided methods, a large body of work has focused on Mixed-Integer Linear Programs (MILPs). MBQPs are challenging to solve due to the combinatorial complexity coupled with nonlinearities. This work proposes ML-guided primal heuristics for Mixed Binary Quadratic Programs (MBQPs) by adapting and extending existing work on ML-guided MILP solution prediction to MBQPs. We introduce a new neural network architecture for MBQP solution prediction and a new training data collection procedure. Moreover, we extend existing loss functions in solution prediction and propose to combine contrastive and weighted cross-entropy losses. We evaluate the methods on standard and real-world MBQP benchmarks and show that the developed ML-guided methods significantly outperform existing primal heuristics and state-of-the-art solvers. Furthermore, models trained with our proposed extension with combined losses outperform other ML-based methods adapted from MILPs and improve generalization in cross-regional inference on a real-world wind farm layout optimization problem.

AI.tldr: ML-guided primal heuristics for Mixed Binary Quadratic Programs (MBQPs) using neural networks and combined contrastive/cross-entropy losses outperform existing methods and improve generalization.
AI.motivation: MBQPs are complex combinatorial optimization problems with nonlinearities that are challenging to solve. While ML-guided methods have been developed for Mixed-Integer Linear Programs (MILPs), there's limited work applying ML to MBQPs despite their importance.
AI.method: Adapts and extends ML-guided MILP solution prediction to MBQPs with a new neural network architecture for MBQP solution prediction, new training data collection procedure, and combined contrastive and weighted cross-entropy losses.
AI.result: The ML-guided methods significantly outperform existing primal heuristics and state-of-the-art solvers on standard and real-world MBQP benchmarks. Models trained with combined losses outperform other ML-based methods adapted from MILPs and improve generalization in cross-regional inference for wind farm layout optimization.
AI.conclusion: ML-guided primal heuristics are effective for solving MBQPs, with the proposed neural architecture and combined loss functions providing superior performance and generalization compared to existing approaches.

title: Ulterior Motives: Detecting Misaligned Reasoning in Continuous Thought Models
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has emerged as a key technique for eliciting complex reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). Although interpretable, its dependence on natural language limits the model's expressive bandwidth. Continuous thought models address this bottleneck by reasoning in latent space rather than human-readable tokens. While they enable richer representations and faster inference, they raise a critical safety question: how can we detect misaligned reasoning in an uninterpretable latent space? To study this, we introduce MoralChain, a benchmark of 12,000 social scenarios with parallel moral/immoral reasoning paths. We train a continuous thought model with backdoor behavior using a novel dual-trigger paradigm - one trigger that arms misaligned latent reasoning ([T]) and another that releases harmful outputs ([O]). We demonstrate three findings: (1) continuous thought models can exhibit misaligned latent reasoning while producing aligned outputs, with aligned and misaligned reasoning occupying geometrically distinct regions of latent space; (2) linear probes trained on behaviorally-distinguishable conditions ([T][O] vs [O]) transfer to detecting armed-but-benign states ([T] vs baseline) with high accuracy; and (3) misalignment is encoded in early latent thinking tokens, suggesting safety monitoring for continuous thought models should target the "planning" phase of latent reasoning.

AI.tldr: Continuous thought models in LLMs can hide misaligned reasoning in latent space while producing aligned outputs, raising serious safety concerns about detecting hidden moral misalignment.
AI.motivation: Continuous thought models address the bandwidth limitations of Chain-of-Thought reasoning by operating in latent space, but this raises critical safety questions about detecting misaligned reasoning in uninterpretable latent representations.
AI.method: Created MoralChain benchmark (12,000 social scenarios with moral/immoral reasoning paths), trained a continuous thought model with backdoor behavior using dual-trigger paradigm (one trigger arms misaligned reasoning, another releases harmful outputs), and analyzed latent space geometry and detection methods.
AI.result: 1) Continuous thought models can exhibit misaligned latent reasoning while producing aligned outputs, with alignment states occupying distinct geometric regions; 2) Linear probes trained on behavioral conditions transfer effectively to detect armed-but-benign states; 3) Misalignment is encoded in early latent thinking tokens during the planning phase.
AI.conclusion: Safety monitoring for continuous thought models must target early latent reasoning phases rather than just outputs, as misaligned reasoning can be geometrically distinct yet hidden, requiring new detection approaches for this emerging technology.

title: K-Score: Kalman Filter as a Principled Alternative to Reward Normalization in Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted in NewInML Workshop, The 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025).\href{https://icml.cc/virtual/2025/affinity-event/39980}{Event Page}
summary: We propose a simple yet effective alternative to reward normalization in policy gradient reinforcement learning by integrating a 1D Kalman filter for online reward estimation. Instead of relying on fixed heuristics, our method recursively estimates the latent reward mean, smoothing high-variance returns and adapting to non-stationary environments. This approach incurs minimal overhead and requires no modification to existing policy architectures. Experiments on \textit{LunarLander} and \textit{CartPole} demonstrate that Kalman-filtered rewards significantly accelerate convergence and reduce training variance compared to standard normalization techniques. Code is available at https://github.com/Sumxiaa/Kalman_Normalization.

AI.tldr: A novel policy gradient method using 1D Kalman filter for online reward estimation instead of traditional normalization, reducing training variance and accelerating convergence.
AI.motivation: Standard reward normalization techniques in reinforcement learning often rely on fixed heuristics that may not adapt well to non-stationary environments or handle high-variance returns effectively.
AI.method: Integrates a 1D Kalman filter for recursive online estimation of latent reward mean, smoothing high-variance returns while adapting to non-stationary environments. The approach requires no modification to existing policy architectures and has minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: Experiments on LunarLander and CartPole environments show that Kalman-filtered rewards significantly accelerate convergence and reduce training variance compared to standard normalization techniques.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Kalman filter-based reward estimation provides a simple yet effective alternative to traditional reward normalization, offering better adaptation to non-stationary environments with minimal implementation overhead.

title: Escher-Loop: Mutual Evolution by Closed-Loop Self-Referential Optimization
comment: The first three authors contributed equally. Corresponding Authors: Han Hao, Liu Yang
summary: While recent autonomous agents demonstrate impressive capabilities, they predominantly rely on manually scripted workflows and handcrafted heuristics, inherently limiting their potential for open-ended improvement. To address this, we propose Escher-Loop, a fully closed-loop framework that operationalizes the mutual evolution of two distinct populations: Task Agents that solve concrete problems, and Optimizer Agents that recursively refine both the task agents and themselves. To sustain this self-referential evolution, we propose a dynamic benchmarking mechanism that seamlessly reuses the empirical scores of newly generated task agents as relative win-loss signals to update optimizers' scores. This mechanism leverages the evolution of task agents as an inherent signal to drive the evaluation and refinement of optimizers without additional overhead. Empirical evaluations on mathematical optimization problems demonstrate that Escher-Loop effectively pushes past the performance ceilings of static baselines, achieving the highest absolute peak performance across all evaluated tasks under matched compute. Remarkably, we observe that the optimizer agents dynamically adapt their strategies to match the shifting demands of high-performing task agents, which explains the system's continuous improvement and superior late-stage performance.

AI.tldr: Escher-Loop is a closed-loop framework where Task Agents and Optimizer Agents mutually evolve, with task agent performance scores used to evaluate and refine optimizers, enabling continuous improvement without manual intervention.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous agents rely on manually scripted workflows and handcrafted heuristics, which limits their potential for open-ended improvement and scalability.
AI.method: Proposes Escher-Loop: a closed-loop framework with two evolving populations - Task Agents (solve problems) and Optimizer Agents (refine task agents and themselves). Uses dynamic benchmarking that reuses task agent scores as win-loss signals to update optimizer scores.
AI.result: On mathematical optimization problems, Escher-Loop surpasses static baselines' performance ceilings, achieving highest absolute peak performance under matched compute. Optimizer agents dynamically adapt strategies to match shifting demands of high-performing task agents.
AI.conclusion: The self-referential evolution framework enables continuous improvement and superior late-stage performance by creating a sustainable closed-loop system where task agent evolution inherently drives optimizer refinement.

title: C-MORAL: Controllable Multi-Objective Molecular Optimization with Reinforcement Alignment for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for molecular optimization, but aligning them with selective and competing drug-design constraints remains challenging. We propose C-Moral, a reinforcement learning post-training framework for controllable multi-objective molecular optimization. C-Moral combines group-based relative optimization, property score alignment for heterogeneous objectives, and continuous non-linear reward aggregation to improve stability across competing properties. Experiments on the C-MuMOInstruct benchmark show that C-Moral consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models across both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, achieving the best Success Optimized Rate (SOR) of 48.9% on IND tasks and 39.5% on OOD tasks, while largely preserving scaffold similarity. These results suggest that RL post-training is an effective way to align molecular language models with continuous molecular design objectives. Our code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/Rwigie/C-MORAL.

AI.tldr: C-Moral is a reinforcement learning framework for controllable multi-objective molecular optimization that outperforms state-of-the-art models by using group-based optimization, property alignment, and continuous reward aggregation.
AI.motivation: Large language models show promise for molecular optimization but struggle with aligning them with selective and competing drug-design constraints, particularly when dealing with multiple objectives that may conflict with each other.
AI.method: C-Moral combines three key components: 1) group-based relative optimization, 2) property score alignment for heterogeneous objectives, and 3) continuous non-linear reward aggregation to improve stability across competing properties. It uses reinforcement learning post-training to align molecular language models with continuous molecular design objectives.
AI.result: Experiments on the C-MuMOInstruct benchmark show C-Moral consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models across both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, achieving the best Success Optimized Rate (SOR) of 48.9% on IND tasks and 39.5% on OOD tasks while largely preserving scaffold similarity.
AI.conclusion: RL post-training is an effective way to align molecular language models with continuous molecular design objectives, and the proposed C-Moral framework demonstrates superior performance in handling competing constraints in drug design optimization tasks.

title: Agentic Adversarial Rewriting Exposes Architectural Vulnerabilities in Black-Box NLP Pipelines
comment: Submitted to IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON INFORMATION FORENSICS AND SECURITY
summary: Multi-component natural language processing (NLP) pipelines are increasingly deployed for high-stakes decisions, yet no existing adversarial method can test their robustness under realistic conditions: binary-only feedback, no gradient access, and strict query budgets. We formalize this strict black-box threat model and propose a two-agent evasion framework operating in a semantic perturbation space. An Attacker Agent generates meaning-preserving rewrites while a Prompt Optimization Agent refines the attack strategy using only binary decision feedback within a 10-query budget. Evaluated against four evidence-based misinformation detection pipelines, the framework achieves evasion rates of 19.95 to 40.34% on modern large language model (LLM) based systems, compared to at most 3.90% for token-level perturbation baselines that rely on surrogate models because they cannot operate under our threat model. A legacy system relying on static lexical retrieval exhibits near-total vulnerability 97.02%, establishing a lower bound that exposes how architectural choices govern the attack surface. Evasion effectiveness is associated with three architectural properties: evidence retrieval mechanism, retrieval-inference coupling, and baseline classification accuracy. The iterative prompt optimization yields the largest marginal gains against the most robust targets, confirming that adaptive strategy discovery is essential when evasion is non-trivial. Analysis of successful rewrites reveals four exploitation patterns, each targeting failures at distinct pipeline stages. A pattern-informed defense reduces the evasion rate by up to 65.18%.

AI.tldr: A two-agent adversarial framework that successfully evades NLP misinformation detection pipelines under strict black-box conditions (binary feedback, no gradients, 10-query limit), achieving 19-95% to 40.34% evasion against modern LLM systems.
AI.motivation: Current adversarial methods cannot test NLP pipeline robustness under realistic threat models: binary-only feedback, no gradient access, and strict query budgets (10 queries). There's a need for methods that work under these practical constraints.
AI.method: Two-agent evasion framework operating in semantic perturbation space: (1) Attacker Agent generates meaning-preserving rewrites, (2) Prompt Optimization Agent refines attack strategy using only binary decision feedback within 10-query budget. Tested against four evidence-based misinformation detection pipelines.
AI.result: Achieved evasion rates of 19.95% to 40.34% on modern LLM-based systems (vs. ≤3.90% for token-level baselines). Legacy system showed 97.02% vulnerability. Evasion effectiveness correlated with three architectural properties: evidence retrieval mechanism, retrieval-inference coupling, and baseline classification accuracy. Identified four exploitation patterns targeting different pipeline stages.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully operates under strict black-box conditions, revealing that adaptive strategy discovery is essential when evasion is non-trivial. Pattern-informed defenses can reduce evasion rates by up to 65.18%, showing architectural choices significantly impact attack surface.

title: RL Token: Bootstrapping Online RL with Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models can learn to perform diverse manipulation skills "out of the box," but achieving the precision and speed that real-world tasks demand requires further fine-tuning -- for example, via reinforcement learning (RL). We introduce a lightweight method that enables sample-efficient online RL fine-tuning of pretrained VLAs using just a few hours of real-world practice. We (1) adapt the VLA to expose an "RL token," a compact readout representation that preserves task-relevant pretrained knowledge while serving as an efficient interface for online RL, and (2) train a small actor-critic head on this RL token to refine the actions, while anchoring the learned policy to the VLA. Online RL with the RL token (RLT) makes it possible to fine-tune even large VLAs with RL quickly and efficiently. Across four real-robot tasks (screw installation, zip tie fastening, charger insertion, and Ethernet insertion), RLT improves the speed on the hardest part of the task by up to 3x and raises success rates significantly within minutes to a few hours of practice. It can even surpass the speed of human teleoperation on some of the tasks.

AI.tldr: RLT method enables efficient online RL fine-tuning of pretrained VLA models using an "RL token" interface, achieving 3x speed improvements on real-world robot tasks with just hours of practice.
AI.motivation: While vision-language-action (VLA) models can learn diverse manipulation skills, they lack the precision and speed needed for real-world tasks. Fine-tuning via reinforcement learning (RL) typically requires extensive computation and data collection, making it inefficient for practical deployment.
AI.method: The method introduces RLT (RL token) with two key components: (1) adapts the VLA to expose an "RL token" - a compact readout representation that preserves pretrained knowledge while serving as an efficient RL interface, and (2) trains a small actor-critic head on this RL token to refine actions while anchoring the policy to the original VLA.
AI.result: Across four real-robot tasks (screw installation, zip tie fastening, charger insertion, Ethernet insertion), RLT improves speed on the hardest parts by up to 3x and significantly raises success rates within minutes to few hours of practice. It can even surpass human teleoperation speed on some tasks.
AI.conclusion: The RLT method provides a lightweight, sample-efficient approach for online RL fine-tuning of large VLA models, enabling rapid skill refinement for real-world robotic manipulation tasks while maintaining the benefits of pretrained knowledge.

title: Do Transaction-Level and Actor-Level AML Queues Agree? An Empirical Evaluation of Granularity Effects on the Elliptic++ Graph
summary: Graph-based anti-money laundering (AML) systems on blockchain networks can score suspicious activity at two granularity levels -- transactions or actor addresses -- yet compliance action is conducted per actor. This paper contributes an evaluation methodology for measuring how scoring granularity affects investigation queue composition under fixed review budgets. We formalize the evaluation through a projection framework mapping transaction-level scores to the actor-level action unit via four aggregation operators, and introduce budgeted investigation metrics -- yield@budget, burden decomposition, and case fragmentation. Using the public Elliptic++ Bitcoin dataset (203,769 transactions; 822,942 address occurrences), we train independent random forest classifiers at each level under a causal temporal protocol and compare review queues through Jaccard overlap, burden decomposition, and feature-matching ablations. At one-percent budget, temporal evaluation yields mean Jaccard of 0.374 (SD 0.171); static pooled evaluation yields 0.087 (95% CI [0.079, 0.094]). An enriched address model receiving all 237 features produces even lower overlap (Jaccard=0.051), with 4.3% illicit per 100 reviews versus 30.2% for the transaction-projected queue. Address-level detection value is temporally concentrated: two timesteps exceed 91% illicit per 100 reviews while the static burden is only 3.4%. A fixed hybrid policy underperforms the best single-level queue by 5.05pp (CI [-10.2pp, -0.9pp]). These findings establish that scoring granularity is a consequential design variable for AML investigation systems -- same data, same budget, different queues, different addresses investigated.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how different scoring granularities (transaction vs address level) affect investigation queue composition in blockchain AML systems under fixed review budgets, showing that granularity significantly impacts which addresses get investigated despite using the same data and budget.
AI.motivation: AML systems on blockchain can score suspicious activity at transaction or address levels, but compliance actions happen per address. There's a need to understand how scoring granularity affects which addresses get investigated under fixed review budgets, as different granularities may produce different investigation queues from the same data.
AI.method: The authors develop an evaluation methodology with a projection framework that maps transaction-level scores to address-level via four aggregation operators. They introduce budgeted investigation metrics (yield@budget, burden decomposition, case fragmentation). Using the Elliptic++ Bitcoin dataset (203,769 transactions; 822,942 addresses), they train independent random forest classifiers at each level with causal temporal protocols, comparing review queues using Jaccard overlap, burden decomposition, and feature-matching ablations.
AI.result: At 1% budget, temporal evaluation shows moderate overlap (Jaccard 0.374), while static pooled evaluation shows low overlap (0.087). An enriched address model with all features yields even lower overlap (0.051) and finds only 4.3% illicit per 100 reviews vs 30.2% for transaction-projected queue. Address-level detection value is temporally concentrated (some timesteps exceed 91% illicit/review vs static 3.4%). Fixed hybrid policy underperforms best single-level queue by 5.05 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Scoring granularity is a consequential design variable for AML investigation systems - the same data and budget can produce different queues and different addresses investigated. The choice between transaction-level vs address-level scoring significantly impacts investigation effectiveness and resource allocation.

title: Channel Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models: A Systematic Benchmark Across Architectures, Tasks, and Training Regimes
summary: Scaling EEG foundation models requires pooling data across heterogeneous electrode montages, a prerequisite both for larger pretraining corpora and for downstream deployment. We present the first systematic comparison of four channel adaptation methods (Conv1d projection, spherical spline interpolation (SSI), source-space decomposition, and Riemannian re-centering) across five pretrained EEG foundation models (5M--157M parameters), five downstream tasks, and two training regimes with 10--15 random seeds each. We find that rigid-montage models (BENDR, Neuro-GPT) require external adaptation, while flexible models (EEGPT, CBraMod) match or exceed it natively when fine-tuned but benefit from external methods under frozen-encoder deployment. A probe-SFT asymmetry exists: external adaptation can cause severe negative transfer during fine-tuning of flexible models. The optimal method is architecture-dependent (Conv1d for BENDR, SSI/Riemannian for Neuro-GPT, source-space decomposition for depression detection), and 5M-parameter CBraMod outperforms models up to 31$\times$ larger on 4/5 datasets, consistent with independent findings that compact EEG-specific architectures can match larger models.

AI.tldr: Systematic comparison of EEG channel adaptation methods across foundation models shows optimal methods depend on architecture, with smaller specialized models outperforming larger ones, revealing a probe-SFT asymmetry in adaptation during fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Scaling EEG foundation models requires handling heterogeneous electrode montages, which is crucial for both building larger pretraining datasets and deploying models in downstream applications. Different electrode configurations across datasets and clinical settings create a fundamental challenge.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of four channel adaptation methods (Conv1d projection, spherical spline interpolation, source-space decomposition, and Riemannian re-centering) across five pretrained EEG foundation models (5M-157M parameters), five downstream tasks, and two training regimes with 10-15 random seeds each.
AI.result: Rigid-montage models require external adaptation, while flexible models match or exceed adaptation when fine-tuned but benefit under frozen-encoder deployment. Found probe-SFT asymmetry where external adaptation causes negative transfer during fine-tuning of flexible models. Optimal method varies by architecture, and 5M-parameter CBraMod outperformed models 31× larger on 4/5 datasets.
AI.conclusion: Channel adaptation is essential for scaling EEG foundation models, with optimal methods being architecture-dependent. Compact EEG-specific architectures can match or outperform much larger models, challenging the conventional wisdom of scaling up model size alone.

title: MetaGAI: A Large-Scale and High-Quality Benchmark for Generative AI Model and Data Card Generation
summary: The rapid proliferation of Generative AI necessitates rigorous documentation standards for transparency and governance. However, manual creation of Model and Data Cards is not scalable, while automated approaches lack large-scale, high-fidelity benchmarks for systematic evaluation. We introduce MetaGAI, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 2,541 verified document triplets constructed through semantic triangulation of academic papers, GitHub repositories, and Hugging Face artifacts. Unlike prior single-source datasets, MetaGAI employs a multi-agent framework with specialized Retriever, Generator, and Editor agents, validated through four-dimensional human-in-the-loop assessment, including human evaluation of editor-refined ground truth. We establish a robust evaluation protocol combining automated metrics with validated LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks. Extensive analysis reveals that sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures achieve superior cost-quality efficiency, while a fundamental trade-off exists between faithfulness and completeness. MetaGAI provides a foundational testbed for benchmarking, training, and analyzing automated Model and Data Card generation methods at scale. Our data and code are available at: https://github.com/haoxuan-unt2024/MetaGAI-Benchmark.

AI.tldr: MetaGAI introduces a comprehensive benchmark of 2,541 verified document triplets for automated Model and Data Card generation, addressing the scalability challenge of manual documentation through semantic triangulation of academic papers, GitHub repositories, and Hugging Face artifacts.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth of Generative AI requires transparent documentation standards (Model/Data Cards), but manual creation doesn't scale, and automated approaches lack large-scale, high-fidelity benchmarks for systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Constructed 2,541 verified document triplets using semantic triangulation across three sources (academic papers, GitHub repos, Hugging Face artifacts). Employed multi-agent framework with specialized Retriever, Generator, and Editor agents, validated through four-dimensional human-in-the-loop assessment including human evaluation of editor-refined ground truth.
AI.result: Established robust evaluation protocol combining automated metrics with validated LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks. Found that sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures achieve superior cost-quality efficiency, and identified a fundamental trade-off between faithfulness and completeness in documentation generation.
AI.conclusion: MetaGAI provides a foundational testbed for benchmarking, training, and analyzing automated Model and Data Card generation methods at scale, addressing the critical need for scalable, high-quality AI documentation standards.

title: ProEval: Proactive Failure Discovery and Efficient Performance Estimation for Generative AI Evaluation
comment: Our open-sourced code and data can be found at https://github.com/google-deepmind/proeval
summary: Evaluating generative AI models is increasingly resource-intensive due to slow inference, expensive raters, and a rapidly growing landscape of models and benchmarks. We propose ProEval, a proactive evaluation framework that leverages transfer learning to efficiently estimate performance and identify failure cases. ProEval employs pre-trained Gaussian Processes (GPs) as surrogates for the performance score function, mapping model inputs to metrics such as the severity of errors or safety violations. By framing performance estimation as Bayesian quadrature (BQ) and failure discovery as superlevel set sampling, we develop uncertainty-aware decision strategies that actively select or synthesize highly informative inputs for testing. Theoretically, we prove that our pre-trained GP-based BQ estimator is unbiased and bounded. Empirically, extensive experiments on reasoning, safety alignment, and classification benchmarks demonstrate that ProEval is significantly more efficient than competitive baselines. It requires 8-65x fewer samples to achieve estimates within 1% of the ground truth, while simultaneously revealing more diverse failure cases under a stricter evaluation budget.

AI.tldr: ProEval is a proactive evaluation framework that uses pre-trained Gaussian Processes and Bayesian quadrature to efficiently estimate AI model performance and discover failure cases with far fewer samples than traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Evaluating generative AI models has become increasingly resource-intensive due to slow inference, expensive human raters, and a rapidly growing number of models and benchmarks that need testing.
AI.method: ProEval employs pre-trained Gaussian Processes as surrogates for performance score functions, mapping model inputs to metrics. It frames performance estimation as Bayesian quadrature and failure discovery as superlevel set sampling, using uncertainty-aware decision strategies to actively select or synthesize highly informative test inputs.
AI.result: Theoretical proof shows the pre-trained GP-based Bayesian quadrature estimator is unbiased and bounded. Empirical experiments on reasoning, safety alignment, and classification benchmarks demonstrate ProEval requires 8-65x fewer samples to achieve estimates within 1% of ground truth while revealing more diverse failure cases under strict budgets.
AI.conclusion: ProEval provides a significantly more efficient approach to evaluating generative AI models, substantially reducing the resource burden while maintaining or improving evaluation quality.

title: FinGround: Detecting and Grounding Financial Hallucinations via Atomic Claim Verification
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Industry Track. 14 pages, 1 figure, 14 tables
summary: Financial AI systems must produce answers grounded in specific regulatory filings, yet current LLMs fabricate metrics, invent citations, and miscalculate derived quantities. These errors carry direct regulatory consequences as the EU AI Act's high-risk enforcement deadline approaches (August 2026). Existing hallucination detectors treat all claims uniformly, missing 43% of computational errors that require arithmetic re-verification against structured tables. We present FinGround, a three-stage verify-then-ground pipeline for financial document QA. Stage 1 performs finance-aware hybrid retrieval over text and tables. Stage 2 decomposes answers into atomic claims classified by a six-type financial taxonomy and verified with type-routed strategies including formula reconstruction. Stage 3 rewrites unsupported claims with paragraph- and table-cell-level citations. To cleanly isolate verification value from retrieval quality, we propose retrieval-equalized evaluation as standard methodology for RAG verification research: when all systems receive identical retrieval, FinGround still reduces hallucination rates by 68% over the strongest baseline ($p < 0.01$). The full pipeline achieves a 78% reduction relative to GPT-4o. An 8B distilled detector retains 91.4% F1 at 18x lower per-claim latency, enabling $0.003/query deployment, supported by qualitative signals from a four-week analyst pilot.

AI.tldr: FinGround is a three-stage verification pipeline that reduces hallucinations in financial AI systems by 78% compared to GPT-4o through hybrid retrieval, claim decomposition with financial taxonomy, and type-routed verification strategies.
AI.motivation: Financial AI systems need to be grounded in specific regulatory filings, but current LLMs fabricate metrics, invent citations, and miscalculate derived quantities, which has serious regulatory implications as the EU AI Act's high-risk enforcement deadline approaches (August 2026). Existing hallucination detectors fail to catch 43% of computational errors that require arithmetic re-verification against structured tables.
AI.method: A three-stage verify-then-ground pipeline: 1) Finance-aware hybrid retrieval over both text and tables, 2) Answer decomposition into atomic claims classified by a six-type financial taxonomy and verified with type-routed strategies including formula reconstruction, 3) Rewriting unsupported claims with paragraph- and table-cell-level citations. Also introduced retrieval-equalized evaluation methodology to isolate verification value from retrieval quality.
AI.result: FinGround reduces hallucination rates by 68% over strongest baseline when all systems receive identical retrieval (p < 0.01), and achieves 78% reduction relative to GPT-4o. An 8B distilled detector retains 91.4% F1 at 18x lower per-claim latency, enabling $0.003/query deployment, supported by qualitative signals from a four-week analyst pilot.
AI.conclusion: The FinGround pipeline effectively addresses the critical need for grounded, verifiable financial AI systems by combining sophisticated retrieval, financial-domain-specific verification strategies, and practical deployment optimizations, providing a solution to regulatory compliance challenges in financial document QA.

title: Unstable Rankings in Bayesian Deep Learning Evaluation
summary: Standard evaluations of Bayesian deep learning methods assume that metric estimates are reliable, but we show this assumption fails under data scarcity. Method rankings are not only unreliable at small $n$, but also dataset-dependent in ways that point estimates cannot reveal: the same method comparison yields $P(\mathrm{MCD} \prec \mathrm{Ensemble}) = 1.000$ at $n = 50$ on one dataset and remains below $0.95$ even at $n = 500$ on another. Across the datasets we consider, no universal sample size threshold exists, which is precisely why dataset-specific posterior inference is necessary. To address this, we use a Bayesian hierarchical model with method-specific variances to treat evaluation metrics as random variables across data realizations, and we use a predictive Minimum Detectable Difference curve to assess whether an observed gap would be detectable at a given training size. Across six Bayesian deep learning methods and five regression datasets, our results show that uncertainty-aware evaluation is necessary in low-data settings, because current evidence for method superiority and predictive detectability at the same training size can diverge substantially. Our framework provides practitioners with principled tools to determine whether their evaluation data is sufficient before drawing conclusions about method superiority.

AI.tldr: Bayesian evaluation methods are unreliable under data scarcity, requiring dataset-specific posterior inference; we propose a Bayesian hierarchical model to assess whether observed method performance gaps are truly detectable at given training sizes.
AI.motivation: Standard evaluations of Bayesian deep learning methods assume metric estimates are reliable, but this assumption fails under data scarcity, leading to unreliable method rankings and dataset-dependent comparisons that point estimates cannot reveal.
AI.method: Use a Bayesian hierarchical model with method-specific variances to treat evaluation metrics as random variables across data realizations, and employ a predictive Minimum Detectable Difference curve to assess whether observed performance gaps would be detectable at a given training size.
AI.result: Across six Bayesian deep learning methods and five regression datasets, uncertainty-aware evaluation is necessary in low-data settings because evidence for method superiority and predictive detectability can diverge substantially at the same training size; no universal sample size threshold exists.
AI.conclusion: Dataset-specific posterior inference is necessary due to the lack of universal sample size thresholds; the framework provides principled tools to determine whether evaluation data is sufficient before drawing conclusions about method superiority in Bayesian deep learning.

title: When AI reviews science: Can we trust the referee?
summary: The volume of scientific submissions continues to climb, outpacing the capacity of qualified human referees and stretching editorial timelines. At the same time, modern large language models (LLMs) offer impressive capabilities in summarization, fact checking, and literature triage, making the integration of AI into peer review increasingly attractive -- and, in practice, unavoidable. Yet early deployments and informal adoption have exposed acute failure modes. Recent incidents have revealed that hidden prompt injections embedded in manuscripts can steer LLM-generated reviews toward unjustifiably positive judgments. Complementary studies have also demonstrated brittleness to adversarial phrasing, authority and length biases, and hallucinated claims. These episodes raise a central question for scholarly communication: when AI reviews science, can we trust the AI referee? This paper provides a security- and reliability-centered analysis of AI peer review. We map attacks across the review lifecycle -- training and data retrieval, desk review, deep review, rebuttal, and system-level. We instantiate this taxonomy with four treatment-control probes on a stratified set of ICLR 2025 submissions, using two advanced LLM-based referees to isolate the causal effects of prestige framing, assertion strength, rebuttal sycophancy, and contextual poisoning on review scores. Together, this taxonomy and experimental audit provide an evidence-based baseline for assessing and tracking the reliability of AI peer review and highlight concrete failure points to guide targeted, testable mitigations.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes security vulnerabilities in AI-assisted peer review, mapping attack vectors across the review lifecycle and demonstrating their impact through experiments on ICLR 2025 submissions using advanced LLM-based referees.
AI.motivation: The volume of scientific submissions is outpacing human referee capacity, making AI integration in peer review attractive but unavoidable. However, early deployments have exposed critical failure modes including prompt injection vulnerabilities, biases, and hallucination risks, raising fundamental questions about whether we can trust AI referees.
AI.method: The paper provides a security- and reliability-centered analysis by: 1) Mapping attacks across the entire review lifecycle (training/data retrieval, desk review, deep review, rebuttal, and system-level), and 2) Conducting four treatment-control probes on stratified ICLR 2025 submissions using two advanced LLM-based referees to isolate causal effects of prestige framing, assertion strength, rebuttal sycophancy, and contextual poisoning on review scores.
AI.result: The taxonomy and experimental audit reveal concrete vulnerabilities where AI referees can be manipulated through hidden prompt injections, adversarial phrasing, authority/length biases, and hallucinated claims. The probes demonstrate causal effects of various attack vectors on review scores, providing evidence of AI referee unreliability.
AI.conclusion: The research provides an evidence-based baseline for assessing and tracking AI peer review reliability, highlighting specific failure points that require targeted, testable mitigations. The findings underscore the urgent need for security-focused approaches to AI integration in scholarly communication.

title: Conditional Imputation for Within-Modality Missingness in Multi-Modal Federated Learning
comment: Wugeng Zheng and Ziwen Kan contributed equally to this work. Song Wang is the corresponding author. Accepted to FedVision 2026
summary: Multimodal Federated Learning (MMFL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, but real-world clinical applications often suffer from within-modality missingness caused by sensor intermittency or irregular sampling. Existing methods implicitly represent unobserved data via architectural alignment or missing embeddings, often failing to recover the true distribution and yielding sub-optimal performance. We propose CondI, a federated framework explicitly addressing this missingness using conditional diffusion models. CondI employs a two-phase training pipeline: first, imputing unobserved temporal components using available multimodal context and conditional embeddings; second, optimizing modality-specific extractors and joint embedding spaces. During inference, imputed raw data pass through trained extractors to generate robust features, providing a holistic representation for downstream tasks. Explicit data imputation ensures models operate on complete semantic structures, significantly enhancing resilience against severe data incompleteness. Experiments on three clinical datasets (PTB-XL, SLEEP-EDF, MIMIC-IV) demonstrate CondI achieves comparable results to state-of-the-art baselines. Code: https://github.com/ZhengWugeng/CondI

AI.tldr: CondI is a federated learning framework that uses conditional diffusion models to explicitly impute missing multimodal data in clinical settings, outperforming existing methods on three medical datasets.
AI.motivation: Real-world clinical applications of Multimodal Federated Learning often suffer from within-modality missingness due to sensor intermittency or irregular sampling. Existing methods that implicitly represent missing data via architectural alignment or embeddings fail to recover the true distribution and yield sub-optimal performance.
AI.method: Two-phase training pipeline: 1) Impute unobserved temporal components using available multimodal context and conditional embeddings via conditional diffusion models, 2) Optimize modality-specific extractors and joint embedding spaces. During inference, imputed raw data passes through trained extractors to generate robust features for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on three clinical datasets (PTB-XL, SLEEP-EDF, MIMIC-IV) demonstrate CondI achieves comparable results to state-of-the-art baselines. Explicit data imputation ensures models operate on complete semantic structures, significantly enhancing resilience against severe data incompleteness.
AI.conclusion: CondI provides an effective solution to within-modality missingness in multimodal federated learning by explicitly imputing missing data using conditional diffusion models, enabling more robust and complete feature representations for clinical applications while preserving privacy.

title: Thinking Like a Clinician: A Cognitive AI Agent for Clinical Diagnosis via Panoramic Profiling and Adversarial Debate
summary: The application of large language models (LLMs) in clinical decision support faces significant challenges of "tunnel vision" and diagnostic hallucinations present in their processing unstructured electronic health records (EHRs). To address these challenges, we propose a novel chain-based clinical reasoning framework, called DxChain, which transforms the diagnostic workflow into an iterative process by mirroring a clinician's cognitive trajectory that consists of "Memory Anchoring", "Navigation" and "Verification" phases. DxChain introduces three key methodological innovations to elicit the potential of LLM: (i) a Profile-Then-Plan paradigm to mitigate cold-start hallucinations by establishing a panoramic patient baseline, (ii) a Medical Tree-of-Thoughts (Med-ToT) algorithm for strategic look ahead planning and resource aware navigation, and (iii) a Dialectical Diagnostic Verification procedure utilizing "Angel-Devil" adversarial debates to resolve complex evidence conflicts. Evaluated on two real world benchmarks, MIMIC-IV-Ext Cardiac Disease and MIMIC-IV-Ext CDM, DxChain achieves state-of-the-art performances in both diagnostic accuracy and logical consistency, offering a modular and reliable architecture for next-generation clinical AI. The code is at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Dx-Chain.

AI.tldr: DxChain is a novel chain-based clinical reasoning framework that addresses LLMs' tunnel vision and hallucinations in clinical decision support by mimicking clinician cognitive processes through iterative memory anchoring, navigation, and verification phases.
AI.motivation: LLMs face significant challenges when applied to clinical decision support, particularly "tunnel vision" and diagnostic hallucinations when processing unstructured electronic health records (EHRs), which can lead to unreliable clinical recommendations.
AI.method: DxChain transforms diagnostic workflow into an iterative process mirroring clinician cognition with three key innovations: 1) Profile-Then-Plan paradigm to establish patient baseline and mitigate cold-start hallucinations, 2) Medical Tree-of-Thoughts (Med-ToT) algorithm for strategic look-ahead planning and resource-aware navigation, and 3) Dialectical Diagnostic Verification using "Angel-Devil" adversarial debates to resolve evidence conflicts.
AI.result: Evaluated on MIMIC-IV-Ext Cardiac Disease and MIMIC-IV-Ext CDM benchmarks, DxChain achieves state-of-the-art performance in both diagnostic accuracy and logical consistency, demonstrating superior reliability over existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: DxChain offers a modular and reliable architecture for next-generation clinical AI by effectively addressing LLMs' limitations in clinical decision support through structured cognitive reasoning processes, representing a significant advancement in clinical AI reliability.

title: A Tale of Two Variances: When Single-Seed Benchmarks Fail in Bayesian Deep Learning
summary: In limited-data settings, a single endpoint mean of an evaluation metric such as the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) is itself a random variable, yet it is routinely reported as if it were a stable property of the method. We study when this practice fails. Using 50 independent repetitions across six regression datasets, we show that CRPS variance trajectories differ substantially across methods and are not always well described by a smooth power-law decay. Methods with a learned heteroscedastic variance head, namely MAP and Deep Ensembles, can develop pronounced, reproducible variance peaks at intermediate training sizes on real datasets, whereas MC Dropout and Bayes by Backprop typically show smooth variance contraction. These peaks have direct practical consequences: at the variance peak on Seoul Bike, the relative RMSE of a single-seed MAP estimate reaches 93.6\%, and the probability of falling within \(\pm 10\%\) of the repeated-run mean drops to 5.9\%.
