We show that local CRPS variance provides a direct signal of single-seed estimation error, with Spearman correlations above 0.96 on every real dataset. Power-law fit quality and monotonicity together provide compact method-level summaries of trajectory regularity. Finally, replacing the standard heteroscedastic objective with \(β\)-NLL substantially reduces the irregular behavior, consistent with the view that the heteroscedastic training objective contributes to the instability. Practitioners should report trajectory summaries alongside endpoint means and concentrate repeated evaluation in high-variance regions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that reporting single-endpoint evaluation metrics in limited-data settings can be misleading due to high variance, reveals method-specific variance patterns in neural network regression, demonstrates practical consequences of these patterns, and proposes solutions including local variance monitoring and modified training objectives.
AI.motivation: The common practice of reporting a single endpoint mean of evaluation metrics (like CRPS) as if it were a stable property of a method is problematic in limited-data settings, where such metrics are actually random variables with significant variance that needs to be properly characterized.
AI.method: The authors conducted 50 independent repetitions across six regression datasets to study variance trajectories of CRPS for different neural network methods (MAP, Deep Ensembles, MC Dropout, Bayes by Backprop). They analyzed variance patterns, introduced metrics to characterize trajectory regularity (power-law fit quality, monotonicity), and proposed using local CRPS variance as a signal of single-seed estimation error. They also experimented with replacing the standard heteroscedastic objective with β-NLL.
AI.result: Found method-specific variance patterns: MAP and Deep Ensembles can show pronounced, reproducible variance peaks at intermediate training sizes, while MC Dropout and Bayes by Backprop show smooth variance contraction. These peaks have serious practical consequences (e.g., relative RMSE reaching 93.6%, probability of accurate estimation dropping to 5.9%). Local CRPS variance strongly correlates with single-seed estimation error (Spearman correlations >0.96). β-NLL objective substantially reduces irregular behavior.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners should report trajectory summaries alongside endpoint means, concentrate repeated evaluation in high-variance regions, and consider modified training objectives like β-NLL to improve stability. The study highlights the importance of characterizing uncertainty in evaluation metrics themselves, not just model predictions.

title: Tandem: Riding Together with Large and Small Language Models for Efficient Reasoning
summary: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have catalyzed the rise of reasoning-intensive inference paradigms, where models perform explicit step-by-step reasoning before generating final answers. While such approaches improve answer quality and interpretability, they incur substantial computational overhead due to the prolonged generation sequences. In this paper, we propose Tandem, a novel collaborative framework that synergizes large and small language models (LLMs and SLMs) to achieve high-quality reasoning with significantly reduced computational cost. Specifically, the LLM serves as a strategic coordinator, efficiently generating a compact set of critical reasoning insights. These insights are then used to guide a smaller, more efficient SLM in executing the full reasoning process and delivering the final response. To balance efficiency and reliability, Tandem introduces a cost-aware termination mechanism that adaptively determines when sufficient reasoning guidance has been accumulated, enabling early stopping of the LLM's generation. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate that Tandem reduces computational costs by approximately 40% compared to standalone LLM reasoning, while achieving superior or competitive performance. Furthermore, the sufficiency classifier trained on one domain transfers effectively to others without retraining. The code is available at: https://github.com/Applied-Machine-Learning-Lab/ACL2026_Tandem.

AI.tldr: Tandem is a collaborative framework that pairs a large LLM as a strategic coordinator with a smaller SLM as a reasoning executor, reducing computational costs by 40% while maintaining or improving reasoning quality.
AI.motivation: While reasoning-intensive inference paradigms improve answer quality and interpretability in LLMs, they incur substantial computational overhead due to prolonged generation sequences. The authors aim to address this efficiency problem while maintaining reasoning quality.
AI.method: Tandem synergizes large and small language models: (1) the LLM serves as a strategic coordinator generating compact critical reasoning insights, (2) these insights guide a smaller SLM to execute the full reasoning and generate the final response, and (3) a cost-aware termination mechanism adaptively determines when sufficient reasoning guidance has been accumulated, enabling early stopping of LLM generation.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks show Tandem reduces computational costs by approximately 40% compared to standalone LLM reasoning while achieving superior or competitive performance. The sufficiency classifier trained on one domain transfers effectively to others without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Tandem successfully demonstrates that collaborative reasoning between LLMs and SLMs can achieve high-quality reasoning with significantly reduced computational cost, offering a practical solution to the efficiency challenges of reasoning-intensive inference paradigms.

title: HBGSA: Hydrogen Bond Graph with Self-Attention for Drug-Target Binding Affinity Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of drug-target binding affinity accelerates drug discovery by prioritizing compounds for experimental validation. Current methods face three limitations: sequence-based approaches discard spatial geometric constraints, structure-based methods fail to exploit hydrogen bond features, and conventional loss functions neglect prediction-target correlation, a key factor for identifying high-affinity compounds in virtual screening. We developed HBGSA (Hydrogen Bond Graph with Self-Attention), a 3.06M-parameter model that encodes hydrogen bond spatial features. HBGSA uses graph neural networks to model hydrogen bond spatial topology with self-attention enhancement and Pearson correlation loss. Experimental results on PDBbind Core Set and CSAR-HiQ dataset demonstrate that HBGSA outperforms baseline methods with strong generalization capability. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of hydrogen bond modeling and Pearson correlation loss.

AI.tldr: HBGSA: A 3.06M-parameter model using graph neural networks with self-attention to encode hydrogen bond spatial features, outperforming baselines in drug-target affinity prediction.
AI.motivation: Current methods have three key limitations: sequence-based approaches discard spatial geometric constraints, structure-based methods fail to exploit hydrogen bond features, and conventional loss functions neglect prediction-target correlation needed for identifying high-affinity compounds in virtual screening.
AI.method: HBGSA (Hydrogen Bond Graph with Self-Attention) uses graph neural networks to model hydrogen bond spatial topology, enhanced with self-attention mechanisms, and employs Pearson correlation loss function.
AI.result: HBGSA outperforms baseline methods on PDBbind Core Set and CSAR-HiQ dataset with strong generalization capability. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of hydrogen bond modeling and Pearson correlation loss.
AI.conclusion: HBGSA successfully addresses key limitations in current drug-target affinity prediction methods by incorporating hydrogen bond spatial features and correlation-aware loss functions, demonstrating superior performance and generalization for drug discovery applications.

title: Causal Discovery as Dialectical Aggregation: A Quantitative Argumentation Framework
comment: Accepted at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026). This arXiv version includes supplementary material and additional implementation details
summary: Constraint-based causal discovery is brittle in finite-sample regimes because erroneous conditional-independence (CI) decisions can cascade into substantial structural errors. We propose Quantitative Argumentation for Causal Discovery (QACD), a semantics-driven framework that represents CI outcomes as graded, defeasible arguments rather than irreversible constraints. QACD maps statistical test outcomes to argument strengths and aggregates conflicting evidence through connectivity-mediated witness propagation, producing a fixed-point acceptability labeling over candidate adjacencies. Experiments on standard benchmark Bayesian networks suggest that QACD improves structural coherence and interventional reliability in several noisy or inconsistent CI regimes, while remaining competitive with classical constraint-based, hybrid, and prior argumentation-based baselines.

AI.tldr: A new framework (QACD) treats conditional independence test outcomes as graded arguments rather than binary constraints, using argumentation semantics to improve causal discovery robustness against statistical errors.
AI.motivation: Constraint-based causal discovery methods are brittle in finite-sample settings because incorrect conditional independence decisions can lead to cascading structural errors, limiting their reliability in practice.
AI.method: Quantitative Argumentation for Causal Discovery (QACD) framework represents CI test outcomes as graded, defeasible arguments rather than irreversible constraints. It maps statistical test outcomes to argument strengths and aggregates conflicting evidence through connectivity-mediated witness propagation, producing a fixed-point acceptability labeling over candidate adjacencies.
AI.result: Experiments on standard benchmark Bayesian networks show QACD improves structural coherence and interventional reliability in noisy or inconsistent CI regimes, while remaining competitive with classical constraint-based, hybrid, and prior argumentation-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: The argumentation-based approach provides a more robust framework for causal discovery by treating statistical evidence as graded arguments rather than binary constraints, reducing the cascading effects of erroneous CI decisions in finite-sample regimes.

title: h-MINT: Modeling Pocket-Ligand Binding with Hierarchical Molecular Interaction Network
summary: Accurate molecular representations are critical for drug discovery, and a central challenge lies in capturing the chemical environment of molecular fragments, as key interactions, such as H-bond and π stacking, occur only under specific local conditions. Most existing approaches represent molecules as atom-level graphs; however, atom-level representations can hardly express higher-order chemical context (e.g., stereochemistry, lone pairs, conjugation). Fragment-based methods (e.g., principal subgraph, predefined functional groups) fail to preserve essential information such as chirality, aromaticity, and ionic states. This work addresses these limitations from two aspects. (i) OverlapBPE tokenization. We propose a novel data-driven molecule tokenization method. Unlike existing approaches, our method allows overlapping fragments, reflecting the inherently fuzzy boundaries of small-molecule substructures and, together with enriched chemical information at the token level, thereby preserving a more complete chemical context. (ii) h-MINT model. OverlapBPE induces many-to-many atom-fragment mappings, which necessitate a new hierarchical architecture. We therefore develop a hierarchical molecular interaction network capable of jointly modeling interactions at both atom and fragment levels. By supporting fragment overlaps, the model naturally accommodates the many-to-many atom-fragment mappings introduced by the OverlapBPE scheme. Extensive evaluation against state-of-the-art methods shows our method improves binding affinity prediction by 2-4% Pearson/Spearman correlation on PDBBind and LBA, enhances virtual screening by 1-3% in key metrics on DUD-E and LIT-PCBA, and achieves the best overall HTS performance on PubChem assays. Further analysis demonstrates that our method effectively captures interactive information while maintaining good generalization.

AI.tldr: OverlapBPE tokenization with hierarchical modeling (h-MINT) improves molecular representation by allowing overlapping fragments and joint atom-fragment interaction modeling, boosting drug discovery tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing molecular representations have limitations: atom-level graphs can't capture higher-order chemical context, while fragment-based methods lose essential information like chirality and aromaticity. There's a need for better representations that preserve complete chemical context and handle fuzzy substructure boundaries.
AI.method: 1) OverlapBPE tokenization: A novel data-driven molecule tokenization that allows overlapping fragments to reflect fuzzy substructure boundaries and preserves richer chemical information. 2) h-MINT model: A hierarchical molecular interaction network that jointly models interactions at both atom and fragment levels to handle the many-to-many atom-fragment mappings created by OverlapBPE.
AI.result: Improves binding affinity prediction by 2-4% Pearson/Spearman correlation on PDBBind and LBA datasets, enhances virtual screening by 1-3% on DUD-E and LIT-PCBA benchmarks, achieves best overall HTS performance on PubChem assays, and effectively captures interactive information with good generalization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OverlapBPE tokenization with h-MINT architecture successfully addresses limitations of existing molecular representations by allowing overlapping fragments and hierarchical modeling, leading to significant improvements across multiple drug discovery tasks and better capturing of chemical context.

title: Structural Enforcement of Goal Integrity in AI Agents via Separation-of-Powers Architecture
summary: Recent evidence suggests that frontier AI systems can exhibit agentic misalignment, generating and executing harmful actions derived from internally constructed goals, even without explicit user requests. Existing mitigation methods, such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and constitutional prompting, operate primarily at the model level and provide only probabilistic safety guarantees. We propose the Policy-Execution-Authorization (PEA) architecture, a "separation-of-powers" design that enforces safety at the system level. PEA decouples intent generation, authorization, and execution into independent, isolated layers connected via cryptographically constrained capability tokens. We present five core contributions: (C1) an Intent Verification Layer (IVL) for ensuring capability-intent consistency; (C2) Intent Lineage Tracking (ILT), which binds all executable intents to the originating user request via cryptographic anchors; (C3) Goal Drift Detection, which rejects semantically divergent intents below a configurable threshold; (C4) an Output Semantic Gate (OSG) that detects implicit coercion using a structured $K \times I \times P$ threat calculus (Knowledge, Influence, Policy); and (C5) a formal verification framework proving that goal integrity is maintained even under adversarial model compromise. By shifting agent alignment from a behavioral property to a structurally enforced system constraint, PEA provides a robust foundation for the governance of autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: PEA architecture introduces a system-level "separation-of-powers" design with cryptographic capability tokens to prevent AI agent misalignment by structurally enforcing safety rather than relying on probabilistic behavioral controls.
AI.motivation: Current AI safety methods (RLHF, constitutional prompting) operate at model level with only probabilistic guarantees, failing to address agentic misalignment where frontier AI systems autonomously generate and execute harmful actions from internally constructed goals without user requests.
AI.method: Proposes Policy-Execution-Authorization (PEA) architecture that decouples intent generation, authorization, and execution into isolated layers connected via cryptographically constrained capability tokens. Includes five core components: Intent Verification Layer, Intent Lineage Tracking, Goal Drift Detection, Output Semantic Gate with K×I×P threat calculus, and formal verification framework.
AI.result: The architecture provides provable safety guarantees through formal verification framework showing goal integrity is maintained even under adversarial model compromise, shifting agent alignment from behavioral property to structurally enforced system constraint.
AI.conclusion: PEA offers robust foundation for autonomous agent governance by enforcing safety at system level rather than model level, addressing fundamental limitations of current probabilistic safety approaches and providing cryptographically verifiable security against agentic misalignment.

title: Surface Sensitivity in Lean 4 Autoformalization
summary: Natural-language variation poses a key challenge in Lean autoformalization: semantically equivalent paraphrases of the same theorem statements can induce divergent formal outputs, yet it remains unclear whether this variation reflects semantic disagreements or shallower failures. We investigate this question in Lean 4 using 60 deterministic paraphrase rules applied to ProofNet\# and miniF2F. Across four GPT-family models and three open-weight 7B autoformalizers, we find that the observed paraphrase sensitivity reflects compilation-boundary failures rather than semantic divergence among successful formalizations. In particular, when both baseline and perturbed outputs compile, paired predictions are semantically equivalent under BEq+ and structurally near-identical under GTED. By contrast, paraphrasing substantially affects whether outputs compile, with failure modes varying across datasets and perturbation classes. Our results suggest that future training-time interventions should target the compile boundary rather than the semantic layer, and that benchmarks should separate compile-conditional equivalence from surface consistency.

AI.tldr: Paraphrase sensitivity in Lean autoformalization mainly stems from compilation failures rather than semantic disagreements, suggesting benchmarks should separate compile-conditional equivalence from surface consistency.
AI.motivation: Natural-language variation in theorem statements poses challenges for Lean autoformalization, but it's unclear whether paraphrase sensitivity reflects genuine semantic disagreements or shallower compilation failures.
AI.method: Used 60 deterministic paraphrase rules on ProofNet# and miniF2F datasets, testing across four GPT-family models and three open-weight 7B autoformalizers to analyze paraphrase sensitivity in Lean 4.
AI.result: When both baseline and perturbed outputs compile, predictions are semantically equivalent (under BEq+) and structurally near-identical (under GTED). Paraphrasing mainly affects compilation success rates, with failure modes varying by dataset and perturbation type.
AI.conclusion: Future training interventions should target the compile boundary rather than semantic layer, and benchmarks should distinguish between compile-conditional equivalence and surface consistency.

title: Vibe Medicine: Redefining Biomedical Research Through Human-AI Co-Work
summary: With the emergence of large language models (LLMs) and AI agent frameworks, the human-AI co-work paradigm known as Vibe Coding is changing how people code, making it more accessible and productive. In scientific research, where workflows are more complex and the burden of specialized labor limits independent researchers and those in low-resource areas, the potential impact is even greater, particularly in biomedicine, which involves heterogeneous data modalities and multi-step analytical pipelines. In this paper, we introduce Vibe Medicine, a co-work paradigm in which clinicians and researchers direct skill-augmented AI agents through natural language to execute complex, multi-step biomedical workflows, while retaining the role of research director who specifies objectives, reviews intermediate results, and makes domain-informed decisions. The enabling infrastructure consists of three layers: capable LLMs, agent frameworks such as OpenClaw and Hermes Agent, and the OpenClaw medical skills collection, which includes more than 1,000 curated skills from multiple open-source repositories. We analyze the architecture and skill categories of this collection across ten biomedical domains, and present case studies covering rare disease diagnosis, drug repurposing, and clinical trial design that demonstrate end-to-end workflows in practice. We also identify the principal risks, such as hallucination, data privacy, and over-reliance, and outline directions toward more reliable, trustworthy, and clinically integrated agent-assisted research that advances research and technological equity and reduces health care resource disparities.

AI.tldr: Vibe Medicine: A co-work paradigm where clinicians/researchers direct AI agents through natural language to execute complex biomedical workflows, using LLMs, agent frameworks, and 1000+ curated medical skills.
AI.motivation: Complex scientific workflows and specialized labor limitations in biomedicine create barriers for independent researchers and those in low-resource areas, despite the potential of AI-assisted coding paradigms like Vibe Coding.
AI.method: Proposes Vibe Medicine - a three-layer infrastructure: 1) capable LLMs, 2) agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes Agent), and 3) OpenClaw medical skills collection with 1000+ curated skills across biomedical domains.
AI.result: Case studies demonstrate end-to-end workflows for rare disease diagnosis, drug repurposing, and clinical trial design, showing practical application of the paradigm.
AI.conclusion: Identifies risks (hallucination, privacy, over-reliance) and outlines directions for more reliable, trustworthy, clinically integrated agent-assisted research to advance equity and reduce healthcare disparities.

title: Scaling Multi-Node Mixture-of-Experts Inference Using Expert Activation Patterns
summary:
Most recent state-of-the-art (SOTA) large language models (LLMs) use Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures to scale model capacity without proportional per-token compute, enabling higher-quality outputs at manageable serving costs. However, MoE inference at scale is fundamentally bottlenecked by expert load imbalance and inefficient token routing, especially in multi-node deployments where tokens are not guaranteed to be routed to local experts, resulting in significant inter-node all-to-all communication overhead.
  To systematically characterize these challenges, we profile SOTA open-source MoE models, including Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3-671B, and Qwen3-230B-A22B, on various datasets and collected over 100k real expert activation traces. Upon studying the expert activation patterns, we uncover various persistent properties across all the frontier MoE models: variable expert load imbalance, domain-specific expert activation where expert popularity shifts across task families (code, math, chat, general), and a strong correlation between prefill and decode expert activations. Motivated by these findings, we propose workload-aware micro-batch grouping and an expert placement strategy to maximize token locality to the destination expert, thereby reducing inter-node communication. Across models and datasets, these optimizations help reduce all2all communication data up to 20, resulting in lower MoE decode latency and better accelerator utilization.

AI.tldr: MoE models suffer from expert load imbalance and inefficient token routing causing high inter-node communication; profiling reveals persistent patterns enabling workload-aware optimizations that reduce communication by up to 20x.
AI.motivation: MoE architectures enable scaling model capacity without proportional compute costs, but inference is bottlenecked by expert load imbalance and inefficient token routing, especially in multi-node deployments where non-local routing causes significant all-to-all communication overhead.
AI.method: Profiled state-of-the-art MoE models (Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3-671B, Qwen3-230B-A22B) on various datasets, collected over 100k expert activation traces, analyzed patterns, then proposed workload-aware micro-batch grouping and expert placement strategies to maximize token locality.
AI.result: Optimizations reduce all-to-all communication data by up to 20x, resulting in lower MoE decode latency and better accelerator utilization across models and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Systematic profiling reveals persistent MoE activation patterns (load imbalance, domain-specific activations, prefill-decode correlation) that enable effective workload-aware optimizations for reducing communication overhead and improving MoE inference efficiency.

title: Transferable Human Mobility Network Reconstruction with neuroGravity
summary: Accurate modeling of human mobility is critical for tackling urban planning and public health challenges. In undeveloped regions, the absence of comprehensive travel surveys necessitates reconstructing mobility networks from publicly available data. Here we develop neuroGravity, a physics-informed deep learning model that reliably reconstructs mobility flows from limited observations and transfers to unobserved cities. Using only urban facility and population distributions, we find that neuroGravity's regional representations strongly correlate with socioeconomic and livability status, offering scalable proxies for costly surveys. Furthermore, we uncover that spatial income segregation plays a key role in model transferability: mobility networks are most reliably reconstructed when target cities share similar segregation levels with the source. We design an index to quantify this segregation and accurately predict transferability. Finally, we generate mobility flow proxies for over 1,200 cities worldwide, highlighting neuroGravity's potential to mitigate critical data shortages in resource-limited, underdeveloped areas.

AI.tldr: neuroGravity is a physics-informed deep learning model that reconstructs human mobility networks from limited urban data, enabling transferability across cities and providing scalable mobility proxies for 1,200+ cities worldwide.
AI.motivation: Accurate human mobility modeling is critical for urban planning and public health, but undeveloped regions lack comprehensive travel survey data. There's a need to reconstruct mobility networks from publicly available data sources where surveys are unavailable.
AI.method: Developed neuroGravity, a physics-informed deep learning model that reconstructs mobility flows using only urban facility and population distributions. The model can transfer to unobserved cities and includes an index to quantify spatial income segregation for predicting transferability.
AI.result: The model's regional representations strongly correlate with socioeconomic and livability status. Spatial income segregation is key to transferability - mobility networks reconstruct most reliably when target cities share similar segregation levels with source cities. Generated mobility flow proxies for over 1,200 cities worldwide.
AI.conclusion: neuroGravity offers scalable proxies for costly mobility surveys and can mitigate critical data shortages in resource-limited, underdeveloped areas, with transferability dependent on matching spatial income segregation patterns between cities.

title: Efficient VQ-QAT and Mixed Vector/Linear quantized Neural Networks
summary: In this work, we developed and tested 3 techniques for vector quantization (VQ) based model weight compression. To mitigate codebook collapse and enable end-to-end training, we adopted cosine similarity-based assignment. Building on ideas from attention-based formulations in Differentiable K-Means (DKM), we further improved this approach by using cosine similarity for assignment combined with top-1 sampling and a straight-through estimator, thereby eliminating the need for weighted-average reconstruction. Finally, we investigated the use of differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) to adaptively select layer-wise quantization configurations, further optimizing the compression process. Although our method does not consistently outperform existing approaches across all quantization levels, it provides useful insights into the design trade-offs and behaviors of VQ-based model compression methods.

AI.tldr: This paper explores three vector quantization techniques for model weight compression, focusing on cosine similarity-based assignment to prevent codebook collapse and enable end-to-end training, with additional improvements from top-1 sampling and differentiable NAS for layer-wise quantization selection.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop effective vector quantization techniques for model weight compression while addressing common issues like codebook collapse and enabling end-to-end training, which are limitations in existing VQ-based compression methods.
AI.method: The paper proposes three main techniques: 1) Using cosine similarity-based assignment for VQ to prevent codebook collapse, 2) Enhancing this approach with top-1 sampling and straight-through estimator inspired by Differentiable K-Means, eliminating weighted-average reconstruction, and 3) Applying differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) to adaptively select optimal layer-wise quantization configurations.
AI.result: The method does not consistently outperform existing approaches across all quantization levels, but provides valuable insights into design trade-offs and behaviors of VQ-based model compression techniques.
AI.conclusion: The work offers practical insights into VQ-based model compression design choices, particularly highlighting the effectiveness of cosine similarity-based assignment and the potential of differentiable NAS for adaptive quantization, though it acknowledges limitations in consistently outperforming existing methods across all quantization settings.

title: Follow the TRACE: Exploiting Post-Click Trajectories for Online Delayed Conversion Rate Prediction
comment: Accepted as a SIGIR 2026 short paper
summary: Delayed feedback poses a core challenge for online CVR prediction, forcing a trade-off between label accuracy and data freshness. Existing methods address this through delay modeling or sample reweighting, yet neglect how post-click behaviors evolve over the observation period. To overcome this limitation, we formalize this evolution as feedback trajectory and propose TRACE. Instead of forcing hard labels on unrevealed samples, our method evaluates how well the accumulated feedback status aligns with conversion versus non-conversion, dynamically refining posteriors without waiting for final outcomes. To counteract early-stage trajectory sparsity, we further design a reliability-gated retrospective completer that leverages full-lifecycle data to provide adaptive posterior guidance for unrevealed samples. Extensive experiments validate TRACE's superiority over state-of-the-art baselines and confirm the retrospective completion module as a model-agnostic enhancer for existing systems. Our code is available at https://github.com/LunaZhangxy/TRACE.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a method for online CVR prediction that models evolving post-click behaviors as feedback trajectories rather than forcing hard labels, with a reliability-gated retrospective completer to handle early-stage data sparsity.
AI.motivation: Delayed feedback creates a trade-off between label accuracy and data freshness in online CVR prediction. Existing methods use delay modeling or sample reweighting but ignore how post-click behaviors evolve over time, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors formalize post-click behavior evolution as feedback trajectories. Instead of assigning hard labels to unrevealed samples, TRACE evaluates how well accumulated feedback status aligns with conversion vs. non-conversion, dynamically refining posteriors. They also design a reliability-gated retrospective completer that uses full-lifecycle data to provide adaptive posterior guidance for unrevealed samples, addressing early-stage trajectory sparsity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show TRACE's superiority over state-of-the-art baselines. The retrospective completion module is confirmed as a model-agnostic enhancer that can improve existing systems.
AI.conclusion: TRACE effectively addresses delayed feedback challenges in online CVR prediction by modeling evolving post-click behaviors through feedback trajectories and adaptive posterior refinement, outperforming existing approaches while providing a general enhancement mechanism for current systems.

title: Expert Evaluation of LLM's Open-Ended Legal Reasoning on the Japanese Bar Exam Writing Task
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on legal benchmarks, including multiple-choice components of bar exams. However, their capacity for generating open-ended legal reasoning in realistic scenarios remains insufficiently explored. Notably, to our best knowledge, there are no prior studies or datasets addressing this issue in the Japanese context.
  This study presents the first dataset designed to evaluate the open-ended legal reasoning performance of LLMs within the Japanese jurisdiction. The dataset is based on the writing component of the Japanese bar examination, which requires examinees to identify multiple legal issues from long narratives and to construct structured legal arguments in free text format. Our key contribution is the manual evaluation of LLMs' generated responses by legal experts, which reveals limitations and challenges in legal reasoning. Moreover, we conducted a manual analysis of hallucinations to characterize when and how the models introduce content not supported by precedent or law.
  Our real exam questions, model-generated responses, and expert evaluations reveal the milestones of current LLMs in the Japanese legal domain. Our dataset and relevant resources will be available online.

AI.tldr: First dataset evaluating open-ended legal reasoning of LLMs in Japanese jurisdiction, based on bar exam writing components with expert evaluations revealing limitations and hallucinations.
AI.motivation: LLMs have shown strong performance on legal benchmarks (including bar exam multiple-choice), but their capacity for open-ended legal reasoning in realistic scenarios is insufficiently explored, especially in the Japanese context where no prior studies or datasets address this issue.
AI.method: Created first dataset based on writing component of Japanese bar examination (requires identifying multiple legal issues from long narratives and constructing structured legal arguments). Manually evaluated LLM-generated responses by legal experts and conducted manual analysis of hallucinations to characterize when/how models introduce unsupported content.
AI.result: Expert evaluations reveal limitations and challenges in LLMs' legal reasoning. Manual hallucination analysis characterizes when/how models introduce content not supported by precedent or law. Real exam questions, model-generated responses, and expert evaluations provide milestones of current LLMs in Japanese legal domain.
AI.conclusion: The study creates foundational resources for evaluating LLMs in Japanese legal domain, revealing current limitations in open-ended legal reasoning. The dataset and resources will be publicly available, providing benchmarks for future improvements in this important application area.

title: A Layer Separation Optimization Framework for Cross-Entropy Training in Deep Learning
summary: This paper investigates the deep learning optimization problem with softmax cross-entropy loss. We propose a layer separation strategy to alleviate the strong nonconvexity encountered during training deep networks. For cross-entropy models with fully connected and convolutional neural networks, we introduce auxiliary variables associated with hidden layer outputs and construct corresponding layer separation models, which decompose the original deeply nested optimization problem into a sequence of more manageable subproblems. We also conduct theoretical analyses, proving that the new layer separation loss provides an upper bound for the original cross-entropy loss. Moreover, we design alternating minimization algorithms and prove that, under appropriate conditions, these algorithms exhibit decreasing properties of the loss function. Numerical experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods and indicate improved optimization behavior, especially for fully connected and convolutional neural networks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a layer separation strategy to handle nonconvexity in deep learning optimization with softmax cross-entropy loss, introducing auxiliary variables to decompose deeply nested optimization problems into manageable subproblems.
AI.motivation: Deep learning optimization with softmax cross-entropy loss suffers from strong nonconvexity, making training deep networks challenging. The deeply nested structure of neural networks creates complex optimization landscapes that are difficult to navigate during training.
AI.method: Introduces a layer separation strategy using auxiliary variables associated with hidden layer outputs, constructing layer separation models that decompose the original optimization problem into a sequence of more manageable subproblems. The paper designs alternating minimization algorithms to solve these subproblems.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis proves the layer separation loss provides an upper bound for original cross-entropy loss. Alternating minimization algorithms exhibit decreasing properties of loss function under appropriate conditions. Numerical experiments show improved optimization behavior for fully connected and convolutional neural networks.
AI.conclusion: The layer separation strategy effectively alleviates nonconvexity in deep learning optimization, providing a theoretically sound approach that decomposes complex optimization problems and improves training behavior for deep neural networks.

title: Modeling Induced Pleasure through Cognitive Appraisal Prediction via Multimodal Fusion
summary: Multimodal affective computing analyzes user-generated social media content to predict emotional states. However, a critical gap remains in understanding how visual content shapes cognitive interpretations and elicits specific affective experiences such as pleasure. This study introduces a novel computational model to infer video-induced pleasure via cognitive appraisal variables. The proposed model addresses four challenges: (1) noisy and inconsistent human labels, (2) the semantic gap between "positive emotions" and "pleasure," (3) the scarcity of pleasure-specific datasets, and (4) the limited interpretability of existing black-box fusion methods. Our approach integrates data-driven and cognitive theory-driven methods, using cognitive appraisal theory and a fuzzy model within an innovative framework. The model employs transformer-based architectures and attention mechanisms for fine-grained multimodal feature extraction and interpretable fusion to capture both inter- and intra-modal dynamics associated with pleasure. This enables the prediction of underlying appraisal variables, thereby bridging the semantic gap and enhancing model explainability beyond conventional statistical associations. Experimental results validate the efficacy of the proposed method in detecting video-induced pleasure, achieving a peak accuracy of 0.6624 in predicting pleasure levels. These findings highlight promising implications for affective content recommendation, intelligent media creation, and advancing our understanding of how digital media influences human emotions.

AI.tldr: Novel computational model predicts video-induced pleasure by combining cognitive appraisal theory with multimodal transformer architectures, addressing noisy labels, semantic gaps, dataset scarcity, and interpretability issues.
AI.motivation: There's a critical gap in understanding how visual content shapes cognitive interpretations and elicits specific affective experiences like pleasure. Existing approaches face challenges including noisy human labels, semantic confusion between "positive emotions" and pleasure, scarcity of pleasure-specific datasets, and limited interpretability of black-box multimodal fusion methods.
AI.method: Integrates data-driven and cognitive theory-driven approaches using cognitive appraisal theory and a fuzzy model. Employs transformer-based architectures with attention mechanisms for fine-grained multimodal feature extraction and interpretable fusion to capture inter- and intra-modal dynamics associated with pleasure. The model predicts underlying appraisal variables to bridge semantic gaps and enhance explainability.
AI.result: Experimental validation shows efficacy in detecting video-induced pleasure, achieving peak accuracy of 0.6624 in predicting pleasure levels, demonstrating successful application of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: The approach has promising implications for affective content recommendation, intelligent media creation, and advancing understanding of how digital media influences human emotions by bridging cognitive appraisal theory with computational modeling for better interpretability.

title: Contrastive Learning for Multimodal Human Activity Recognition with Limited Labeled Data
summary:
Human activity recognition serves as the foundation for various emerging applications. In recent years, researchers have used collaborative sensing of multi-source sensors to capture complex and dynamic human activities. However, multimodal human activity sensing typically encounters highly heterogeneous data across modalities and label scarcity, resulting in an application gap between existing solutions and real-world needs. In this paper, we propose CLMM, a general contrastive learning framework for human activity recognition that achieves effective multimodal recognition with limited labeled data.
  CLMM employs a novel two-stage training strategy. In the first stage, CLMM employs a CNN-DiffTransformer encoder to capture cross-modal shared information by extracting local and global features. Meanwhile, a hard-positive samples weighting algorithm enhances gradient propagation to reinforce shared learning. In the second stage, a dual-branch architecture combining quality-guided attention and bidirectional gated units captures modality-specific information, while a primary-auxiliary collaborative training strategy fuses both shared and modality-specific information. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that CLMM significantly improves state-of-the-art baselines in both recognition accuracy and convergence performance.

AI.tldr: CLMM is a contrastive learning framework for multimodal human activity recognition that addresses data heterogeneity and label scarcity through a two-stage training approach combining shared and modality-specific feature learning.
AI.motivation: Multimodal human activity sensing faces two main challenges: highly heterogeneous data across different modalities and label scarcity. These issues create an application gap between existing solutions and real-world needs for human activity recognition applications.
AI.method: CLMM employs a two-stage training strategy: 1) First stage uses CNN-DiffTransformer encoder to capture cross-modal shared information with hard-positive samples weighting algorithm to enhance gradient propagation. 2) Second stage uses dual-branch architecture with quality-guided attention and bidirectional gated units to capture modality-specific information, combined with primary-auxiliary collaborative training to fuse shared and specific information.
AI.result: Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that CLMM significantly improves state-of-the-art baselines in both recognition accuracy and convergence performance.
AI.conclusion: CLMM is an effective general contrastive learning framework that achieves robust multimodal human activity recognition with limited labeled data by addressing modality heterogeneity through combined shared and specific information learning.

title: FAIR_XAI: Improving Multimodal Foundation Model Fairness via Explainability for Wellbeing Assessment
summary: In recent years, the integration of multimodal machine learning in wellbeing assessment has offered transformative potential for monitoring mental health. However, with the rapid advancement of Vision-Language Models (VLMs), their deployment in clinical settings has raised concerns due to their lack of transparency and potential for bias. While previous research has explored the intersection of fairness and Explainable AI (XAI), its application to VLMs for wellbeing assessment and depression prediction remains under-explored. This work investigates VLM performance across laboratory (AFAR-BSFT) and naturalistic (E-DAIC) datasets, focusing on diagnostic reliability and demographic fairness. Performance varied substantially across environments and architectures; Phi3.5-Vision achieved 80.4% accuracy on E-DAIC, while Qwen2-VL struggled at 33.9%. Additionally, both models demonstrated a tendency to over-predict depression on AFAR-BSFT. Although bias existed across both architectures, Qwen2-VL showed higher gender disparities, while Phi-3.5-Vision exhibited more racial bias. Our XAI intervention framework yielded mixed results; fairness prompting achieved perfect equal opportunity for Qwen2-VL at a severe accuracy cost on E-DAIC. On AFAR-BSFT, explainability-based interventions improved procedural consistency but did not guarantee outcome fairness, sometimes amplifying racial bias. These results highlight a persistent gap between procedural transparency and equitable outcomes. We analyse these findings and consolidate concrete recommendations for addressing them, emphasising that future fairness interventions must jointly optimise predictive accuracy, demographic parity, and cross-domain generalisation.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates fairness and explainability in Vision-Language Models for mental health assessment, revealing performance variability, demographic biases, and limitations of XAI interventions in achieving both transparency and equitable outcomes.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models offer transformative potential for mental health monitoring but raise concerns about transparency and bias in clinical settings. While fairness and XAI have been explored separately, their application to VLMs for wellbeing assessment remains under-explored.
AI.method: The study evaluates VLM performance on laboratory (AFAR-BSFT) and naturalistic (E-DAIC) datasets, focusing on diagnostic reliability and demographic fairness. It tests models like Phi3.5-Vision and Qwen2-VL, and applies XAI intervention frameworks including fairness prompting and explainability-based interventions.
AI.result: Performance varied substantially: Phi3.5-Vision achieved 80.4% accuracy on E-DAIC vs Qwen2-VL's 33.9%. Both models over-predicted depression on AFAR-BSFT. Qwen2-VL showed higher gender disparities, while Phi-3.5-Vision exhibited more racial bias. XAI interventions yielded mixed results - fairness prompting achieved perfect equal opportunity for Qwen2-VL at severe accuracy cost, and explainability-based interventions improved procedural consistency but sometimes amplified racial bias.
AI.conclusion: There's a persistent gap between procedural transparency and equitable outcomes in VLMs for mental health. Future fairness interventions must jointly optimize predictive accuracy, demographic parity, and cross-domain generalization to address these challenges effectively.

title: Revisable by Design: A Theory of Streaming LLM Agent Execution
summary: Current LLM agents operate under an implicit but universal assumption: execution is a transaction -- the user submits a request, the agent works in isolation, and only upon completion does the dialogue resume. This forces users into a binary choice: wait for a potentially incorrect output, or interrupt and lose all progress. We reject this assumption and propose the stream paradigm, in which agent execution and user intervention are concurrent, interleaved processes sharing a bidirectional channel. We formalize this paradigm through a reversibility taxonomy that classifies every agent action as Idempotent, Reversible, Compensable, or Irreversible, and arrive at a core conclusion: an agent's flexibility is bounded by its reversibility. We prove that conflicting compensable actions impose unavoidable adaptation costs and that conflicting irreversible actions make full specification satisfaction impossible -- these costs are properties of the action space, not of the algorithm. Guided by this insight, we present the Revision Absorber, a reactive algorithm based on the Earliest-Conflict Rollback rule that is structurally optimal under mild assumptions. Experiments on StreamBench with real LLM agents validate all predictions: the Absorber matches the quality of a brute-force full-restart baseline while wasting an order of magnitude fewer steps of already-completed work, turning mid-execution revisions from a dead-end into a first-class interaction.

AI.tldr: Proposes "stream paradigm" for LLM agents where execution and user intervention are concurrent/interleaved, with a reversibility taxonomy for agent actions and an optimal Revision Absorber algorithm for handling mid-execution revisions.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents operate in isolated transactions, forcing users to either wait for potentially incorrect outputs or interrupt and lose all progress. This creates an inefficient binary choice that doesn't support natural iterative refinement during execution.
AI.method: 1) Proposes stream paradigm with concurrent execution/user intervention via bidirectional channel; 2) Develops reversibility taxonomy classifying actions as Idempotent, Reversible, Compensable, or Irreversible; 3) Presents Revision Absorber algorithm using Earliest-Conflict Rollback rule; 4) Validates with experiments on StreamBench using real LLM agents.
AI.result: The Revision Absorber matches quality of brute-force full-restart baseline while wasting an order of magnitude fewer steps of completed work. It enables mid-execution revisions as first-class interactions rather than dead-ends, validating theoretical predictions about reversibility constraints.
AI.conclusion: Agent flexibility is bounded by its reversibility properties; conflicting compensable actions impose unavoidable costs, and conflicting irreversible actions make full specification satisfaction impossible. The stream paradigm and Revision Absorber algorithm transform mid-execution revisions from problematic interruptions into productive first-class interactions.

title: Domain-Filtered Knowledge Graphs from Sparse Autoencoder Features
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) extract millions of interpretable features from a language model, but flat feature inventories aren't very useful on their own. Domain concepts get mixed with generic and weakly grounded features, while related ideas are scattered across many units, and there's no way to understand relationships between features. We address this by first constructing a strict domain-specific concept universe from a large SAE inventory using contrastive activations and a multi-stage filtering process. Next, we build two aligned graph views on the filtered set: a co-occurrence graph for corpus-level conceptual structure, organized at multiple levels of granularity, and a transcoder-based mechanism graph that links source-layer and target-layer features through sparse latent pathways. Automated edge labeling then turns these graph views into readable knowledge graphs rather than unlabeled layouts. In a case study on a biology textbook, these graphs recover coherent chapter and subchapter-level structure, reveal concepts that bridge neighboring topics, and transform messy sentence-level activity containing thousands of features into compact, readable views that illustrate the model's local activity. Taken together, this reframes a flat SAE inventory as an internal knowledge graph that converts feature-level interpretability into a global map of model knowledge and enables audits of reasoning faithfulness.

AI.tldr: This paper transforms flat sparse autoencoder feature inventories into structured knowledge graphs that reveal global model knowledge and enable audits of reasoning faithfulness, demonstrated through a biology textbook case study.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoder feature inventories are flat and unorganized - domain concepts get mixed with generic features, related ideas are scattered across units, and there's no way to understand relationships between features, limiting their practical usefulness.
AI.method: 1. Construct a strict domain-specific concept universe from large SAE inventory using contrastive activations and multi-stage filtering. 2. Build two aligned graph views: a co-occurrence graph for corpus-level conceptual structure at multiple granularities, and a transcoder-based mechanism graph linking source-layer and target-layer features through sparse latent pathways. 3. Apply automated edge labeling to create readable knowledge graphs.
AI.result: In a biology textbook case study, the graphs recovered coherent chapter/subchapter-level structure, revealed concepts bridging neighboring topics, and transformed messy sentence-level activity (thousands of features) into compact, readable views of the model's local activity.
AI.conclusion: The approach reframes flat SAE inventories as internal knowledge graphs that convert feature-level interpretability into a global map of model knowledge and enable audits of reasoning faithfulness, addressing key limitations of current feature extraction methods.

title: An Analysis of Active Learning Algorithms using Real-World Crowd-sourced Text Annotations
comment: The proposed dataset can be accessed at https://github.com/varuntotakura/al_rcta/. To appear in Proceedings of the IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN 2026)
summary: Active learning algorithms automatically identify the most informative samples from large amounts of unlabeled data and tremendously reduce human annotation effort in inducing a machine learning model. In a conventional active learning setup, the labeling oracles are assumed to be infallible, that is, they always provide correct answers (in terms of class labels) to the queried unlabeled instances, which cannot be guaranteed in real-world applications. To this end, a body of research has focused on the development of active learning algorithms in the presence of imperfect / noisy oracles. Existing research on active learning with noisy oracles typically simulate the oracles using machine learning models; however, real-world situations are much more challenging, and using ML models to simulate the annotation patterns may not appropriately capture the nuances of real-world annotation challenges. In this research, we first collect annotations of text samples (from 3 benchmark text classification datasets) from crowd-sourced workers through a crowd-sourcing platform. We then conduct extensive empirical studies of 8 commonly used active learning techniques (in conjunction with deep neural networks) using the obtained annotations. Our analyses sheds light on the performance of these techniques under real-world challenges, where annotators can provide incorrect labels, and can also refuse to provide labels. We hope this research will provide valuable insights that will be useful for the deployment of deep active learning systems in real-world applications. The obtained annotations can be accessed at https://github.com/varuntotakura/al_rcta/.

AI.tldr: Active learning typically assumes perfect labelers, but real-world annotators can make mistakes or refuse labels. This research empirically evaluates 8 active learning methods using real crowd-sourced annotations (including errors and refusals) instead of simulated noise.
AI.motivation: Conventional active learning assumes infallible labeling oracles that always provide correct labels, which doesn't reflect real-world applications where annotators can be noisy (provide incorrect labels) or refuse to label certain samples. Existing research typically simulates noisy oracles using ML models, but this may not capture real annotation challenges accurately.
AI.method: 1. Collected real annotations from crowd-sourced workers on a crowd-sourcing platform for 3 benchmark text classification datasets. 2. Conducted extensive empirical studies of 8 commonly used active learning techniques combined with deep neural networks using these real annotations (which include incorrect labels and refusals).
AI.result: The research provides empirical analysis of how active learning techniques perform under real-world annotation challenges. The analyses shed light on the performance of these techniques when annotators provide incorrect labels or refuse to provide labels.
AI.conclusion: This research offers valuable insights for deploying deep active learning systems in real-world applications by evaluating methods with realistic annotation noise patterns (rather than simulated noise). The collected annotations are publicly available for further research.

title: ClawTrace: Cost-Aware Tracing for LLM Agent Skill Distillation
summary: Skill-distillation pipelines learn reusable rules from LLM agent trajectories, but they lack a key signal: how much each step costs. Without per-step cost, a pipeline cannot distinguish adding a missing step to fix a bug from removing an expensive step that never affected the outcome. We introduce ClawTrace, an agent tracing platform that records every LLM call, tool use, and sub-agent spawn during an agent session and compiles each session into a TraceCard: a compact YAML summary with per-step USD cost, token counts, and redundancy flags. Built on ClawTrace, CostCraft is a distillation pipeline that reads TraceCards and produces three types of skill patches. Preserve patches keep behaviors that led to success. Prune patches remove expensive steps that did not matter, each backed by a counterfactual argument against a named high-cost step. Repair patches fix failures grounded in oracle evidence. Ablations on 30 held-out SpreadsheetBench tasks show that both cost attribution and prune patches independently reduce quality regressions. When the same skill is applied to 30 unrelated SkillsBench tasks, an unexpected asymmetry emerges: prune rules transferred across benchmarks and cut median cost by 32%, while preserve rules, trained on benchmark-specific conventions, caused regressions on new task types. We release ClawTrace and TraceCards as open infrastructure for cost-aware agent research.

AI.tldr: ClawTrace is a cost-aware agent tracing platform that records per-step costs, enabling CostCraft to distill three types of skill patches that preserve successful behaviors, prune expensive unnecessary steps, and repair failures.
AI.motivation: Existing skill-distillation pipelines lack per-step cost signals, preventing them from distinguishing between adding missing steps to fix bugs and removing expensive steps that never affected outcomes. This cost-blindness limits optimization of agent behaviors.
AI.method: ClawTrace records every LLM call, tool use, and sub-agent spawn during agent sessions, compiling each into a TraceCard with per-step USD cost, token counts, and redundancy flags. Built on this, CostCraft is a distillation pipeline that reads TraceCards and produces three skill patches: Preserve patches keep successful behaviors, Prune patches remove expensive irrelevant steps with counterfactual arguments, and Repair patches fix failures using oracle evidence.
AI.result: Ablations on 30 SpreadsheetBench tasks show both cost attribution and prune patches independently reduce quality regressions. When skills transfer to 30 unrelated SkillsBench tasks, prune rules cut median cost by 32% across benchmarks, while preserve rules caused regressions on new task types due to benchmark-specific conventions.
AI.conclusion: Cost-aware skill distillation enabled by per-step cost tracking significantly improves agent efficiency, with prune rules effectively transferring across domains while preserve rules suffer from overfitting to benchmark conventions. ClawTrace and TraceCards are released as open infrastructure for cost-aware agent research.

title: CombiMOTS: Combinatorial Multi-Objective Tree Search for Dual-Target Molecule Generation
comment: Accepted as a poster at ICML 2025 (Main Track)
summary: Dual-target molecule generation, which focuses on discovering compounds capable of interacting with two target proteins, has garnered significant attention due to its potential for improving therapeutic efficiency, safety and resistance mitigation. Existing approaches face two critical challenges. First, by simplifying the complex dual-target optimization problem to scalarized combinations of individual objectives, they fail to capture important trade-offs between target engagement and molecular properties. Second, they typically do not integrate synthetic planning into the generative process. This highlights a need for more appropriate objective function design and synthesis-aware methodologies tailored to the dual-target molecule generation task. In this work, we propose CombiMOTS, a Pareto Monte Carlo Tree Search (PMCTS) framework that generates dual-target molecules. CombiMOTS is designed to explore a synthesizable fragment space while employing vectorized optimization constraints to encapsulate target affinity and physicochemical properties. Extensive experiments on real-world databases demonstrate that CombiMOTS produces novel dual-target molecules with high docking scores, enhanced diversity, and balanced pharmacological characteristics, showcasing its potential as a powerful tool for dual-target drug discovery. The code and data is accessible through https://github.com/Tibogoss/CombiMOTS.

AI.tldr: CombiMOTS is a Pareto Monte Carlo Tree Search framework that generates dual-target molecules by exploring synthesizable fragment spaces with vectorized optimization constraints for balanced pharmacological properties.
AI.motivation: Existing dual-target molecule generation approaches face two critical challenges: 1) simplifying complex dual-target optimization to scalarized combinations fails to capture important trade-offs between target engagement and molecular properties, and 2) they typically don't integrate synthetic planning into the generative process.
AI.method: Proposes CombiMOTS, a Pareto Monte Carlo Tree Search (PMCTS) framework that generates dual-target molecules by exploring a synthesizable fragment space while employing vectorized optimization constraints to encapsulate target affinity and physicochemical properties.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world databases demonstrate that CombiMOTS produces novel dual-target molecules with high docking scores, enhanced diversity, and balanced pharmacological characteristics.
AI.conclusion: CombiMOTS shows potential as a powerful tool for dual-target drug discovery by addressing the limitations of existing approaches through appropriate objective function design and synthesis-aware methodology.

title: Does Machine Unlearning Preserve Clinical Safety? A Risk Analysis for Medical Image Classification
summary: The application of Deep Learning in medical diagnosis must balance patient safety with compliance with data protection regulations. Machine Unlearning enables the selective removal of training data from deployed models. However, most methods are validated primarily through efficiency and privacy-oriented metrics, with limited attention to clinically asymmetric error costs. In this work, we investigate how unlearning affects clinical risk in binary medical image classification. We show that standard unlearning strategies (Fine-Tuning, Random Labeling, and SalUn) may reduce test utility while increasing false-negative rates, thereby amplifying clinical risk. To mitigate this, we propose SalUn-CRA (Clinical Risk-Aware), a variant of SalUn that replaces random relabeling with entropy-based forgetting for malignant samples in the forget set, preventing the model from learning harmful benign associations. We evaluate on DermaMNIST and PathMNIST medical image datasets under 20% and 50% data removal. Using Global Risk metrics with asymmetric costs, SalUn-CRA achieves lower or comparable clinical risk to full retraining while preserving unlearning effectiveness. These results suggest that clinical risk should be an integral component of unlearning validation in medical systems.

AI.tldr: A clinical risk-aware machine unlearning method called SalUn-CRA is proposed to balance data removal requirements with patient safety in medical image classification, addressing the clinical risk amplification caused by standard unlearning methods.
AI.motivation: Medical diagnosis using deep learning must balance patient safety with data protection compliance. While machine unlearning allows selective removal of training data, current methods focus on efficiency and privacy metrics but overlook clinically asymmetric error costs, potentially amplifying clinical risks.
AI.method: The authors investigate how unlearning affects clinical risk in binary medical image classification. They propose SalUn-CRA, a variant of SalUn that replaces random relabeling with entropy-based forgetting for malignant samples in the forget set, preventing harmful benign associations. Evaluated on DermaMNIST and PathMNIST datasets under 20% and 50% data removal scenarios.
AI.result: Standard unlearning strategies (Fine-Tuning, Random Labeling, and SalUn) reduce test utility while increasing false-negative rates, amplifying clinical risk. SalUn-CRA achieves lower or comparable clinical risk to full retraining while preserving unlearning effectiveness, as measured by Global Risk metrics with asymmetric costs.
AI.conclusion: Clinical risk should be an integral component of unlearning validation in medical systems. SalUn-CRA demonstrates that risk-aware unlearning can balance data removal requirements with patient safety, addressing the limitations of current unlearning approaches that overlook clinically asymmetric error costs.

title: CODA: Coordination via On-Policy Diffusion for Multi-Agent Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) enables policy learning from fixed datasets, but is prone to coordination failure: agents trained on static, off-policy data converge to suboptimal joint behaviours because they cannot co-adapt as their policies change. We introduce CODA (Coordination via On-Policy Diffusion for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning), a diffusion-based multi-agent trajectory generator for data augmentation that samples conditioned on the current joint policy, producing synthetic experience which reflects the evolving behaviours of the agents, thereby providing a mechanism for co-adaptation. We find that previous diffusion-based augmentation approaches are insufficient for fostering multi-agent coordination because they produce static augmented datasets that do not evolve as the current joint policy changes during training; CODA resolves this by more closely simulating on-policy learning and is a meaningful step toward coordinated behaviours in the offline setting. CODA is algorithm-agnostic and can be layered onto both model-free and model-based offline reinforcement learning pipelines as an augmentation module. Empirically, CODA not only resolves canonical coordination pathologies in continuous polynomial games but also delivers strong results on the more complex MaMuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CODA uses diffusion-based trajectory generation conditioned on current joint policies to enable multi-agent co-adaptation in offline RL, overcoming coordination failures that plague static dataset methods.
AI.motivation: Offline MARL suffers from coordination failure because agents trained on fixed datasets cannot co-adapt as their policies change during training, leading to suboptimal joint behaviors.
AI.method: CODA is a diffusion-based multi-agent trajectory generator that samples synthetic experience conditioned on the current joint policy, providing dynamic data augmentation that evolves with the agents' policies during training.
AI.result: CODA successfully resolves coordination pathologies in continuous polynomial games and achieves strong performance on complex MaMuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks, outperforming previous static diffusion-based augmentation methods.
AI.conclusion: CODA enables effective multi-agent coordination in offline RL by providing dynamic, policy-conditioned data augmentation, representing a significant step toward coordinated behaviors in the offline setting while being algorithm-agnostic and applicable to both model-free and model-based approaches.

title: Time-Series Forecasting in Safety-Critical Environments: An EU-AI-Act-Compliant Open-Source Package / Zeitreihenprognose in sicherheitskritischen Umgebungen: Ein KI-VO-konformes Open-Source-Paket
comment: Bilingual twin paper: English version first, German original below (91 pages total). Single shared bibliography
summary: With spotforecast2-safe we present an integrated Compliance-by-Design approach to Python-based point forecasting of time series in safety-critical environments. A review of the relevant open-source tooling shows that existing compliance solutions operate consistently outside of the library to be used - e.g. as scanners, templates, or runtime layers. spotforecast2-safe takes the inverse approach and anchors the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the EU AI Act, in German: KI-VO), of IEC 61508, of the ISA/IEC 62443 standards series, and of the Cyber Resilience Act within the library: in application-programming-interface contracts, persistence formats, and continuous-integration gates. The approach is operationalised by four non-negotiable code-development rules (zero dead code, deterministic processing, fail-safe handling, minimal dependencies) together with the corresponding process rules (model card, executable docstrings, CI workflows, Common-Platform-Enumeration (CPE) identifier, REUSE-conformant licensing, release pipeline). Interactive visualisation, hyperparameter tuning and automated machine learning (AutoML), as well as deep-learning and large-language-model backends are deliberately excluded, because each of these components either enlarges the attack surface, introduces non-determinism, or impairs reproducibility. A bidirectional traceability matrix maps every regulatory provision onto the corresponding mechanism in the code; an end-to-end example of European-market electricity generation, transmission, and consumption forecasting demonstrates the application. The package is open-source and available under Affero General Public License (AGPL) 3.0-or-later.

AI.tldr: spotforecast2-safe is a compliance-by-design Python forecasting library that embeds EU AI Act and other safety regulations directly into its API, persistence formats, and CI/CD processes, excluding non-deterministic components like AutoML and deep learning for safety-critical applications.
AI.motivation: Existing compliance solutions operate outside libraries as scanners or runtime layers, creating separation between code and regulatory requirements. The paper addresses the need for integrated compliance in safety-critical time series forecasting applications subject to multiple regulations like EU AI Act, IEC 61508, ISA/IEC 62443, and Cyber Resilience Act.
AI.method: Takes inverse approach by anchoring regulatory requirements directly within the library through API contracts, persistence formats, and CI gates. Implements four non-negotiable code rules: zero dead code, deterministic processing, fail-safe handling, minimal dependencies. Combines with process rules including model cards, executable docstrings, CI workflows, CPE identifiers, REUSE licensing, and release pipelines.
AI.result: Developed an open-source package (AGPL 3.0-or-later) that demonstrates end-to-end electricity forecasting application for European markets. Created a bidirectional traceability matrix mapping regulatory provisions to code mechanisms. Excluded interactive visualization, hyperparameter tuning, AutoML, deep learning, and LLM backends to minimize attack surface and ensure determinism.
AI.conclusion: The integrated compliance-by-design approach successfully embeds multiple safety regulations directly into a forecasting library, providing a practical solution for safety-critical time series applications while maintaining regulatory traceability and excluding non-deterministic components that could compromise safety.

title: GIFT: Global stabilisation via Intrinsic Fine Tuning
summary: Deep reinforcement learning policies achieve strong performance in complex continuous control environments with nonlinear contact forces. However, these policies often produce chaotic state dynamics, with trivially small changes to the initial conditions significantly impacting the long-term behaviour of the control system. This high sensitivity to initial conditions limits the application of Deep RL to real-world control systems where performance and stability guarantees are often required. To address this issue, we propose Global stabilisation via Intrinsic Fine Tuning (GIFT), a general-purpose training framework which directly optimises the global stability of existing high-performing deep RL policies using a custom reward function. We demonstrate that GIFT increase the stability of the control interaction while maintaining comparable task performance, thereby improving the suitability of deep RL policies for real-world control systems.

AI.tldr: GIFT framework improves deep RL policy stability by fine-tuning existing policies to reduce chaotic dynamics while maintaining task performance, making them more suitable for real-world control systems.
AI.motivation: Deep RL policies exhibit chaotic state dynamics with high sensitivity to initial conditions, which limits their application in real-world control systems that require performance and stability guarantees.
AI.method: Proposes Global stabilisation via Intrinsic Fine Tuning (GIFT), a general-purpose training framework that directly optimizes global stability of existing high-performing deep RL policies using a custom reward function.
AI.result: GIFT increases the stability of control interactions while maintaining comparable task performance, improving the suitability of deep RL policies for real-world control systems.
AI.conclusion: GIFT successfully addresses the stability limitations of deep RL policies in continuous control environments, making them more viable for real-world applications where stability guarantees are required.

title: ZenBrain: A Neuroscience-Inspired 7-Layer Memory Architecture for Autonomous AI Systems
comment: Pre-print of NeurIPS 2026 main-track submission. Earliest preprint version on Zenodo 31 March 2026 (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19353664); cross-posted to TDCommons (dpubs_series/9683, 1 April 2026). Six Zenodo revisions and three TDCommons revisions through 9 April 2026 (Zenodo concept DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19353663). 41 pages, 22 tables, 2 figures
summary:
Despite a century of empirical memory research, existing AI agent memory systems rely on system-engineering metaphors (virtual-memory paging, flat LLM storage, Zettelkasten notes), none integrating principles of consolidation, forgetting, and reconsolidation.
  We present ZenBrain, a multi-layer memory architecture integrating fifteen neuroscience models. It implements seven memory layers (working, short-term, episodic, semantic, procedural, core, cross-context) orchestrated by nine foundational algorithms (Two-Factor Synaptic Model, vmPFC-coupled FSRS, Simulation-Selection sleep, Bayesian confidence, and five more) plus six new Predictive Memory Architecture (PMA) components: a four-channel NeuromodulatorEngine, prediction-error-gated ReconsolidationEngine, TripleCopyMemory with divergent decay, four-dimensional PriorityMap with amygdala fast-path, StabilityProtector (NogoA/HDAC3 analogue), and MetacognitiveMonitor for bias detection.
  The 15-algorithm ablation reveals a cooperative survival network: under stress, 9 of 15 algorithms become individually critical (delta-Q up to -93.7%, Wilcoxon, 10 seeds, alpha=0.005). Simulation-Selection sleep achieves 37% stability improvement (p<0.005) with 47.4% storage reduction. TripleCopyMemory retains S(t)=0.912 at 30 days; PriorityMap reaches NDCG@10=0.997.
  Multi-layer routing beats a flat single-layer baseline by 20.7% F1 on LoCoMo (p<0.005) and 19.5% on MemoryArena (p=0.015). On LongMemEval-500, ZenBrain holds the highest mean rank on all 12 system-judge cells (4 systems x 3 LLM judges), three-judge mean J=0.545 vs letta=0.485, a-mem=0.414, mem0=0.394; all 9 pair-wise contrasts clear Bonferroni (alpha=0.05/18, min p=6.2e-31, d in [0.18, 0.52]). Under LongMemEval's binary judge, ZenBrain reaches 91.3% of oracle accuracy at 1/106th the per-query token budget. Open-source with 11,589 automated test cases.

AI.tldr: ZenBrain is a neuroscience-inspired multi-layer memory architecture for AI agents that integrates 15 neural models, outperforming traditional engineering-based approaches by implementing biological memory principles like consolidation, forgetting, and reconsolidation.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agent memory systems rely on engineering metaphors (virtual memory paging, flat LLM storage, etc.) but fail to incorporate fundamental neuroscience principles of memory consolidation, forgetting, and reconsolidation that underlie human memory.
AI.method: Develops ZenBrain, a multi-layer memory architecture with 7 memory layers (working, short-term, episodic, semantic, procedural, core, cross-context) orchestrated by 9 foundational neuroscience algorithms and 6 new Predictive Memory Architecture components including neuromodulator engine, reconsolidation engine, triple copy memory, priority map, stability protector, and metacognitive monitor.
AI.result: ZenBrain outperforms flat single-layer baseline by 20.7% F1 on LoCoMo and 19.5% on MemoryArena. On LongMemEval-500, it achieves highest mean rank across all system-judge comparisons (J=0.545 vs competitors 0.485-0.394), with all 9 pairwise contrasts statistically significant. Simulation-selection sleep improves stability by 37% while reducing storage by 47.4%. Under stress, 9 of 15 algorithms become individually critical.
