AI.conclusion: Integrating neuroscience principles into AI memory architectures significantly improves performance and efficiency over traditional engineering-based approaches, demonstrating that biological memory mechanisms provide valuable insights for designing more effective and human-like AI memory systems.

title: Layer Embedding Deep Fusion Graph Neural Network
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated impressive performance in learning representations from graph-structured data. However, their message-passing mechanism inherently relies on the assumption of label consistency among connected nodes, limiting their applicability to low-homophily settings. Moreover, since message passing operates as a hierarchical diffusion process, GNNs face challenges in capturing long-range dependencies. As network depth increases, the structural noise along heterophilic edges tends to be amplified, resulting in over-smoothing. This issue becomes especially prominent in highly heterophilic graphs, where the propagation of inconsistent semantics across the topology continually exacerbates misaggregation. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named Layer Embedding Deep Fusion Graph Neural Network (LEDF-GNN). Specifically, we design a Layer Embedding Deep Fusion (LEDF) operator that nonlinearly fuses multi-layer embeddings to capture inter-layer dependencies and effectively alleviate deep propagation degradation. Meanwhile, to mitigate structural heterophily, LEDF-GNN employs a Dual-Topology Parallel Strategy (DTPS) that simultaneously leverages the original and reconstructed topologies, allowing for adaptive structure-semantics co-optimization under diverse homophily conditions. Extensive semi-supervised classification experiments on the citation and image benchmarks demonstrate that, under both homophilic and heterophilic settings, LEDF-GNN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness and generalization capability across diverse graph types.

AI.tldr: LEDF-GNN is a novel graph neural network framework that addresses limitations of traditional GNNs in heterophilic graphs through layer embedding deep fusion and dual-topology processing, achieving superior performance across both homophilic and heterophilic settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional GNNs struggle with heterophilic graphs due to their reliance on label consistency assumptions in message-passing, amplification of structural noise in deep networks, over-smoothing issues, and inability to handle inconsistent semantics across heterophilic edges.
AI.method: The proposed LEDF-GNN framework includes two main components: 1) Layer Embedding Deep Fusion (LEDF) operator that nonlinearly fuses multi-layer embeddings to capture inter-layer dependencies and alleviate deep propagation degradation, and 2) Dual-Topology Parallel Strategy (DTPS) that simultaneously leverages original and reconstructed topologies for adaptive structure-semantics co-optimization across diverse homophily conditions.
AI.result: Extensive semi-supervised classification experiments on citation and image benchmarks show that LEDF-GNN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under both homophilic and heterophilic settings, demonstrating effectiveness and strong generalization capability across diverse graph types.
AI.conclusion: LEDF-GNN successfully addresses the limitations of traditional GNNs in heterophilic settings through innovative layer fusion and dual-topology strategies, providing a robust solution for graph representation learning across diverse homophily conditions with superior performance and generalization ability.

title: MarketBench: Evaluating AI Agents as Market Participants
summary: Markets are a promising way to coordinate AI agent activity for similar reasons to those used to justify markets more broadly. In order to effectively participate in markets, agents need to have informative signals of their own ability to successfully complete a task and the cost of doing so. We propose MarketBench, a benchmark for assessing whether AI agents have these capabilities. We use a 93-task subset of SWE-bench Lite, a software engineering benchmark, with six recently released LLMs as a demonstration. These LLMs are miscalibrated on both success probability and token usage, and auctions built from these self-reports diverge from a full-information allocation. A follow-up intervention where we add information about capabilities from prior experiments to the context improves calibration, but only modestly narrows the gap to a full-information benchmark. We also document the performance of a market-based scaffolding with these LLMs. Our results point to self-assessment as a key bottleneck for market-style coordination of AI agents.

AI.tldr: AI agents need accurate self-assessment of task success probability and costs to effectively participate in markets; current LLMs are miscalibrated in these self-reports, limiting market-based coordination.
AI.motivation: Markets are promising for coordinating AI agent activity, but agents need informative signals about their own ability to complete tasks and associated costs to participate effectively.
AI.method: Proposed MarketBench benchmark using 93-task subset of SWE-bench Lite with six recent LLMs; assessed self-reports of success probability and token usage; conducted auctions based on these reports; performed intervention adding prior capability information to context.
AI.result: LLMs are miscalibrated on both success probability and token usage; auctions from self-reports diverge from full-information allocation; intervention improves calibration but only modestly narrows gap to full-information benchmark; market-based scaffolding shows limited performance.
AI.conclusion: Self-assessment is a key bottleneck for market-style coordination of AI agents, as current LLMs lack accurate calibration of their own capabilities and costs needed for effective market participation.

title: Process Supervision of Confidence Margin for Calibrated LLM Reasoning
summary: Scaling test-time computation with reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a reliable path to improve large language models (LLM) reasoning ability. Yet, outcome-based reward often incentivizes models to be overconfident, leading to hallucinations, unreliable confidence-based control, and unnecessary compute allocation. We introduce Reinforcement Learning with Confidence Margin (\textbf{RLCM}), a calibration-aware RL framework that jointly optimizes correctness and confidence reliability via a margin-enhanced process reward over intermediate-budget completions. Rather than aligning confidence to correctness likelihoods, RLCM encourages to widen the confidence margin between correct and incorrect steps within a single reasoning trajectory. Across mathematical, code, logic and science benchmarks, our method substantially improves calibration while maintaining or improving accuracy. We further show that, with calibrated confidence signals, the resulting models enable more efficient conformal risk control and effective confidence-weighted aggregation.

AI.tldr: RLCM is a reinforcement learning framework that improves LLM reasoning by jointly optimizing correctness and confidence calibration, reducing overconfidence and hallucinations through margin-enhanced process rewards.
AI.motivation: Traditional outcome-based RL rewards in LLM training often lead to overconfidence, causing hallucinations, unreliable confidence-based control, and inefficient compute allocation. Current methods don't properly calibrate confidence to correctness likelihoods.
AI.method: Reinforcement Learning with Confidence Margin (RLCM) - a calibration-aware RL framework that uses margin-enhanced process rewards over intermediate-budget completions. Instead of aligning confidence to correctness likelihoods, it encourages widening the confidence margin between correct and incorrect steps within a single reasoning trajectory.
AI.result: Across mathematical, code, logic and science benchmarks, RLCM substantially improves calibration while maintaining or improving accuracy. The calibrated confidence signals enable more efficient conformal risk control and effective confidence-weighted aggregation.
AI.conclusion: RLCM provides an effective framework for joint optimization of correctness and confidence reliability in LLM reasoning, addressing overconfidence issues while enabling better confidence-based control and compute efficiency.

title: LLM-Augmented Traffic Signal Control with LSTM-Based Traffic State Prediction and Safety-Constrained Decision Support
summary: Traffic signal control is a critical task in intelligent transportation systems, yet conventional fixed-time and rule-based methods often struggle to adapt to dynamic traffic demand and provide limited decision interpretability. This study proposes an LLM-augmented traffic signal control framework that integrates LSTM-based short-term traffic state prediction, predictive phase selection, structured large language model reasoning, and safety-constrained action filtering. The LSTM module forecasts future queue length, waiting time, vehicle count, and lane occupancy based on recent intersection-level observations. A predictive controller then generates candidate signal actions, while the LLM module evaluates these actions using structured traffic-state inputs and produces congestion diagnoses, phase adjustment recommendations, and natural-language explanations. To ensure operational reliability, all LLM-generated recommendations are validated by a safety filter before execution. Simulation-based experiments in SUMO compare the proposed method with fixed-time control, rule-based control, and an LSTM-based predictive baseline under balanced demand, directional peak demand, and sudden surge scenarios. The results indicate that the proposed framework improves traffic efficiency, especially under dynamic and non-recurrent traffic conditions, while maintaining zero constraint violations after safety filtering. Overall, this study demonstrates that LLMs can enhance traffic signal control when used as constrained reasoning and decision-support modules rather than direct low-level controllers. Keywords: Intelligent Transportation Systems; Traffic Signal Control; Large Language Models; LSTM; Traffic State Prediction; Decision Support; Safety-Constrained Control; SUMO Simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-augmented traffic signal control framework that combines LSTM-based traffic prediction with structured LLM reasoning and safety filtering to improve adaptability and interpretability in dynamic traffic conditions.
AI.motivation: Conventional fixed-time and rule-based traffic signal control methods struggle to adapt to dynamic traffic demand and lack decision interpretability, creating a need for more adaptive and explainable intelligent transportation systems.
AI.method: The framework integrates: 1) LSTM-based short-term traffic state prediction (queue length, waiting time, vehicle count, lane occupancy), 2) Predictive phase selection controller, 3) Structured LLM reasoning that evaluates actions and provides explanations, and 4) Safety-constrained action filtering for reliability.
AI.result: Simulation experiments in SUMO showed the proposed framework improves traffic efficiency, especially under dynamic and non-recurrent traffic conditions (balanced demand, directional peaks, sudden surges), while maintaining zero constraint violations after safety filtering compared to fixed-time, rule-based, and LSTM-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively enhance traffic signal control when used as constrained reasoning and decision-support modules rather than direct low-level controllers, providing both improved performance and interpretability while maintaining safety.

title: TEMPO: Transformers for Temporal Disease Progression from Cross-Sectional Data
comment: 31 pages; Published at Conference on Health, Inference, and Learning (CHIL) 2026
summary: Event-Based Models (EBMs) infer biomarker progression from cross-sectional data but typically only as ordinal sequences and rely on rigid model assumptions. We propose \textsc{Tempo}, a Transformer architecture that learns both ordinal and continuous event sequences through simulation-based supervised learning. \textsc{Tempo} uses two Transformer modules: one treats biomarkers as tokens to infer event sequencing; the other treats patients as tokens, representing each by their per-biomarker abnormality profile, to infer patients' disease stages. On synthetic benchmarks, \textsc{Tempo} reduces normalized Kendall's Tau distance by 52.89\% and staging MAE by 25.33\% compared to state-of-the-art SA-EBM, with larger reductions in high-dimensional settings (58.88\% and 61.10\%). Applied to ADNI, \textsc{Tempo} recovers a biologically plausible Alzheimer's progression: early medial temporal atrophy, followed by amyloid accumulation and cognitive decline, and late-stage tau pathology with terminal acceleration of global neurodegeneration -- broadly consistent with established disease models. \textsc{Tempo} also eliminates the need to derive custom inference algorithms and enables rapid empirical comparison of generative hypotheses.

AI.tldr: Tempo is a Transformer-based model that learns both ordinal and continuous biomarker progression sequences from cross-sectional data, outperforming existing methods and revealing biologically plausible Alzheimer's disease progression patterns.
AI.motivation: Traditional Event-Based Models (EBMs) have limitations: they only infer ordinal sequences, rely on rigid model assumptions, and require custom inference algorithms. There's a need for a more flexible approach that can handle continuous progression and enable rapid testing of different disease progression hypotheses.
AI.method: Tempo uses a Transformer architecture with two modules: 1) a biomarker token module that infers event sequencing, and 2) a patient token module that represents patients by their per-biomarker abnormality profiles to infer disease stages. The model is trained using simulation-based supervised learning.
AI.result: On synthetic benchmarks, Tempo reduced normalized Kendall's Tau distance by 52.89% and staging MAE by 25.33% compared to SA-EBM, with even larger improvements (58.88% and 61.10%) in high-dimensional settings. In ADNI data, it recovered biologically plausible Alzheimer's progression patterns consistent with established disease models.
AI.conclusion: Tempo provides a superior alternative to traditional EBMs by enabling both ordinal and continuous biomarker progression inference, eliminating the need for custom inference algorithms, and allowing for rapid empirical comparison of different generative hypotheses about disease progression.

title: Agentic AI platforms for autonomous training and rule induction of human-human and virus-human protein-protein interactions
summary: We instruct an AI agent to construct two separate agentic AI platforms: one for autonomous training of predictive ML models for human-human and virus-human PPI, and the other for inducing explicit general rules governing human-human and virus-human PPI. The first agentic AI platform for autonomous training of predictive ML models for PPI is designed to consist of five AI agents that handle autonomous data collection, data verification, feature embedding, model design, and training and validation on three-way protein-disjoint cross-fold datasets. For human-human and human-virus PPIs, the final three-way protein-disjoint ensemble achieves an accuracy of 87.3% and 86.5%, respectively. For cross-checking and interpretability purposes, the second agentic AI platform is designed to replace ML predictions with human-readable rules derived from protein embeddings, physicochemical autocovariance descriptors, compartment annotations, pathway-domain overlap, and graph contexts. For human-human PPI, it is defined by a two-rule induction, whereas human-virus is induced by a more complex set of weighted rules. The rules induced by the second agentic platform align with the SHAP-identified features from the predictive ML models built by the first agentic platform. Taken together, our work demonstrates the agentic AI's ability to orchestrate from data planning to execution, and from rule induction to explanation in ML, opening the door to various applications.

AI.tldr: The paper presents two agentic AI platforms for protein-protein interaction (PPI) analysis: one autonomously trains predictive ML models for human-human and virus-human PPIs, achieving ~87% accuracy, while the other induces interpretable human-readable rules that align with SHAP-identified features from the ML models.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate the potential of agentic AI in orchestrating complex scientific workflows from data planning to execution, and from rule induction to explanation in machine learning applications for protein-protein interactions.
AI.method: Built two separate agentic AI platforms: 1) A 5-agent system for autonomous ML model training (data collection, verification, feature embedding, model design, training/validation using protein-disjoint cross-fold datasets), and 2) A rule induction platform that derives human-readable rules from protein embeddings, physicochemical autocovariance descriptors, compartment annotations, pathway-domain overlap, and graph contexts.
AI.result: The ML platform achieved 87.3% accuracy for human-human PPIs and 86.5% for human-virus PPIs using three-way protein-disjoint ensemble. The rule induction platform produced a two-rule set for human-human PPI and a more complex weighted rule set for human-virus PPI, with these rules aligning with SHAP-identified features from the predictive models.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully demonstrates agentic AI's ability to orchestrate complete workflows from data planning to execution and from rule induction to explanation, opening doors to various applications in computational biology and beyond.

title: When Context Sticks: Studying Interference in In-Context Learning
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Code available at: https://github.com/nilsvselte/icl-context-stickiness
summary: This paper investigates context stickiness in in-context learning (ICL), a phenomenon where earlier examples in a prompt interfere with a transformer's ability to adapt to later tasks. Using synthetic regression tasks over linear and quadratic functions, we examine how models trained under sequential, mixed, and random curricula handle abrupt task switches during inference. By sweeping over structured combinations of misleading linear examples followed by recovery quadratic examples, we quantify how prior context biases prediction error and how quickly models realign. Our results show strong evidence of persistent interference: more preceding linear examples reliably degrade quadratic predictions, while additional quadratic examples reduce error but with diminishing returns. We further find that training curricula significantly modulate resilience, with sequential training on the target function class yielding the fastest recovery, and surprisingly, random training producing the least robust behavior.

AI.tldr: Transformers exhibit "context stickiness" where early examples in prompts interfere with adaptation to later tasks, with training curriculum significantly affecting recovery resilience.
AI.motivation: To investigate context stickiness in in-context learning (ICL), where earlier examples in a prompt interfere with transformers' ability to adapt to later tasks, using controlled synthetic tasks to understand this interference phenomenon.
AI.method: Used synthetic regression tasks over linear and quadratic functions, examining models trained under sequential, mixed, and random curricula. Swept over structured combinations of misleading linear examples followed by recovery quadratic examples to quantify context bias and recovery speed.
AI.result: Found strong evidence of persistent interference: more preceding linear examples reliably degrade quadratic predictions, while additional quadratic examples reduce error but with diminishing returns. Training curricula significantly modulate resilience, with sequential training on target function class yielding fastest recovery, and surprisingly, random training producing least robust behavior.
AI.conclusion: Context stickiness is a significant phenomenon in ICL where early context interferes with later task adaptation, and training curriculum plays crucial role in determining model's resilience to such interference, with sequential training being most effective for recovery.

title: GAMED.AI: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Educational Game Generation
summary: We introduce GameDAI, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that transforms instructor-provided questions into fully playable, pedagogically grounded educational games validated through formal mechanic contracts. Built on phase-based LangGraph sub-graphs, deterministic Quality Gates, and structured Pydantic schemas, GameDAI supports two template families encompassing 15 interaction mechanics across spatial reasoning, procedural execution, and higher-order Bloom's Taxonomy objectives. Evaluated on 200 questions spanning five subject domains, the system achieves a 90% validation pass rate, 98.3% schema compliance, and 73% token reduction over ReAct agents (${\sim}$73,500 $\rightarrow$ ${\sim}$19,900 tokens/game) at $0.46 per game. Within this model configuration, these results suggest that phase-bounded architectural structure correlates more strongly with alignment quality than prompting strategy alone. Our demonstration lets attendees generate Bloom's-aligned games from natural language in under 60 seconds, inspect Quality Gate outputs at each pipeline phase, and browse a curated library of 50 games spanning all 15 mechanic types.

AI.tldr: GameDAI is a hierarchical multi-agent framework that automatically converts instructor questions into educational games using formal validation, achieving 90% validation success and significant efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need to transform instructor-provided educational questions into engaging, pedagogically sound games without requiring extensive game design expertise. Current approaches may lack formal validation, alignment with learning objectives, or efficiency in game generation.
AI.method: The authors developed a hierarchical multi-agent framework using phase-based LangGraph sub-graphs with deterministic Quality Gates and structured Pydantic schemas. The system supports two template families covering 15 interaction mechanics across spatial reasoning, procedural execution, and Bloom's Taxonomy objectives.
AI.result: GameDAI achieved 90% validation pass rate on 200 questions across five subject domains, with 98.3% schema compliance and 73% token reduction over ReAct agents (from ~73,500 to ~19,900 tokens/game) at $0.46 per game. The system can generate Bloom's-aligned games from natural language in under 60 seconds.
AI.conclusion: Phase-bounded architectural structure correlates more strongly with alignment quality than prompting strategy alone. The framework demonstrates that structured, validated game generation is feasible and efficient for educational applications, enabling rapid creation of pedagogically sound games from instructor questions.

title: V-GRPO: Online Reinforcement Learning for Denoising Generative Models Is Easier than You Think
summary: Aligning denoising generative models with human preferences or verifiable rewards remains a key challenge. While policy-gradient online reinforcement learning (RL) offers a principled post-training framework, its direct application is hindered by the intractable likelihoods of these models. Prior work therefore either optimizes an induced Markov decision process (MDP) over sampling trajectories, which is stable but inefficient, or uses likelihood surrogates based on the diffusion evidence lower bound (ELBO), which have so far underperformed on visual generation. Our key insight is that the ELBO-based approach can, in fact, be made both stable and efficient. By reducing surrogate variance and controlling gradient steps, we show that this approach can beat MDP-based methods. To this end, we introduce Variational GRPO (V-GRPO), a method that integrates ELBO-based surrogates with the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm, alongside a set of simple yet essential techniques. Our method is easy to implement, aligns with pretraining objectives, and avoids the limitations of MDP-based methods. V-GRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance in text-to-image synthesis, while delivering a $2\times$ speedup over MixGRPO and a $3\times$ speedup over DiffusionNFT.

AI.tldr: V-GRPO introduces a stable and efficient ELBO-based reinforcement learning method for aligning denoising generative models that outperforms existing MDP-based approaches in text-to-image synthesis with significant speed improvements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of aligning denoising generative models with human preferences or verifiable rewards. While policy-gradient online RL offers a principled approach, direct application is hindered by intractable likelihoods in these models. Existing approaches either use inefficient MDP-based methods or underperforming ELBO-based methods.
AI.method: The paper introduces Variational GRPO (V-GRPO), which integrates ELBO-based surrogates with the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm. The key insight is making ELBO-based approaches stable and efficient through reduced surrogate variance and controlled gradient steps, combined with simple yet essential techniques.
AI.result: V-GRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance in text-to-image synthesis, delivering a 2× speedup over MixGRPO and a 3× speedup over DiffusionNFT, while being easy to implement and aligning with pretraining objectives.
AI.conclusion: The proposed V-GRPO method successfully overcomes the limitations of both MDP-based and previous ELBO-based approaches, providing a stable, efficient, and high-performing framework for aligning denoising generative models with human preferences through reinforcement learning.

title: Context-Aware Hospitalization Forecasting Evaluations for Decision Support using LLMs
summary: Medical and public health experts must make real-time resource decisions, such as expanding hospital bed capacity, based on projected hospitalization trends during large-scale healthcare disruptions (e.g., operational failures or pandemics). Forecasting models can assist in this task by analyzing large volumes of resource-related data at the facility level, but they must be reliable for decision-making under real-world data conditions. Recent work shows that large language models (LLMs) can incorporate richer forms of context into numerical forecasting. Whereas traditional models rely primarily on temporal context (i.e., past observations), LLMs can also leverage non-temporal public health context such as demographic, geographic, and population-level features. However, it remains unclear how these models should be used to produce stable or decision-relevant predictions in real-world healthcare settings. To evaluate how LLMs can be effectively used in this setting, we evaluate three approaches across 60 counties with low-,mid-, and high-hospitalization intensities in the United States: direct LLM-based forecasting, classical time-series models, and a context-augmented hybrid pipeline (HybridARX) that incorporates LLM-derived signals into structured models. Because the goal is operational decision-making rather than error minimization alone, we evaluate performance with bias and lead-lag alignment in addition to standard forecasting metrics. Our results show that HybridARX improves over classical ARX by yielding more stable and better-calibrated forecasts, particularly when incorporating noisy contextual signals into structured time-series models. These findings suggest that, in non-stationary healthcare resource forecasting, LLMs are most useful when embedded within structured hybrid models.

AI.tldr: A hybrid forecasting approach combining LLMs with classical time-series models improves hospital bed capacity predictions for healthcare resource planning, particularly when dealing with noisy contextual data.
AI.motivation: Healthcare decision-makers need reliable real-time resource allocation forecasts during disruptions (like pandemics), but existing models must handle real-world data challenges and incorporate rich contextual information beyond just temporal patterns.
AI.method: Evaluated three approaches across 60 US counties: direct LLM-based forecasting, classical time-series models, and a hybrid pipeline (HybridARX) that incorporates LLM-derived contextual signals into structured ARX models. Assessment included bias, lead-lag alignment, and standard forecasting metrics.
AI.result: HybridARX outperforms classical ARX by producing more stable and better-calibrated forecasts, especially when incorporating noisy contextual signals. LLMs are most effective when embedded within structured hybrid models rather than used alone.
AI.conclusion: For healthcare resource forecasting in non-stationary environments, LLMs should be integrated into hybrid models with classical time-series approaches to produce decision-relevant predictions that balance accuracy with operational stability.

title: Domain-Adapted Fine-Tuning of ECG Foundation Models for Multi-Label Structural Heart Disease Screening
summary: Transthoracic echocardiography is the reference standard for confirming structural heart disease (SHD), but first-line screening is limited by cost, workflow burden, and specialist availability. We evaluated whether open pretrained electrocardiogram (ECG) foundation models can support echo-confirmed multi-label SHD detection using the public EchoNext Mini-Model benchmark. Six echocardiography-derived abnormalities were targeted: reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, increased left ventricular wall thickness, aortic stenosis, mitral regurgitation, tricuspid regurgitation, and right ventricular systolic dysfunction. Under a common pipeline, we compared engineered ECG features with gradient boosting, end-to-end waveform learning from scratch, and transfer from open ECG foundation models. We then applied in-domain self-supervised adaptation of an ECG foundation model (ECG-FM) on EchoNext waveforms followed by selective supervised fine-tuning, and evaluated trade-offs between discrimination and adaptation cost. Adapted ECG-FM models achieved the best overall performance: peak macro-AUROC 0.8509 and macro-AUPRC 0.4297, while a parameter-efficient operating point preserved AUROC (0.8501) and attained the highest fixed-threshold macro-F1 0.3691. Late fusion with covariates did not improve threshold-independent discrimination, and evaluated LoRA, alternative backbones, and mixture-of-foundations strategies did not surpass the best adapted single-backbone models. These results indicate that for ECG-based case finding and echocardiography triage, combining target-domain self-supervised adaptation with selective supervised updating of a pretrained ECG backbone is the most effective transfer strategy.

AI.tldr: ECG foundation models with self-supervised adaptation outperform traditional methods for multi-label detection of structural heart disease from echocardiography data.
AI.motivation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the gold standard for structural heart disease but faces limitations in cost, workflow burden, and specialist availability for first-line screening.
AI.method: Compared engineered ECG features with gradient boosting, end-to-end waveform learning, and transfer from open ECG foundation models. Applied in-domain self-supervised adaptation of ECG-FM on EchoNext waveforms followed by selective supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Adapted ECG-FM models achieved best overall performance: peak macro-AUROC 0.8509 and macro-AUPRC 0.4297. Parameter-efficient operating point preserved AUROC (0.8501) and attained highest fixed-threshold macro-F1 0.3691.
AI.conclusion: For ECG-based case finding and echocardiography triage, combining target-domain self-supervised adaptation with selective supervised updating of a pretrained ECG backbone is the most effective transfer strategy.

title: An empirical evaluation of the risks of AI model updates using clinical data: stability, arbitrariness, and fairness
comment: Accepted to iEEE EMBC 2026. 4 pages, 3 figures
summary: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML) models used in clinical settings are increasingly deployed to support clinical decision-making. However, when training data become stale due to changes in demographics, environment, or patient behaviors, model performance can degrade substantially. While updating models with new training data is necessary, such updates may also introduce new risks. We evaluated the proposed monitoring framework on four publicly available U.S.-based Type 1 Diabetes datasets containing high-resolution continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data, comprising approximately 11,300 weekly observations from 496 participants under 20 years of age. All datasets included structured sociodemographic information. Using the prediction of severe hyperglycemia events in children with type 1 diabetes as a case study, we examine how different model update strategies can adversely affect model stability (e.g., by causing predictions to "flip" for a large number of cases after an update), increase arbitrariness in predictions, or worsen accuracy equity and the balance of error rates across subpopulations. We propose multiple dimensions for continuous monitoring to detect these issues and argue that such monitoring is essential for the development of trustworthy clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: The paper examines risks in updating clinical AI/ML models for diabetes prediction and proposes a monitoring framework to detect issues with model stability, fairness, and performance when retraining on new data.
AI.motivation: Clinical AI/ML models can degrade as training data becomes stale due to demographic, environmental, or behavioral changes. While updating models is necessary, such updates may introduce new risks like reduced stability, increased arbitrariness, and worsened fairness across patient subpopulations.
AI.method: The study evaluated a monitoring framework using four publicly available U.S.-based Type 1 Diabetes datasets with CGM data (11,300 weekly observations from 496 participants under 20). Using severe hyperglycemia event prediction as a case study, researchers examined how different model update strategies affect model stability, prediction arbitrariness, and accuracy equity across subpopulations.
AI.result: The evaluation revealed that model updates can adversely affect model stability (causing prediction "flipping"), increase arbitrariness in predictions, and worsen accuracy equity and error rate balance across different patient subpopulations. The proposed monitoring framework successfully detected these issues.
AI.conclusion: Continuous monitoring across multiple dimensions is essential for developing trustworthy clinical decision support systems, as it helps detect risks introduced during model updates that could compromise model stability, fairness, and overall reliability in clinical settings.

title: Approximating Uniform Random Rotations by Two-Block Structured Hadamard Rotations in High Dimensions
summary:
Uniform random rotations are a useful primitive in applications such as fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss embeddings, kernel approximation, communication-efficient learning, and recent AI compression pipelines, but they are computationally expensive to generate and apply in high dimensions. A common practical replacement is repeated structured random rotations built from Walsh-Hadamard transforms and random sign diagonals.
  Applying the structured random rotation twice has been shown empirically to be useful, but the supporting theory is still limited. In this paper we study the approximation quality achieved when using this two-block structured Hadamard rotation. Our results are both positive and negative. On the positive side, we prove that every fixed coordinate of the two-block transform converges uniformly, over all inputs, to the corresponding coordinate of a uniformly rotated vector, with an explicit Kolmogorov-distance bound of order $d^{-1/5}$. On the negative side, we prove an explicit lower bound on the Wasserstein distance between the full vector distributions, showing that the two-block transform is not a globally accurate surrogate for a uniform random rotation in the worst case. For the extremal input used in the lower bound, we also prove a matching asymptotic upper bound, showing that the lower-bound scale is sharp for that input.
  Taken together, the results identify a clear separation between one-dimensional marginal behavior, where approximation improves with dimension, and full high-dimensional geometry, where a nonvanishing discrepancy remains. This provides a partial theoretical explanation for the empirical success of structured Hadamard rotations in some algorithms, while also clarifying the limitations of treating them as drop-in replacements for true uniform random rotations.

AI.tldr: Two-block structured Hadamard rotations approximate uniform random rotations well in one-dimensional margins but poorly in high-dimensional geometry, explaining their empirical success while clarifying limitations.
AI.motivation: Uniform random rotations are computationally expensive in high dimensions, leading to practical use of structured Hadamard rotations. While applying them twice has shown empirical utility, theoretical understanding remains limited.
AI.method: Analyze the two-block structured Hadamard rotation (built from Walsh-Hadamard transforms and random sign diagonals) through probabilistic analysis, using Kolmogorov distance for marginal distributions and Wasserstein distance for full vector distributions.
AI.result: Positive: Fixed coordinates converge uniformly with explicit Kolmogorov-distance bound of O(d^{-1/5}). Negative: Explicit lower bound on Wasserstein distance shows worst-case discrepancy remains nonvanishing. Matching asymptotic bounds demonstrate sharpness for extremal inputs.
AI.conclusion: Structured Hadamard rotations work well for one-dimensional marginals but not as global uniform rotation replacements, explaining empirical success in some algorithms while clarifying theoretical limitations.

title: Evolve: A Persistent Knowledge Lifecycle for Small Language Models
comment: 35 pages, 1 figure. Code and evaluation data: https://gitlab.com/dikran.hovagimian/evolve
summary:
Evolve pairs a small local language model with a persistent, teacher-compiled knowledge store -- refined through sleep consolidation and usage-driven refresh -- to deliver substantial accuracy gains over the model's parametric baseline while amortizing teacher costs through cross-query knowledge reuse. Rather than retrieving document fragments at query time, Evolve constructs a store of semantically coherent sections compiled by teacher models at natural conceptual boundaries; new sections are staged on acquisition, consolidated offline through teacher-mediated merging, and refreshed inline when expired. A 2B-parameter local model handles classification and generation; large teacher models are invoked only for knowledge operations.
  Across 750 benchmark queries spanning custom specialist questions, NaturalQuestions, and TriviaQA, the 2B model augmented by Evolve improves from 20-33% baseline accuracy to 60-84% (+40-52pp) while reducing teacher invocations by over 50% through reuse. Post-consolidation compresses the knowledge store by 31-33.5% across three independent benchmarks while preserving accuracy; section-based retrieval outperforms chunk-based retrieval by 5-9pp across every lifecycle condition. The architecture supports two generation modes over the same lifecycle -- suppress (strict section-only grounding, auditable) and augment (section-supplemented responses).

AI.tldr: Evolve uses a small local LLM paired with a persistent knowledge store compiled by teacher models to achieve substantial accuracy gains while reducing teacher costs through knowledge reuse and consolidation.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of small local language models (2B parameters) which have limited parametric knowledge, while reducing the high cost of frequently invoking large teacher models for every query.
AI.method: Pairs a 2B-parameter local model with a persistent knowledge store compiled by teacher models. Knowledge is organized as semantically coherent sections (not document fragments) at natural conceptual boundaries. Uses sleep consolidation for offline merging and inline refresh for expired knowledge. The system supports two modes: suppress (strict section-only grounding) and augment (section-supplemented responses).
AI.result: Across 750 benchmark queries, the 2B model with Evolve improved accuracy from 20-33% baseline to 60-84% (+40-52 percentage points) while reducing teacher invocations by over 50%. Post-consolidation compressed the knowledge store by 31-33.5% while preserving accuracy. Section-based retrieval outperformed chunk-based retrieval by 5-9 percentage points across all conditions.
AI.conclusion: Evolve successfully demonstrates that small local models can achieve substantial accuracy improvements when augmented with teacher-compiled knowledge stores, while amortizing teacher costs through knowledge reuse, consolidation, and efficient section-based retrieval.

title: Representational Curvature Modulates Behavioral Uncertainty in Large Language Models
summary: In autoregressive large language models (LLMs), temporal straightening offers an account of how the next-token prediction objective shapes representations. Models learn to progressively straighten the representational trajectory of input sequences across layers, potentially facilitating next-token prediction via linear extrapolation. However, a direct link between this trajectory and token-level behavior has been missing. We provide such a link by relating contextual curvature-a geometric measure of how sharply the representational trajectory bends over recent context-to next-token entropy. Across two models (GPT-2 XL and Pythia-2.8B), contextual curvature is correlated with entropy, and this relationship emerges during training. Perturbation experiments reveal selective dependence: manipulating curvature through trajectory-aligned interventions reliably modulates entropy, while geometrically misaligned perturbations have no effect. Finally, regularizing representations to be straighter during training modestly reduces token-level entropy without degrading validation loss. These results identify trajectory curvature as a task-aligned representational feature that influences behavioral uncertainty in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Contextual curvature in LLM representations correlates with next-token entropy and can be manipulated to affect uncertainty.
AI.motivation: While temporal straightening (progressive straightening of representational trajectories across layers) has been observed in LLMs as facilitating next-token prediction, the direct link between representational geometry and actual token-level behavior has been missing.
AI.method: The study measures contextual curvature (geometric measure of how sharply representational trajectories bend over recent context) and relates it to next-token entropy. Experiments are conducted across two models (GPT-2 XL and Pythia-2.8B), tracking the relationship's emergence during training. Perturbation experiments manipulate curvature through trajectory-aligned interventions versus geometrically misaligned perturbations. Finally, regularizing representations to be straighter during training is tested.
AI.result: Contextual curvature is correlated with next-token entropy in both models, and this relationship emerges during training. Trajectory-aligned interventions reliably modulate entropy, while misaligned perturbations have no effect. Regularizing representations to be straighter modestly reduces token-level entropy without degrading validation loss.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory curvature serves as a task-aligned representational feature that influences behavioral uncertainty in LLMs, providing the missing link between representational geometry and token-level behavior.

title: When Does Removing LayerNorm Help? Activation Bounding as a Regime-Dependent Implicit Regularizer
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, includes appendices. Code and artifacts: https://github.com/lucky-verma/dyt-composition-study
summary: Dynamic Tanh (DyT) removes LayerNorm by bounding activations with a learned tanh(alpha x). We show that this bounding is a regime-dependent implicit regularizer, not a uniformly beneficial replacement. Across GPT-2-family models spanning 64M to 3.78B parameters and 1M to 118M tokens, with Llama and ViT cross-checks, DyT improves validation loss by 27.3% at 64M/1M but worsens it by 18.8% at 64M/118M; the 1M benefit vanishes with capacity (+1.7% at 3.78B), while the 118M penalty reaches +27.9%. The mechanism is measurable: 49% of DyT activations saturate at 1M versus 23% at 118M, and a 500-step saturation heuristic classifies DyT's sign with 75% raw in-sample accuracy on the 12-cell GPT-2 calibration set (AUC 0.75; 64% when adding Scale 5 stress cells), correctly labels 3/3 Llama checks, but only reaches 50% raw leave-one-scale-out accuracy. Three interventions support the bounding explanation: HardTanh reproduces the regime pattern, increasing alpha at 118M monotonically reduces DyT's penalty, and vanilla+dropout(p=0.5) matches DyT's data-rich loss. We also localize Llama-DyT collapse to SwiGLU gating, where saturation separates collapse from convergence in a 3-seed component ablation (r=0.94). Scope: all experiments are compute-limited (T/P < 1.84), below Chinchilla-optimal training.

AI.tldr: DyT replaces LayerNorm with learned tanh bounding but acts as a regime-dependent implicit regularizer, helping small models with limited data but hurting larger models with more data.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether Dynamic Tanh (DyT) - which removes LayerNorm by bounding activations with learned tanh(alpha x) - is uniformly beneficial or depends on training regime factors like model size and data availability.
AI.method: Conducted systematic experiments across GPT-2-family models (64M to 3.78B parameters) trained on 1M to 118M tokens, with Llama and ViT cross-checks. Measured activation saturation, developed heuristic classification, performed interventions (HardTanh, alpha adjustment, dropout), and analyzed component-level effects.
AI.result: DyT improves validation loss by 27.3% for small models with limited data (64M/1M) but worsens it by 18.8% with more data (64M/118M). Benefits vanish with capacity (+1.7% at 3.78B) while penalties increase (+27.9%). Activation saturation explains effects: 49% saturate at 1M vs 23% at 118M. Heuristic classification achieves 75% raw accuracy on calibration set. Interventions confirm bounding mechanism: adjusting alpha reduces penalty, dropout matches DyT's data-rich loss.
AI.conclusion: DyT is not a drop-in replacement for LayerNorm but acts as a data-dependent implicit regularizer that can help in low-data/high-regularization regimes but hurts in high-data/low-regularization regimes. The bounding mechanism's effects are predictable via activation saturation metrics, with practical implications for architecture design choices.

title: Failure-Centered Runtime Evaluation for Deployed Trilingual Public-Space Agents
summary:
This paper presents PSA-Eval, a failure-centered runtime evaluation framework for deployed trilingual public-space agents. The central claim is that, when the evaluation object shifts from a static input-output mapping to a runtime system, the basic unit of analysis should shift from score to failure. PSA-Eval extends the conventional chain Question -> Answer -> Score -> End into Question -> Batch -> Run -> Score -> Failure Case -> Repair -> Regression Batch, making failures traceable, reviewable, repairable, and regression-testable.
  The framework uses trilingual equivalent inputs as controlled probes for observing group-level cross-language policy drift. We conduct a pilot study on a real trilingual digital front-desk system deployed in the lobby of an international financial institution. The pilot uses a simplified single-foundation-model setting (MA = MB), so the observed drift should not be interpreted as an A/B foundation-model difference. The study contains 81 samples organized into 27 trilingual equivalent question groups. Although the system achieves an average score of 23.15/24, 14 groups show non-zero cross-language score drift, 5 groups show drift of at least 3 points, and the maximum drift reaches 9 points. These results provide initial evidence that failure-centered runtime evaluation can expose structured deployment signals hidden by aggregate scoring.

AI.tldr: PSA-Eval is a failure-centered runtime evaluation framework for multilingual public-space AI agents that shifts evaluation from aggregate scores to traceable failures, enabling systematic failure analysis and repair in deployed systems.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods focus on static input-output scoring, but runtime systems in production require different evaluation approaches. There's a need to move from score-based to failure-centered analysis to understand and improve deployed multilingual AI systems.
AI.method: PSA-Eval extends conventional evaluation (Question→Answer→Score) to Question→Batch→Run→Score→Failure Case→Repair→Regression Batch, making failures traceable and repairable. Uses trilingual equivalent inputs as controlled probes to observe cross-language policy drift in deployed systems.
AI.result: In a pilot study on a real trilingual digital front-desk system (81 samples, 27 trilingual groups), the system achieved high average scores (23.15/24), but revealed significant cross-language drift: 14 groups showed non-zero drift, 5 groups had ≥3 point drift, with maximum drift of 9 points.
AI.conclusion: Failure-centered runtime evaluation can expose structured deployment signals hidden by aggregate scoring. PSA-Eval provides a practical framework for continuous improvement of deployed multilingual AI systems by making failures traceable, reviewable, repairable, and regression-testable.

title: Machine learning models for estimating counterfactuals in a single-arm inflammatory bowel disease study
summary: Single-arm trials accelerate study timelines by reducing the number of patients that must be recruited for a concurrent control group. However, these designs require an alternative comparator to estimate treatment effects. One approach is to construct a virtual control arm using a machine learning (ML) model trained on external control data to predict the counterfactual outcomes of the treatment arm. Our aim in this study was to leverage virtual controls by developing and evaluating ML-based counterfactual outcome models trained on IFX-treated patients to predict 1-year steroid-free clinical remission (SFCR ) and a composite of C-reactive protein remission plus steroid-free clinical remission (CRP-SFCR) for ADA-treated pediatric Crohn's disease patients, and to compare the resulting IFX-versus-ADA treatment effect estimates with those obtained using propensity score matching to external controls. Five ML models were used to train counterfactual models on the observed IFX cohort data. The resulting models were used to predict the counterfactual outcomes for the ADA arm patients. LGBM yields the best OR closest to the propensity score matched reference, and all 95% CI results align with the conclusion from the reference study that no statistical difference in the primary and secondary outcomes has been observed between the patients treated with ADA or IFX. Our study supports virtual controls as a viable and effective substitute for expensive, lengthy or unethical patient recruitment in an inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) trial. The developed gradient boosted prediction model can be used as a pretrained model to generate IFX counterfactual predictions in future studies, pending external validation and assessment of transportability.

AI.tldr: ML-based virtual control arms using counterfactual outcome models provide viable alternatives to traditional control groups in single-arm trials, showing comparable treatment effect estimates to propensity score matching for pediatric Crohn's disease treatments.
AI.motivation: Single-arm trials accelerate study timelines by reducing patient recruitment needs, but require alternative comparators. The motivation is to develop and evaluate ML-based virtual control arms as substitutes for traditional control groups, particularly when patient recruitment is expensive, lengthy, or unethical.
AI.method: Researchers developed ML-based counterfactual outcome models using five different ML algorithms trained on IFX-treated patients. These models predicted 1-year outcomes (SFCR and CRP-SFCR) for ADA-treated pediatric Crohn's disease patients as virtual controls. Results were compared with propensity score matching to external controls as reference.
AI.result: The LGBM (Light Gradient Boosted Machine) model yielded odds ratios closest to the propensity score matched reference. All 95% confidence intervals aligned with the conclusion from the reference study that no statistical difference exists between ADA and IFX treatments for primary and secondary outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Virtual controls using ML-based counterfactual outcome models are viable and effective substitutes for traditional control groups in IBD trials. The developed gradient boosted model can serve as a pretrained model for future studies, pending external validation and transportability assessment.

title: CT-FineBench: A Diagnostic Fidelity Benchmark for Fine-Grained Evaluation of CT Report Generation
summary: The evaluation of generated reports remains a critical challenge in Computed Tomography (CT) report generation, due to the large volume of text, the diversity and complexity of findings, and the presence of fine-grained, disease-oriented attributes. Conventional evaluation metrics offer only coarse measures of lexical overlap or entity matching and fail to reflect the granular diagnostic accuracy required for clinical use. To address this gap, we propose CT-FineBench, a benchmark built from CT-RATE and Merlin to evaluate the fine-grained factual consistency of CT reports, constructed from CT-RATE and Merlin. Our benchmark is constructed through a meticulous, Question-Answering (QA) based process: first, we identify and structure key, finding-specific clinical attributes (like location, size, margin). Second, we systematically transform these attributes into a QA dataset, where questions probe for specific clinical details grounded in gold-standard reports. The evaluation protocol for CT-FineBench involves using this QA dataset to query a machine-generated report and scoring the correctness of the answers. This allows for a comprehensive, interpretable, and clinically-relevant assessment, moving beyond superficial lexical overlap to pinpoint specific clinical errors. Experiments show that CT-FineBench correlates better with expert clinical assessment and is substantially more sensitive to fine-grained factual errors than prior metrics.

AI.tldr: CT-FineBench: A QA-based benchmark for fine-grained evaluation of factual consistency in CT report generation, addressing limitations of conventional metrics.
AI.motivation: Current CT report generation evaluation has critical challenges: large text volume, diverse/complex findings, and fine-grained disease attributes. Conventional metrics only provide coarse lexical overlap or entity matching, failing to reflect granular diagnostic accuracy needed for clinical use.
AI.method: Built CT-FineBench from CT-RATE and Merlin datasets. Meticulous QA-based process: 1) Identify and structure key clinical attributes (location, size, margin, etc.), 2) Transform attributes into QA dataset where questions probe specific clinical details from gold-standard reports. Evaluation protocol uses QA dataset to query machine-generated reports and score answer correctness.
AI.result: CT-FineBench correlates better with expert clinical assessment than prior metrics and is substantially more sensitive to fine-grained factual errors, providing comprehensive, interpretable, and clinically-relevant assessment.
AI.conclusion: CT-FineBench successfully addresses the gap in CT report evaluation by moving beyond superficial lexical overlap to pinpoint specific clinical errors, offering a clinically-relevant assessment framework for generated CT reports.

title: Evaluating CUDA Tile for AI Workloads on Hopper and Blackwell GPUs
summary:
NVIDIA's CUDA Tile (CuTile) introduces a Python-based, tile-centric abstraction for GPU kernel development that aims to simplify programming while retaining Tensor Core and Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) efficiency on modern GPUs. We present the first independent, cross-architecture evaluation of CuTile against established approaches such as cuBLAS, Triton, WMMA, and raw SIMT on three NVIDIA GPUs spanning Hopper and Blackwell: H100 NVL, B200, and RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition. We benchmark representative AI workloads, including GEMM, fused multi-head attention, and end-to-end LLM inference in BF16/FP16 precision, to assess both performance and portability.
  Our results show that CuTile effectiveness is strongly workload- and architecture-dependent. On datacenter-class Blackwell (B200), CuTile achieves up to 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention, outperforming FlashAttention-2 by 2.5x while requiring only 60 lines of Python kernel code. For GEMM, CuTile reaches 52-79% of cuBLAS performance in 22 lines of code (versus 123 for WMMA), making it a practical replacement for hand-written CUDA kernels but not yet for vendor-optimized libraries. However, the same CuTile attention kernel achieves only 53% of FlashAttention-2 throughput on RTX PRO 6000 (sm_120), exposing significant cross-architecture optimization gaps. In contrast, Triton sustains 62-101% of cuBLAS performance across all tested platforms without architecture-specific tuning, demonstrating substantially stronger portability.

AI.tldr: CuTile is a Python-based tile-centric abstraction for GPU kernels that aims to simplify programming while maintaining efficiency, but its performance varies significantly across workloads and GPU architectures compared to alternatives like Triton and cuBLAS.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to simplify GPU kernel development while retaining Tensor Core and TMA efficiency on modern GPUs, offering a more accessible alternative to complex hand-written CUDA kernels while trying to compete with vendor-optimized libraries.
AI.method: They conducted the first independent, cross-architecture evaluation comparing CuTile against established approaches (cuBLAS, Triton, WMMA, raw SIMT) on three NVIDIA GPUs (H100 NVL, B200, RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell). Benchmarked representative AI workloads including GEMM, fused multi-head attention, and end-to-end LLM inference in BF16/FP16 precision to assess both performance and portability.
AI.result: CuTile's effectiveness is strongly workload- and architecture-dependent. On Blackwell B200, it achieved up to 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention (2.5x faster than FlashAttention-2) with only 60 lines of Python. For GEMM, it reached 52-79% of cuBLAS performance in 22 lines (vs 123 for WMMA). However, the same attention kernel only achieved 53% of FlashAttention-2 throughput on RTX PRO 6000. Triton sustained 62-101% of cuBLAS performance across all platforms without architecture-specific tuning.
AI.conclusion: CuTile shows promise as a practical replacement for hand-written CUDA kernels (especially on datacenter-class Blackwell GPUs), offering simplicity with reasonable performance, but it has significant cross-architecture optimization gaps and falls short of vendor-optimized libraries. Triton demonstrates substantially stronger portability across different GPU architectures.

title: QED: An Open-Source Multi-Agent System for Generating Mathematical Proofs on Open Problems
summary: We explore a central question in AI for mathematics: can AI systems produce original, nontrivial proofs for open research problems? Despite strong benchmark performance, producing genuinely novel proofs remains an outstanding challenge for LLMs. Through systematic experiments with frontier LLMs on research-level proof tasks, we identify seven failure modes that prevent reliable proof generation, including context contamination, citation hallucination, hand-waving on key steps and misallocation of proof effort, unstable proof plans, unfocused verification, problem modification and single-model bottleneck. We argue that the gap between benchmark success and research-level proving is primarily one of system design, due to those failure modes. We present QED, an open-source multi-agent proof system in which each architectural decision directly addresses a specific failure mode. Evaluated on five open problems in applied analysis and PDEs contributed by domain experts, QED produces correct proofs for three problems, each verified by the contributing experts as original and nontrivial. QED is released as open-source software at https://github.com/proofQED/QED.

AI.tldr: QED is a multi-agent proof system that addresses LLM failure modes to generate original, nontrivial proofs for open research problems, successfully proving 3 out of 5 problems in applied analysis and PDEs.
AI.motivation: Despite strong benchmark performance, LLMs struggle to produce genuinely novel proofs for open research problems. There's a gap between benchmark success and research-level proving capabilities that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: QED is an open-source multi-agent proof system where each architectural decision directly addresses specific failure modes identified through systematic experiments: context contamination, citation hallucination, hand-waving, misallocation of proof effort, unstable proof plans, unfocused verification, problem modification, and single-model bottleneck.
AI.result: Evaluated on five open problems in applied analysis and PDEs contributed by domain experts, QED produces correct proofs for three problems, each verified by the contributing experts as original and nontrivial.
AI.conclusion: The gap between benchmark success and research-level proving is primarily a system design issue, and QED demonstrates that addressing specific failure modes through multi-agent architecture enables LLMs to produce novel proofs for open problems.

title: Hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph Optimization for Low-Latency Large Language Model Inference
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across natural language and multimodal tasks, yet their practical deployment remains constrained by inference latency and kernel launch overhead, particularly in interactive, short-sequence settings. This paper presents a hybrid runtime framework that combines Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation with CUDA Graph execution to reduce launch overhead while preserving runtime flexibility during autoregressive decoding. The framework partitions transformer inference into static components executed via CUDA Graph replay and dynamic components handled through JIT-compiled kernels, enabling asynchronous graph capture and reuse across decoding steps.
  We evaluate the proposed approach on LLaMA-2 7B using single-GPU, batch-size-one inference across prompt lengths from 10 to 500 tokens. Experimental results show that the hybrid runtime reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 66.0% and achieves lower P99 latency compared with TensorRT-LLM in this regime. These results indicate that hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph execution can effectively reduce inference latency and variance for short-sequence LLM workloads, making it a practical optimization strategy for latency-sensitive AI applications.

AI.tldr: Hybrid JIT+CUDA Graph framework reduces LLM inference latency for short sequences by combining static graph execution with dynamic JIT compilation during autoregressive decoding.
AI.motivation: LLM deployment faces inference latency and kernel launch overhead challenges, especially for interactive, short-sequence applications where prompt processing overhead dominates.
AI.method: Combines JIT compilation with CUDA Graph execution, partitioning transformer inference into static components executed via graph replay and dynamic components handled through JIT-compiled kernels, enabling asynchronous graph capture and reuse.
AI.result: On LLaMA-2 7B with single-GPU, batch-size-one inference: reduces Time-to-First-Token by up to 66.0% and achieves lower P99 latency compared to TensorRT-LLM for prompt lengths from 10-500 tokens.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph execution effectively reduces inference latency and variance for short-sequence LLM workloads, making it a practical optimization strategy for latency-sensitive AI applications.

title: AgentPulse: A Continuous Multi-Signal Framework for Evaluating AI Agents in Deployment
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables. Preprint under review
summary: Static benchmarks measure what AI agents can do at a fixed point in time but not how they are adopted, maintained, or experienced in deployment. We introduce AgentPulse, a continuous evaluation framework scoring 50 agents across 10 workload categories along four factors (Benchmark Performance, Adoption Signals, Community Sentiment, and Ecosystem Health) aggregated from 18 real-time signals across GitHub, package registries, IDE marketplaces, social platforms, and benchmark leaderboards. Three analyses ground the framework. The four factors capture largely complementary information (n=50; $ρ_{\max}=0.61$ for Adoption-Ecosystem, all others $|ρ| \leq 0.37$). A circularity-controlled test (n=35) shows the Benchmark+Sentiment sub-composite, which contains no GitHub-derived signals, predicts external adoption proxies it does not aggregate: GitHub stars ($ρ_s=0.52$, $p<0.01$) and Stack Overflow question volume ($ρ_s=0.49$, $p<0.01$), with VS Code installs ($ρ_s=0.44$, $p<0.05$) reported as illustrative given that only 11 of 35 agents have non-zero installs. On the n=11 subset with published SWE-bench scores, composite and benchmark-only rankings are nearly uncorrelated ($ρ_s=0.25$; 9 of 11 agents shift by at least 2 ranks), driven by a strong negative Adoption-Capability correlation among closed-source high-capability agents within this subset. This is precisely why we rest the framework's validity claim on the broader n=35 test rather than the SWE-bench overlap. AgentPulse surfaces deployment signal absent from benchmarks; it is a methodology, not a ground-truth ranking. The framework, all collected signals, scoring outputs, and evaluation harness are released under CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: AgentPulse introduces a continuous evaluation framework that measures AI agents across multiple factors beyond static benchmarks, using 18 real-time signals from various platforms to assess deployment and adoption metrics.
AI.motivation: Static benchmarks only measure AI agents' capabilities at fixed points in time, but fail to capture how these agents are actually adopted, maintained, and experienced in real-world deployment scenarios. There's a need for continuous evaluation that reflects practical usage.
AI.method: AgentPulse evaluates 50 agents across 10 workload categories using four complementary factors: Benchmark Performance, Adoption Signals, Community Sentiment, and Ecosystem Health. These are aggregated from 18 real-time signals across GitHub, package registries, IDE marketplaces, social platforms, and benchmark leaderboards. The framework uses statistical validation including circularity-controlled tests and correlation analyses.
AI.result: The four factors capture largely complementary information (max correlation ρ=0.61). The framework predicts external adoption proxies not included in the aggregation (GitHub stars ρ=0.52, Stack Overflow questions ρ=0.49). For agents with SWE-bench scores, composite and benchmark-only rankings are nearly uncorrelated (ρ=0.25), with 9 of 11 agents shifting by at least 2 ranks. A strong negative Adoption-Capability correlation was found among closed-source high-capability agents.
AI.conclusion: AgentPulse successfully surfaces deployment signals absent from traditional benchmarks, providing a methodology for continuous evaluation rather than a ground-truth ranking. The framework reveals important adoption-capability trade-offs and offers a more holistic view of AI agent performance in real-world scenarios.

title: GeoCert: Certified Geometric AI for Reliable Forecasting
summary: Forecasting systems in science must be accurate, physically consistent, and certifiably reliable. Most existing models address prediction, constraint enforcement, and verification separately, limiting scalability and interpretability. We introduce GeoCert, a geometric AI framework that unifies forecasting, physical reasoning, and formal verification within a single differentiable computation. GeoCert formulates forecasting as evolution along a hyperbolic manifold, where negative curvature induces contraction dynamics, intrinsic robustness, and logarithmic-time certification. A hierarchical constraint architecture separates universal physical laws from domain-specific dynamics, enabling certified generalization across energy, climate, finance, and transportation systems. GeoCert achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing computational cost by 97.5% and maintaining better certification rates. By embedding verification into the geometry of learning, GeoCert transforms forecasting from empirical approximation to formally verified inference, offering a scalable foundation for trustworthy, reproducible, and physically grounded scientific AI.

AI.tldr: GeoCert is a geometric AI framework that unifies forecasting, physical reasoning, and formal verification using hyperbolic manifolds to achieve accurate, physically consistent, and certifiably reliable predictions with 97.5% computational cost reduction.
AI.motivation: Existing forecasting systems address prediction accuracy, constraint enforcement for physical consistency, and formal verification for reliability separately, which limits scalability and interpretability. There's a need for a unified approach that integrates these three essential components for trustworthy scientific AI.
AI.method: GeoCert formulates forecasting as evolution along hyperbolic manifolds where negative curvature induces contraction dynamics, intrinsic robustness, and logarithmic-time certification. It uses a hierarchical constraint architecture that separates universal physical laws from domain-specific dynamics. The framework embeds verification into the geometry of learning through differentiable computation.
AI.result: GeoCert achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing computational cost by 97.5% and maintaining better certification rates. The framework enables certified generalization across multiple domains including energy, climate, finance, and transportation systems.
AI.conclusion: By unifying forecasting, physical reasoning, and formal verification within a single differentiable geometric framework, GeoCert transforms forecasting from empirical approximation to formally verified inference. It offers a scalable foundation for trustworthy, reproducible, and physically grounded scientific AI that addresses accuracy, physical consistency, and certifiable reliability simultaneously.

title: A2DEPT: Large Language Model-Driven Automated Algorithm Design via Evolutionary Program Trees
summary: Designing heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) is a fundamental yet challenging task that traditionally requires extensive domain expertise. Recently, Large Language Model (LLM)-based Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) has shown promise in autonomously generating heuristic components with minimal human intervention. However, most existing LLM-based AHD methods enforce fixed algorithmic templates to ensure executability, which confines the search to component-level tuning and limits system-level algorithmic expressiveness. To enable open-ended solver synthesis beyond rigid templates, we propose Automated Algorithm Design via Evolutionary Program Trees (A2DEPT), which treats LLMs as system-level algorithm architects. A2DEPT explores the vast program space via a tree-structured evolutionary search with hybrid selection and hierarchical operators, enabling iterative refinement of complete algorithms. To make open-ended generation practical, we enforce executability with a lightweight program-maintenance loop that performs feedback-driven repair. In experiments, A2DEPT consistently outperforms representative LLM-based baselines on both standard and highly constrained benchmarks. On the standard benchmarks, it reduces the mean normalized optimality gap by 9.8% relative to the strongest competing AHD baseline.

AI.tldr: A2DEPT enables open-ended automated algorithm design using evolutionary program trees with LLMs as system-level architects, outperforming template-constrained LLM-based methods on combinatorial optimization benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) methods rely on fixed algorithmic templates, which restricts search to component-level tuning and limits system-level algorithmic expressiveness, preventing true open-ended solver synthesis.
AI.method: A2DEPT uses LLMs as system-level algorithm architects with evolutionary program trees. It employs tree-structured evolutionary search with hybrid selection and hierarchical operators, plus a lightweight program-maintenance loop for executability through feedback-driven repair.
AI.result: A2DEPT consistently outperforms representative LLM-based baselines on both standard and highly constrained benchmarks. On standard benchmarks, it reduces the mean normalized optimality gap by 9.8% relative to the strongest competing AHD baseline.
AI.conclusion: A2DEPT enables practical open-ended algorithm design beyond rigid templates by treating LLMs as system-level architects and combining evolutionary program trees with executability enforcement, advancing automated heuristic design capabilities.

title: Supernodes and Halos: Loss-Critical Hubs in LLM Feed-Forward Layers
summary: We study the organization of channel-level importance in transformer feed-forward networks (FFNs). Using a Fisher-style loss proxy (LP) based on activation-gradient second moments, we show that loss sensitivity is concentrated in a small set of channels within each layer. In Llama-3.1-8B, the top 1% of channels per layer accounts for a median of 58.7% of LP mass, with a range of 33.0% to 86.1%. We call these loss-critical channels supernodes. Although FFN layers also contain strong activation outliers, LP-defined supernodes overlap only weakly with activation-defined outliers and are not explained by activation power or weight norms alone. Around this core, we find a weaker but consistent halo structure: some non-supernode channels share the supernodes' write support and show stronger redundancy with the protected core. We use one-shot structured FFN pruning as a diagnostic test of this organization. At 50% FFN sparsity, baselines that prune many supernodes degrade sharply, whereas our SCAR variants explicitly protect the supernode core; the strongest variant, SCAR-Prot, reaches perplexity 54.8 compared with 989.2 for Wanda-channel. The LP-concentration pattern appears across Mistral-7B, Llama-2-7B, and Qwen2-7B, remains visible in targeted Llama-3.1-70B experiments, and increases during OLMo-2-7B pretraining. These results suggest that LLM FFNs develop a small learned core of loss-critical channels, and that preserving this core is important for reliable structured pruning.

AI.tldr: Transformer FFNs contain a small set of "supernode" channels that are critical for loss, accounting for most loss sensitivity despite weak overlap with activation outliers.
AI.motivation: To understand the internal organization and importance distribution within transformer feed-forward networks, particularly how loss sensitivity is organized across channels.
AI.method: Used Fisher-style loss proxy (LP) based on activation-gradient second moments to identify loss-critical channels, conducted structured FFN pruning experiments to test importance organization, and analyzed multiple LLM architectures.
AI.result: Loss sensitivity is concentrated in top 1% of channels (supernodes), accounting for median 58.7% of LP mass. Protecting these supernodes during pruning dramatically improves performance (perplexity 54.8 vs 989.2). Pattern holds across multiple models and scales.
AI.conclusion: LLM FFNs develop a small learned core of loss-critical channels that should be preserved during structured pruning, revealing an important architectural organization principle.

title: Grounding Before Generalizing: How AI Differs from Humans in Causal Transfer
summary: Extracting abstract causal structures and applying them to novel situations is a hallmark of human intelligence. While Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong performance on a wide range of reasoning tasks, their capacity for interactive causal learning -- inducing latent structures through sequential exploration and transferring them across contexts -- remains uncharacterized. Human learners accomplish such transfer after minimal exposure, whereas classical Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents fail catastrophically. Whether state-of-the-art Artificial Intelligence (AI) models possess human-like mechanisms for abstract causal structure transfer is an open question. Using the OpenLock paradigm requiring sequential discovery of Common Cause (CC) and Common Effect (CE) structures, here we show that models exhibit fundamentally delayed or absent transfer: even successful models require initial environmental-specific mapping -- what we term environmental grounding -- before efficiency gains emerge, whereas humans leverage prior structural knowledge from the very first solution attempt. In the text-only condition, models matched or exceeded human discovery efficiency. In contrast, visual information -- in both the image-only and text-and-image conditions -- overall degraded rather than enhanced performance, revealing a broad reliance on symbolic processing rather than integrated multimodal reasoning. Models further exhibited systematic CC/CE asymmetries absent in humans, suggesting heuristic biases rather than direction-neutral causal abstraction. These findings reveal that large-scale statistical learning does not produce the decontextualized causal schemas underpinning human analogical reasoning, establishing grounding-dependent transfer as a fundamental limitation of current LLMs and VLMs.

AI.tldr: Current LLMs and VLMs fail to show human-like abstract causal structure transfer; they require environmental grounding before efficiency gains, while humans leverage structural knowledge immediately.
