Visual information degrades performance, revealing reliance on symbolic processing rather than integrated multimodal reasoning.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to investigate whether state-of-the-art AI models possess human-like mechanisms for abstract causal structure transfer. While LLMs/VLMs show strong performance on reasoning tasks, their capacity for interactive causal learning - inducing latent structures through sequential exploration and transferring them across contexts - remains uncharacterized.
AI.method: Using the OpenLock paradigm requiring sequential discovery of Common Cause (CC) and Common Effect (CE) structures, researchers tested models' ability to transfer abstract causal structures. They compared model performance in text-only, image-only, and text-and-image conditions against human performance.
AI.result: Models exhibited fundamentally delayed or absent transfer: successful models required environmental-specific mapping (environmental grounding) before efficiency gains emerged, while humans leveraged prior structural knowledge immediately. In text-only conditions, models matched/exceeded human discovery efficiency. Visual information degraded performance. Models showed systematic CC/CE asymmetries absent in humans.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale statistical learning does not produce decontextualized causal schemas underpinning human analogical reasoning. Grounding-dependent transfer represents a fundamental limitation of current LLMs and VLMs, revealing they rely on symbolic processing rather than integrated multimodal reasoning and exhibit heuristic biases rather than direction-neutral causal abstraction.

title: Do Synthetic Trajectories Reflect Real Reward Hacking? A Systematic Study on Monitoring In-the-Wild Hacking in Code Generation
summary: Reward hacking in code generation, where models exploit evaluation loopholes to obtain full reward without correctly solving the tasks, poses a critical challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL) and the deployment of reasoning models. Existing studies have been conducted primarily on synthetic hacking trajectories. However, whether these synthetic behaviors faithfully represent naturally emerging hacking in the wild remains unclear. In this work, we present a systematic analysis of the synthetic vs. in-the-wild discrepancy in reward hacking. We examine to what extent hacking behaviors induced by prompting resemble those emerging during RL training, and whether monitors trained on synthetic trajectories generalize to naturally arising but previously unseen hacking. To scale up the curation of in-the-wild reward hacking trajectories, we modified Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) by injecting conflicting unit tests as tracers and applying a "resampling-until-hack" mechanism. Through controlled comparisons between monitors trained on synthetic versus in-the-wild data, we find that (1) synthetic-data-trained monitors fail to generalize to "in-the-wild" hacking, and (2) monitors trained on our "in-the-wild" trajectories demonstrate stronger generalizability to unseen hacking types. Our results indicate that synthetic reward hacking data may not fully reflect natural reward hacking behaviors, and that relying solely on synthetic data can lead to misleading conclusions. The codebase is available at https://github.com/LichenLillc/CoTMonitoring.git

AI.tldr: Synthetic reward hacking data fails to generalize to real-world hacking behaviors, and monitors trained on in-the-wild hacking trajectories show better generalizability to unseen hacking types in code generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Reward hacking in code generation poses a critical challenge for RL and reasoning model deployment. Existing studies use synthetic hacking trajectories, but it's unclear whether these faithfully represent naturally emerging hacking behaviors in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of synthetic vs. in-the-wild discrepancy in reward hacking. Modified Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) by injecting conflicting unit tests as tracers and applying "resampling-until-hack" mechanism to curate in-the-wild reward hacking trajectories. Controlled comparisons between monitors trained on synthetic vs. in-the-wild data.
AI.result: (1) Synthetic-data-trained monitors fail to generalize to in-the-wild hacking, (2) Monitors trained on in-the-wild trajectories demonstrate stronger generalizability to unseen hacking types.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic reward hacking data may not fully reflect natural reward hacking behaviors, and relying solely on synthetic data can lead to misleading conclusions about model hacking behaviors and detection capabilities.

title: An Information-Geometric Framework for Stability Analysis of Large Language Models under Entropic Stress
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes and operational settings, evaluation strategies based solely on aggregate accuracy are often insucient to characterize system reliability. This study proposes a thermodynamic inspired modeling framework for analyzing the stability of LLM outputs under conditions of uncertainty and perturbation. The framework introduces a composite stability score that integrates task utility, entropy as a measure of external uncertainty, and two internal structural proxies: internal integration and aligned reective capacity. Rather than interpreting these quantities as physical variables, the formulation is intended as an interpretable abstraction that captures how internal structure may modulate the impact of disorder on model behavior. Using the IST-20 benchmarking protocol and associated metadata, we analyze 80 modelscenario observations across four contemporary LLMs. The proposed formulation consistently yields higher stability scores than a reduced utilityentropy baseline, with a mean improvement of 0.0299 (95% CI: 0.02470.0351). The observed gain is more pronounced under higher entropy conditions, suggesting that the framework captures a form of nonlinear attenuation of uncertainty. We do not claim a fundamental physical law or a complete theory of machine ethics. Instead, the contribution of this work is a compact and interpretable modeling perspective that connects uncertainty, performance, and internal structure within a unied evaluation lens. The framework is intended to complement existing benchmarking approaches and to support ongoing discussions in AI safety, reliability, and governance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a thermodynamic-inspired framework to evaluate LLM stability under uncertainty, integrating task utility, entropy, and internal structural proxies into a composite stability score.
AI.motivation: Traditional aggregate accuracy metrics are insufficient for evaluating LLM reliability in high-stakes operational settings, especially under conditions of uncertainty and perturbation. There's a need for more nuanced evaluation frameworks that account for how internal structure modulates response to disorder.
AI.method: The authors developed a thermodynamic-inspired modeling framework that computes a composite stability score combining: task utility (performance), entropy (external uncertainty), and two internal structural proxies - internal integration and aligned reflective capacity. They tested this framework using the IST-20 benchmarking protocol on 80 model-scenario observations across four contemporary LLMs.
AI.result: The proposed formulation consistently outperformed a reduced utility-entropy baseline, showing a mean improvement of 0.0299 (95% CI: 0.0247-0.0351). The stability gain was more pronounced under higher entropy conditions, suggesting the framework captures nonlinear attenuation of uncertainty effects.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a compact, interpretable modeling perspective that connects uncertainty, performance, and internal structure within a unified evaluation lens. It's not claimed as a fundamental physical law or complete theory, but rather as a complementary tool to existing benchmarking approaches for AI safety, reliability, and governance discussions.

title: Interpretable Physics-Informed Load Forecasting for U.S. Grid Resilience: SHAP-Guided Ensemble Validation in Hybrid Deep Learning Under Extreme Weather
summary: Accurate short-term electricity load forecasting is a cornerstone of U.S. grid reliability; however, prevailing deep learning models remain opaque, limiting operator trust during extreme weather. A unified, interpretable, physics-informed ensemble framework is proposed, integrating a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) branch for local feature extraction and a Transformer branch for long-range dependency modeling; the branches are fused through a validation-optimized weighted ensemble and regularized by a physics-informed loss derived from the piecewise parabolic temperature-demand relationship of the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) system. Post-hoc interpretability is provided through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) with the DeepExplainer backend, yielding global and event-level attributions. Using eight years of ERCOT hourly load data (2018-2025) fused with Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) records from three Texas stations, the framework achieves 713 MW MAE, 812 MW RMSE, and 1.18% MAPE on the test window. For Hampel-flagged extreme events, MAPE falls by 20.7% relative to its Transformer branch and by 40.5% relative to its CNN branch; an ablation confirms that the parabolic and ramp constraints drive a 14.7% RMSE reduction. SHAP analysis reveals a regime shift: temperature dominates under normal operation, whereas wind speed and precipitation become more influential during cold fronts and heatwaves.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed ensemble framework combining CNN and Transformer branches improves electricity load forecasting accuracy and interpretability, especially during extreme weather events.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning models for electricity load forecasting lack interpretability, limiting operator trust during extreme weather conditions, which is crucial for grid reliability.
AI.method: Proposes a unified ensemble framework with CNN branch for local features, Transformer branch for long-range dependencies, physics-informed regularization based on temperature-demand relationships, and post-hoc SHAP interpretability analysis.
AI.result: Achieves 713 MW MAE, 812 MW RMSE, and 1.18% MAPE on test data. For extreme events, MAPE improves by 20.7% vs Transformer and 40.5% vs CNN alone. Physics constraints reduce RMSE by 14.7%. SHAP reveals temperature dominates normal operation while wind/precipitation gain importance during extremes.
AI.conclusion: The physics-informed ensemble framework provides more accurate and interpretable load forecasting, enhancing operator trust during extreme weather events through explainable AI techniques.

title: The Kerimov-Alekberli Model: An Information-Geometric Framework for Real-Time System Stability
summary: This study introduces the Kerimov-Alekberli model, a novel information-geometric framework that redefines AI safety by formally linking non-equilibrium thermodynamics to stochastic control for the ethical alignment of autonomous systems. By establishing a formal isomorphism between non-equilibrium thermodynamics and stochastic control, we define systemic anomalies as deviations from a Riemannian manifold. The model utilizes the Kullback-Leibler divergence as the primary metric, governed by a dynamic threshold derived from the Fisher Information Metric. We further ground this framework in the Landauer Principle, proving that adversarial perturbations perform measurable physical work by increasing the system's informational entropy. Validation on the NSL-KDD dataset and unmanned aerial vehicle trajectory simulations demonstrated that our model achieves effective real-time detection via the FPT trigger, with strong performance metrics (e.g., high accuracy and low FPR) on benchmark datasets. This study provides a rigorous physical foundation for AI safety, transitioning from heuristic, rule-based ethical frameworks to a thermodynamics-based stability paradigm by grounding ethical violations in quantifiable physical work and entropic information.

AI.tldr: The Kerimov-Alekberli model establishes a physics-based AI safety framework using non-equilibrium thermodynamics and information geometry to detect ethical violations in autonomous systems via measurable physical work and entropy changes.
AI.motivation: Current AI safety approaches rely on heuristic, rule-based ethical frameworks without rigorous physical foundations. The paper aims to move beyond these subjective methods by grounding AI safety in quantifiable physical principles, specifically linking ethical violations to measurable physical work and entropy.
AI.method: The model creates an information-geometric framework by establishing a formal isomorphism between non-equilibrium thermodynamics and stochastic control. It defines systemic anomalies as deviations from a Riemannian manifold, uses Kullback-Leibler divergence as the primary metric with dynamic thresholds from the Fisher Information Metric, and grounds everything in the Landauer Principle to prove adversarial perturbations perform measurable physical work by increasing informational entropy.
AI.result: Validation on NSL-KDD dataset and UAV trajectory simulations shows effective real-time detection via First Passage Time (FPT) trigger, achieving strong performance metrics including high accuracy and low false positive rates on benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a rigorous physical foundation for AI safety, transitioning from heuristic ethical frameworks to a thermodynamics-based stability paradigm where ethical violations are grounded in quantifiable physical work and entropic information.

title: Autocorrelation Reintroduces Spectral Bias in KANs for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Existing theory suggests that Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) can overcome the spectral bias commonly observed in neural networks under the assumption that inputs are statistically independent. However, this assumption does not hold in time series forecasting (TSF), where inputs are lagged observations with strong temporal autocorrelation. Through theoretical analysis and empirical validation, we obtain an unexpected finding: temporal autocorrelation reintroduces spectral bias in KANs, and the bias becomes increasingly pronounced as the degree of autocorrelation increases. This suggests that standard KANs may face substantial difficulties in TSF with strongly autocorrelated inputs. To address this problem, we introduce the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) to reduce the correlations among the network inputs. As expected, experimental results reveal that DCT preprocessing substantially reduces the observed low-frequency preference in TSF. This result also corroborates that the spectral bias of KANs in TSF tasks is indeed induced by the autocorrelation among input variables.

AI.tldr: KANs suffer from spectral bias in time series forecasting due to temporal autocorrelation, which can be mitigated using DCT preprocessing to decorrelate inputs.
AI.motivation: Existing theory suggests KANs overcome spectral bias in neural networks, but this assumes independent inputs. In time series forecasting, inputs are lagged observations with strong temporal autocorrelation, which breaks this assumption. The authors investigate whether temporal autocorrelation affects KANs' performance in TSF tasks.
AI.method: 1. Theoretical analysis of KANs under autocorrelated inputs. 2. Empirical validation of spectral bias in KANs for TSF. 3. Introduction of Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) preprocessing to reduce correlations among network inputs. 4. Experimental comparison of standard KANs vs DCT-enhanced KANs.
AI.result: 1. Temporal autocorrelation reintroduces spectral bias in KANs. 2. The spectral bias becomes increasingly pronounced as the degree of autocorrelation increases. 3. DCT preprocessing substantially reduces the observed low-frequency preference in TSF. 4. This confirms that spectral bias in KANs for TSF is induced by autocorrelation among input variables.
AI.conclusion: Standard KANs face substantial difficulties in time series forecasting with strongly autocorrelated inputs due to spectral bias. DCT preprocessing effectively mitigates this bias by decorrelating inputs, making KANs more suitable for TSF tasks. The findings establish that input autocorrelation is the root cause of spectral bias in KANs for time series applications.

title: SemML 2.0: Synthesizing Controllers for LTL
summary: Synthesizing a reactive system from specifications given in linear temporal logic (LTL) is a classical problem, finding its applications in safety-critical systems design. These systems are typically represented using either Mealy machines or AIGER circuits. We present the second version of SemML, which outperforms all state-of-the-art tools for finding either solution. Aside from implementing the classical automata-theoretic approach, our tool utilizes partial exploration and machine-learning guidance for obtaining solutions efficiently, and numerous heuristics and improvements of classic algorithms for extracting small representations of these solutions. We evaluate our tool against the existing state-of-the-art tools (in particular Strix, LtlSynt, and the previous version of SemML) on the dataset of the synthesis competition SYNTCOMP. We show that we solve significantly more instances and do so much faster than other tools, while maintaining state-of-the-art solution quality.

AI.tldr: SemML v2 outperforms all state-of-the-art LTL synthesis tools (including Strix, LtlSynt, and previous SemML) by solving more instances faster while maintaining high solution quality, using partial exploration and machine-learning guidance.
AI.motivation: LTL synthesis for reactive systems is crucial for safety-critical systems design, but existing tools have limitations in efficiency and scalability. There's a need for improved synthesis tools that can handle more instances faster while producing high-quality solutions.
AI.method: Implements classical automata-theoretic approach combined with partial exploration and machine-learning guidance for efficient solution finding. Uses numerous heuristics and improvements of classic algorithms for extracting small solution representations (Mealy machines or AIGER circuits).
AI.result: SemML v2 solves significantly more instances than all state-of-the-art tools (Strix, LtlSynt, previous SemML) and does so much faster, while maintaining state-of-the-art solution quality as evaluated on SYNTCOMP competition dataset.
AI.conclusion: SemML v2 represents a significant advancement in LTL synthesis technology, outperforming all existing tools through innovative combination of classical approaches with machine learning and heuristic improvements.

title: When PINNs Go Wrong: Pseudo-Time Stepping Against Spurious Solutions
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide a promising machine learning framework for solving partial differential equations, but their training often breaks down on challenging problems, sometimes converging to physically incorrect solutions despite achieving small residual losses. This failure, we argue, is not merely an optimization difficulty. Rather, it reflects a fundamental weakness of the empirical PDE residual loss, which can admit trivial or spurious solutions during training. From this perspective, we revisit pseudo-time stepping, a technique that has recently shown strong empirical success in PINNs. We show that its main benefit is not simply to ease optimization; instead, when combined with collocation-point resampling, it helps reveal and avoid spurious solutions. At the same time, we find that the effectiveness of pseudo-time stepping depends critically on the choice of step size, which cannot be tuned reliably from the training loss alone. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive pseudo-time stepping strategy that selects the step size from a finite-difference surrogate of the local residual Jacobian, yielding the largest step permitted by local stability without per-problem tuning. Across a diverse set of PDE benchmarks, the proposed method consistently improves both accuracy and robustness. Together, these findings provide a clearer understanding of why PINNs fail and suggest a practical pathway toward more reliable physics-informed learning. All code and data accompanying this manuscript are available at https://github.com/sifanexisted/jaxpi2.

AI.tldr: PINNs often fail due to spurious solutions despite small residual losses; pseudo-time stepping with collocation resampling helps avoid these, but requires careful step size tuning; an adaptive strategy using residual Jacobian improves reliability.
AI.motivation: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) frequently fail on challenging PDE problems, sometimes converging to physically incorrect solutions despite achieving small residual losses. This failure is not just an optimization issue but reflects a fundamental weakness of empirical PDE residual loss that can admit trivial or spurious solutions.
AI.method: The paper revisits pseudo-time stepping with collocation-point resampling, showing it helps reveal and avoid spurious solutions. To address the critical dependency on step size tuning, the authors propose an adaptive pseudo-time stepping strategy that selects step size from a finite-difference surrogate of the local residual Jacobian, yielding the largest step permitted by local stability without per-problem tuning.
AI.result: The adaptive pseudo-time stepping method consistently improves both accuracy and robustness across a diverse set of PDE benchmarks. The approach provides a practical way to make PINNs more reliable while avoiding spurious solutions.
AI.conclusion: The failure of PINNs stems from fundamental weaknesses in empirical PDE residual loss rather than mere optimization difficulties. Pseudo-time stepping with collocation resampling helps avoid spurious solutions, and adaptive step size selection based on residual Jacobian enhances reliability, providing a clearer understanding of PINN failures and a pathway toward more robust physics-informed learning.

title: An Analysis of the Coordination Gap between Joint and Modular Learning for Job Shop Scheduling with Transportation Resources
comment: Supported by the Chips Joint Undertaking and its members, including top-up funding by National Authorities, within the Cynergy4MIE project (Grant Agreement No. 101140226). This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Efficient job-shop scheduling with transportation resources is critical for high-performance manufacturing. With the rise of "decentralized factories", multi-agent reinforcement learning has emerged as a promising approach for the combined scheduling of production and transportation tasks. Prior work has largely focused on developing novel cooperative architectures while overlooking the question of when joint training is necessary. Joint training denotes the simultaneous training of job and automatic guided vehicle scheduling agents, whereas modular training involves independently training each agent followed by post-hoc integration. In this study, we systematically investigate the conditions under which joint training is essential for optimal performance in the job-shop scheduling problem with transportation resources. Through a rigorous sensitivity analysis of resource scarcity and temporal dominance, we quantify the coordination gap -- the performance difference between these two training modalities. In our evaluation, the joint training can produce superior performance compared to the best-performing combinations of dispatching rules and modular training. However, the coordination gap advantage diminishes in bottleneck environments, particularly under severe transport and processing constraints. These findings indicate that modular training represents a viable alternative in environments where a single scheduling task dominates. Overall, our work provides practical guidance for selecting between training modalities based on environmental conditions, enabling decision-makers to optimize reinforcement learning-based scheduling performance.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent RL can solve combined production+transport scheduling, but joint vs modular training choice depends on resource scarcity and bottleneck conditions, not always needing joint training.
AI.motivation: Previous research focused on cooperative architectures but ignored when joint training (simultaneous training of job and AGV agents) is necessary versus modular training (independent training then integration). Need systematic investigation of conditions requiring joint training for optimal performance.
AI.method: Systematically investigated conditions for joint training necessity using sensitivity analysis of resource scarcity and temporal dominance. Quantified "coordination gap" - performance difference between joint and modular training modalities.
AI.result: Joint training produces superior performance compared to best dispatching rules and modular training, but coordination gap advantage diminishes in bottleneck environments with severe transport/processing constraints. Modular training is viable when single scheduling task dominates.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical guidance for selecting training modalities based on environmental conditions. Decision-makers can optimize RL-based scheduling performance by choosing joint training for balanced coordination needs or modular training when one task dominates in bottleneck environments.

title: On the Memorization of Consistency Distillation for Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models are central to modern generative modeling, and understanding how they balance memorization and generalization is critical for reliable deployment. Recent work has shown that memorization in diffusion models is shaped by training dynamics, with generalization and memorization emerging at different stages of training. However, deployed diffusion models are often further distilled, introducing an additional training phase whose impact on memorization is not well understood. In this work, we analyze how distillation reshapes memorization behavior in diffusion models, taking consistency distillation as a representative framework. Empirically, we show that when applied to a teacher model that has memorized data, consistency distillation significantly reduces transferred memorization in the student while preserving, and sometimes improving, sample quality. To explain this behavior, we provide a theoretical analysis using a random feature neural network model [Bonnaire et al., 2025], showing that consistency distillation suppresses unstable feature directions associated with memorization while preserving stable, generalizable modes. Our findings suggest that distillation can serve not only as an acceleration tool, but also as a mechanism for improving the memorization-generalization trade-off.

AI.tldr: Consistency distillation reduces data memorization in diffusion models while maintaining or improving sample quality, serving as both an acceleration tool and a mechanism to improve the memorization-generalization trade-off.
AI.motivation: While diffusion models are widely deployed and often undergo additional distillation after training, the impact of this distillation phase on memorization behavior is not well understood. Understanding how distillation reshapes the memorization-generalization balance is critical for reliable deployment of diffusion models.
AI.method: The study uses consistency distillation as a representative framework and employs empirical analysis on teacher models that have memorized data, combined with theoretical analysis using a random feature neural network model to explain the observed behavior.
AI.result: Consistency distillation significantly reduces transferred memorization from teacher to student models while preserving or sometimes improving sample quality. Theoretical analysis shows distillation suppresses unstable feature directions associated with memorization while preserving stable, generalizable modes.
AI.conclusion: Distillation can serve not only as an acceleration tool for diffusion models but also as a mechanism for improving the memorization-generalization trade-off, potentially enhancing reliability for deployment.

title: Right-to-Act: A Pre-Execution Non-Compensatory Decision Protocol for AI Systems
comment: 14 pages, 3 figures. Introduces a pre-execution decision protocol for AI systems
summary: Current AI systems increasingly operate in contexts where their outputs directly trigger real-world actions. Most existing approaches to AI safety, risk management, and governance focus on post-hoc validation, probabilistic risk estimation, or certification of model behavior. However, these approaches implicitly assume that once a decision is produced, it is eligible for execution. In this work, we introduce the Right-to-Act protocol, a deterministic, non-compensatory pre-execution decision layer that evaluates whether an AI-generated decision is permitted to be realized at all. Unlike compensatory systems, where high-confidence signals can override failed conditions, the proposed framework enforces strict structural constraints: if any required condition is unmet, execution is halted or deferred. We formalize the distinction between compensatory and non-compensatory decision regimes and define a pre-execution legitimacy boundary. Through a scenario-based case study, we demonstrate how identical AI outputs can lead to divergent outcomes when evaluated under a Right-to-Act protocol, preserving reversibility and preventing premature or irreversible actions. The proposed approach reframes AI control from optimizing decisions to governing their admissibility, introducing a protocol-level abstraction that operates independently of model architecture or training methodology.

AI.tldr: A Right-to-Act protocol is introduced as a deterministic, non-compensatory pre-execution decision layer that evaluates whether AI-generated decisions are permitted to be realized, enforcing strict structural constraints where any unmet condition halts execution.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems operate in contexts where outputs directly trigger real-world actions, but existing safety approaches focus on post-hoc validation, probabilistic risk estimation, or certification, implicitly assuming decisions are eligible for execution once produced. There's a need for pre-execution control to prevent premature or irreversible actions.
AI.method: Introduces the Right-to-Act protocol as a deterministic, non-compensatory decision layer that evaluates AI-generated decisions before execution. Formalizes the distinction between compensatory and non-compensatory decision regimes and defines a pre-execution legitimacy boundary. Uses scenario-based case studies to demonstrate how identical AI outputs lead to different outcomes under this protocol.
AI.result: The Right-to-Act protocol preserves reversibility and prevents premature or irreversible actions by creating a protocol-level abstraction that operates independently of model architecture or training methodology. It demonstrates how identical AI outputs can lead to divergent outcomes when evaluated under this framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach reframes AI control from optimizing decisions to governing their admissibility, introducing a protocol-level abstraction for pre-execution evaluation that operates independently of model specifics, offering a more robust safety mechanism than existing post-hoc approaches.

title: CAPSULE: Control-Theoretic Action Perturbations for Safe Uncertainty-Aware Reinforcement Learning
summary: Ensuring safe exploration in high-dimensional systems with unknown dynamics remains a significant challenge. Existing safe reinforcement learning methods often provide safety guarantees only in expectation, which can still lead to safety violations. Control-theoretic approaches, in contrast, offer hard constraint-based safety guarantees but typically assume access to known system dynamics or require accurate estimation of control-affine models. In this paper, we propose a safe reinforcement learning framework that learns a probabilistic control-affine dynamics model in an offline setting. The learned model is leveraged to explicitly construct control barrier functions (CBFs) that incorporate model uncertainty to provide conservative safety constraints. These CBF constraints are enforced through an online constraint-based action correction mechanism, enabling safe exploration without overly restricting task performance. Empirical evaluations on nonlinear, complex continuous-control benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves returns comparable to those of existing baselines while significantly reducing safety violations.

AI.tldr: A safe RL framework that learns control-affine dynamics offline, constructs probabilistic CBFs with uncertainty, and enforces safety constraints online to reduce violations while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Existing safe RL methods only provide safety guarantees in expectation, still allowing violations, while control-theoretic approaches require known dynamics or accurate control-affine models, creating a gap for practical safe exploration in high-dimensional systems with unknown dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes a two-phase approach: 1) Offline learning of a probabilistic control-affine dynamics model, 2) Online use of the learned model to explicitly construct control barrier functions (CBFs) that incorporate model uncertainty for conservative safety constraints, enforced through constraint-based action correction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on nonlinear, complex continuous-control benchmarks show the approach achieves returns comparable to existing baselines while significantly reducing safety violations.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully bridges the gap between RL and control theory by providing hard constraint-based safety guarantees through learned probabilistic models, enabling safer exploration in high-dimensional systems with unknown dynamics without compromising task performance.

title: Multi-Dimensional Evaluation of Sustainable City Trips with LLM-as-a-Judge and Human-in-the-Loop
summary: Evaluating nuanced conversational travel recommendations is challenging when human annotations are costly and standard metrics ignore stakeholder-centric goals. We study LLMs-as-Judges for sustainable city-trip lists across four dimensions -- relevance, diversity, sustainability, and popularity balance, and propose a three-phase calibration framework: (1) baseline judging with multiple LLMs, (2) expert evaluation to identify systematic misalignment, and (3) dimension-specific calibration via rules and few-shot examples. Across two recommendation settings, we observe model-specific biases and high dimension-level variance, even when judges agree on overall rankings. Calibration clarifies reasoning per dimension but exposes divergent interpretations of sustainability, highlighting the need for transparent, bias-aware LLM evaluation. Prompts and code are released for reproducibility: https://github.com/ashmibanerjee/trs-llm-calibration.

AI.tldr: A framework for calibrating LLMs-as-judges in evaluating sustainable travel recommendations, addressing biases and improving dimension-specific evaluation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Evaluating nuanced conversational travel recommendations is challenging due to costly human annotations and standard metrics that ignore stakeholder-centric goals. There's a need for better LLM-based evaluation methods for multi-dimensional recommendation assessment.
AI.method: Proposes a three-phase calibration framework: (1) baseline judging with multiple LLMs, (2) expert evaluation to identify systematic misalignment, and (3) dimension-specific calibration via rules and few-shot examples, applied to sustainable city-trip recommendations across four dimensions.
AI.result: Found model-specific biases and high dimension-level variance even when judges agree on overall rankings. Calibration clarifies reasoning per dimension but exposes divergent interpretations of sustainability. The framework improves evaluation transparency but reveals inherent challenges.
AI.conclusion: Highlights the need for transparent, bias-aware LLM evaluation in recommendation systems, showing that calibration helps but also reveals fundamental disagreements about concepts like sustainability. Provides open-source prompts and code for reproducibility.

title: Hamiltonian Graph Inference Networks: Joint structure discovery and dynamics prediction for lattice Hamiltonian systems from trajectory data
summary: Lattice Hamiltonian systems underpin models across condensed matter, nonlinear optics, and biophysics, yet learning their dynamics from data is obstructed by two unknowns: the interaction topology and whether node dynamics are homogeneous. Existing graph-based approaches either assume the graph is given or, as in $α$-separable graph Hamiltonian network, infer it only for separable Hamiltonians with homogeneous node dynamics. We introduce the Hamiltonian Graph Inference Network (HGIN), which jointly recovers the interaction graph and predicts long-time trajectories from state data alone, for both separable and non-separable Hamiltonians and under heterogeneous node dynamics. HGIN couples a structure-learning module -- a learnable weighted adjacency matrix trained under a Hamilton's-equations loss -- with a trajectory-prediction module that partitions edges into physically distinct subgraphs via $k$-means clustering, assigning each subgraph its own encoder and thereby breaking the parameter-sharing bottleneck of conventional GNNs. On three benchmarks -- a Klein--Gordon lattice with long-range interactions and two discrete nonlinear Schrödinger lattices (homogeneous and heterogeneous) -- HGIN reduces long-time energy prediction error and trajectory prediction error by six to thirteen orders of magnitude relative to baselines. A symmetry argument on the Hamiltonian loss further shows that the learned weights encode the parity of the underlying pair potential, yielding an interpretable readout of the system's interaction structure.

AI.tldr: HGIN is a neural network that simultaneously infers interaction graphs and predicts long-term dynamics from state data alone, handling both separable/non-separable Hamiltonians and heterogeneous node dynamics without prior knowledge of topology.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for learning lattice Hamiltonian dynamics have limitations: they either assume the interaction topology is known or can only infer it for simple cases (separable Hamiltonians with homogeneous node dynamics). There's a need for a method that can handle more complex real-world scenarios.
AI.method: HGIN combines a structure-learning module (learnable weighted adjacency matrix trained with Hamilton's equations loss) with a trajectory-prediction module that partitions edges into physically distinct subgraphs via k-means clustering. Each subgraph gets its own encoder, breaking the parameter-sharing bottleneck of traditional GNNs.
AI.result: On three benchmarks (Klein-Gordon lattice with long-range interactions, homogeneous and heterogeneous discrete nonlinear Schrödinger lattices), HGIN reduces long-time energy prediction error and trajectory prediction error by 6-13 orders of magnitude compared to baselines. The learned weights also encode the parity of underlying pair potentials.
AI.conclusion: HGIN successfully addresses the dual challenge of unknown interaction topology and heterogeneous node dynamics in Hamiltonian systems, providing both accurate long-term predictions and interpretable insights into the system's physical structure through symmetry analysis.

title: Credal Concept Bottleneck Models for Epistemic-Aleatoric Uncertainty Decomposition
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) predict through human-interpretable concepts, but they typically output point concept probabilities that conflate epistemic uncertainty (reducible model underspecification) with aleatoric uncertainty (irreducible input ambiguity). This makes concept-level uncertainty hard to interpret and, more importantly, hard to act upon. We introduce CREDENCE (Credal Ensemble Concept Estimation), a CBM framework that decomposes concept uncertainty by construction. CREDENCE represents each concept as a credal prediction (a probability interval), derives epistemic uncertainty from disagreement across diverse concept heads, and estimates aleatoric uncertainty via a dedicated ambiguity output trained to match annotator disagreement when available. The resulting signals support prescriptive decisions: automate low-uncertainty cases, prioritize data collection for high-epistemic cases, route high-aleatoric cases to human review, and abstain when both are high. Across several tasks, we show that epistemic uncertainty is positively associated with prediction errors, whereas aleatoric uncertainty closely tracks annotator disagreement, providing guidance beyond error correlation. Our implementation is available at the following link: https://github.com/Tankiit/Credal_Sets/tree/ensemble-credal-cbm

AI.tldr: CREDENCE introduces a CBM framework that decomposes concept uncertainty into epistemic and aleatoric components using credal predictions (probability intervals) and dedicated ambiguity outputs, enabling prescriptive decision-making for automated systems.
AI.motivation: Current Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) output point concept probabilities that conflate epistemic uncertainty (model underspecification) with aleatoric uncertainty (input ambiguity), making concept-level uncertainty hard to interpret and act upon for decision-making.
AI.method: CREDENCE represents each concept as a credal prediction (probability interval), derives epistemic uncertainty from disagreement across diverse concept heads, and estimates aleatoric uncertainty via a dedicated ambiguity output trained to match annotator disagreement when available.
AI.result: The framework shows that epistemic uncertainty is positively associated with prediction errors, while aleatoric uncertainty closely tracks annotator disagreement, providing actionable guidance beyond mere error correlation for decision-making.
AI.conclusion: CREDENCE successfully decomposes concept uncertainty in CBMs, enabling prescriptive decisions: automating low-uncertainty cases, prioritizing data collection for high-epistemic cases, routing high-aleatoric cases to human review, and abstaining when both uncertainties are high.

title: Rank, Head-Channel Non-Identifiability, and Symmetry Breaking: A Precise Analysis of Representational Collapse in Transformers
comment: 36 pages, 8 figures, 1 table. Submitted to Artificial Intelligence (Elsevier)
summary:
A widely cited result by Dong et al. (2021) showed that Transformers built from self-attention alone, without skip connections or feed-forward layers, suffer from rapid rank collapse: all token representations converge to a single direction. The proposed remedy was the MLP. We show that this picture, while correct in the regime studied by Dong, is incomplete in ways that matter for architectural understanding.
  Three results are established. First, layer normalisation is precisely affine-rank-neutral: it preserves the affine rank of the token representation set exactly. The widespread claim that LN "plays no role" is imprecise; the correct statement is sharper. Second, residual connections generically obstruct rank collapse in real Transformers such as BERT-base, in a measure-theoretic sense, without contribution from the MLP. The MLP's irreplaceable function is different: generating feature directions outside the linear span of the original token embeddings, which no stack of attention layers can produce. Third, a phenomenon distinct from rank collapse is identified: head-channel non-identifiability. After multi-head attention sums per-head outputs through the output projection, individual contributions cannot be canonically attributed to a specific head; n(H-1)d_k degrees of freedom per layer remain ambiguous when recovering a single head from the mixed signal. The MLP cannot remedy this because it acts on the post-summation signal.
  A constructive partial remedy is proposed: a position-gated output projection (PG-OP) at parameter overhead below 1.6% of the standard output projection. The four collapse phenomena identified in the literature -- rank collapse in depth, in width, head-channel non-identifiability, and entropy collapse -- are unified under a symmetry-breaking framework, each corresponding to a distinct symmetry of the Transformer's forward pass.

AI.tldr: The paper re-examines rank collapse in Transformers, showing that while attention-only architectures suffer from rank collapse, layer normalization preserves affine rank exactly, residual connections prevent collapse, and MLPs create new feature directions. It identifies head-channel non-identifiability as a distinct problem and proposes a position-gated output projection solution.
AI.motivation: To provide a more complete understanding of architectural collapse phenomena in Transformers beyond Dong et al.'s findings, addressing the precise roles of layer normalization, residual connections, and MLPs, and identifying head-channel non-identifiability as a distinct collapse phenomenon.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of Transformer components: establishing mathematical properties of layer normalization (affine-rank-neutral), analyzing residual connections' role in preventing rank collapse, examining MLP function in creating new feature directions, and identifying head-channel non-identifiability in multi-head attention. Proposes a position-gated output projection (PG-OP) as a partial remedy.
AI.result: Three key results: 1) Layer normalization exactly preserves affine rank of token representations, 2) Residual connections prevent rank collapse without MLP contribution, while MLPs generate new feature directions, 3) Head-channel non-identifiability is identified as a distinct collapse phenomenon where individual head contributions cannot be recovered from the mixed signal. Proposed PG-OP addresses this with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.conclusion: Transformer collapse phenomena (rank collapse in depth/width, head-channel non-identifiability, entropy collapse) can be unified under a symmetry-breaking framework. Understanding these distinct roles of architectural components provides deeper insight into Transformer design, with practical implications for addressing identifiability issues through the proposed PG-OP modification.

title: Explanation Quality Assessment as Ranking with Listwise Rewards
summary: We reformulate explanation quality assessment as a ranking problem rather than a generation problem. Instead of optimizing models to produce a single "best" explanation token-by-token, we train reward models to discriminate among multiple candidate explanations and learn their relative quality. Concretely, we construct per-instance candidate sets with graded quality levels and train listwise and pairwise ranking models (ListNet, LambdaRank, RankNet) to preserve ordinal structure and avoid score compression typical of pointwise regression or binary preference objectives. We observe three findings: First, ranking losses consistently outperform regression on score separation across all domains tested. Second, the optimal ranking loss depends on data characteristics: listwise objectives excel with well-separated quality tiers, while pairwise methods are more robust to noisy natural annotations. Third, when trained on carefully curated and well-structured data, small encoder models can match models that are orders of magnitude larger, suggesting that data quality matters more than model scale. Finally, when used as rewards in policy optimization, ranking-based scores enable stable convergence in settings where regression-based rewards fail entirely. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/Tankiit/PPO_Learning_to_rank

AI.tldr: This paper reformulates explanation quality assessment as a ranking problem instead of generation, training reward models to discriminate among multiple candidate explanations using ranking losses (ListNet, LambdaRank, RankNet) rather than regression approaches.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve explanation quality assessment by shifting from generative approaches that produce a single "best" explanation to ranking-based approaches that can discriminate relative quality among multiple candidates, addressing issues like score compression in traditional regression or binary preference methods.
AI.method: The method involves constructing per-instance candidate sets with graded quality levels and training listwise (ListNet) and pairwise (LambdaRank, RankNet) ranking models to preserve ordinal structure. This approach contrasts with pointwise regression or binary preference objectives that typically suffer from score compression.
AI.result: Key results: 1) Ranking losses consistently outperform regression on score separation across all domains; 2) Optimal ranking loss depends on data characteristics - listwise objectives excel with well-separated quality tiers, while pairwise methods are more robust to noisy natural annotations; 3) Small encoder models can match much larger models when trained on carefully curated data; 4) Ranking-based scores enable stable convergence in policy optimization where regression-based rewards fail.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that reformulating explanation quality assessment as a ranking problem yields significant benefits over regression approaches, with data quality being more important than model scale, and ranking-based rewards enabling stable policy optimization convergence where traditional methods fail.

title: Can an MLP Absorb Its Own Skip Connection?
summary: We study when a skip connection around a single-hidden-layer MLP can be absorbed into a residual-free MLP of the same width. We first show that for any architecture whose skip branch is an invertible linear map (including Hyper-Connections and their manifold-constrained variants), the problem reduces to the identity skip case. For homogeneous activations of degree $k \neq 1$, such as ReLU$^2$ and ReGLU, absorption is unconditionally impossible by a degree argument. For gated activations whose gate is differentiable at the origin with $g(0) = 0$, including SwiGLU and GeGLU, a linearization argument gives the same conclusion. These impossibility results extend to arbitrary depth: a composition of $L$ residual blocks using such activations cannot be replicated by any composition of $L$ residual-free blocks of the same width. For ungated ReLU and GELU, the situation is richer. For generic weight matrices, absorption holds at the single-block level if and only if there exists an index set $S$ of size at least $d$ such that $W_{\mathrm{down}}[:,S]\,W_{\mathrm{up}}[S,:] = -I_d$. This condition is non-generic (it fails with probability one under continuous weight distributions), so skip-connected and residual-free MLPs of the same width represent generically disjoint function classes. Whether this disjointness persists for deep compositions of ReLU or GELU blocks remains open.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when skip connections in single-hidden-layer MLPs can be absorbed into residual-free MLPs of the same width, finding that for many activations (ReLU², ReGLU, SwiGLU, GeGLU) absorption is impossible, while for ReLU/GELU it's possible only under non-generic weight conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental differences between skip-connected and residual-free neural architectures, specifically investigating whether skip connections can be mathematically absorbed into equivalent residual-free networks of the same width, which has implications for network expressivity and function representation.
AI.method: The study uses mathematical analysis techniques including: 1) Reduction of various skip connections to identity skip cases via invertible linear maps, 2) Degree arguments for homogeneous activations of degree k ≠ 1, 3) Linearization arguments for gated activations with specific properties, 4) Algebraic conditions analysis for ReLU and GELU activations with weight matrix constraints.
AI.result: For homogeneous activations like ReLU² and ReGLU (degree k ≠ 1), absorption is unconditionally impossible. For gated activations like SwiGLU and GeGLU (with g(0)=0), absorption is also impossible. For ReLU and GELU, absorption at single-block level requires a specific non-generic weight condition: ∃ index set S of size ≥ d such that W_down[:,S]W_up[S,:] = -I_d, which fails with probability one under continuous distributions, making skip-connected and residual-free MLPs represent generically disjoint function classes.
AI.conclusion: Skip connections in single-hidden-layer MLPs generally cannot be absorbed into residual-free architectures of the same width, creating fundamentally different function classes. For most activations (ReLU², ReGLU, SwiGLU, GeGLU) absorption is impossible, while for ReLU/GELU it requires non-generic weight conditions, leaving deep composition questions open for ReLU/GELU blocks.

title: Adaptive ToR: Complexity-Aware Tree-Based Retrieval for Pareto-Optimal Multi-Intent NLU
summary: Multi-intent natural language understanding requires retrieval systems that simultaneously achieve high accuracy and computational efficiency, yet existing approaches apply either uniform single-step retrieval that compromises recall or fixed-depth hierarchical decomposition that introduces excessive latency regardless of query complexity. This paper proposes Adaptive Tree-of-Retrieval (Adaptive ToR), a complexity-aware retrieval architecture that dynamically configures retrieval topology based on query characteristics. The system integrates four components: (1) a Query Tree Classifier computing a Query Complexity Index from weighted linguistic signals to route queries to either a rapid single-step path or an adaptive-depth hierarchical path; (2) a Tree-Based Retrieval module that recursively decomposes complex queries into focused sub-queries calibrated to predicted complexity; (3) an Adaptive Pruning Module employing two-stage filtering combining quantitative similarity gating with semantic relevance evaluation to suppress exponential node growth; and (4) a Retrieval Reranking Layer featuring a deduplicator-first pipeline and global LLM rescoring for production efficiency. Evaluation on the NLU++ benchmark (2,693 multi-intent queries across Banking and Hotel domains) yields 29.07% Subset Accuracy and 71.79% Micro-F1, a 9.7% relative improvement over fixed-depth baselines, while reducing latency by 37.6%, LLM invocations by 43.0%, and token consumption by 9.8%. Depth-wise analysis reveals that 26.92% of queries resolve within three seconds (2.45s mean latency) via single-step routing (d=0: 37.9% Subset Accuracy, 74.8% Micro-F1), while token consumption scales by 4.9x across depths, validating complexity-aware resource allocation and establishing Pareto-optimal balance across accuracy, latency, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Tree-of-Retrieval is a complexity-aware architecture that dynamically routes queries between single-step and hierarchical retrieval paths based on query complexity, achieving better accuracy and computational efficiency than fixed approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-intent retrieval systems face a trade-off: uniform single-step retrieval sacrifices recall, while fixed-depth hierarchical decomposition introduces excessive latency regardless of query complexity. There's a need for a system that adapts retrieval strategy to query characteristics.
AI.method: Proposes Adaptive ToR with four components: 1) Query Tree Classifier computes Query Complexity Index to route queries; 2) Tree-Based Retrieval recursively decomposes complex queries; 3) Adaptive Pruning Module uses two-stage filtering to control node growth; 4) Retrieval Reranking Layer with deduplicator-first pipeline and LLM rescoring.
AI.result: On NLU++ benchmark (2,693 queries across Banking/Hotel domains): 29.07% Subset Accuracy and 71.79% Micro-F1 (9.7% relative improvement over baselines). Reduces latency by 37.6%, LLM invocations by 43.0%, token consumption by 9.8%. 26.92% of queries resolve in ≤3 seconds via single-step routing.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive ToR establishes Pareto-optimal balance across accuracy, latency, and computational efficiency by dynamically adapting retrieval topology to query complexity, validating complexity-aware resource allocation for multi-intent natural language understanding systems.

title: OptProver: Bridging Olympiad and Optimization through Continual Training in Formal Theorem Proving
summary: Recent advances in formal theorem proving have focused on Olympiad-level mathematics, leaving undergraduate domains largely unexplored. Optimization, fundamental to machine learning, operations research, and scientific computing, remains underserved by existing provers. Its reliance on domain-specific formalisms (convexity, optimality conditions, and algorithmic analysis) creates significant distribution shift, making naive domain transfer ineffective. We present OptProver, a trained model that achieves robust transfer from Olympiad to undergraduate optimization. Starting from a strong Olympiad-level prover, our pipeline mitigates distribution shift through two key innovations. First, we employ large-scale optimization-focused data curation via expert iteration. Second, we introduce a specialized preference learning objective that integrates perplexity-weighted optimization with a mechanism to penalize valid but non-progressing proof steps. This not only addresses distribution shifts but also guides the search toward efficient trajectories. To enable rigorous evaluation, we construct a novel benchmark in Lean 4 focused on optimization. On this benchmark, OptProver achieves state-of-the-art Pass@1 and Pass@32 among comparably sized models while maintaining competitive performance on general theorem-proving tasks, demonstrating effective domain transfer without catastrophic forgetting.

AI.tldr: OptProver is a theorem prover that successfully transfers from Olympiad-level math to undergraduate optimization problems through expert iteration data curation and specialized preference learning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on a new optimization benchmark.
AI.motivation: Formal theorem proving has focused on Olympiad-level mathematics while neglecting undergraduate domains like optimization, which is crucial for machine learning, operations research, and scientific computing. Existing provers struggle with the distribution shift caused by optimization's specialized formalisms (convexity, optimality conditions, algorithmic analysis).
AI.method: Starting from a strong Olympiad-level prover, the approach uses: 1) Large-scale optimization-focused data curation via expert iteration to mitigate distribution shift, and 2) A specialized preference learning objective that combines perplexity-weighted optimization with a penalty mechanism for valid but non-progressing proof steps to guide search toward efficient trajectories.
AI.result: OptProver achieves state-of-the-art Pass@1 and Pass@32 performance on a novel Lean 4 optimization benchmark among comparably sized models, while maintaining competitive performance on general theorem-proving tasks, demonstrating effective domain transfer without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: The OptProver model successfully bridges the gap between Olympiad-level theorem proving and undergraduate optimization, showing that with appropriate data curation and specialized training objectives, robust domain transfer is achievable without sacrificing general theorem-proving capabilities.

title: Generative Design of a Gas Turbine Combustor Using Invertible Neural Networks
summary: The need to burn 100% H2 in high efficient gas turbines featuring low NOx combustion in premix mode require the complete redesign of the combustion system to ensure stable operation without any flashback. Since all engine frames featuring a power range from 4 MW up to 600 MW are affected, a huge design effort is expected. To reduce this effort, especially to transfer knowledge between the different engine classes, generative design methods using latest AI technology will provide promising potential. In this work, this challenge is approached utilizing the current advances in generative artificial intelligence. We train an Invertible Neural Network (INN) on an expandable database of geometrically parameterized combustor designs with simulated performance labels. Utilizing the INN in its inverse direction, multiple design proposals are generated which fulfill specified performance labels.

AI.tldr: Using invertible neural networks to generate hydrogen gas turbine combustor designs that meet performance targets, reducing redesign effort for H2 combustion systems.
AI.motivation: The transition to 100% hydrogen combustion in gas turbines requires complete redesign of combustion systems for stable operation without flashback. This affects all engine frames from 4 MW to 600 MW, creating a huge design effort. Generative AI methods offer potential to reduce this effort and transfer knowledge between engine classes.
AI.method: Train an Invertible Neural Network (INN) on an expandable database of geometrically parameterized combustor designs with simulated performance labels. Use the INN in its inverse direction to generate multiple design proposals that fulfill specified performance targets.
AI.result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract, but the approach enables generation of design proposals meeting performance specifications through inverse modeling using INNs on parameterized combustor designs.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI methods, particularly invertible neural networks, show promising potential for reducing the design effort in transitioning gas turbines to 100% hydrogen combustion by enabling knowledge transfer between engine classes and generating designs that meet performance targets.

title: Quasi-Equivariant Metanetworks
summary: Metanetworks are neural architectures designed to operate directly on pretrained weights to perform downstream tasks. However, the parameter space serves only as a proxy for the underlying function class, and the parameter-function mapping is inherently non-injective: distinct parameter configurations may yield identical input-output behaviors. As a result, metanetworks that rely solely on raw parameters risk overlooking the intrinsic symmetries of the architecture. Reasoning about functional identity is therefore essential for effective metanetwork design, motivating the development of equivariant metanetworks, which incorporate equivariance principles to respect architectural symmetries. Existing approaches, however, typically enforce strict equivariance, which imposes rigid constraints and often leads to sparse and less expressive models. To address this limitation, we introduce the novel concept of quasi-equivariance, which allows metanetworks to move beyond the rigidity of strict equivariance while still preserving functional identity. We lay down a principled basis for this framework and demonstrate its broad applicability across diverse neural architectures, including feedforward, convolutional, and transformer networks. Through empirical evaluation, we show that quasi-equivariant metanetworks achieve good trade-offs between symmetry preservation and representational expressivity. These findings advance the theoretical understanding of weight-space learning and provide a principled foundation for the design of more expressive and functionally robust metanetworks.

AI.tldr: Quasi-equivariant metanetworks: A framework that moves beyond strict equivariance constraints while preserving functional identity in weight-space learning, achieving better expressivity-symmetry trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Metanetworks operating on pretrained weights overlook architectural symmetries due to the non-injective parameter-function mapping. Strict equivariance constraints limit expressivity, creating a need for more flexible approaches.
AI.method: Introduces quasi-equivariance framework that relaxes strict equivariance constraints while preserving functional identity. Provides principled basis applicable to various neural architectures (feedforward, convolutional, transformer networks).
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows quasi-equivariant metanetworks achieve good trade-offs between symmetry preservation and representational expressivity across diverse architectures.
AI.conclusion: Advances theoretical understanding of weight-space learning and provides principled foundation for designing more expressive, functionally robust metanetworks through quasi-equivariance.

title: Certified geometric robustness -- Super-DeepG
comment: ICCPS / HSCC 2026, CPS IoT Week, May 2026, Saint Malo (Palais du Grand Large), France
summary: Safety-critical applications are required to perform as expected in normal operations. Image processing functions are often required to be insensitive to small geometric perturbations such as rotation, scaling, shearing or translation. This paper addresses the formal verification of neural networks against geometric perturbations on their image dataset. Our method Super-DeepG improves the reasoning used in linear relaxation techniques and Lipschitz optimization, and provides an implementation that leverages GPU hardware. By doing so, Super-DeepG achieves both precision and computational efficiency of robustness certification, to an extent that outperforms prior work. Super-DeepG is shared as an open-source tool on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Super-DeepG: A GPU-accelerated tool for formal verification of neural networks against geometric perturbations with improved precision and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical applications require neural networks to perform reliably in normal operations, especially against geometric perturbations like rotation, scaling, shearing, or translation of images. There's a need for formal verification methods that can certify network robustness against such perturbations.
AI.method: Super-DeepG improves linear relaxation techniques and Lipschitz optimization for geometric perturbation verification. The implementation leverages GPU hardware acceleration for computational efficiency.
AI.result: Super-DeepG achieves both improved precision and computational efficiency in robustness certification, outperforming prior work. The tool is available as open-source on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Super-DeepG provides an effective solution for formally verifying neural networks against geometric perturbations, combining theoretical improvements in verification reasoning with practical GPU acceleration to achieve state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and speed.

title: Impact of Age Specialized Models for Hypoglycemia Classification
comment: Accepted for IEEE CAI 2026. 13 pages, 6 Figures, and 10 Tables
summary: Disease progression varies with age and is influenced by underlying genetic, biochemical, and hormonal etiologies, suggesting the need for tailored monitoring, care, and medication beyond standard clinical guidelines. Specifically, in autoimmune diseases like type 1 diabetes (T1D), where patients depend on exogenous insulin to compensate for insulin deficiency, medication dosing and the physiological response reflected in vital signs can differ. Insulin therapy can lead to hypoglycemia, a dangerous condition characterized by decreased blood glucose levels ($\leq$70). This risk can be mitigated through improved diabetes management supported by data analytics. Notably, leveraging data from continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) devices, hypoglycemia onset can be predicted. However, while glucose variability, auto-antibody levels, and hypoglycemia occurrence differ across age groups, hypoglycemia classification most often only relies on population-based models specialized in specific age ranges. In this work, we classify hypoglycemia 0, 5-15, 20-45, and 50-120 minutes before onset using DiaData, a large CGM dataset of patients with T1D ranging from children to seniors. In particular, we investigate: 1) the generalizability of a population-based model including all age groups, 2) the impact of age-segmented models trained separately per age group, and 3) the effect of model individualization through transfer learning. The results show that a global population-based model yields similar or superior performance compared to age-segmented models. These findings suggest that data from children, teenagers, and adults can be combined for training models on hypoglycemia classification. While glucose variation differs across age groups, short-term hypoglycemic patterns are similar. However, data of children obtain their best recall with age specialized model.

AI.tldr: A study examining hypoglycemia prediction in type 1 diabetes patients across different age groups using CGM data, finding that combined age-group models perform similarly to age-specific models except for children.
AI.motivation: Hypoglycemia risk varies with age in type 1 diabetes patients due to different genetic, biochemical, and hormonal factors. Current hypoglycemia classification typically uses population-based models specialized for specific age ranges, but age-related differences in glucose variability and hypoglycemia occurrence suggest the need for more tailored approaches.
AI.method: Used DiaData, a large CGM dataset of T1D patients from children to seniors. Classified hypoglycemia at 0, 5-15, 20-45, and 50-120 minutes before onset. Investigated three approaches: 1) population-based model including all age groups, 2) age-segmented models trained separately per age group, and 3) model individualization through transfer learning.
AI.result: Global population-based model (all age groups combined) yielded similar or superior performance compared to age-segmented models. This suggests glucose variation differs across age groups but short-term hypoglycemic patterns are similar enough to allow combined training. However, children's data achieved best recall with age-specialized models.
AI.conclusion: Data from children, teenagers, and adults with T1D can be combined for training hypoglycemia classification models, except that children benefit from age-specialized models for optimal recall. The similarity in short-term hypoglycemic patterns across age groups enables effective combined modeling approaches.

title: Aligning with Your Own Voice: Self-Corrected Preference Learning for Hallucination Mitigation in LVLMs
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) frequently suffer from hallucinations. Existing preference learning-based approaches largely rely on proprietary models to construct preference datasets. We identify that this reliance introduces a distributional mismatch between the proprietary and target models that hinders efficient alignment. To address this, we propose Alignment via VErified Self-correction DPO (AVES-DPO), a framework that aligns LVLMs using in-distribution data derived from the model's intrinsic knowledge. Our approach employs a consensus-based verification mechanism to diagnose diverse hallucinations and guides the model to self-correct, thereby generating preference pairs strictly compatible with its internal distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AVES-DPO surpasses existing baselines in hallucination mitigation while requiring only 5.2k samples.

AI.tldr: AVES-DPO is a novel framework that reduces hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models by using the model's own verified self-correction to create in-distribution preference pairs, achieving state-of-the-art results with minimal data.
AI.motivation: Existing preference learning methods for hallucination mitigation rely on proprietary models to construct preference datasets, creating distributional mismatch between proprietary and target models that hinders efficient alignment.
AI.method: Proposes Alignment via VErified Self-correction DPO (AVES-DPO) framework that: 1) Uses model's intrinsic knowledge to generate in-distribution data, 2) Employs consensus-based verification to diagnose hallucinations, 3) Guides model to self-correct and generate preference pairs compatible with its internal distribution.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show AVES-DPO surpasses existing baselines in hallucination mitigation while requiring only 5.2k samples, demonstrating efficient and effective alignment.
AI.conclusion: AVES-DPO provides a practical and efficient solution to hallucination reduction in LVLMs by leveraging the model's own verified self-correction to create in-distribution preference pairs, eliminating distributional mismatch issues of existing methods.

title: Transformer as an Euler Discretization of Score-based Variational Flow
summary: Despite the Transformer's dominance across machine learning, its architecture remains largely heuristic and lacks a unified theoretical foundation. We introduce Score-based Variational Flow (SVFlow), a continuous-time dynamical system for representation learning in which the state evolves according to a variational posterior-weighted average of conditional log-likelihood scores, and provide a principled basis for regularization through variational consistency. We show that forward Euler discretization of spherical SVFlow exactly recovers the Transformer architecture. Multi-head attention approximates SVFlow vector field via a vMF kernel-smoothed posterior, while MoE/FFN approximates it in a relaxed network-based way, and the residual-normalization block implements a relaxed retraction that maintains spherical geometry. This unification explains why attention trains stably without explicit regularization while MoE requires auxiliary balancing losses. Experiments on pre-trained language models with prefix shuffling show that SVFlow-induced metrics correlate with task performance, reveal depth-dependent sensitivity, and reflect the intrinsic dynamics of attention.

AI.tldr: SVFlow is a continuous-time dynamical system that unifies the Transformer architecture, showing how attention, MoE/FFN, and residual blocks correspond to different approximations in this framework.
AI.motivation: Despite the Transformer's widespread success, its architecture lacks a unified theoretical foundation and remains largely heuristic. The paper aims to provide a principled theoretical basis for understanding Transformer components.
AI.method: Introduces Score-based Variational Flow (SVFlow), a continuous-time dynamical system where state evolves via variational posterior-weighted average of conditional log-likelihood scores. The framework uses variational consistency for regularization. Forward Euler discretization of spherical SVFlow recovers the Transformer architecture, with attention, MoE/FFN, and residual-normalization blocks mapping to different approximations within this framework.
AI.result: Shows that multi-head attention approximates SVFlow vector field via vMF kernel-smoothed posterior, MoE/FFN approximates it in a relaxed network-based way, and residual-normalization block implements a relaxed retraction that maintains spherical geometry. This unification explains why attention trains stably without explicit regularization while MoE requires auxiliary balancing losses. Experiments on pre-trained language models with prefix shuffling show SVFlow-induced metrics correlate with task performance, reveal depth-dependent sensitivity, and reflect attention's intrinsic dynamics.
AI.conclusion: SVFlow provides a unified theoretical foundation for Transformers, explaining architectural choices and training behaviors through a continuous-time dynamical systems perspective. The framework offers principled regularization and reveals insights into attention mechanisms and model depth.

title: PhysNote: Self-Knowledge Notes for Evolvable Physical Reasoning in Vision-Language Model
comment: 11 pages. Accepted by ICLR 2026 Workshop ES-Reasoning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on textbook-style physics problems, yet they frequently fail when confronted with dynamic real-world scenarios that require temporal consistency and causal reasoning across frames. We identify two fundamental challenges underlying these failures: (1) spatio-temporal identity drift, where objects lose their physical identity across successive frames and break causal chains, and (2) volatility of inference-time insights, where a model may occasionally produce correct physical reasoning but never consolidates it for future reuse. To address these challenges, we propose PhysNote, an agentic framework that enables VLMs to externalize and refine physical knowledge through self-generated "Knowledge Notes." PhysNote stabilizes dynamic perception through spatio-temporal canonicalization, organizes self-generated insights into a hierarchical knowledge repository, and drives an iterative reasoning loop that grounds hypotheses in visual evidence before consolidating verified knowledge. Experiments on PhysBench demonstrate that PhysNote achieves 56.68% overall accuracy, a 4.96% improvement over the best multi-agent baseline, with consistent gains across all four physical reasoning domains.

AI.tldr: PhysNote is an agentic framework that enables Vision-Language Models to externalize and refine physical knowledge through self-generated "Knowledge Notes," improving performance on dynamic real-world physics problems by addressing spatio-temporal identity drift and inference volatility.
AI.motivation: While VLMs perform well on textbook-style physics problems, they frequently fail in dynamic real-world scenarios requiring temporal consistency and causal reasoning across frames. The paper identifies two key challenges: spatio-temporal identity drift (objects losing physical identity across frames) and volatility of inference-time insights (correct reasoning not being consolidated for future reuse).
AI.method: PhysNote is an agentic framework that externalizes physical knowledge through self-generated "Knowledge Notes." It stabilizes dynamic perception through spatio-temporal canonicalization, organizes insights into a hierarchical knowledge repository, and implements an iterative reasoning loop that grounds hypotheses in visual evidence before consolidating verified knowledge.
AI.result: Experiments on PhysBench show PhysNote achieves 56.68% overall accuracy, representing a 4.96% improvement over the best multi-agent baseline, with consistent gains across all four physical reasoning domains tested.
AI.conclusion: PhysNote successfully addresses the fundamental challenges of spatio-temporal identity drift and inference volatility in VLMs for physical reasoning, demonstrating that externalizing knowledge through self-generated notes significantly improves performance on dynamic real-world physics problems requiring temporal and causal consistency.

title: SFT-then-RL Outperforms Mixed-Policy Methods for LLM Reasoning
