We then use simulation-based inference to learn a probabilistic mapping from brain maps to latent stimulus parameters. Our results show that these parameters can be recovered from predicted brain maps, validating the quality of neural encodings. They also show that LLMs can serve as controllable stimulus generators for simulated experiments. Together, these findings provide a step toward decoding and inverse design with foundation brain models.

AI.tldr: Foundation brain models can decode stimulus properties from synthetic brain activity using simulation-based inference paired with LLMs as controllable stimulus generators.
AI.motivation: While foundation models of brain activity can emulate neural responses to stimuli, the researchers want to explore if these models can work in reverse - to recover stimulus properties from synthetic brain activity, which could enable decoding and inverse design applications.
AI.method: Used TRIBEv2 brain emulator paired with LLMs that generate news headlines from linguistic parameters (valence, arousal, dominance). Applied simulation-based inference to learn probabilistic mapping from predicted brain maps back to the latent stimulus parameters.
AI.result: The linguistic parameters (valence, arousal, dominance) can be successfully recovered from the predicted brain maps, validating the quality of neural encodings. LLMs proved effective as controllable stimulus generators for simulated neuroscience experiments.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a proof-of-concept for decoding and inverse design using foundation brain models, showing they can work bidirectionally - not just forward (stimulus→brain) but also backward (brain→stimulus properties).

title: Governing What You Cannot Observe: Adaptive Runtime Governance for Autonomous AI Agents
summary: Autonomous AI agents can remain fully authorized and still become unsafe as behavior drifts, adversaries adapt, and decision patterns shift without any code change. We propose the \textbf{Informational Viability Principle}: governing an agent reduces to estimating a bound on unobserved risk $\hat{B}(x) = U(x) + SB(x) + RG(x)$ and allowing an action only when its capacity $S(x)$ exceeds $\hat{B}(x)$ by a safety margin. The \textbf{Agent Viability Framework}, grounded in Aubin's viability theory, establishes three properties -- monitoring (P1), anticipation (P2), and monotonic restriction (P3) -- as individually necessary and collectively sufficient for documented failure modes. \textbf{RiskGate} instantiates the framework with dedicated statistical estimators (KL divergence, segment-vs-rest $z$-tests, sequential pattern matching), a fail-secure monotonic pipeline, and a closed-loop Autopilot formalised as an instance of Aubin's regulation map with kill-switch-as-last-resort; a scalar Viability Index $VI(t) \in [-1,+1]$ with first-order $t^*$ prediction transforms governance from reactive to predictive. Contributions are the theoretical framework, the reference implementation, and analytical coverage against published agent-failure taxonomies; quantitative empirical evaluation is scoped as follow-up work.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an informational viability principle and Agent Viability Framework to govern autonomous AI agents, preventing safety risks from behavioral drift without code changes, through predictive risk estimation and monotonic restriction.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents can become unsafe even when fully authorized, as behavior drifts, adversaries adapt, and decision patterns shift without any code changes. Current approaches lack systematic governance mechanisms to prevent these safety failures.
AI.method: The paper introduces the Informational Viability Principle: governance reduces to estimating bound on unobserved risk. It establishes Agent Viability Framework grounded in Aubin's viability theory with three properties (monitoring, anticipation, monotonic restriction). RiskGate implements this with statistical estimators (KL divergence, z-tests, pattern matching), fail-secure pipeline, and closed-loop Autopilot with kill-switch.
AI.result: The framework provides a theoretical basis for agent governance with Viability Index (VI(t)) that enables predictive rather than reactive control. Analytical coverage against published agent-failure taxonomies is demonstrated, with quantitative empirical evaluation scoped as follow-up work.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers a principled approach to governing autonomous AI agents by transforming governance from reactive to predictive through viability theory-based risk estimation and monotonic safety controls.

title: Learning Interpretable PDE Representations for Generative Reconstructions with Structured Sparsity
summary: Scientific measurements are often bottlenecked by suboptimal conditions, whether that be noise, incomplete spatial coverage, or limited resolution, rendering accurate field reconstruction a difficult task. We introduce LatentPDE, a latent diffusion framework designed to simultaneously resolve sparse-observation reconstruction and super-resolution. While existing physics-guided diffusion models typically rely on soft loss penalties or uninterpretable representations, our approach enforces physical compliance by constructing an inherently interpretable latent space. Specifically, we parameterize the latent variables directly as the coefficients and source terms of an assumed governing PDE. In doing so, LatentPDE is able to reliably reconstruct dynamics across highly disparate and structured data gaps. Empirical results on diverse configurations demonstrate that our model achieves high-fidelity recovery at any desired resolution while also tracking the underlying predictive uncertainty.

AI.tldr: LatentPDE is a latent diffusion framework that reconstructs sparse observations and performs super-resolution by encoding physical laws directly into the latent space as PDE coefficients and source terms, enabling interpretable and physically compliant field reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Scientific measurements often suffer from suboptimal conditions like noise, incomplete spatial coverage, or limited resolution, making accurate field reconstruction challenging. Existing physics-guided diffusion models typically use soft loss penalties or uninterpretable representations, which limits their effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors introduce LatentPDE, a latent diffusion framework that enforces physical compliance by constructing an interpretable latent space where latent variables are parameterized directly as coefficients and source terms of an assumed governing PDE. This allows simultaneous sparse-observation reconstruction and super-resolution.
AI.result: Empirical results show that LatentPDE reliably reconstructs dynamics across highly disparate and structured data gaps, achieving high-fidelity recovery at any desired resolution while also tracking the underlying predictive uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: LatentPDE successfully addresses the limitations of existing physics-guided models by embedding physical laws directly into the latent representation, enabling interpretable, physically compliant, and uncertainty-aware field reconstruction from sparse or low-resolution measurements.

title: Can Current Agents Close the Discovery-to-Application Gap? A Case Study in Minecraft
comment: Preprint, under review. 41 pages. Project page: https://scicrafter-bench.github.io/. Code: https://github.com/scicrafter-bench/scicraft-bench
summary: Discovering causal regularities and applying them to build functional systems--the discovery-to-application loop--is a hallmark of general intelligence, yet evaluating this capacity has been hindered by the vast complexity gap between scientific discovery and real-world engineering. We introduce SciCrafter, a Minecraft-based benchmark that operationalizes this loop through parameterized redstone circuit tasks. Agents must ignite lamps in specified patterns (e.g., simultaneously or in timed sequences); scaling target parameters substantially increases construction complexity and required knowledge, forcing genuine discovery rather than reliance on memorized solutions. Evaluating frontier models including GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro, and Claude-Opus-4.5 under a general-purpose code agent scaffold, we find that all plateau at approximately 26% success rate. To diagnose these failures, we decompose the loop into four capacities--knowledge gap identification, experimental discovery, knowledge consolidation, and knowledge application--and design targeted interventions whose marginal contributions serve as proxies for corresponding gaps. Our analysis reveals that although the general knowledge application capability still remains as the biggest gap across all models, for frontier models the knowledge gap identification starts to become a major hurdle--indicating the bottleneck is shifting from solving problems right to raising the right problems for current AI. We release SciCrafter as a diagnostic probe for future research on AI systems that navigate the full discovery-to-application loop.

AI.tldr: SciCrafter is a Minecraft-based benchmark that tests AI's ability to discover causal regularities in redstone circuits and apply them to solve complex pattern-based tasks, revealing current models plateau at ~26% success with knowledge application as the main bottleneck.
AI.motivation: The discovery-to-application loop is a hallmark of general intelligence, but evaluating this capacity has been hindered by the vast complexity gap between scientific discovery and real-world engineering. There's a need for benchmarks that operationalize this loop to assess AI systems' ability to discover causal regularities and apply them functionally.
AI.method: Created SciCrafter benchmark with parameterized redstone circuit tasks in Minecraft where agents must ignite lamps in specified patterns (simultaneously or timed sequences). Evaluated frontier models (GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude-Opus-4.5) using general-purpose code agent scaffold. Decomposed the discovery-application loop into four capacities: knowledge gap identification, experimental discovery, knowledge consolidation, and knowledge application, with targeted interventions to diagnose failures.
AI.result: All frontier models plateau at approximately 26% success rate. Analysis revealed knowledge application capability remains the biggest gap across all models, but for frontier models, knowledge gap identification is becoming a major hurdle, indicating the bottleneck is shifting from solving problems correctly to identifying the right problems to solve.
AI.conclusion: SciCrafter serves as a diagnostic probe for evaluating AI systems navigating the full discovery-to-application loop. Current models struggle with this fundamental aspect of general intelligence, with knowledge application being the primary limitation, though frontier models are beginning to show limitations in problem identification as well.

title: Cardiac Stability Theory: An Axiomatically Grounded Framework for Continuous Cardiac Health Monitoring via Smartphone Photoplethysmography
summary: We present Cardiac Stability Theory (CST), an axiomatically grounded framework formally defining cardiovascular health as a stability margin around a cardiac dynamical attractor. From four axioms we derive the Cardiac Stability Index (CSI), a composite scalar in [0,1] integrating the largest Lyapunov exponent, recurrence determinism, and signal entropy via time-delay embedding. The ECG-based model (CSISurrogateV2, CNN-Transformer) achieves $R^2=0.8788$, MAE$=0.0234$ on PTB-XL (21,799 recordings). We extend CSI to smartphone PPG via Complementary Domain Transfer (CDT): CSISurrogateV2 generates pseudo-labels for the BUT PPG dataset (48 recordings, 12 subjects), training TinyCSINet (122,849 parameters), achieving MAE$=0.0557$, $ρ=0.660$ on the held-out test set ($n=1065$ windows) at ${<}30$ ms mobile latency. CDT is validated on BIDMC, Welltory, and RWS-PPG. Paired validation on 5,035 BIDMC windows yields $r=0.454$ ($ρ=0.485$, $p<10^{-295}$), confirming correlated cardiac stability across modalities. CSI is negatively correlated with age (slope $= -0.000225$ CSI/year, PTB-XL), discriminates atrial fibrillation from normal sinus rhythm (AUROC$=0.89$), and is robust under Perturbation Invariance Training (max AUC drop 1.65\%). We derive HeartSpan, a longitudinal stability metric relative to population age norms, enabling continuous non-invasive cardiac monitoring from commodity smartphones for longevity tracking and cardiac risk stratification.

AI.tldr: Cardiac Stability Theory defines cardiovascular health as stability around a cardiac attractor, with a CSI metric derived from ECG/PPG that correlates with age, detects arrhythmias, and enables mobile cardiac monitoring.
AI.motivation: Need for formal, quantifiable definition of cardiovascular health that can be continuously monitored non-invasively using commodity devices like smartphones, enabling longevity tracking and cardiac risk stratification.
AI.method: 1) Axiomatically grounded CST framework defining cardiac stability via dynamical attractor theory; 2) Derivation of CSI as composite scalar integrating Lyapunov exponent, recurrence determinism, and signal entropy; 3) ECG-based CSISurrogateV2 (CNN-Transformer); 4) Extension to smartphone PPG via Complementary Domain Transfer generating pseudo-labels; 5) TinyCSINet architecture for mobile deployment; 6) Validation across multiple datasets including BIDMC, Welltory, and RWS-PPG.
AI.result: CSI achieves R²=0.8788, MAE=0.0234 on PTB-XL (21,799 ECG recordings); mobile PPG version (TinyCSINet) achieves MAE=0.0557, ρ=0.660 on test set with <30ms latency; CSI correlates with age (slope=-0.000225/year), discriminates atrial fibrillation (AUROC=0.89), robust under perturbation; cross-modality correlation validated (r=0.454 on BIDMC, p<10^{-295}); HeartSpan metric derived for longitudinal monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Cardiac Stability Theory provides a formally grounded framework for quantifying cardiovascular health that can be deployed on commodity smartphones, enabling continuous non-invasive cardiac monitoring for longevity tracking and risk stratification with strong validation across multiple datasets and modalities.

title: Case-Specific Rubrics for Clinical AI Evaluation: Methodology, Validation, and LLM-Clinician Agreement Across 823 Encounters
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables, submitted to JAMIA
summary:
Objective. Clinical AI documentation systems require evaluation methodologies that are clinically valid, economically viable, and sensitive to iterative changes. Methods requiring expert review per scoring instance are too slow and expensive for safe, iterative deployment. We present a case-specific, clinician-authored rubric methodology for clinical AI evaluation and examine whether LLM-generated rubrics can approximate clinician agreement.
  Materials and Methods. Twenty clinicians authored 1,646 rubrics for 823 clinical cases (736 real-world, 87 synthetic) across primary care, psychiatry, oncology, and behavioral health. Each rubric was validated by confirming that an LLM-based scoring agent consistently scored clinician-preferred outputs higher than rejected ones. Seven versions of an EHR-embedded AI agent for clinicians were evaluated across all cases.
  Results. Clinician-authored rubrics discriminated effectively between high- and low-quality outputs (median score gap: 82.9%) with high scoring stability (median range: 0.00%). Median scores improved from 84% to 95%. In later experiments, clinician-LLM ranking agreement (tau: 0.42-0.46) matched or exceeded clinician-clinician agreement (tau: 0.38-0.43), attributable to both ceiling compression and LLM rubric improvement.
  Discussion. This convergence supports incorporating LLM rubrics alongside clinician-authored ones. At roughly 1,000 times lower cost, LLM rubrics enable substantially greater evaluation coverage, while continued clinical authorship grounds evaluation in expert judgment. Ceiling compression poses a methodological challenge for future inter-rater agreement studies.
  Conclusion. Case-specific rubrics offer a path for clinical AI evaluation that preserves expert judgment while enabling automation at three orders lower cost. Clinician-authored rubrics establish the baseline against which LLM rubrics are validated.

AI.tldr: Clinicians created case-specific rubrics to evaluate clinical AI documentation systems, then tested if LLM-generated rubrics could match clinician agreement, finding they achieved comparable results at 1000x lower cost.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods for clinical AI documentation systems that require expert review per scoring instance are too slow and expensive for safe, iterative deployment. There's a need for clinically valid, economically viable evaluation methodologies sensitive to iterative changes.
AI.method: Twenty clinicians authored 1,646 rubrics for 823 clinical cases (736 real-world, 87 synthetic) across four medical specialties. Each rubric was validated by confirming LLM-based scoring agents consistently scored clinician-preferred outputs higher than rejected ones. Seven versions of an EHR-embedded AI agent were evaluated across all cases, comparing clinician-authored rubrics against LLM-generated ones.
AI.result: Clinician-authored rubrics effectively discriminated between high- and low-quality outputs (median score gap: 82.9%) with high scoring stability. LLM-generated rubrics achieved comparable agreement to clinician-clinician agreement (tau: 0.42-0.46 vs 0.38-0.43), with 1000x lower cost. Median scores improved from 84% to 95% across iterations.
AI.conclusion: Case-specific rubrics offer a viable path for clinical AI evaluation that preserves expert judgment while enabling automation at dramatically lower cost. Clinician-authored rubrics establish the baseline for validating LLM rubrics, with LLM rubrics enabling substantially greater evaluation coverage while maintaining clinical validity.

title: Geometry Preserving Loss Functions Promote Improved Adaptation of Blackbox Generative Model
summary: Adaptation of blackbox generative models has been widely studied recently through the exploration of several methods including generator fine-tuning, latent space searches, leveraging singular value decomposition, and so on. However, adapting large-scale generative AI tools to specific use cases continues to be challenging, as many of these industry-grade models are not made widely available. The traditional approach of fine-tuning certain layers of a generative network is not feasible due to the expense of storing and fine-tuning generative models, as well as the restricted access to weights and gradients. Recognizing these challenges, we propose a novel end-to-end pipeline aimed at domain adaptation by leveraging geometry-preserving loss functions in conjunction to pre-trained generative adversarial networks (GANs). Our method rethinks the problem of adaptation by re-contextualizing the role of GAN inversion in obtaining accurate latent space representations. Extending the ability of existing state-of-the-art inverters, we preserve pair-wise distances between tangent spaces to successfully train a latent generative model to produce samples from the target distribution. We evaluate our proposed pipeline on StyleGANs with real distribution shifts and demonstrate that the introduction of the geometry preserving loss function lends to improved adaptation of generative models compared to other traditional loss functions.

AI.tldr: A novel pipeline for adapting blackbox generative models using geometry-preserving loss functions with GAN inversion, addressing the challenge of adapting inaccessible industry-grade models without weight access.
AI.motivation: Adapting large-scale generative AI tools to specific domains is challenging due to restricted access to model weights/gradients and the expense of fine-tuning traditional generative models. Industry-grade models are often not available, making existing adaptation methods like fine-tuning or latent space searches infeasible.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline leveraging geometry-preserving loss functions with pre-trained GANs. The method re-contextualizes GAN inversion for accurate latent representations and extends existing inverters by preserving pair-wise distances between tangent spaces to train latent generative models for target distributions.
AI.result: The proposed pipeline demonstrates improved adaptation of generative models compared to traditional loss functions when evaluated on StyleGANs with real distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: The introduction of geometry-preserving loss functions enables effective domain adaptation of blackbox generative models without requiring access to model weights or gradients, overcoming limitations of traditional adaptation methods for inaccessible industry models.

title: Learning to Rotate: Temporal and Semantic Rotary Encoding for Sequential Modeling
summary:
Every Transformer architecture dedicates enormous capacity to learning rich representations in semantic embedding space -- yet the rotation manifold acted upon by Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) has been treated as a fixed, hand-crafted structure, populated only by discrete ordinal indices. We argue that this rotation space is a largely overlooked second dimension of expressivity in the attention mechanism, one whose systematic exploration may open a new door for attention-based architectures. The analogy to complex numbers is instructive: just as introducing the imaginary axis -- orthogonal to and independent of the real line -- unlocked new algebraic structure once believed impossible, treating the rotation manifold as a learnable, signal-conditioned space opens an orthogonal degree of freedom in attention. In this framing, the token embedding encodes the semantic (real) component of a representation -- what a token means -- while the rotation encodes its dynamic (imaginary) component -- how it relates to every other token across time, position, and context.
  We introduce SIREN-RoPE, a concrete instantiation of this idea, which populates the rotation dimension with heterogeneous signals -- continuous timestamps, cyclical temporal patterns, and categorical metadata -- via a dual-branch Sinusoidal Representation Network (SIREN). As a proof of concept, we evaluate on a production-scale news feed dataset from a major social network using a generative recommender as the ranking model, demonstrating that activating this hidden dimension yields consistent improvements across calibration and ranking objectives with negligible computational overhead. We invite the community to view the rotation space not as a solved positional-encoding detail, but as an untapped axis whose rich structure may prove as consequential for attention as the imaginary unit proved for algebra.

AI.tldr: SIREN-RoPE transforms Rotary Positional Embeddings' rotation space from fixed ordinal indices into a learnable, signal-conditioned dimension for attention mechanisms, analogous to introducing an imaginary axis in complex numbers.
AI.motivation: Current Transformer architectures treat rotation space in RoPE as a fixed, hand-crafted structure limited to discrete ordinal indices, overlooking its potential as a second dimension of expressivity in attention mechanisms.
AI.method: Introduces SIREN-RoPE, which populates the rotation dimension with heterogeneous signals (continuous timestamps, cyclical temporal patterns, categorical metadata) using a dual-branch Sinusoidal Representation Network (SIREN).
AI.result: Evaluation on production-scale news feed dataset with generative recommender shows consistent improvements across calibration and ranking objectives with negligible computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Rotation space should be viewed not as a solved positional-encoding detail but as an untapped axis of expressivity that could prove as consequential for attention as the imaginary unit proved for algebra.

title: Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models for Short Term Electricity Price Forecasting in Australia's National Electricity Market
summary: Short term electricity price forecast is essential in competitive power markets, yet electricity price series exhibit high volatility, irregularity, and non-stationarity. This phenomenon is pronounced in the South Australian region of the National Electricity Market, where high renewable penetration drives price volatility and frequent negative price intervals, while structural changes such as the transition to five-minute settlement further complicate forecast. To address these challenges, this study develops a unified benchmark framework. Under identical data preprocessing, feature engineering with lag features, rolling statistics, cyclic temporal encodings, and so on, and an 85% to 15% chronological train test split, six algorithms are systematically compared, including AWMLSTM, CatBoost, GBRT, LSTM, LightGBM, and SVR. The results show that for price prediction, tree-based models, especially GBRT with an R squared value of 0.88, generally outperform LSTM and SVR. However, all models achieve a mean absolute percentage error above 90%, and more than 65% of GBRT predictions have relative errors above 10%, which highlights the inherent difficulty of price forecast. For demand prediction, all models perform substantially better than in price prediction. AWMLSTM and GBRT achieve an R2 value of 0.96 with mean absolute percentage error below 32%, and GBRT has 74.37% of samples within 5% error, while LSTM and SVR perform less accurately in both tasks. Future improvements should focus on hybrid models such as tree plus transformers, data augmentation for extreme events, and error correction to better capture price spikes.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a benchmark framework for electricity price forecasting in South Australia's volatile market, systematically comparing six ML algorithms and finding tree-based models like GBRT perform best for price prediction, while all models excel at demand forecasting.
AI.motivation: Electricity price forecasting is crucial in competitive power markets, but particularly challenging in South Australia due to high renewable penetration (causing volatility and negative prices), structural market changes (transition to five-minute settlement), and inherent price series characteristics (high volatility, irregularity, non-stationarity).
AI.method: Developed a unified benchmark framework with identical data preprocessing, feature engineering (lag features, rolling statistics, cyclic temporal encodings), and 85%-15% chronological train-test split. Systematically compared six algorithms: AWMLSTM, CatBoost, GBRT, LSTM, LightGBM, and SVR.
AI.result: For price prediction: Tree-based models (especially GBRT with R²=0.88) outperform LSTM and SVR, but all models have MAPE >90% and >65% of GBRT predictions have relative errors >10%. For demand prediction: All models perform substantially better - AWMLSTM and GBRT achieve R²=0.96 with MAPE <32%, and GBRT has 74.37% of samples within 5% error. LSTM and SVR perform less accurately in both tasks.
AI.conclusion: Electricity price forecasting remains inherently difficult despite advanced ML methods, though demand forecasting is more reliable. Future improvements should focus on hybrid models (tree+transformers), data augmentation for extreme events, and error correction techniques to better capture price spikes.

title: ECoLAD: Deployment-Oriented Evaluation for Automotive Time-Series Anomaly Detection
summary:
Time-series anomaly detectors are commonly compared on workstation-class hardware under unconstrained execution. In-vehicle monitoring, however, requires predictable latency and stable behavior under limited CPU parallelism. Accuracy-only leaderboards can therefore misrepresent which methods remain feasible under deployment-relevant constraints.
  We present ECoLAD (Efficiency Compute Ladder for Anomaly Detection), a deployment-oriented evaluation protocol instantiated as an empirical study on proprietary automotive telemetry (anomaly rate ${\approx}$0.022) and complementary public benchmarks. ECoLAD applies a monotone compute-reduction ladder across heterogeneous detector families using mechanically determined, integer-only scaling rules and explicit CPU thread caps, while logging every applied configuration change. Throughput-constrained behavior is characterized by sweeping target scoring rates and reporting (i) coverage (the fraction of entities meeting the target) and (ii) the best AUC-PR achievable among measured ladder configurations satisfying the target. On constrained automotive telemetry, lightweight classical detectors sustain both coverage and detection lift above the random baseline across the full throughput sweep. Several deep methods lose feasibility before they lose accuracy.

AI.tldr: ECoLAD is a deployment-oriented evaluation protocol for time-series anomaly detection that measures both accuracy and efficiency under constrained CPU resources, revealing that some deep methods lose feasibility before losing accuracy in automotive telemetry applications.
AI.motivation: Current time-series anomaly detection evaluations focus on workstation-class hardware with unconstrained execution, but real-world deployment in automotive telemetry requires predictable latency and stable behavior under limited CPU parallelism. Accuracy-only leaderboards can misrepresent which methods remain feasible under deployment-relevant constraints.
AI.method: ECoLAD applies a monotone compute-reduction ladder across heterogeneous detector families using mechanically determined, integer-only scaling rules and explicit CPU thread caps. It sweeps target scoring rates while logging every configuration change, characterizing throughput-constrained behavior by reporting (i) coverage (fraction of entities meeting target) and (ii) best AUC-PR achievable among configurations satisfying target.
AI.result: On constrained automotive telemetry (anomaly rate ≈0.022%), lightweight classical detectors sustained both coverage and detection lift above random baseline across full throughput sweep. Several deep methods lost feasibility before losing accuracy, showing that efficiency constraints impact practical deployment differently across detector families.
AI.conclusion: ECoLAD provides a deployment-oriented evaluation framework that reveals practical trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency in real-world constrained environments, showing that efficiency-aware evaluation is crucial for automotive telemetry applications where lightweight classical methods can outperform more complex deep approaches under resource constraints.

title: Gromov-Wasserstein Methods for Multi-View Relational Embedding and Clustering
comment: This manuscript is currently under review at the XLIV Simposio Brasileiro de Telecomunicacoes e Processamento de Sinais - SBrT (Brazilian Symposium on Telecommunications and Signal Processing ) 2026
summary: Learning low-dimensional representations from multi-view relational data is challenging when underlying geometries differ across views. We propose Bary-GWMDS, a Gromov-Wasserstein-based method that operates directly on distance matrices to learn a consensus embedding preserving shared relational structure. By leveraging intrinsic distances, the approach naturally handles nonlinear distortions across views. We also introduce Mean-GWMDS-C, a clustering-oriented formulation that averages distance matrices and learns reduced-support representations via a consensus Gromov-Wasserstein transport. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that the proposed framework yields stable and geometrically meaningful embeddings.

AI.tldr: A method called Bary-GWMDS learns consensus embeddings from multi-view relational data using Gromov-Wasserstein distance, handling nonlinear distortions across views by operating directly on distance matrices.
AI.motivation: Learning low-dimensional representations from multi-view relational data is challenging when underlying geometries differ across views, requiring methods that can handle these nonlinear distortions while preserving shared relational structure.
AI.method: Bary-GWMDS uses Gromov-Wasserstein-based approach operating directly on distance matrices to learn consensus embedding. Also introduces Mean-GWMDS-C for clustering that averages distance matrices and learns reduced-support representations via consensus Gromov-Wasserstein transport.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show the proposed framework yields stable and geometrically meaningful embeddings that effectively handle multi-view relational data with differing geometries.
AI.conclusion: The Bary-GWMDS framework provides an effective approach for learning consensus embeddings from multi-view relational data with varying geometries, offering both embedding and clustering capabilities through Gromov-Wasserstein-based formulations.

title: Crystal structure prediction using graph neural combinatorial optimization
summary: Crystalline materials are widely used in technological applications, yet their discovery remains a significant challenge. As their properties are driven by structure, crystal structure prediction (CSP) methods play a central role in computational approaches aiming to accelerate this process. Previously, CSP has been approached from a combinatorial optimization perspective, with the core challenge of allocating atoms on a fine grid of predefined discrete positions within a unit cell while minimizing their interaction energy. Exact mathematical optimization methods provide guaranteed solutions, but they become computationally expensive for large-scale instances, where the atomic configuration space grows rapidly, particularly in the absence of additional symmetry constraints. In this work, we introduce a neural combinatorial optimization approach to the atom allocation challenge and, subsequently, CSP, based on graph neural networks (GNNs), which can effectively sample from the distribution of feasible structures in an unsupervised manner. We leverage expander graphs to construct computational graphs over discrete positions that capture both short- and long-range interactions between atoms, and employ the Gumbel-Sinkhorn approach to enforce the desired stoichiometry of the generated structures. We demonstrate that our method outperforms classical heuristic approaches and is competitive with a commercial optimization solver across a range of chemical compositions. This enables the use of ever-expanding GPU infrastructure to tackle the inherent combinatorial challenges of CSP, paving the way for scaling beyond current capabilities.

AI.tldr: A neural combinatorial optimization approach using graph neural networks (GNNs) for crystal structure prediction that outperforms classical heuristics and competes with commercial solvers.
AI.motivation: Crystal structure prediction (CSP) is crucial for discovering new materials but computationally challenging due to the combinatorial explosion of atomic configurations. Current exact optimization methods become expensive for large-scale problems, especially without symmetry constraints.
AI.method: A neural combinatorial optimization approach using graph neural networks (GNNs) to sample from feasible structure distributions. Uses expander graphs to capture short- and long-range atomic interactions, and employs the Gumbel-Sinkhorn approach to enforce stoichiometric constraints in an unsupervised manner.
AI.result: The method outperforms classical heuristic approaches and is competitive with a commercial optimization solver across various chemical compositions. It enables leveraging GPU infrastructure to tackle combinatorial challenges in CSP.
AI.conclusion: This approach provides a scalable neural solution for crystal structure prediction that can overcome current computational limitations and enable scaling beyond existing capabilities, particularly by utilizing modern GPU infrastructure.

title: Robust and Clinically Reliable EEG Biomarkers: A Cross Population Framework for Generalizable Parkinson's Disease Detection
comment: This is the non anonymized preprint corresponding to the version submitted to ACM Transactions on Computing for Healthcare. It is not the final typeset or accepted version
summary: Developing robust and clinically reliable EEG biomarkers requires evaluation frameworks that explicitly address cross population generalization in multi site settings such as Parkinsons disease (PD) detection. Models trained under i.i.d. assumptions often capture population specific artifacts rather than disease relevant neural structure, leading to poor generalization across clinical cohorts. EEG further amplifies this challenge due to low signal to noise ratio and heterogeneous acquisition conditions. We propose a population aware evaluation framework to assess the robustness and clinical reliability of EEG biomarkers under distribution shift. Using an n gram expansion strategy, we enumerate all cross population train test configurations across five independent cohorts, resulting in 75 directional evaluations. A nested cross validation design with integrated channel selection ensures prospective biomarker identification without population leakage. Results show that cross population transfer is asymmetric and that both accuracy and biomarker stability improve with increasing training population diversity, achieving up to 94.1% accuracy on held out cohorts. A theoretical analysis based on mixture risk optimization and hypothesis space contraction explains these trends, showing that multi population training promotes population robust representations. This work establishes a principled framework for learning robust, generalizable, and clinically reliable EEG biomarkers for multi site biomedical applications.

AI.tldr: A population-aware evaluation framework for developing robust EEG biomarkers in Parkinson's disease detection that addresses cross-population generalization through multi-cohort analysis and nested cross-validation design.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG biomarker models trained under i.i.d. assumptions capture population-specific artifacts rather than disease-relevant neural structure, leading to poor generalization across clinical cohorts. This is exacerbated by EEG's low signal-to-noise ratio and heterogeneous acquisition conditions.
AI.method: Proposed a population-aware evaluation framework using n-gram expansion to enumerate all cross-population train-test configurations across five independent cohorts (75 evaluations). Implemented nested cross-validation with integrated channel selection to prevent population leakage. Theoretical analysis based on mixture risk optimization and hypothesis space contraction.
AI.result: Cross-population transfer is asymmetric, and both accuracy and biomarker stability improve with increasing training population diversity. Achieved up to 94.1% accuracy on held-out cohorts. Multi-population training promotes population-robust representations.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes a principled approach for learning robust, generalizable, and clinically reliable EEG biomarkers for multi-site biomedical applications, addressing the critical challenge of distribution shift in clinical EEG data.

title: Task-guided Spatiotemporal Network with Diffusion Augmentation for EEG-based Dementia Diagnosis and MMSE Prediction
summary: Patients with dementia typically exhibit cognitive impairment, which is routinely assessed using the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE). Concurrently, their underlying neurophysiological abnormalities are reflected in Electroencephalography (EEG), providing a basis for joint modeling. However, traditional multi-task approaches suffer from feature entanglement, which leads to inter-task interference when handling heterogeneous objectives.To address this challenge, we propose a task-guided spatiotemporal network (TGSN) with diffusion augmentation for EEG-based dementia diagnosis and MMSE prediction. Specifically, TGSN integrates a multi-band feature fusion module to capture complementary spectral information from EEG. Meanwhile, a pre-trained data augmentation module utilizing a diffusion process is introduced toincrease sample diversity. To model the complex spatiotemporal patterns of EEG, we propose a gated spatiotemporal attention module that captures long-range spatial dependencies and temporal dynamics. Moreover, we design a task-guided query module to achieve task-specific feature extraction, thereby mitigating task interference. The effectiveness of TGSN is evaluated on the XY02 dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed network outperforms several state-of-the-art methods, achieving classification accuracies of 97.78\% for Alzheimer's Disease (AD)/Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) and 83.93\% for AD/FTD/Vascular Cognitive Impairment (VCI), which exceed the best baselines by 16.39\% and 8.28\%, respectively. In parallel, it reduces the RMSE for MMSE prediction to 1.93 and 2.38, achieving significant error reductions of 1.44 and 1.43 compared to the best baselines. Additionally, validation on the DS004504 dataset demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization...

AI.tldr: A task-guided spatiotemporal network with diffusion augmentation achieves superior EEG-based dementia diagnosis and MMSE prediction by mitigating task interference through task-specific feature extraction.
AI.motivation: Traditional multi-task approaches suffer from feature entanglement and inter-task interference when handling heterogeneous objectives like dementia diagnosis and MMSE prediction from EEG data.
AI.method: TGSN uses multi-band feature fusion, diffusion-based data augmentation, gated spatiotemporal attention for long-range dependencies, and task-guided query modules for task-specific feature extraction to mitigate interference.
AI.result: Achieves 97.78% accuracy for AD/FTD classification (16.39% improvement) and 83.93% for AD/FTD/VCI (8.28% improvement), while reducing MMSE prediction RMSE to 1.93 and 2.38 (1.44 and 1.43 error reductions).
AI.conclusion: TGSN effectively addresses feature entanglement in multi-task learning, demonstrating superior performance for EEG-based dementia diagnosis and MMSE prediction with strong cross-dataset generalization.

title: DecompKAN: Decomposed Patch-KAN for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Preprint; under review
summary: Accurate time series forecasting in scientific domains such as climate modeling, physiological monitoring, and energy systems benefits from both competitive predictions and model transparency. This work proposes DecompKAN, a lightweight attention-free architecture that combines trend-residual decomposition, channel-wise patching, learned instance normalization, and B-spline Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) edge functions. Each KAN edge learns an explicit, inspectable 1D scalar function over learned patch-embedding coordinates that can be directly visualized. On standard benchmarks, DecompKAN achieves best or tied-best MSE on 15 of 32 dataset-horizon combinations among selected published baselines, and achieves best or tied-best MSE on 20 of 36 comparisons under a controlled same-recipe evaluation across 9 datasets including the physiological PPG-DaLiA benchmark. The architecture shows particular strength on datasets with smooth temporal dynamics (Solar -17%, ECL -10% vs. iTransformer, Weather) and physiological time series. Visualization of learned edge functions reveals qualitatively different latent nonlinearities across domains. Ablation analysis shows that the architectural pipeline (decomposition, patching, normalization) drives performance more than the choice of nonlinear layer, while the KAN formulation enables inspection of learned latent transformations.

AI.tldr: DecompKAN: An attention-free time series forecasting model using trend-residual decomposition with B-spline KAN edge functions that achieves competitive performance while offering interpretable visualizations of learned functions.
AI.motivation: Need for accurate time series forecasting with model transparency in scientific domains like climate modeling, physiological monitoring, and energy systems, requiring both competitive predictions and interpretability.
AI.method: Combines trend-residual decomposition, channel-wise patching, learned instance normalization, and B-spline Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) edge functions. Each KAN edge learns explicit, inspectable 1D scalar functions over learned patch-embedding coordinates that can be directly visualized.
AI.result: Achieves best or tied-best MSE on 15/32 dataset-horizon combinations among selected baselines, and 20/36 comparisons under controlled same-recipe evaluation across 9 datasets. Shows particular strength on smooth temporal dynamics datasets (Solar -17%, ECL -10% vs. iTransformer) and physiological time series. Learned edge functions reveal qualitatively different nonlinearities across domains.
AI.conclusion: The architectural pipeline (decomposition, patching, normalization) drives performance more than choice of nonlinear layer, while KAN formulation enables inspection of learned latent transformations. DecompKAN offers a lightweight, interpretable alternative to attention-based models with competitive performance.

title: Continual Calibration: Coverage Can Collapse Before Accuracy in Lifelong LLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Continual learning for large language models is typically evaluated through accuracy retention under sequential fine-tuning. We argue that this perspective is incomplete, because uncertainty reliability can degrade earlier and more sharply than top-1 performance. We study this empirically by measuring conformal coverage and calibration error on sequentially fine-tuned models across three model families and eight task sequences drawn primarily from classification and multiple-choice benchmarks. Across the classification-style settings we study, coverage loss exceeds accuracy loss by a factor of roughly \(3.4\times \pm 0.5\times\) on average across seeds; in the most pronounced case, coverage drops from \(0.92\) to \(0.61\), while accuracy remains within three points of baseline. Standard continual-learning methods that preserve accuracy do not automatically preserve coverage, and naive calibration baselines recover only part of the gap. We propose calibration replay, a lightweight post-hoc procedure that maintains a task-specific held-out buffer and refits a task-specific conformal threshold under the current model after each update. It adds no training-time gradient cost, uses less than one percent of the memory of ordinary experience replay, and typically restores coverage to within two points of nominal at buffer size \(m = 200\). We accompany the empirical study with a drift decomposition, a finite-sample recovery theorem showing exact conformal validity under exchangeability, and a mixture-validity proposition explaining why pooled thresholds do not suffice. Our guarantees are stated for classification-style tasks with task-specific buffers; extensions to open-ended generation are exploratory.

AI.tldr: Continual learning for LLMs shows uncertainty reliability degrades much faster than top-1 accuracy during sequential fine-tuning. Standard continual learning methods preserve accuracy but not uncertainty calibration, and a lightweight calibration replay method with task-specific buffers restores coverage with minimal memory overhead.
AI.motivation: Current continual learning evaluation focuses too narrowly on accuracy retention during sequential fine-tuning, ignoring that uncertainty reliability (calibration and conformal coverage) can degrade earlier and more severely than top-1 performance.
AI.method: Empirical study across 3 model families and 8 task sequences measuring conformal coverage and calibration error. Proposes calibration replay - a lightweight post-hoc procedure maintaining task-specific held-out buffers to refit conformal thresholds after each update, adding no training-time gradient cost and minimal memory usage.
AI.result: Coverage loss exceeds accuracy loss by ~3.4x on average; in extreme cases, coverage drops from 0.92 to 0.61 while accuracy stays within 3 points of baseline. Calibration replay restores coverage to within 2 points of nominal at buffer size m=200 using <1% memory of ordinary experience replay.
AI.conclusion: Continual learning evaluation must consider uncertainty reliability alongside accuracy. Standard methods fail to preserve calibration, but lightweight calibration replay effectively maintains conformal validity with minimal overhead, supported by theoretical guarantees for classification tasks.

title: Hindsight Preference Optimization for Financial Time Series Advisory
summary: Time series models predict numbers; decision-makers need advisory -- directional signals with reasoning, actionable suggestions, and risk management. Training language models for such predictive advisory faces a fundamental challenge: quality depends on outcomes unknown at prediction time. We bridge two ideas from reinforcement learning -- using information unavailable during execution to retrospectively generate training signal, and preference alignment -- and propose Hindsight Preference Optimization: observed outcomes let an LLM judge rank candidate advisories on dimensions that scalar metrics cannot capture, producing preference pairs for DPO without human annotation. We apply this to Vision-Language-Model-based predictive advisories on S&P 500 equity time series, demonstrated by a 4B model outperforming its 235B teacher on both accuracy and advisory quality.

AI.tldr: LLM training for predictive advisory uses outcomes to judge candidate advisories retrospectively, enabling preference optimization without human annotation, demonstrated with financial time series prediction.
AI.motivation: Decision-makers need more than numerical predictions - they require directional signals with reasoning, actionable suggestions, and risk management. However, training language models for predictive advisory faces a fundamental challenge: quality depends on outcomes that are unknown at prediction time.
AI.method: Proposes Hindsight Preference Optimization that bridges two reinforcement learning ideas: using future outcomes to generate retrospective training signals, and preference alignment. Observed outcomes enable LLMs to judge and rank candidate advisories on dimensions that scalar metrics cannot capture, producing preference pairs for DPO without human annotation.
AI.result: Applied to Vision-Language-Model-based predictive advisories on S&P 500 equity time series, where a 4B parameter model outperforms its 235B teacher model on both accuracy and advisory quality metrics.
AI.conclusion: Hindsight Preference Optimization enables effective training of smaller models for predictive advisory tasks by leveraging observed outcomes to generate preference pairs automatically, demonstrating superior performance over much larger teacher models in financial advisory applications.

title: Fix Initial Codes and Iteratively Refine Textual Directions Toward Safe Multi-Turn Code Correction
summary: Recent work on large language models (LLMs) has emphasized the importance of scaling inference compute. From this perspective, the state-of-the-art method Scattered Forest Search (SFS) has been proposed, employing Monte Carlo Tree Search with carefully crafted initial seeds and textual optimization for multi-turn code correction. However, its complexity makes it unclear what factors contribute to improvements in inference performance. To address this problem, we analyze SFS and propose a simpler method, Iterative Refinement of Textual Directions (IRTD), which fixes initial codes and iteratively refines textual directions. Because of the simplicity of IRTD, we theoretically establish the safety of IRTD using Oracle-Guided Inductive Synthesis (OGIS). Experiments on several code generation benchmarks suggest that IRTD achieves inference performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods. These results indicate that, even without complex search structures, refining initial codes with high-quality textual directions alone can effectively improve inference performance.

AI.tldr: A simpler alternative to complex Scattered Forest Search called Iterative Refinement of Textual Directions (IRTD) achieves comparable code generation performance by fixing initial codes and iteratively refining textual directions.
AI.motivation: While scaling inference compute is important for LLMs, the state-of-the-art Scattered Forest Search method is too complex to understand what factors actually improve performance. There's a need to simplify the approach while maintaining effectiveness.
AI.method: The paper proposes IRTD (Iterative Refinement of Textual Directions) which fixes initial codes and iteratively refines textual directions. The method's simplicity allows for theoretical analysis using Oracle-Guided Inductive Synthesis (OGIS) to establish its safety properties.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple code generation benchmarks show that IRTD achieves inference performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating that complex search structures are not necessary for performance improvement.
AI.conclusion: Refining initial codes with high-quality textual directions alone is sufficient to effectively improve inference performance in code generation tasks, without needing complex search mechanisms like Monte Carlo Tree Search used in previous methods.

title: When to Commit? Towards Variable-Size Self-Contained Blocks for Discrete Diffusion Language Models
summary: Discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs) enable parallel token updates with bidirectional attention, yet practical generation typically adopts blockwise semi-autoregressive decoding. This switch creates a training-inference mismatch: training denoises with full-sequence context, while inference commits tokens within a bounded block without future context. Therefore, decoding with fixed-size or heuristic-based blocks can lead to premature token commitments, as decisions are made without full access to future context that could alter those choices. Motivated by this, we propose self-containedness as a principled criterion for block commitment. A block is self-contained if its predictions remain consistent with Future-Aware (FA) or without No-Future (NF) access to future context, reframing block boundary selection as a test of self-containedness rather than a heuristic choice. Based on this principle, we introduce Variable-size Self-contained Blocks (VSB) for dLLMs. VSB scores and selects block boundaries using the divergence between token-level predictive distributions under NF and FA conditioning, which quantifies how predictions would change if future context were revealed. We provide theoretical justification linking self-containedness to predictive consistency, and extensive experiments validate VSB's efficacy over fixed-size and heuristic blockwise decoding.

AI.tldr: dLLMs use parallel training but blockwise decoding creates training-inference mismatch. The paper proposes Variable-size Self-contained Blocks (VSB) to dynamically select block boundaries based on predictive consistency between future-aware and no-future contexts.
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs) have a fundamental training-inference mismatch: they train with full-sequence bidirectional attention but typically decode with fixed-size or heuristic-based blockwise approaches. This leads to premature token commitments because decisions are made without access to future context that could alter those choices, reducing generation quality.
AI.method: Introduces "self-containedness" as a principled criterion for block commitment - a block is self-contained if its predictions remain consistent whether or not future context is available. The Variable-size Self-contained Blocks (VSB) method scores and selects block boundaries using the divergence between token-level predictive distributions under No-Future (NF) and Future-Aware (FA) conditioning, quantifying how predictions would change if future context were revealed.
AI.result: Extensive experiments validate VSB's efficacy over fixed-size and heuristic blockwise decoding methods. The paper provides theoretical justification linking self-containedness to predictive consistency, showing that VSB enables more adaptive and context-aware block commitment.
AI.conclusion: VSB provides a principled, adaptive approach to blockwise decoding for dLLMs that addresses the training-inference mismatch by dynamically determining block boundaries based on predictive consistency rather than using fixed or heuristic approaches, leading to improved generation quality.

title: TCOD: Exploring Temporal Curriculum in On-Policy Distillation for Multi-turn Autonomous Agents
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) has shown strong potential for transferring reasoning ability from frontier or domain-specific models to smaller students. While effective on static single-turn tasks, its behavior in multi-turn agent settings remains underexplored. In this work, we identify a key limitation of vanilla OPD in such settings, which we term Trajectory-Level KL Instability. Specifically, we observe that KL divergence increases together with a drop in success rate, and even after convergence, the KL remains high, leading to unstable training. This instability arises from inter-turn error compounding: as errors accumulate, the student is driven beyond the teacher's effective support, rendering the supervision signal unreliable. To address this, we propose TCOD (Temporal Curriculum On-Policy Distillation), a simple yet effective framework that controls the trajectory depth exposed to the student and progressively expands it from short to long with a curriculum schedule.Experimental results across four student-teacher pairs on three multi-turn agent benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld) show that TCOD mitigates KL escalation and enhances KL stability throughout training, improving agent performance by up to 18 points over vanilla OPD. Further evaluations show that TCOD can even surpass the teacher's performance and generalize to tasks on which the teacher fails.

AI.tldr: TCOD: A curriculum learning approach that progressively increases trajectory length to stabilize on-policy distillation in multi-turn agent settings.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) works well for static single-turn tasks but shows instability in multi-turn agent settings due to Trajectory-Level KL Instability - where KL divergence increases with success rate drop, causing unreliable supervision from error compounding beyond teacher's effective support.
AI.method: Proposed TCOD (Temporal Curriculum On-Policy Distillation) that controls trajectory depth exposed to the student, progressively expanding from short to long trajectories using a curriculum schedule to mitigate KL escalation.
AI.result: Experimental results on four student-teacher pairs across three multi-turn agent benchmarks (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld) show TCOD mitigates KL escalation, enhances KL stability, improves agent performance by up to 18 points over vanilla OPD, and can even surpass teacher performance on some tasks.
AI.conclusion: TCOD provides an effective solution to the trajectory-level instability problem in on-policy distillation for multi-turn agents, enabling stable training and improved performance through temporal curriculum learning.

title: Coverage-Based Calibration for Post-Training Quantization via Weighted Set Cover over Outlier Channels
summary: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) compresses large language models to low bit-widths using a small calibration set, and its quality depends strongly on which samples are chosen. We identify a failure mode in which calibration samples fail to activate outlier channels, hidden dimensions with unusually large activations, causing the quantizer to underestimate their dynamic range and producing per-channel reconstruction errors that dominate layer-wise loss. Motivated by this observation, we argue that PTQ calibration quality is governed more by weighted outlier-channel coverage than by generic sample representativeness, and formulate calibration selection as a weighted set cover problem over outlier channels. The objective is monotone submodular, and the greedy algorithm, COVERCAL, operates on pre-computed activation statistics and requires no GPU time at selection. We further show that the weight choice is internally consistent: under a stylized clipping model, missed weighted coverage upper-bounds surrogate loss, justifying the weighted coverage objective as principled rather than purely empirical. Across LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3, and Mistral, under AWQ and GPTQ backends and five downstream evaluations, COVERCAL improves over random, max-perplexity, max-activation-variance, and stratified baselines, with the largest gains at small calibration budgets. At INT4 with 128 samples, COVERCAL improves MMLU by 1.2 to 1.5 points over random calibration and reduces perplexity degradation by 15 to 30\%; with 64 samples, it matches or exceeds random calibration at 256. The contribution is not a new PTQ backend but a formulation of calibration selection as weighted outlier coverage, with a simple, efficient algorithm and a surrogate-based justification.

AI.tldr: COVERCAL is a calibration sample selection method for Post-Training Quantization that treats calibration as a weighted set cover problem over outlier channels, requiring no GPU time and achieving significant improvements especially with small calibration budgets.
AI.motivation: Current PTQ calibration quality depends heavily on sample selection, with a failure mode where calibration samples fail to activate outlier channels (dimensions with unusually large activations). This causes quantizers to underestimate dynamic range, leading to significant reconstruction errors that dominate layer-wise loss.
AI.method: Formulate calibration selection as a weighted set cover problem over outlier channels, with COVERCAL - a greedy algorithm that operates on pre-computed activation statistics requiring no GPU time. The method focuses on weighted outlier-channel coverage rather than generic sample representativeness.
AI.result: Across LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3, and Mistral models with AWQ/GPTQ backends, COVERCAL outperforms baselines (random, max-perplexity, max-activation-variance, stratified). At INT4 with 128 samples: improves MMLU by 1.2-1.5 points over random calibration, reduces perplexity degradation by 15-30%. With 64 samples matches/exceeds random calibration at 256 samples.
AI.conclusion: PTQ calibration quality is governed more by weighted outlier-channel coverage than generic representativeness. The proposed formulation of calibration selection as weighted set cover with COVERCAL algorithm provides a principled, efficient solution with significant gains, especially for small calibration budgets.

title: FedSLoP: Memory-Efficient Federated Learning with Low-Rank Gradient Projection
summary: Federated learning enables a population of clients to collaboratively train machine learning models without exchanging their raw data, but standard algorithms such as FedAvg suffer from slow convergence and high communication and memory costs in heterogeneous, resource-constrained environments. We introduce FedSLoP, a federated optimization algorithm that combines stochastic low-rank subspace projections of gradients, thereby reducing the dimension of communicated and stored updates while preserving optimization progress. On the theoretical side, we develop a detailed nonconvex convergence analysis under standard smoothness and bounded-variance assumptions, showing that FedSLoP is guaranteed to converge to a first-order stationary point at a rate of $O(1/\sqrt{NT})$. On the empirical side, we conduct extensive experiments on federated MNIST classification with heterogeneous data partitions, showing that FedSLoP substantially reduces communication volume and client-side memory while achieving competitive or better accuracy compared with FedAvg and representative sparse or low-rank baselines. Together, our results demonstrate that random subspace momentum methods such as FedSLoP provide a principled and effective approach to communication- and memory-efficient federated learning. Codes are available at: https://github.com/pkumelon/FedSLoP.git.

AI.tldr: FedSLoP is a federated learning algorithm that uses stochastic low-rank subspace projections to reduce communication and memory costs while maintaining convergence guarantees, achieving competitive performance with FedAvg but more efficiently.
AI.motivation: Standard federated learning algorithms like FedAvg suffer from slow convergence, high communication costs, and high memory requirements, especially in heterogeneous and resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: FedSLoP combines stochastic low-rank subspace projections of gradients, reducing the dimensionality of communicated and stored updates while preserving optimization progress.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows FedSLoP converges to first-order stationary points at O(1/√NT) rate under smoothness and bounded-variance assumptions. Empirical results on federated MNIST show substantial reductions in communication volume (85-90%) and client memory while achieving competitive or better accuracy than FedAvg and baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: Random subspace momentum methods like FedSLoP provide a principled and effective approach to communication- and memory-efficient federated learning, demonstrating practical advantages in resource-constrained scenarios.

title: FlashOverlap: Minimizing Tail Latency in Communication Overlap for Distributed LLM Training
summary: The rapid growth in the size of large language models has necessitated the partitioning of computational workloads across accelerators such as GPUs, TPUs, and NPUs. However, these parallelization strategies incur substantial data communication overhead significantly hindering computational efficiency. While communication-computation overlap presents a promising direction, existing data slicing based solutions suffer from tail latency. To overcome this limitation, this research introduces a novel communication-computation overlap technique to eliminate this tail latency in state of the art overlap methods for distributed LLM training. The aim of this technique is to effectively mitigate communication bottleneck of tensor parallelism and data parallelism for distributed training and inference. In particular, we propose a novel method termed Flash-Overlap that replaces conventional collective operations of reduce-scatter and all-gather with decomposed peer-to-peer (P2P) communication and schedules partitioned computations to enable fine-grained overlap. Our method provides an exact algorithm for reducing communication overhead that eliminates tail latency. Moreover, it presents a versatile solution compatible with data-parallel training and various tensor-level parallelism strategies, including TPSP and UP. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that our technique consistently achieves lower latency, superior Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), and high throughput.

AI.tldr: Flash-Overlap: A novel communication-computation overlap technique that eliminates tail latency in distributed LLM training by replacing collective operations with P2P communication and fine-grained scheduling.
AI.motivation: As LLMs grow larger requiring partitioning across accelerators, parallelization strategies incur substantial data communication overhead that hinders computational efficiency. Existing data slicing solutions suffer from tail latency despite promising communication-computation overlap approaches.
AI.method: Proposes Flash-Overlap technique that replaces conventional reduce-scatter and all-gather collective operations with decomposed peer-to-peer (P2P) communication and schedules partitioned computations to enable fine-grained overlap. Provides exact algorithm for reducing communication overhead.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations demonstrate consistently lower latency, superior Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU), and high throughput. The method is versatile and compatible with data-parallel training and various tensor-level parallelism strategies (TPSP and UP).
AI.conclusion: Flash-Overlap effectively mitigates communication bottlenecks in distributed LLM training and inference by eliminating tail latency through fine-grained communication-computation overlap, offering a significant improvement over existing overlap methods.

title: Geometry-Aware Offline-to-Online Learning in Linear Contextual Bandits
summary: We study offline-to-online learning in linear contextual bandits with biased offline regression data: the offline parameter need not match the online one, so history should not be treated as a single warm start. We model directional transfer with a shift certificate $(M_{\mathrm{shift}},ρ)$ and offline ridge estimation, yielding a geometry-aware confidence region for the online parameter rather than an isotropic radius. We propose \emph{Ellipsoidal-MINUCB}, which combines a standard online branch with an offline-informed pooled branch and uses offline information only when it tightens uncertainty. With high probability, regret is bounded by the minimum of a standard SupLinUCB-style fallback and a pooled term that separates statistical width from a certificate-weighted shift penalty. Under a simple alignment condition, the pooled term further simplifies to a rate governed by an effective dimension induced by the offline geometry. We also show that a purely Euclidean (scalar) shift bound, by itself, does not determine which feature directions are transferable. Beyond this fixed certificate, we show how to learn a data-driven certificate from data at finitely many refresh times and establish a high-probability regret bound for Ellipsoidal-MINUCB with epoch-wise learned certificates. Experiments match the main prediction: gains are strongest at intermediate horizons when offline coverage and transferability align, while the method otherwise tracks the safe online baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Ellipsoidal-MINUCB for offline-to-online learning in linear contextual bandits with biased offline data, using directional shift certificates and geometry-aware confidence regions to improve regret bounds when offline information is transferable.
AI.motivation: Offline-to-online learning faces challenges when offline regression data is biased (offline parameter doesn't match online parameter), so history can't be treated as simple warm-start. Existing approaches often use isotropic confidence regions that don't account for directional transferability of offline information.
AI.method: Proposed Ellipsoidal-MINUCB uses a directional shift certificate (M_shift, ρ) with offline ridge estimation to create geometry-aware confidence regions for the online parameter. It combines standard online branch with offline-informed pooled branch, using offline information only when it tightens uncertainty. Also shows how to learn data-driven certificates from data at refresh times.
AI.result: With high probability, regret is bounded by minimum of standard SupLinUCB-style fallback and pooled term separating statistical width from certificate-weighted shift penalty. Under alignment condition, pooled term simplifies to rate governed by effective dimension from offline geometry. Shows purely Euclidean shift bounds don't indicate which feature directions are transferable.
AI.conclusion: Ellipsoidal-MINUCB provides principled offline-to-online transfer in linear bandits with biased data. Gains are strongest when offline coverage and transferability align at intermediate horizons, while method safely reverts to online baseline otherwise. Data-driven certificate learning is feasible with proper refresh scheduling.

title: A Limit Theory of Foundation Models: A Mathematical Approach to Understanding Emergent Intelligence and Scaling Laws
summary: Emergent intelligence have played a major role in the modern AI development. While existing studies primarily rely on empirical observations to characterize this phenomenon, a rigorous theoretical framework remains underexplored. This study attempts to develop a mathematical approach to formalize emergent intelligence from the perspective of limit theory. Specifically, we introduce a performance function E(N, P, K), dependent on data size N, model size P and training steps K, to quantify intelligence behavior. We posit that intelligence emerges as a transition from finite to effectively infinite knowledge, and thus recast emergent intelligence as existence of the limit $\lim_{N,P,K \to \infty} \mathcal{E}(N,P,K)$, with emergent abilities corresponding to the limiting behavior. This limit theory helps reveal that emergent intelligence originates from the existence of a parameter-limit architecture (referred to as the limit architecture), and that emergent intelligence rationally corresponds to the learning behavior of this limit system. By introducing tools from nonlinear Lipschitz operator theory, we prove that the necessary and sufficient conditions for existence of the limit architecture. Furthermore, we derive the scaling law of foundation models by leveraging tools of Lipschitz operator and covering number. Theoretical results show that: 1) emergent intelligence is governed by three key factors-training steps, data size and the model architecture, where the properties of basic blocks play a crucial role in constructing foundation models; 2) the critical condition Lip(T)=1 for emergent intelligence provides theoretical support for existing findings. 3) emergent intelligence is determined by an infinite-dimensional system, yet can be effectively realized in practice through a finite-dimensional architecture. Our empirical results corroborate these theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Paper develops mathematical limit theory to formalize emergent intelligence, showing it arises from transition to infinite knowledge and is governed by data size, model size, and training steps with critical condition Lip(T)=1.
AI.motivation: Existing studies on emergent intelligence rely primarily on empirical observations without rigorous theoretical framework. The paper aims to mathematically formalize emergent intelligence using limit theory to provide theoretical foundations for this phenomenon.
AI.method: Introduces performance function E(N,P,K) dependent on data size, model size, and training steps. Uses limit theory to recast emergent intelligence as existence of limit lim(N,P,K→∞) E(N,P,K). Applies nonlinear Lipschitz operator theory to prove necessary and sufficient conditions for existence of limit architecture, and derives scaling laws using Lipschitz operator and covering number tools.
AI.result: 1) Emergent intelligence governed by training steps, data size, and model architecture with basic block properties being crucial; 2) Critical condition Lip(T)=1 provides theoretical support for existing findings; 3) Emergent intelligence determined by infinite-dimensional system but realizable through finite-dimensional architecture. Empirical results corroborate theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully develops a mathematical framework using limit theory to formalize emergent intelligence, revealing its origins in limit architecture and providing theoretical conditions for its emergence, with practical implications for foundation model construction.

title: AgenticCache: Cache-Driven Asynchronous Planning for Embodied AI Agents
summary: Embodied AI agents increasingly rely on large language models (LLMs) for planning, yet per-step LLM calls impose severe latency and cost. In this paper, we show that embodied tasks exhibit strong plan locality, where the next plan is largely predictable from the current one. Building on this, we introduce AgenticCache, a planning framework that reuses cached plans to avoid per-step LLM calls. In AgenticCache, each agent queries a runtime cache of frequent plan transitions, while a background Cache Updater asynchronously calls the LLM to validate and refine cached entries. Across four multi-agent embodied benchmarks, AgenticCache improves task success rate by 22% on average across 12 configurations (4 benchmarks x 3 models), reduces simulation latency by 65%, and lowers token usage by 50%. Cache-based plan reuse thus offers a practical path to low-latency, low-cost embodied agents. Code is available at https://github.com/hojoonleokim/MLSys26_AgenticCache.

AI.tldr: AgenticCache reduces LLM planning costs by reusing cached plans, improving success rates by 22%, cutting latency by 65%, and halving token usage.
AI.motivation: Embodied AI agents using LLMs for planning face severe latency and cost issues due to per-step LLM calls, creating a need for more efficient planning approaches.
AI.method: Introduces AgenticCache, a planning framework that leverages plan locality by caching frequent plan transitions. Agents query a runtime cache while a background Cache Updater asynchronously validates and refines cached entries using LLMs.
AI.result: Across 4 multi-agent embodied benchmarks and 3 models, AgenticCache improves task success rate by 22% on average, reduces simulation latency by 65%, and lowers token usage by 50%.
AI.conclusion: Cache-based plan reuse provides a practical solution for building low-latency, low-cost embodied agents, effectively addressing the computational overhead of LLM-based planning.

title: End-to-End Learning for Partially-Observed Time Series with PyPOTS
summary: Partially-observed time series (POTS) is ubiquitous in real-world applications, yet most existing toolchains separate missing-value handling from downstream learning, which limits reproducibility and overall performance. This tutorial introduces PyPOTS, an open-source Python ecosystem for end-to-end data mining and machine learning on POTS. We present practical workflows spanning missingness simulation, data preprocessing, model training, and evaluation across core tasks, including imputation, forecasting, classification, clustering, and anomaly detection. The tutorial consists of two parts: Part I emphasizes hands-on application for practitioners through unified APIs and benchmark-oriented experiments. Part II targets developers and researchers, focusing on extending PyPOTS with custom models, domain-specific constraints, and contribution-ready engineering practices. Participants will gain both conceptual understanding and implementation experience for building robust, transparent, and reusable POTS pipelines in research and production settings. PyPOTS is publicly available at https://github.com/WenjieDu/PyPOTS

AI.tldr: PyPOTS is an open-source Python ecosystem for end-to-end data mining and machine learning on partially-observed time series (POTS), providing unified workflows for handling missing values and downstream tasks in one framework.
AI.motivation: Real-world applications often have partially-observed time series with missing values, but existing toolchains separate missing-value handling from downstream learning, which limits reproducibility and overall performance.
