AI.method: PyPOTS provides a comprehensive ecosystem with: 1) Missingness simulation capabilities, 2) Data preprocessing tools, 3) Model training for multiple tasks (imputation, forecasting, classification, clustering, anomaly detection), and 4) Evaluation frameworks. It offers unified APIs and supports custom model development.
AI.result: The tutorial provides a two-part structure: Part I offers hands-on application for practitioners with benchmark-oriented experiments, while Part II targets developers/researchers for extending PyPOTS with custom models and contribution-ready practices.
AI.conclusion: PyPOTS enables building robust, transparent, and reusable POTS pipelines for both research and production settings, addressing the gap between missing-value handling and downstream learning in time series analysis.

title: Generalising maximum mean discrepancy: kernelised functional Bregman divergences
summary: Bregman divergences play a pivotal role in statistics, machine learning and computational information geometry. Particularly in the context of machine learning, they are central to clustering, exponential families, parameter estimation and optimisation, among other things. Despite this, the full toolkit of Hilbert spaces and in particular reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces have not been systematically developed and applied to functional Bregman divergences, where points are functions rather than finite-dimensional parameter vectors. While other types of functional Bregman divergences have been studied, these are typically in a Banach space rather than more directly aligned with kernel methods and Hilbert-space geometry commonly used in machine learning. We consider functional Bregman divergences on a Hilbert space, where the self-dual pairing and Riesz representer afford us particularly convenient calculus. Further specialising Bregman generators as a composition involving a kernel mean embedding makes such divergences easy to estimate. We discuss applications in clustering, universal estimation, robust estimation and generative modelling, and contrast our approach with other types of Bregman divergences.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces functional Bregman divergences in Hilbert spaces, making them compatible with kernel methods and providing convenient calculus through self-dual pairing and Riesz representers.
AI.motivation: Bregman divergences are crucial in statistics and machine learning but lack systematic development in Hilbert spaces, particularly RKHS. Current functional Bregman divergences are typically in Banach spaces, not aligned with kernel methods and Hilbert-space geometry commonly used in ML.
AI.method: The authors consider functional Bregman divergences on Hilbert spaces, leveraging self-dual pairing and Riesz representers for convenient calculus. They specialize Bregman generators as compositions involving kernel mean embeddings to make divergences easy to estimate.
AI.result: The approach provides functional Bregman divergences that are compatible with kernel methods and offer practical estimation techniques. Applications discussed include clustering, universal estimation, robust estimation, and generative modeling.
AI.conclusion: This work bridges the gap between Bregman divergences and kernel methods by developing functional Bregman divergences in Hilbert spaces, offering a framework that aligns with modern machine learning approaches while maintaining practical estimability.

title: FreeScale: Distributed Training for Sequence Recommendation Models with Minimal Scaling Cost
comment: 14 pages, 11 figures. Accepted to the 9th MLSys Conference, Bellevue, WA, USA, 2026
summary: Modern industrial Deep Learning Recommendation Models typically extract user preferences through the analysis of sequential interaction histories, subsequently generating predictions based on these derived interests. The inherent heterogeneity in data characteristics frequently result in substantial under-utilization of computational resources during large-scale training, primarily due to computational bubbles caused by severe stragglers and slow blocking communications. This paper introduces FreeScale, a solution designed to (1) mitigate the straggler problem through meticulously load balanced input samples (2) minimize the blocking communication by overlapping prioritized embedding communications with computations (3) resolve the GPU resource competition during computation and communication overlapping by communicating through SM-Free techniques. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that FreeScale achieves up to 90.3% reduction in computational bubbles when applied to real-world workloads running on 256 H100 GPUs.

AI.tldr: FreeScale is a system that reduces computational bubbles in deep learning recommendation models by balancing input loads, overlapping communications with computations, and using SM-Free techniques for GPU communication.
AI.motivation: Modern industrial Deep Learning Recommendation Models suffer from computational inefficiency due to heterogeneous data characteristics causing severe stragglers and slow blocking communications, leading to substantial under-utilization of computational resources during large-scale training.
AI.method: FreeScale uses three key techniques: (1) meticulously load balanced input samples to mitigate the straggler problem, (2) overlapping prioritized embedding communications with computations to minimize blocking communication, and (3) SM-Free techniques for communication to resolve GPU resource competition during computation and communication overlapping.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that FreeScale achieves up to 90.3% reduction in computational bubbles when applied to real-world workloads running on 256 H100 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: FreeScale effectively addresses the computational inefficiency problem in large-scale training of deep learning recommendation models through innovative techniques that balance loads, overlap operations, and optimize GPU resource usage.

title: Explaining Temporal Graph Predictions With Shapley Values
summary: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) have become increasingly popular in recent years due to their superior predictive performance by combining both spatial and temporal information. However, how these models utilize the information to make predictions is rather unexplored, leading to potentially faulty or biased models. This work introduces two novel model-agnostic explainers for local explanations of TGNNs based on Shapley and Owen values. The first method, an event-level (edge-level) Shapley explainer, applies the KernelSHAP algorithm to estimate contribution scores for individual temporal events, providing interpretable descriptions for model behavior. The second, a feature-level Shapley explainer, extends this framework by decomposing event-level Shapley values into Owen values, and thereby uncovers hierarchical dependencies of the event and its features. The explainers outperform SOTA explainers on different metrics and datasets. Additionally, the Feature Explainer reveals a faulty extraction of actual timestamps of a commonly used TGAT implementation, helping to further understand performance drops on very sparse explanations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces two model-agnostic explainers for Temporal Graph Neural Networks based on Shapley and Owen values for event-level and feature-level explanations, outperforming SOTA methods and revealing implementation issues.
AI.motivation: TGNNs have shown superior performance but lack understanding of how they use spatial and temporal information for predictions, potentially leading to faulty or biased models that need explanation methods.
AI.method: Two novel model-agnostic explainers: 1) Event-level Shapley explainer using KernelSHAP algorithm to estimate contribution scores for individual temporal events. 2) Feature-level Shapley explainer that decomposes event-level Shapley values into Owen values to uncover hierarchical dependencies between events and their features.
AI.result: The explainers outperform state-of-the-art explainers on different metrics and datasets. The Feature Explainer revealed a faulty extraction of actual timestamps in a commonly used TGAT implementation, helping understand performance drops on sparse explanations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Shapley and Owen value-based explainers provide effective local explanations for TGNNs, improve interpretability, and can identify implementation issues, contributing to more trustworthy temporal graph neural network models.

title: Meta-Ensemble Learning with Diverse Data Splits for Improved Respiratory Sound Classification
summary: Training reliable respiratory sound classification models remains challenging due to the limited size and subject diversity of datasets. Ensemble methods can improve robustness, but when base models are trained on identical data, models tend to overfit and produce highly correlated predictions, thereby reducing the effectiveness of ensembling. In this work, we investigate a meta-ensemble learning methodology that enhances prediction diversity by training base models on diverse data splits and combining their outputs through a trained meta-model. Specifically, we train base models on the ICBHI dataset using two data split settings: fixed 80-20% split and five-fold cross-validation split, under two data granularity settings: patient- and sample-level. The resulting diversity in base model predictions enables the meta-model to better generalize. Our approach achieves new state-of-the-art performance on the ICBHI benchmark, reaching a Score of 66.49% and showing improved generalization on two out-of-distribution datasets, indicating its potential applicability to real-world clinical data.

AI.tldr: Meta-ensemble learning improves respiratory sound classification by training base models on diverse data splits to increase prediction diversity, achieving state-of-the-art ICBHI performance and better generalization.
AI.motivation: Respiratory sound classification models face challenges due to limited dataset size and subject diversity. Traditional ensemble methods often fail because base models trained on identical data overfit and produce correlated predictions, reducing ensemble effectiveness.
AI.method: Meta-ensemble learning methodology that enhances prediction diversity by training base models on diverse data splits (fixed 80-20% split and five-fold cross-validation) at two granularity levels (patient- and sample-level), then combining outputs through a trained meta-model.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance on ICBHI benchmark with Score of 66.49%, and shows improved generalization on two out-of-distribution datasets.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively addresses overfitting and prediction correlation in ensemble methods, demonstrating potential applicability to real-world clinical data through improved generalization to out-of-distribution datasets.

title: Fed-DLoRA: Efficient Wireless Federated Learning with Dynamic Low-Rank Adaptation
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures. Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: Federated learning (FL) offers a promising distributed learning paradigm for internet of vehicles (IoV) applications. However, it faces challenges from communication overhead and dynamic environments. Model compression techniques reduce computing and communication burden yet create trade-offs between compression ratios and vehicle participation strategies. In this paper, we propose a lightweight FL algorithm named federated learning with dynamic low-rank adaptation (Fed-DLoRA), which is combined with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to effectively reduce parameters and communication costs while enhancing training efficiency. The convergence analysis of Fed-DLoRA is conducted through stochastic gradient descent optimization coupled with singular value decomposition. This analysis establishes the theoretical relationships among LoRA rank, vehicular scheduling strategies and the model's convergence characteristics. Building on these insights, we formulate a joint optimization problem aimed at maximizing system performance. To address this problem, we propose an adaptive rank, bandwidth and vehicle selection (ARBVS) algorithm that integrates enumeration with greedy optimization strategies. The algorithm provides efficient rank selection and resource scheduling strategies for each FL communication round, thereby achieving effective performance improvements for the FL system. Experimental results demonstrate that Fed-DLoRA achieves superior performance compared to conventional federated learning approaches, exhibiting enhanced accuracy, faster convergence, and improved communication efficiency.

AI.tldr: Fed-DLoRA combines lightweight federated learning with low-rank adaptation to reduce communication overhead in IoT vehicle networks while maintaining model performance through adaptive resource scheduling.
AI.motivation: Federated learning for internet of vehicles faces challenges from communication overhead and dynamic environments, creating trade-offs between compression ratios and vehicle participation strategies that need to be optimized.
AI.method: Propose Fed-DLoRA algorithm combining federated learning with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to reduce parameters and communication costs. Analyze convergence using SGD with SVD, formulate joint optimization problem, and develop ARBVS algorithm for adaptive rank/bandwidth/vehicle selection using enumeration and greedy strategies.
AI.result: Fed-DLoRA achieves superior performance over conventional FL approaches with enhanced accuracy, faster convergence, and improved communication efficiency through efficient rank selection and resource scheduling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Fed-DLoRA framework with adaptive resource scheduling effectively addresses communication challenges in vehicular FL, providing a practical solution for efficient distributed learning in dynamic IoT environments.

title: Leveraging Human Feedback for Semantically-Relevant Skill Discovery
comment: Accepted at the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026)
summary: Unsupervised skill discovery in reinforcement learning aims to intrinsically motivate agents to discover diverse and useful behaviours. However, unconstrained approaches can produce unsafe, unethical, or misaligned behaviours. To mitigate these risks and improve the practical desireability of discovered skills, recent work grounds the discovery process by leveraging human preference feedback. However, preference-based approaches are feedback-inefficient and inherently ill-equipped to deal with skill spaces composed of a variety of different skills such as running, jumping, walking, etc. To overcome this limitation, we introduce semantic labelling, a novel and feedback-efficient approach that leverages human cognitive strengths to identify and label semantically meaningful behaviours. Based on semantic labelling, we propose Semantically Relevant Skill Discovery (SRSD), a novel human-in-the-loop approach that collects semantic labels from human feedback and learns a reward function to encourage skills to be more semantically diverse and relevant. Through our experiments in a 2D navigation environment and four locomotion environments, we demonstrate that SRSD can improve semantic diversity and discover relevant behaviours while scaling effectively to a large variety of behaviours.

AI.tldr: SRSD introduces semantic labeling and human feedback to guide unsupervised skill discovery toward safe, ethical, and semantically diverse behaviors.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised skill discovery can produce unsafe/unethical behaviors, and preference-based approaches are feedback-inefficient and struggle with diverse skill spaces.
AI.method: Semantic labeling leverages human cognitive strengths to identify/label behaviors; SRSD collects semantic labels and learns a reward function to encourage semantic diversity and relevance.
AI.result: Experiments in 2D navigation and four locomotion environments show SRSD improves semantic diversity, discovers relevant behaviors, and scales effectively to large behavior varieties.
AI.conclusion: SRSD addresses safety/ethical concerns in unsupervised skill discovery while being feedback-efficient and capable of handling diverse skill spaces through human semantic labeling.

title: Machine-Learning-Based Classification of Radio Frequency Building Loss
comment: Accepted as a short paper in International Conference on Telecommunications (ICT) 2026
summary: Accurate modeling of outdoor-to-indoor (O2I) and indoor-to-indoor (I2I) signal loss is important for improving indoor wireless network performance in dense urban areas. Traditional on-site measurements are expensive, time-consuming, and difficult to conduct across wide regions. Real-world datasets also tend to be noisy and imbalanced, which makes signal loss prediction challenging. This study presents a machine learning framework for classifying radio frequency (RF) building loss. The framework combines passively collected, crowdsourced user equipment (UE) data from 3GPP-compliant networks with public building information. We evaluated Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and a voting classifier using both supervised (SL) and semi-supervised learning (SSL). Compared to SL-only inference, the proposed SL and SSL framework improved both prediction accuracy and confidence under identical data constraints, achieving up to 12.6% relative accuracy gain for O2I loss and 3.4% for I2I loss, while reducing prediction entropy by up to 8.4%. Among the evaluated models, SSL XGBoost provided the most confident O2I loss classification, whereas SSL LightGBM achieved the best performance for I2I loss. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach provides a practical, data-driven alternative to traditional models, with promising potential to support better network planning and indoor coverage optimization.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework using crowdsourced UE data and building information for classifying RF building loss (O2I and I2I), combining supervised and semi-supervised learning to outperform traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional on-site RF signal loss measurements for outdoor-to-indoor (O2I) and indoor-to-indoor (I2I) scenarios are expensive, time-consuming, and difficult to conduct at scale. Real-world datasets are noisy and imbalanced, making signal loss prediction challenging.
AI.method: A machine learning framework that combines passively collected, crowdsourced user equipment (UE) data from 3GPP-compliant networks with public building information. Evaluated Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and a voting classifier using both supervised (SL) and semi-supervised learning (SSL).
AI.result: The proposed SL+SSL framework improved prediction accuracy and confidence under identical data constraints, achieving up to 12.6% relative accuracy gain for O2I loss and 3.4% for I2I loss, while reducing prediction entropy by up to 8.4%. SSL XGBoost provided the most confident O2I loss classification, while SSL LightGBM achieved the best performance for I2I loss.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a practical, data-driven alternative to traditional models with promising potential to support better network planning and indoor coverage optimization, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining supervised and semi-supervised learning with crowdsourced data.

title: Progressive Approximation in Deep Residual Networks: Theory and Validation
summary: The Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT) guarantees universal function approximation but does not explain how residual models distribute approximation across layers. We reframe residual networks as a layer-wise approximation process that builds an approximation trajectory from input to target, and prove the existence of progressive trajectories where error decreases monotonically with depth. It reveals that residual networks can implement structured, step-by-step refinement rather than end-to-end (E2E) black-box mapping. Building on this, we propose Layer-wise Progressive Approximation (LPA), a theoretically grounded training principle that explicitly aligns each layer with its residual target to realize such trajectories. LPA is architecture-agnostic: we observe progressive behavior in residual FNNs, ResNets, and Transformers across tasks including complex surface fitting, image classification, and NLP with LLMs for generation and classification. Crucially, this enables ``train once, use $N$ models": a single network yields useful predictions at every depth, supporting efficient shallow inference without retraining. Our work unifies approximation theory with practical deep learning, providing a new lens on representation learning and a flexible framework for multi-depth deployment. The source code will be released unpon acceptance at https://(open\_upon\_acceptance).

AI.tldr: Residual networks can be viewed as progressive layer-wise approximation where error decreases monotonically with depth, enabling a single network to provide useful predictions at every layer for efficient shallow inference.
AI.motivation: The Universal Approximation Theorem explains that neural networks can approximate functions but doesn't clarify how residual networks distribute approximation across layers. The authors want to understand the layer-wise approximation process in residual networks and leverage this for practical benefits like efficient shallow inference.
AI.method: Reframe residual networks as layer-wise approximation processes building trajectories from input to target. Propose Layer-wise Progressive Approximation (LPA) training principle that explicitly aligns each layer with its residual target to create progressive trajectories where error decreases monotonically. Test LPA across architectures (residual FNNs, ResNets, Transformers) and tasks (surface fitting, image classification, NLP with LLMs).
AI.result: Shows existence of progressive trajectories in residual networks where error decreases monotonically with depth. LPA enables "train once, use N models" - a single network yields useful predictions at every depth, supporting efficient shallow inference without retraining. Works across diverse architectures and tasks.
AI.conclusion: Residual networks can implement structured step-by-step refinement rather than end-to-end black-box mapping. This unifies approximation theory with practical deep learning, provides new insights into representation learning, and offers flexible framework for multi-depth deployment with practical efficiency benefits.

title: Meta-Aligner: Bidirectional Preference-Policy Optimization for Multi-Objective LLMs Alignment
summary: Multi-Objective Alignment aims to align Large Language Models (LLMs) with diverse and often conflicting human values by optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously. Existing methods predominantly rely on static preference weight construction strategies. However, rigidly aligning to fixed targets discards valuable intermediate information, as training responses inherently embody valid preference trade-offs even when deviating from the target. To address this limitation, we propose Meal, i.e., MEta ALigner, a bi-level meta-learning framework enabling bidirectional optimization between preferences and policy responses, generating instructive dynamic preferences for steadier training. Specifically, we introduce a preference-weight-net as a meta-learner to generate adaptive preference weights based on input prompts and update the preference weights as learnable parameters, while the LLM policy acts as a base-learner optimizing response generation conditioned on these preferences with rejection sampling strategy. Extensive empirical results demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on several multi-objective benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of the dynamic bidirectional preference-policy optimization framework.

AI.tldr: Meal is a meta-learning framework that enables dynamic bidirectional optimization between preferences and policy responses for multi-objective LLM alignment, generating adaptive preference weights instead of using fixed targets.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-objective alignment methods use static preference weight strategies that discard valuable intermediate information from training responses, even when they deviate from target preferences. Rigid alignment to fixed targets fails to capture valid preference trade-offs inherent in training responses.
AI.method: Proposes Meal (MEta ALigner), a bi-level meta-learning framework with: (1) preference-weight-net as meta-learner generating adaptive preference weights based on input prompts, updated as learnable parameters; (2) LLM policy as base-learner optimizing response generation conditioned on these preferences using rejection sampling strategy.
AI.result: Extensive empirical results demonstrate superior performance on several multi-objective benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of the dynamic bidirectional preference-policy optimization framework.
AI.conclusion: The Meal framework successfully addresses limitations of static preference alignment by enabling dynamic bidirectional optimization, leading to better multi-objective alignment performance through adaptive preference weighting.

title: CMGL: Confidence-guided Multi-omics Graph Learning for Cancer Subtype Classification
comment: 24 pages, 15 figures, 13 tables, 2 algorithms (main paper + supplementary materials)
summary:
Motivation: Multi-omics integration can improve cancer subtyping, but modality informativeness and noise vary across cancer types and patients. Existing graph-based methods optimize modality weights jointly with the classification objective and therefore lack independent reliability estimates, so low-quality omics distort patient similarity graphs and amplify noise through message passing.
  Results: We propose CMGL, a two-stage framework that estimates per-sample modality reliability through evidential deep learning and uses the frozen confidence scores to guide cross-omics fusion and graph construction. On four MLOmics cancer-subtype tasks and the 32-class pan-cancer task, CMGL consistently improves over the strongest baseline, surpassing it by 4.03% in average accuracy on the four single-cancer tasks. Its representations recover the PAM50 intrinsic subtypes of breast invasive carcinoma (BRCA), and the BRCA-trained model transfers without fine-tuning to kidney renal clear cell carcinoma (KIRC), stratifying patients into prognostically distinct groups.

AI.tldr: CMGL is a two-stage multi-omics cancer subtyping framework that first estimates per-sample modality reliability using evidential deep learning, then uses frozen confidence scores to guide cross-omics fusion and graph construction, outperforming baselines on cancer subtype classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Current graph-based multi-omics integration methods optimize modality weights jointly with classification, lacking independent reliability estimates. This causes low-quality omics data to distort patient similarity graphs and amplify noise through message passing, reducing subtyping accuracy.
AI.method: CMGL uses a two-stage approach: 1) employs evidential deep learning to estimate per-sample modality reliability scores, 2) uses these frozen confidence scores to guide cross-omics fusion and construct patient similarity graphs, enabling noise-resistant integration.
AI.result: CMGL consistently outperformed strongest baselines on four MLOmics cancer-subtype tasks and 32-class pan-cancer task, achieving 4.03% higher average accuracy on single-cancer tasks. It successfully recovered PAM50 BRCA subtypes and transferred without fine-tuning to KIRC, identifying prognostically distinct patient groups.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage reliability estimation and fusion approach of CMGL effectively addresses modality noise issues in multi-omics cancer subtyping, improving accuracy across cancer types and enabling model transferability across different cancer datasets.

title: IMPA-Net: Meteorology-Aware Multi-Scale Attention and Dynamic Loss for Extreme Convective Radar Nowcasting
summary: Short-range prediction of convective precipitation from weather radar observations is essential for severe weather warnings. However, deep learning models trained with pixel-wise error metrics tend to produce overly smooth forecasts that suppress intense echoes critical for hazard detection. This issue is exacerbated by insufficient multi-scale feature interaction and suboptimal fusion of heterogeneous geophysical inputs. We propose IMPA-Net (Integrated Multi-scale Predictive Attention Network), a deterministic 0-2 hour nowcasting framework that addresses these limitations through meteorologically-informed designs at the input, architecture, and loss function levels. A parameter-free Spatial Mixer reorganizes heterogeneous input channels at the mesoscale-$γ$ neighborhood (~2 km) via deterministic channel permutation, providing a structured cross-field prior. An integrated multi-scale predictive attention module serves as the spatiotemporal translator, capturing dynamics from mesoscale-$β$ to mesoscale-$γ$ scales. A Meteorologically-Aware Dynamic Loss employs three-level asymmetric weighting -- adapting across training epochs, storm intensity, and forecast lead time -- to counteract regression-to-the-mean. Evaluated against seven baselines on a multi-source radar dataset over eastern China, IMPA-Net raises the Heidke Skill Score at $\geq$45 dBZ from 0.049 (SimVP baseline) to 0.143 under matched settings. Relative to pySTEPS, it provides a better trade-off between severe-event detection and false-alarm control. Spectral analysis confirms preserved energy across mesoscale bands where competing methods show progressive smoothing. These improvements are shown within a single domain and convective regime; generalizability to other orographic and climatic regions remains to be tested.

AI.tldr: IMPA-Net is a deep learning framework for 0-2 hour precipitation nowcasting that addresses overly smooth forecasts by incorporating meteorological awareness through multi-scale attention, spatial mixing of inputs, and dynamic loss weighting.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models for radar-based precipitation nowcasting tend to produce overly smooth forecasts that suppress intense echoes critical for severe weather warnings. Issues include insufficient multi-scale feature interaction and suboptimal fusion of heterogeneous geophysical inputs.
AI.method: IMPA-Net uses three meteorologically-informed designs: 1) Spatial Mixer for deterministic channel permutation at mesoscale-γ (~2 km), 2) integrated multi-scale predictive attention module capturing dynamics from mesoscale-β to mesoscale-γ scales, and 3) Meteorologically-Aware Dynamic Loss with three-level asymmetric weighting across epochs, storm intensity, and forecast lead time.
AI.result: Evaluated on multi-source radar data over eastern China, IMPA-Net improved Heidke Skill Score at ≥45 dBZ from 0.049 (SimVP baseline) to 0.143. It provides better trade-off between severe-event detection and false-alarm control compared to pySTEPS, with spectral analysis showing preserved energy across mesoscale bands.
AI.conclusion: IMPA-Net effectively addresses regression-to-the-mean in precipitation nowcasting through meteorological awareness at input, architecture, and loss levels, showing significant improvements in severe echo prediction while maintaining improvements within tested domain and convective regime.

title: GeoEdit: Local Frames for Fast, Training-Free On-Manifold Editing in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models are a leading paradigm for data generation, but training-free editing typically re-runs the full denoising trajectory for every edit strength, making iterative refinement expensive. To address this issue, we instead edit near the data manifold, where small local updates can replace repeated re-synthesis. To enable this, we estimate a local manifold tangent space directly from perturbed samples and prove that this sample-based estimator closely approximates the true tangent. Building on this guarantee, we devise a Jacobian-free algorithm that constructs a tangent frame via small perturbations to the initial noise and alternates small tangent moves with diffusion-based projections. Updates within this frame follow principled on-manifold directions while suppressing off-manifold drift, enabling fine-grained edits without full re-diffusion or additional training. Edit strength is controlled by the number of steps for rapid, continuous adjustments that preserve fidelity and plug into existing samplers. Empirically, the resulting tangent directions yield smooth, semantic unsupervised traversals and effective CLIP-guided optimization, demonstrating practical interactive continuous editing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a tangent-space editing method for diffusion models that enables efficient, training-free editing by operating near the data manifold rather than re-running full denoising trajectories.
AI.motivation: Traditional diffusion model editing requires re-running the full denoising trajectory for every edit strength, making iterative refinement computationally expensive and inefficient for interactive editing scenarios.
AI.method: The method estimates local manifold tangent spaces directly from perturbed samples, proves this sample-based estimator approximates the true tangent, and develops a Jacobian-free algorithm that constructs a tangent frame via small perturbations to initial noise, then alternates between small tangent moves and diffusion-based projections.
AI.result: The approach enables fine-grained edits without full re-diffusion or additional training, with edit strength controlled by step count for rapid continuous adjustments. Empirically, tangent directions yield smooth semantic unsupervised traversals and effective CLIP-guided optimization for practical interactive editing.
AI.conclusion: By editing near the data manifold using local tangent spaces, the method provides an efficient, training-free approach for continuous, interactive editing of diffusion models that preserves fidelity and integrates with existing samplers.

title: BitRL: Reinforcement Learning with 1-bit Quantized Language Models for Resource-Constrained Edge Deployment
comment: 6pages, 1 Figure, IEEE International Conference of Frontiers of Engineering and Emerging Technologies 2026
summary:
The deployment of intelligent reinforcement learning (RL) agents on resource-constrained edge devices remains a fundamental challenge due to the substantial memory, computational, and energy requirements of modern deep learning systems. While large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful architectures for decision-making agents, their multi-billion parameter scale confines them to cloud-based deployment, raising concerns about latency, privacy, and connectivity dependence.
  We introduce BitRL, a framework for building RL agents using 1-bit quantized language models that enables practical on-device learning and inference under severe resource constraints. Leveraging the BitNet b1.58 architecture with ternary weights (-1, 0, +1) and an optimized inference stack, BitRL achieves 10-16x memory reduction and 3-5x energy efficiency improvements over full-precision baselines while maintaining 85-98 percent of task performance across benchmarks.
  We provide theoretical analysis of quantization as structured parameter perturbation, derive convergence bounds for quantized policy gradients under frozen-backbone architectures, and identify the exploration-stability trade-off in extreme quantization. Our framework systematically integrates 1-bit quantized language models with reinforcement learning for edge deployment and demonstrates effectiveness on commodity hardware.

AI.tldr: BitRL is a framework for running RL agents on edge devices using 1-bit quantized language models, achieving massive memory/energy savings while maintaining task performance.
AI.motivation: Deploying intelligent RL agents on resource-constrained edge devices is challenging due to high memory, computational, and energy requirements of deep learning. LLMs are powerful but too large for edge deployment, causing latency, privacy, and connectivity issues in cloud-based setups.
AI.method: BitRL uses 1-bit quantized language models based on BitNet b1.58 architecture with ternary weights (-1, 0, +1) and an optimized inference stack. The approach includes theoretical analysis of quantization as structured parameter perturbation, convergence bounds for quantized policy gradients, and systematic integration of 1-bit LLMs with RL.
AI.result: Achieves 10-16x memory reduction and 3-5x energy efficiency improvements over full-precision baselines while maintaining 85-98% of task performance across benchmarks. Framework is effective on commodity hardware.
AI.conclusion: BitRL enables practical on-device learning and inference under severe resource constraints by systematically integrating 1-bit quantized language models with reinforcement learning, addressing the edge deployment challenge while maintaining competitive performance.

title: Model-Free Inference of Investor Preferences: A Relative Entropy IRL Approach
summary: We present a framework using Relative Entropy Inverse Reinforcement Learning (RE-IRL) to recover investor reward functions from observed investment actions and market conditions. Unlike traditional IRL algorithms, RE-IRL is employed to account for environments where transition probabilities are unknown or inaccessible. To address the challenge of data sparsity, we utilize a $K$-nearest neighbor approach to estimate the observed behavior policy. Furthermore, we propose a statistical testing framework to evaluate the validity and robustness of the estimated results.

AI.tldr: RE-IRL framework recovers investor reward functions from observed actions and market data, handles unknown transition probabilities, uses KNN for sparse data, and includes statistical validation tests.
AI.motivation: Traditional IRL algorithms require known transition probabilities, which are often unavailable in real-world investment scenarios. There's a need to recover investor reward functions from observed actions and market conditions when transition probabilities are unknown or inaccessible.
AI.method: Uses Relative Entropy Inverse Reinforcement Learning (RE-IRL) to handle environments with unknown transition probabilities. Employs K-nearest neighbor approach to estimate observed behavior policy to address data sparsity. Proposes a statistical testing framework to evaluate the validity and robustness of estimated results.
AI.result: Developed a complete framework for investor reward function recovery that works with unknown transition probabilities, handles sparse data through KNN estimation, and provides statistical validation methods for the estimated reward functions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RE-IRL framework provides a practical solution for recovering investor reward functions in real-world settings where transition probabilities are unavailable, addressing both data sparsity and statistical validation challenges.

title: Latent-Hysteresis Graph ODEs: Modeling Coupled Topology-Feature Evolution via Continuous Phase Transitions
summary: Graph neural ordinary differential equations (Graph ODEs) extend graph learning from discrete message-passing layers to continuous-time representation flows. While it supports adaptive long-range propagation, we show that Graph ODEs with strictly positive irreducible mixing operators face an inherent \emph{monostability trap}: in the long-time regime, information leakage is unavoidable and the dynamics converge to a single global consensus attractor. We propose the \textbf{Hysteresis Graph ODE (HGODE)}, which couples feature evolution with a latent topological potential driven by a learned pairwise force. A double-well edge potential and bipolarized gate allow edge states to polarize into connected or insulated phases while preserving differentiability. We provide asymptotic analysis of the collapse mechanism and the proposed hysteretic topology dynamics, and validate HGODE on theory-driven synthetic diagnostics and real-world graph benchmarks.

AI.tldr: HGODE prevents information collapse in Graph ODEs by introducing hysteretic topology dynamics with learnable edge potentials that can polarize into connected or insulated phases.
AI.motivation: Standard Graph ODEs with positive irreducible mixing operators suffer from a monostability trap where information leaks and the dynamics converge to a single global consensus attractor in the long-time regime, preventing effective long-range propagation.
AI.method: Proposes Hysteresis Graph ODE (HGODE) that couples feature evolution with a latent topological potential driven by learned pairwise forces. Uses a double-well edge potential and bipolarized gate to allow edge states to polarize into connected or insulated phases while preserving differentiability.
AI.result: Validated on theory-driven synthetic diagnostics and real-world graph benchmarks, showing improved performance by preventing information collapse through hysteretic topology dynamics.
AI.conclusion: HGODE successfully addresses the monostability trap in Graph ODEs by introducing hysteretic topology dynamics that can adaptively control information flow through learnable edge potentials, enabling effective long-range propagation without information leakage.

title: SolarTformer: A Transformer Based Deep Learning Approach for Short Term Solar Power Forecasting
summary: Accurate forecasting of solar power output is essential for efficient integration of renewable energy into the grid. In this study, an attention-based deep learning model, inspired by transformer architecture, is used for short-term solar power forecasting. Our proposed model, "SolarTformer", is designed to predict solar power output from meteorological data. Unlike traditional models, SolarTformer leverages self-attention mechanisms to effectively capture temporal dependencies and spatial variability in solar irradiance. In addition, the proposed methodology includes feeding power station-specific metadata into the model, which helps to generalize between power stations located at different locations and with different panel configurations and in different seasons. Our experiments demonstrate that SolarTformer significantly outperforms previous models on the same data set. In particular, the model exhibits strong performance on both clear and cloudy days, indicating high robustness and generalizability. These findings highlight the potential of attention-based architectures in enhancing the accuracy of solar forecasting, contributing to a more reliable management of renewable energy.

AI.tldr: SolarTformer, an attention-based transformer model for solar power forecasting, outperforms traditional models by effectively capturing temporal and spatial patterns and generalizing across different power station conditions.
AI.motivation: Accurate solar power forecasting is essential for efficient renewable energy grid integration, requiring models that can handle temporal dependencies, spatial variability, and generalize across different power station conditions.
AI.method: Developed "SolarTformer" - an attention-based deep learning model inspired by transformer architecture that uses self-attention mechanisms to capture temporal dependencies and spatial variability. The model incorporates power station-specific metadata to improve generalization across different locations, panel configurations, and seasons.
AI.result: SolarTformer significantly outperforms previous models on the same dataset, showing strong performance on both clear and cloudy days, demonstrating high robustness and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based architectures like SolarTformer have significant potential to enhance solar forecasting accuracy, contributing to more reliable renewable energy management through better handling of complex temporal-spatial patterns and station-specific variations.

title: Self-Abstraction Learning for Effective and Stable Training of Deep Neural Networks
comment: Submitted to IEEE Access. Under review
summary: Training large-scale deep neural networks effectively and stably is essential for applying deep learning across various fields. However, conventional methods, which rely on training a single large network, often encounter challenges such as gradient vanishing, overfitting and unstable learning. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Self-Abstraction Learning (SAL), a hierarchical framework. In SAL, networks are arranged by structural complexity, where the simplest topmost network is trained first and its hidden and output layers serve as guidance for the successively more complex networks below. This top-down sequential guidance effectively mitigates optimization issues, enabling stable training of deep architectures. Various experiments across MLP, CNN, and RNN architectures demonstrate that SAL consistently outperforms conventional methods, ensuring robust generalization even in data-scarce and complex network regimes.

AI.tldr: SAL is a hierarchical training framework where simpler networks train first and guide more complex ones in a top-down fashion, improving stability and performance over conventional single-network training.
AI.motivation: Training large-scale deep neural networks faces challenges like gradient vanishing, overfitting, and unstable learning with conventional single-network methods. The authors aim to overcome these optimization issues for more effective and stable training.
AI.method: Self-Abstraction Learning (SAL) arranges networks by structural complexity in a hierarchy. The simplest topmost network trains first, then its hidden and output layers guide training of successively more complex networks below in a top-down sequential guidance approach.
AI.result: Experiments across MLP, CNN, and RNN architectures show SAL consistently outperforms conventional methods. It ensures robust generalization even in data-scarce scenarios and with complex network architectures.
AI.conclusion: SAL's hierarchical, top-down sequential guidance effectively mitigates optimization issues in deep learning, enabling stable training of deep architectures and superior performance compared to conventional single-network training approaches.

title: Mitigating Error Amplification in Fast Adversarial Training
summary: Fast Adversarial Training (FAT) has proven effective in enhancing model robustness by encouraging networks to learn perturbation-invariant representations. However, FAT often suffers from catastrophic overfitting (CO), where the model overfits to the training attack and fails to generalize to unseen ones. Moreover, robustness oriented optimization typically leads to notable performance degradation on clean inputs, and such degradation becomes increasingly severe as the perturbation budget grows. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of how guidance strength affects model performance by modulating perturbation and supervision levels across distinct confidence groups. The findings reveal that low confidence samples are the primary contributors to CO and the robustness accuracy trade off. Building on this insight, we propose a Distribution-aware Dynamic Guidance (DDG) strategy that dynamically adjusts both the perturbation budget and supervision signal. Specifically, DDG scales the perturbation magnitude according to the sample confidence at the ground truth class, thereby guiding samples toward consistent decision boundaries while mitigating the influence of learning spurious correlations. Simultaneously, it dynamically adjusts the supervision signal based on the prediction state of each sample, preventing overemphasis on incorrect signals. To alleviate potential gradient instability arising from dynamic guidance, we further design a weighted regularization constraint. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that DDG effectively alleviates both CO and the robustness accuracy trade off.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DDG (Distribution-aware Dynamic Guidance), a method that dynamically adjusts perturbation budgets and supervision signals based on sample confidence to alleviate catastrophic overfitting and the robustness-accuracy trade-off in Fast Adversarial Training.
AI.motivation: Fast Adversarial Training suffers from catastrophic overfitting (where models overfit to training attacks) and severe performance degradation on clean inputs as perturbation budgets increase. The authors identify that low-confidence samples are primarily responsible for these issues in robustness training.
AI.method: The authors propose DDG which dynamically adjusts both perturbation budget (scaling magnitude based on sample confidence at ground truth class) and supervision signal (adjusting based on prediction state). They also design a weighted regularization constraint to address gradient instability from dynamic guidance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show that DDG effectively alleviates both catastrophic overfitting and the robustness-accuracy trade-off in adversarial training.
AI.conclusion: By analyzing sample confidence distributions and implementing dynamic adjustments to both perturbations and supervision, DDG provides an effective solution to key limitations of Fast Adversarial Training while maintaining better clean accuracy.

title: Perfecting Aircraft Maneuvers with Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper evaluates an advanced jet trainer's utilization of artificial intelligence (AI)-based aircraft aerobatic maneuvers with the intention of developing an AI-assisted pilot training module for specific aircraft maneuvers. A multitude of aircraft maneuvers have been simulated using reinforcement learning (RL) agents, which will serve as a training tool for future pilots.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed an AI-assisted pilot training module using reinforcement learning to simulate aerobatic maneuvers for jet trainer aircraft.
AI.motivation: To create an effective AI-assisted pilot training module for advanced jet trainers by developing realistic simulations of aircraft aerobatic maneuvers.
AI.method: Used reinforcement learning agents to simulate a multitude of aircraft aerobatic maneuvers through computational modeling and simulation techniques.
AI.result: Successfully developed simulated aerobatic maneuvers using RL agents that can serve as a training tool for future pilots.
AI.conclusion: AI-based reinforcement learning is a promising approach for developing pilot training modules for aerobatic maneuvers, potentially improving pilot training effectiveness and safety.

title: Unveiling the Backdoor Mechanism Hidden Behind Catastrophic Overfitting in Fast Adversarial Training
summary: Fast Adversarial Training (FAT) has attracted significant attention due to its efficiency in enhancing neural network robustness against adversarial attacks. However, FAT is prone to catastrophic overfitting (CO), wherein models overfit to the specific attack used during training and fail to generalize to others. While existing methods introduce diverse hypotheses and propose various strategies to mitigate CO, a systematic and intuitive explanation of CO remains absent. In this work, we innovatively interpret CO through the lens of backdoor. Through validations on pathway division, diverse feature predictions, and universal class distinguishable triggers in CO, we conceptualize CO as a weak trigger variant of unlearnable tasks, unifying CO, backdoor attacks, and unlearnable tasks under a common theoretical framework. Guided by this, we leverage several backdoor inspired strategies to mitigate CO: (i) Recalibrate CO affected model parameters using vanilla fine tuning, linear probing, or reinitialization-based techniques; (ii) Introduce a weight outlier suppression constraint to regulate abnormal deviations in model weights. Extensive experiments support our interpretation of CO and show the efficacy of the proposed mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: Fast Adversarial Training suffers from catastrophic overfitting where models overfit to training attacks. The paper interprets this as a backdoor-like phenomenon and proposes mitigation strategies inspired by backdoor defense techniques.
AI.motivation: Current Fast Adversarial Training methods are prone to catastrophic overfitting (CO) where models overfit to specific attacks used during training and fail to generalize to other attacks. Existing methods lack a systematic and intuitive explanation for this phenomenon.
AI.method: The authors interpret catastrophic overfitting through the lens of backdoor attacks, conceptualizing it as a weak trigger variant of unlearnable tasks. They propose mitigation strategies inspired by backdoor defense: 1) recalibrating affected model parameters using fine-tuning, linear probing, or reinitialization, and 2) introducing a weight outlier suppression constraint to regulate abnormal weight deviations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments validate the interpretation of catastrophic overfitting as a backdoor-like phenomenon and demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed mitigation strategies. The paper unifies catastrophic overfitting, backdoor attacks, and unlearnable tasks under a common theoretical framework.
AI.conclusion: Catastrophic overfitting in Fast Adversarial Training can be effectively interpreted and mitigated using insights from backdoor attack research, providing a unified theoretical framework and practical solutions for this challenging problem in adversarial robustness training.

title: Diffusion Templates: A Unified Plugin Framework for Controllable Diffusion
summary: Controllable diffusion methods have substantially expanded the practical utility of diffusion models, but they are typically developed as isolated, backbone-specific systems with incompatible training pipelines, parameter formats, and runtime hooks. This fragmentation makes it difficult to reuse infrastructure across tasks, transfer capabilities across backbones, or compose multiple controls within a single generation pipeline. We present Diffusion Templates, a unified and open plugin framework that decouples base-model inference from controllable capability injection. The framework is organized around three components: Template models that map arbitrary task-specific inputs to an intermediate capability representation, a Template cache that functions as a standardized interface for capability injection, and a Template pipeline that loads, merges, and injects one or more Template caches into the base diffusion runtime. Because the interface is defined at the systems level rather than tied to a specific control architecture, heterogeneous capability carriers such as KV-Cache and LoRA can be supported under the same abstraction. Based on this design, we build a diverse model zoo spanning structural control, brightness adjustment, color adjustment, image editing, super-resolution, sharpness enhancement, aesthetic alignment, content reference, local inpainting, and age control. These case studies show that Diffusion Templates can unify a broad range of controllable generation tasks while preserving modularity, composability, and practical extensibility across rapidly evolving diffusion backbones. All resources will be open sourced, including code, models, and datasets.

AI.tldr: Diffusion Templates is a unified plugin framework that decouples base diffusion model inference from controllable capability injection, enabling modular composition of diverse control methods across different backbones.
AI.motivation: Current controllable diffusion methods are fragmented with incompatible training pipelines, parameter formats, and runtime hooks, making it difficult to reuse infrastructure across tasks, transfer capabilities across backbones, or compose multiple controls within a single generation pipeline.
AI.method: A three-component framework: (1) Template models that map task-specific inputs to intermediate capability representations, (2) Template cache as standardized interface for capability injection, and (3) Template pipeline that loads, merges, and injects multiple Template caches into base diffusion runtime. Supports heterogeneous capability carriers like KV-Cache and LoRA under same abstraction.
AI.result: Built a diverse model zoo spanning structural control, brightness/color adjustment, image editing, super-resolution, sharpness enhancement, aesthetic alignment, content reference, local inpainting, and age control. Case studies show the framework can unify broad range of controllable generation tasks while preserving modularity and composability.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion Templates provides a unified open plugin framework that solves fragmentation in controllable diffusion methods, enabling practical extensibility across rapidly evolving diffusion backbones with all resources to be open sourced.

title: An Aircraft Upset Recovery System with Reinforcement Learning
summary: This article explores the progress made in the creation of a pilot activated recovery system (PARS) for advanced jet trainers that utilizes artificial intelligence (AI) in an effort to enhance operational efficiency. The PARS model employs an advanced reinforcement learning (RL) architecture, incorporating a cutting-edge soft-actor critic (SAC) model and hyper-parameter optimization methods. Negative-g punishments and other handcrafted features remarked upon by control engineers and domain experts regarding PARS are also taken into account by the system. When evaluated by them, the AI model's behavior is deemed more desirable than that of conventional control methods.

AI.tldr: AI-enhanced pilot activated recovery system (PARS) for jet trainers using reinforcement learning with soft-actor critic model outperforms conventional control methods.
AI.motivation: To enhance operational efficiency of advanced jet trainers by creating an AI-powered pilot activated recovery system (PARS) that can outperform conventional control methods.
AI.method: Developed PARS using advanced reinforcement learning architecture with soft-actor critic (SAC) model and hyper-parameter optimization. Incorporated domain-specific features including negative-g punishments and other expert-specified constraints.
AI.result: The AI model's behavior was evaluated by control engineers and domain experts who deemed it more desirable than conventional control methods for PARS applications.
AI.conclusion: AI-enhanced PARS using reinforcement learning shows promising results for improving jet trainer recovery systems, with expert validation confirming superior performance over traditional approaches.

title: DPRM: A Plug-in Doob h transform-induced Token-Ordering Module for Diffusion Language Models
summary:
Diffusion language models generate without a fixed left-to-right order, making token ordering a central algorithmic choice: which tokens should be revealed, retained, revised or verified at each step? Existing systems mainly use random masking or confidence-driven ordering. Random masking creates train--test mismatch, while confidence-only rules are efficient but can be myopic and suppress useful exploration.
  We introduce DPRM (Doob h-transform Process Reward Model), a plug-in token-ordering module for diffusion language models. DPRM keeps the host architecture, denoising objective and supervision unchanged, and changes only the ordering policy. It starts from confidence-driven progressive ordering and gradually shifts to Doob h transform Process Reward guided ordering through online estimates.
  We characterize the exact DPRM policy as a reward-tilted Gibbs reveal law, prove O(1/N) convergence of the stagewise Soft-BoN approximation, and show that the online bucketized controller tracks the exact DPRM score at empirical-Bernstein rates. Under tractable optimization assumptions, DPRM also yields a sample-complexity advantage over random and confidence-only ordering.
  DPRM improves over confidence-based baselines in pretraining, post-training, test-time scaling, and single-cell masked diffusion, with particularly strong gains on harder reasoning subsets. In protein, molecular generation and DNA design, the effect is more multi-objective: ordering-aware variants significantly improve selected structural or fragment-constrained metrics while not uniformly dominating the host baseline on every quality metric. These results identify token ordering as a fundamental control axis in diffusion language models and establish DPRM as a general-purpose module for improving it. Code is available at https://github.com/DakeBU/DPRM-DLLM.

AI.tldr: DPRM is a plug-in token-ordering module for diffusion language models that improves over random or confidence-based ordering by using Doob h-transform Process Reward guidance, achieving better performance on reasoning tasks while maintaining architectural compatibility.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion language models face challenges with token ordering: random masking creates train-test mismatch, while confidence-only ordering is efficient but myopic and suppresses useful exploration. There's a need for better token-ordering strategies that balance efficiency and exploration.
AI.method: DPRM keeps the host architecture, denoising objective, and supervision unchanged, only modifying the ordering policy. It starts with confidence-driven progressive ordering and gradually shifts to Doob h-transform Process Reward guided ordering using online estimates. The method uses a reward-tilted Gibbs reveal law and implements stagewise Soft-BoN approximation with bucketized controller tracking.
AI.result: DPRM outperforms confidence-based baselines in pretraining, post-training, test-time scaling, and single-cell masked diffusion, with particularly strong gains on harder reasoning subsets. In protein, molecular generation and DNA design, it improves selected structural or fragment-constrained metrics while not uniformly dominating all quality metrics.
AI.conclusion: Token ordering is a fundamental control axis in diffusion language models, and DPRM establishes itself as a general-purpose module for improving it. The approach offers theoretical guarantees (O(1/N) convergence) and practical advantages, demonstrating that ordering-aware variants can enhance specific metrics in multi-objective generation tasks.

title: SAGE: Sparse Adaptive Guidance for Dependency-Aware Tabular Data Generation
summary: Generating high-fidelity synthetic tabular data remains a critical challenge for enhancing data availability in privacy-sensitive and low-resource domains. Recent approaches leverage LLMs by representing table rows as sequences, yet suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) they model feature dependencies densely, introducing spurious correlations; and (2) they assume static relationships between features, ignoring how these dependencies vary with feature values. To overcome these limitations, we introduce SAGE (Sparse Adaptive Guidance), a novel LLM-based generation framework that enforces sparse and dynamic dependency guidance. SAGE discretizes features into value-aware pseudo-features and constructs a mutual information-based sparse dependency graph. This graph adaptively guides generation through explicit context selection or implicit logit correction, enabling LLMs to focus on truly relevant information during synthesis. Our extensive experiments across six datasets and multiple tasks reveal that SAGE not only improves data fidelity and downstream utility, boosting F1 scores by 10% compared to previous LLM-based methods, but also reduces policy violations by one point. These results highlight the importance of adaptive structure in tabular data generation and provide new insights into context-sensitive control of LLMs.

AI.tldr: SAGE is an LLM-based framework for generating synthetic tabular data that enforces sparse and dynamic dependency guidance to improve fidelity and reduce spurious correlations.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based approaches for tabular data generation have two key limitations: (1) they model feature dependencies too densely, creating spurious correlations, and (2) they assume static relationships between features, ignoring how dependencies vary with feature values. This makes generating high-fidelity synthetic data challenging for privacy-sensitive and low-resource domains.
AI.method: SAGE (Sparse Adaptive Guidance) discretizes features into value-aware pseudo-features and constructs a mutual information-based sparse dependency graph. This graph adaptively guides generation through either explicit context selection or implicit logit correction, allowing LLMs to focus only on truly relevant information during synthesis.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across six datasets show SAGE improves data fidelity and downstream utility, boosting F1 scores by 10% compared to previous LLM-based methods, while also reducing policy violations by one point.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the importance of adaptive structure in tabular data generation and provide new insights into context-sensitive control of LLMs, offering a more effective approach for generating synthetic data in privacy-sensitive applications.

title: PathMoG: A Pathway-Centric Modular Graph Neural Network for Multi-Omics Survival Prediction
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables. Source code available at https://github.com/wangzoyou/pathmog
summary: Cancer survival prediction from multi-omics data remains challenging because prognostic signals are high-dimensional, heterogeneous, and distributed across interacting genes and pathways. We propose PathMoG, a pathway-centric modular graph neural network for multi-omics survival prediction. PathMoG reorganizes genome-scale inputs into 354 KEGG-informed pathway modules, introduces a Hierarchical Omics Modulation module to condition gene-expression representations on mutation, copy number variation, pathway, and clinical context, and uses dual-level attention to capture both intra-pathway driver signals and inter-pathway clinical relevance. We evaluated PathMoG on 5,650 patients across 10 TCGA cancer types and observed consistent improvements over representative survival baselines. The framework further provides gene-level, pathway-level, and patient-level interpretability, supporting biologically grounded and clinically relevant risk stratification.

AI.tldr: PathMoG is a pathway-centric modular graph neural network that improves cancer survival prediction by organizing multi-omics data into pathway modules and using hierarchical conditioning with dual-level attention.
AI.motivation: Cancer survival prediction from multi-omics data is challenging due to high-dimensional, heterogeneous prognostic signals that are distributed across interacting genes and pathways.
AI.method: PathMoG reorganizes genome-scale inputs into 354 KEGG-informed pathway modules, uses a Hierarchical Omics Modulation module to condition gene-expression representations on mutation, copy number variation, pathway, and clinical context, and employs dual-level attention to capture intra-pathway driver signals and inter-pathway clinical relevance.
AI.result: The method was evaluated on 5,650 patients across 10 TCGA cancer types and showed consistent improvements over representative survival baselines.
AI.conclusion: PathMoG provides gene-level, pathway-level, and patient-level interpretability, supporting biologically grounded and clinically relevant risk stratification for cancer survival prediction.

title: Complexity of Linear Regions in Self-supervised Deep ReLU Networks
comment: Accepted for publication in 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition - Findings Track (CVPRF)
summary: There has been growing interest in studying the complexity of Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) based activation networks. Recent work investigates the evolution of the number of piecewise-linear partitions (linear regions) that are formed during training. However, current research is limited to examining the complexity of models trained in a supervised way. Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) differs in that it directly optimises the representation space using a loss function to enhance the model's performance across multiple downstream tasks. This study investigates the local distribution of linear regions produced by SSL models. We demonstrate that the evolution of linear regions correlates with the representation quality by utilising SplineCam to extract two-dimensional polytopes near the data distribution. We track the number, area, eccentricity, and boundaries of regions throughout training. The study compares supervised, contrastive, and self-distillation methods over two standard benchmark datasets, MNIST and FashionMNIST. The analysis of the experimental results shows that self-supervised methods create substantially fewer regions to achieve comparable accuracy to supervised models. Contrastive methods rapidly expand regions over time, whereas self-distillation methods tend to consolidate by merging neighbouring regions. Lastly, we can detect representation collapse early within the geometric space of linear regions. Our analysis suggests that polytopal metrics can serve as reliable indicators of representation quality and model performance.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised learning methods produce significantly fewer linear regions than supervised methods while achieving comparable accuracy, with different SSL methods showing distinct region evolution patterns that correlate with representation quality.
AI.motivation: Previous research on ReLU network complexity focused on supervised learning, but SSL's different optimization approach (directly optimizing representation space for multiple downstream tasks) motivates investigation of how SSL affects linear region formation and whether region characteristics correlate with representation quality.
AI.method: Used SplineCam to extract 2D polytopes near data distribution and tracked number, area, eccentricity, and boundaries of linear regions throughout training. Compared supervised, contrastive, and self-distillation methods on MNIST and FashionMNIST datasets.
AI.result: SSL methods create substantially fewer regions than supervised models for comparable accuracy. Contrastive methods rapidly expand regions over time, while self-distillation methods consolidate by merging neighboring regions. Representation collapse can be detected early in the geometric space of linear regions.
AI.conclusion: Polytopal metrics (linear region characteristics) serve as reliable indicators of representation quality and model performance in SSL, providing geometric insights into how different SSL approaches shape the representation space.

title: An Automatic Ground Collision Avoidance System with Reinforcement Learning
summary: This article evaluates an artificial intelligence (AI)-based Automatic Ground Collision Avoidance System (AGCAS) designed for advanced jet trainers to enhance operational effectiveness. In the continuously evolving field of aerospace engineering, the integration of AI is crucial for advancing operations with improved timing constraints and efficiency. Our study explores the design process of an AI-driven AGCAS, specifically tailored for advanced jet trainers, focusing on addressing the AGCAS problem within a limited observation space. The system utilizes line-of-sight queries on a terrain server to ensure precise and efficient collision avoidance. This approach aims to significantly improve the safety and operational capabilities of advanced jet trainers.

AI.tldr: AI-based Automatic Ground Collision Avoidance System designed for advanced jet trainers using terrain server queries for improved safety and operational effectiveness.
AI.motivation: To enhance operational effectiveness and safety of advanced jet trainers through AI integration, addressing the AGCAS problem within limited observation space constraints in the evolving aerospace engineering field.
AI.method: Uses AI-driven AGCAS design with line-of-sight queries on a terrain server for precise and efficient collision avoidance, specifically tailored for advanced jet trainers.
AI.result: The system aims to significantly improve safety and operational capabilities of advanced jet trainers through precise collision avoidance with improved timing constraints.
AI.conclusion: AI-based AGCAS represents a crucial advancement for jet trainer safety, leveraging terrain server integration to address collision avoidance challenges in limited observation spaces.

title: Advancing Ligand-based Virtual Screening and Molecular Generation with Pretrained Molecular Embedding Distance
summary: Molecular similarity plays a central role in ligand-based drug discovery, such as virtual screening, analog searching, and goal-directed molecular generation. However, traditional similarity measures, ranging from fingerprint-based Tanimoto coefficients to 3D shape overlays, are often computationally expensive at scale or rely on hand-crafted molecular descriptors. Meanwhile, many deep learning approaches to similarity-aware design still depend on similarity-specific supervision or costly data curation, limiting their generality across targets. In this work, we propose pretrained embedding distance (PED) as an effective alternative, computed directly from pretrained molecular models without task-specific training. Experimental results show that PED exhibits distinct correlations with traditional similarity metrics, and performs effectively in both ranking molecules for virtual screening and guiding molecular generation via reward design. These findings suggest that pretrained molecular embeddings capture rich structural information and can serve as a promising and scalable similarity measurement for modern AI-aided drug discovery.

AI.tldr: PED (pretrained embedding distance) is a new molecular similarity measure derived from pretrained molecular models that outperforms traditional methods in virtual screening and molecular generation without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Traditional molecular similarity measures are computationally expensive at scale and rely on hand-crafted descriptors, while deep learning approaches often require similarity-specific supervision or costly data curation, limiting their generality across drug discovery targets.
AI.method: Proposed PED (pretrained embedding distance) computed directly from pretrained molecular models without task-specific training, using embeddings from existing pretrained models as similarity measurements.
AI.result: PED exhibits distinct correlations with traditional similarity metrics, performs effectively in ranking molecules for virtual screening, and successfully guides molecular generation via reward design in experiments.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained molecular embeddings capture rich structural information and can serve as a promising, scalable similarity measurement for modern AI-aided drug discovery without requiring task-specific training.

title: SceneSelect: Selective Learning for Trajectory Scene Classification and Expert Scheduling
comment: This paper has been accepted by ICIC 2026
summary: Accurate trajectory prediction is fundamentally challenging due to high scene heterogeneity - the severe variance in motion velocity, spatial density, and interaction patterns across different real-world environments. However, most existing approaches typically train a single unified model, expecting a fixed-capacity architecture to generalize universally across all possible scenarios. This conventional model-centric paradigm is fundamentally flawed when confronting such extreme heterogeneity, inevitably leading to a severe generalization gap, degraded accuracy, and massive computational waste. To overcome this bottleneck, rather than refining restricted model-centric architectures, we propose selective learning, a novel scene-centric paradigm. It explicitly analyzes the characteristics of the underlying scene to dynamically route inputs to the most appropriate expert models. As a concrete implementation of this paradigm, we introduce SceneSelect. Specifically, SceneSelect utilizes unsupervised clustering on interpretable geometric and kinematic features to discover a latent scene taxonomy. A highly decoupled classification module is then trained to assign real-time inputs to these scene categories, and a highly extensible, plug-and-play scheduling policy automatically dispatches the trajectory sequence to the optimal expert predictor. Crucially, this decoupled design ensures excellent generalization capabilities, allowing seamless integration with different off-the-shelf models and robust adaptation across new datasets without requiring computationally expensive joint retraining. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks (ETH-UCY, SDD, and NBA) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong single-model and ensemble baselines, achieving an average improvement of 10.5%, showcasing the effectiveness of scene-aware selective learning.

AI.tldr: SceneSelect: A scene-centric paradigm for trajectory prediction that routes inputs to specialized expert models based on scene characteristics rather than using a single unified model.
AI.motivation: Traditional trajectory prediction models use a single fixed-capacity architecture that struggles with high scene heterogeneity (varying motion velocity, spatial density, interaction patterns). This leads to generalization gaps, degraded accuracy, and computational inefficiency when dealing with diverse real-world scenarios.
AI.method:
1) Proposes selective learning as a scene-centric paradigm
2) SceneSelect implementation: uses unsupervised clustering on interpretable geometric/kinematic features to discover latent scene taxonomy
3) Trains decoupled classification module to assign inputs to scene categories
4) Uses plug-and-play scheduling policy to dispatch sequences to optimal expert predictors
5) Decoupled design allows integration with off-the-shelf models
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks (ETH-UCY, SDD, NBA) show consistent outperformance of strong single-model and ensemble baselines, achieving average 10.5% improvement.
AI.conclusion: The scene-centric selective learning paradigm effectively addresses scene heterogeneity in trajectory prediction, offering better generalization, seamless integration with existing models, and robust adaptation without expensive retraining.

title: Prior-Agnostic Robust Forecast Aggregation
summary:
Robust forecast aggregation combines the predictions of multiple information sources to perform well in the worst case across all possible information structures. Previous work largely focuses on settings with a known binary state space, where the state is either 0 or 1. We study prior-agnostic robust forecast aggregation in which the aggregator observes only experts' reports, yet is ignorant of both the underlying joint information structure and the full prior, including the underlying state space. Unlike the standard model that fixes the binary state space {0, 1}, we allow the (binary) unknown state values to be arbitrary numbers in [0, 1], so the same reported probability may correspond to very different realized outcome frequencies across environments.
