  Our main contribution is a simple, explicit, closed-form log-odds aggregator that linearly pools forecasts in logit space, together with (nearly-)tight minimax-regret guarantees across three knowledge regimes. We first show that under conditionally independent (CI) signals, robust aggregation with an unknown state space is strictly harder than in the known-state setting by establishing a larger lower bound, and our aggregation rule can achieve a worst-case regret of 0.0255. Along the way, we also characterize tight regret bounds for Blackwell-ordered structures and for general information structures. In the classical setting with known state space {0,1}, our aggregator achieves regret strictly below 0.0226 for CI structures. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first explicit closed-form aggregator that achieves a regret upper bound strictly less than 0.0226. Finally, we extend the model where the aggregator additionally knows each expert's marginal forecast distribution; in this setting, with the CI structures, we show that a generalized log-odds rule achieves regret of 0.0228, complementing with a lower bound of 0.0225.

AI.tldr: Prior-agnostic robust forecast aggregation with unknown binary state values in [0,1], using closed-form log-odds aggregator with tight minimax-regret guarantees across different knowledge regimes.
AI.motivation: Previous robust forecast aggregation work focuses on known binary state space {0,1}, but in practice both information structure and prior (including state values) may be unknown. When state values can be arbitrary in [0,1], the same reported probability corresponds to different outcome frequencies across environments, making aggregation more challenging.
AI.method: Develops a simple explicit closed-form log-odds aggregator that linearly pools forecasts in logit space. Analyzes three knowledge regimes: 1) Conditionally independent (CI) signals with unknown state space, 2) Blackwell-ordered structures, 3) General information structures. Extends to setting where aggregator knows experts' marginal forecast distributions.
AI.result: For CI signals with unknown state space, achieves worst-case regret of 0.0255 (higher lower bound than known-state setting). For known state space {0,1} CI structures, achieves regret <0.0226 - first explicit closed-form aggregator below this threshold. With known marginal distributions and CI structures, generalized log-odds rule achieves regret 0.0228 with lower bound 0.0225.
AI.conclusion: Robust aggregation with unknown state space is strictly harder than known-state setting. The proposed log-odds aggregator provides near-optimal performance across different information structures with tight regret guarantees, addressing practical scenarios where state values and information structure may be unknown.

title: A Reward-Free Viewpoint on Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
summary: Many sequential decision-making tasks involve optimizing multiple conflicting objectives, requiring policies that adapt to different user preferences. In multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL), one widely studied approach} addresses this by training a single policy network conditioned on preference-weighted rewards. In this paper, we explore a novel algorithmic perspective: leveraging reward-free reinforcement learning (RFRL) for MORL. While RFRL has historically been studied independently of MORL, it learns optimal policies for any possible reward function, making it a natural fit for MORL's challenge of handling unknown user preferences. We propose using the RFRL's training objective as an auxiliary task to enhance MORL, enabling more effective knowledge sharing beyond the multi-objective reward function given at training time. To this end, we adapt a state-of-the-art RFRL algorithm to the MORL setting and introduce a preference-guided exploration strategy that focuses learning on relevant parts of the environment. Through extensive experiments and ablation studies, we demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art MORL methods across diverse MO-Gymnasium tasks, achieving superior performance and data efficiency. This work provides the first systematic adaptation of RFRL to MORL, demonstrating its potential as a scalable and empirically effective solution to multi-objective policy learning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using reward-free reinforcement learning as an auxiliary task to enhance multi-objective reinforcement learning, enabling better knowledge sharing and achieving state-of-the-art performance on multi-objective tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional MORL methods train policies conditioned on preference-weighted rewards, but this can limit knowledge sharing across different objectives. The authors recognize that reward-free RL learns optimal policies for any reward function, making it naturally suited for handling unknown user preferences in MORL settings.
AI.method: Adapts a state-of-the-art RFRL algorithm to the MORL setting and introduces a preference-guided exploration strategy that focuses learning on relevant parts of the environment. Uses RFRL's training objective as an auxiliary task to enhance MORL beyond the given multi-objective reward function.
AI.result: The approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art MORL methods across diverse MO-Gymnasium tasks, achieving superior performance and data efficiency. Extensive experiments and ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first systematic adaptation of RFRL to MORL, demonstrating its potential as a scalable and empirically effective solution for multi-objective policy learning, opening new algorithmic perspectives for handling conflicting objectives.

title: Stochastic simultaneous optimistic optimization
comment: Published in International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2013)
summary: We study the problem of global maximization of a function f given a finite number of evaluations perturbed by noise. We consider a very weak assumption on the function, namely that it is locally smooth (in some precise sense) with respect to some semi-metric, around one of its global maxima. Compared to previous works on bandits in general spaces (Kleinberg et al., 2008; Bubeck et al., 2011a) our algorithm does not require the knowledge of this semi-metric. Our algorithm, StoSOO, follows an optimistic strategy to iteratively construct upper confidence bounds over the hierarchical partitions of the function domain to decide which point to sample next. A finite-time analysis of StoSOO shows that it performs almost as well as the best specifically-tuned algorithms even though the local smoothness of the function is not known.

AI.tldr: StoSOO is a stochastic bandit algorithm for global optimization under noise that doesn't require prior knowledge of the local smoothness structure, using optimistic hierarchical partitioning instead.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of global maximization of noisy functions with minimal assumptions - only requiring local smoothness around global maxima rather than global smoothness knowledge needed by previous bandit algorithms.
AI.method: The StoSOO algorithm uses an optimistic strategy to iteratively construct upper confidence bounds over hierarchical partitions of the function domain to select sampling points, without requiring knowledge of the semi-metric defining local smoothness.
AI.result: A finite-time analysis shows StoSOO performs nearly as well as the best specifically-tuned algorithms that do have knowledge of the local smoothness structure.
AI.conclusion: StoSOO provides a practical optimization algorithm that achieves strong performance without requiring prior knowledge of function smoothness, bridging the gap between theoretical assumptions and practical applications in noisy global optimization.

title: Dialysis Risk Prediction and Treatment Effect Estimation for AKI patients using Longitudinal Electronic Health Records
summary: Progression to dialysis or end-stage renal disease is a rare but clinically important outcome. Clinicians need evidence on how medication exposures influence downstream risk. We constructed a fixed-window EHR cohort (90-day observation, 730-day prediction; N=81401; dialysis/ESRD prevalence: 1.1%) and modeled sequences of diagnoses, procedures, and medications with kidney laboratory trends (creatinine, BUN, eGFR). A transformer-based causal multi-head model was trained to estimate drug- and ingredient-level average treatment effects (ATEs) using counterfactual exposure removal and insertion under a full medication history setup. On test set, predictive performance reached an AUC of 0.694 and PR-AUC of 0.094. At the selected decision threshold (0.883), the model achieved an F1 score of 0.201 with a Brier score of 0.018. Post-hoc causal analyses of lab changes (eGFR, creatinine, BUN) using IPTW, AIPW, naive, and covariate-adjusted OLS methods assessed clinical directionality. Results showed partial protective-direction support for ACE/ARB exposures and worsening-direction signals for loop diuretics.

AI.tldr: A transformer-based causal model using EHR data to estimate drug effects on kidney disease progression, finding protective signals for ACE/ARBs and worsening signals for loop diuretics.
AI.motivation: Clinicians need evidence on how medications influence the risk of progression to dialysis or end-stage renal disease, which is rare but clinically important. There's a need for causal analysis of drug effects using real-world EHR data.
AI.method: Constructed a fixed-window EHR cohort (90-day observation, 730-day prediction) with N=81,401 patients. Modeled sequences of diagnoses, procedures, and medications with kidney lab trends using a transformer-based causal multi-head model to estimate drug- and ingredient-level ATEs via counterfactual exposure removal/insertion. Applied post-hoc causal analysis of lab changes using IPTW, AIPW, naive, and covariate-adjusted OLS methods.
AI.result: Model achieved AUC of 0.694 and PR-AUC of 0.094 on test set. At decision threshold 0.883: F1 score 0.201, Brier score 0.018. Causal analysis showed partial protective-direction support for ACE/ARB exposures and worsening-direction signals for loop diuretics.
AI.conclusion: The transformer-based causal modeling approach can provide clinically relevant insights into medication effects on kidney disease progression using EHR data, with evidence supporting protective effects of ACE/ARBs and potential worsening effects of loop diuretics.

title: GradMAP: Gradient-Based Multi-Agent Proximal Learning for Grid-Edge Flexibility
summary: Coordinating large populations of grid-edge devices requires learning methods that remain fully decentralised in deployment while still respecting three-phase AC distribution-network physics. This paper proposes gradient-based multi-agent proximal learning (GradMAP) to address this challenge. GradMAP trains independent neural-network policies for each agent without any parameter sharing, and each agent uses only its own local observation for online decision-making without communication. During offline training, GradMAP embeds a differentiable three-phase AC power-flow model in a primal-dual learning loop and uses implicit differentiation to propagate exact network-constraint violations to update the policy parameters. To speed up training, GradMAP reuses expensive environment gradients through a proximal surrogate within a trust region defined in the more direct policy-output (action) space, instead of the probability distribution space used in other works, such as PPO. In case studies with 1,000 agents managing batteries, heat pumps, and controllable generators on the IEEE 123-bus feeder, GradMAP learns decentralised policies that minimise three-phase AC load-flow constraint violations within 15 minutes of training on a single workstation-class NVIDIA RTX PRO 5000 Blackwell 48GB GPU. This is a 3--5x training speed-up over gradient-based self-supervised learning benchmarks and substantially better training efficiency than multi-agent reinforcement-learning benchmarks. In out-of-sample tests, GradMAP also delivers among the lowest operating cost and constraint violations.

AI.tldr: GradMAP enables fast decentralized training of neural network policies for thousands of grid-edge devices using a differentiable AC power-flow model and proximal optimization, achieving 3-5x speedup over benchmarks while maintaining low constraint violations and operating costs.
AI.motivation: There's a need for learning methods that coordinate large populations of grid-edge devices in a fully decentralized manner while respecting three-phase AC distribution-network physics, avoiding parameter sharing and communication during deployment.
AI.method: GradMAP uses gradient-based multi-agent proximal learning with independent neural-network policies per agent. It embeds a differentiable three-phase AC power-flow model in a primal-dual learning loop, uses implicit differentiation for constraint violation propagation, and employs proximal surrogate optimization with trust regions in policy-output space (rather than probability distribution space) to speed up training.
AI.result: GradMAP learns decentralized policies for 1,000 agents managing batteries, heat pumps, and controllable generators on the IEEE 123-bus feeder within 15 minutes on a single workstation GPU. It achieves 3-5x training speed-up over gradient-based self-supervised learning benchmarks and substantially better efficiency than multi-agent reinforcement learning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: GradMAP successfully addresses the challenge of coordinating large grid-edge device populations with decentralized learning that respects AC network physics, demonstrating superior training speed, efficiency, and performance in both in-sample and out-of-sample tests with minimal constraint violations and operating costs.

title: Efficient learning by implicit exploration in bandit problems with side observations
comment: Published at Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) 2014
summary: We consider online learning problems under a partial observability model capturing situations where the information conveyed to the learner is between full information and bandit feedback. In the simplest variant, we assume that in addition to its own loss, the learner also gets to observe losses of some other actions. The revealed losses depend on the learner's action and a directed observation system chosen by the environment. For this setting, we propose the first algorithm that enjoys near-optimal regret guarantees without having to know the observation system before selecting its actions. Along similar lines, we also define a new partial information setting that models online combinatorial optimization problems where the feedback received by the learner is between semi-bandit and full feedback. As the predictions of our first algorithm cannot be always computed efficiently in this setting, we propose another algorithm with similar properties and with the benefit of always being computationally efficient, at the price of a slightly more complicated tuning mechanism. Both algorithms rely on a novel exploration strategy called implicit exploration, which is shown to be more efficient both computationally and information-theoretically than previously studied exploration strategies for the problem.

AI.tldr: A partial observability online learning framework with two algorithms for settings between bandit and full information, featuring novel implicit exploration strategy.
AI.motivation: Address online learning under partial observability where learner gets to observe not only own loss but also losses of some other actions, bridging full information and bandit feedback. Also models combinatorial optimization with feedback between semi-bandit and full feedback.
AI.method: Proposes first algorithm with near-optimal regret without requiring knowledge of observation system beforehand. Second algorithm for combinatorial settings with computational efficiency but more complex tuning. Both use novel implicit exploration strategy.
AI.result: First algorithm achieves near-optimal regret guarantees without prior knowledge of observation system. Second algorithm maintains similar properties with computational efficiency in combinatorial settings. Implicit exploration shown to be more efficient computationally and information-theoretically than previous strategies.
AI.conclusion: Presents effective algorithms for partial observability online learning that bridge information spectrum between bandit and full feedback, with improved exploration strategy offering both computational and theoretical advantages.

title: Fraud Detection in Cryptocurrency Markets with Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, Accepted at the SDS2026: IEEE Swiss Conference on Data Science and AI
summary: Technological advancements in cryptocurrency markets have increased accessibility for investors, but concurrently exposed them to the risks of market manipulations. Existing fraud detection mechanisms typically rely on machine learning methods that treat each financial asset (i.e., token) and its related transactions independently. However, market manipulation strategies are rarely isolated events, but are rather characterized by coordination, repetition, and frequent transfers among related assets. This suggests that relational structure constitutes an integral component of the signal and can be effectively represented through graphical means. In this paper, we propose three graph construction methods that rely on aggregated hourly market data. The proposed graphs are processed by a unified spatio-temporal Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture that combines attention-based spatial aggregation with temporal Transformer encoding. We evaluate our methodology on a real-world dataset comprised of pump-and-dump schemes in cryptocurrency markets, spanning a period of over three years. Our comparative results showcase that our graph-based models achieve significant improvements over standard machine learning baselines in detecting anomalous events. Our work highlights that learned market connectivity provides substantial gains for detecting coordinated market manipulation schemes.

AI.tldr: Graph-based approach using spatio-temporal GNNs outperforms traditional ML methods for detecting coordinated cryptocurrency market manipulation schemes by modeling relational patterns among tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing fraud detection methods treat cryptocurrency tokens and transactions independently, but market manipulation strategies involve coordination, repetition, and transfers among related assets, suggesting that relational structure is crucial for detection.
AI.method: Three graph construction methods using aggregated hourly market data, processed by a unified spatio-temporal Graph Neural Network architecture combining attention-based spatial aggregation with temporal Transformer encoding.
AI.result: Evaluation on real-world pump-and-dump dataset spanning over three years shows graph-based models achieve significant improvements over standard machine learning baselines in detecting anomalous events.
AI.conclusion: Learned market connectivity provides substantial gains for detecting coordinated market manipulation schemes, highlighting the importance of modeling relational structures in cryptocurrency fraud detection.

title: Uncovering Latent Patterns in Social Media Usage and Mental Health: A Clustering-Based Approach Using Unsupervised Machine Learning
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, International Conference on Advancement in Healthcare Technology and Biomedical Engineering, Vancouver, BC, Canada
summary: The widespread adoption of social media has heightened interest in its psychological effects, particularly on mental health indicators such as anxiety, depression, loneliness, and sleep quality, as these platforms increasingly influence social interactions and well-being. Although previous research has examined correlations between social media use and mental health, few studies have utilized unsupervised machine learning to segment users based on behavioral and psychological patterns, leaving a gap in identifying distinct risk profiles across diverse groups. This study seeks to address this by segmenting individuals according to their social media usage and psychological well-being, employing clustering to reveal hidden patterns and evaluate their mental health implications. Data from 551 participants, collected via an online survey, were preprocessed using KNN imputation for missing values, one-hot encoding for categorical variables like Gender with 5 unique values, and outlier detection via IQR and Z-score methods. K-Means clustering, optimized at 6 clusters using the Elbow Method and a Silhouette Score of 0.32, was applied, with PCA reducing 22 dimensions for visualization and a correlation heatmap highlighting relationships, such as a 0.28 correlation between social media hours and anxiety.

AI.tldr: This study uses unsupervised machine learning (K-Means clustering) to segment social media users based on behavioral and psychological patterns, identifying distinct risk profiles for mental health outcomes like anxiety and depression.
AI.motivation: While social media's psychological effects on mental health indicators (anxiety, depression, loneliness, sleep quality) are of increasing interest, most research only examines correlations. There's a gap in using unsupervised machine learning to identify distinct user segments based on behavioral and psychological patterns across diverse groups.
AI.method: Data from 551 participants via online survey; preprocessing with KNN imputation for missing values, one-hot encoding for categorical variables (Gender with 5 values), outlier detection via IQR and Z-score; K-Means clustering optimized at 6 clusters using Elbow Method (Silhouette Score 0.32); PCA for 22-dimensional visualization; correlation analysis including heatmap.
AI.result: Successful segmentation of users into 6 clusters based on social media usage patterns and psychological well-being. Identified relationships like 0.28 correlation between social media hours and anxiety. Revealed hidden patterns in user behavior and mental health implications.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that unsupervised machine learning can effectively identify distinct risk profiles among social media users, providing insights beyond simple correlations that can inform targeted mental health interventions and platform design considerations.

title: Cortex-Inspired Continual Learning: Unsupervised Instantiation and Recovery of Functional Task Networks
summary: Block-sequential continual learning demands that a single model both protect prior solutions from catastrophic forgetting and efficiently infer at inference time which prior solution matches the current input without task labels. We present Functional Task Networks (FTN), a parameter-isolation method inspired by structural and dynamical motifs found in the mammalian neocortex. Similar to mixture-of-experts, this method uses a high dimensional, self-organizing binary mask over a large population of small but deep networks, inspired by dendritic models of pyramidal neurons. The mask is produced by a three-stage procedure: (1) gradient descent on a continuous mask identifies task-relevant neurons, (2) a smoothing kernel biases the result toward spatial contiguity, (3) and k-winner-take-all binarizes the resulting group at a fixed capacity budget. Like mixture-of-experts, each neuron is an independent deep network, so disjoint masks give exactly disjoint gradient updates, providing structural guarantees against catastrophic forgetting. This three-stage procedure recovers the sub-network of a previously-trained task in a single gradient step, providing unsupervised task segmentation at inference time. We test it on three continual-learning benchmarks: (1) a synthetic multi-task classification/regression generator, (2) MNIST with shuffled class labels (pure concept shift), and (3) Permuted MNIST (domain shift). On all three, FTN with fine grained smoothing (FTN-Slow) results in nearly zero forgetting. FTN with a large kernel and only 2 iterations of smoothing (FTN-Fast) trades off some retention for increased speed. We show that the spatial organization mechanism reduces the effective mask search from the combinatorial top-k subset problem in O(C(H,K)) to the complexity of a near-linear scan in O(H) over compact cortical neighborhoods, which is parallelized by the gradient-based update.

AI.tldr: Functional Task Networks (FTN) is a parameter-isolation method for continual learning that uses binary masks over small deep networks to prevent catastrophic forgetting and enable unsupervised task segmentation at inference time.
AI.motivation: Block-sequential continual learning requires models to both protect prior solutions from catastrophic forgetting and efficiently infer which prior solution matches current inputs without task labels at inference time.
AI.method: FTN uses a three-stage procedure: (1) gradient descent on continuous mask to identify task-relevant neurons, (2) smoothing kernel for spatial contiguity, (3) k-winner-take-all binarization at fixed capacity budget. Inspired by mammalian neocortex and uses high-dimensional, self-organizing binary masks over small deep networks.
AI.result: FTN with fine-grained smoothing (FTN-Slow) achieves nearly zero forgetting on three benchmarks: synthetic multi-task classification/regression, MNIST with shuffled labels (concept shift), and Permuted MNIST (domain shift). FTN-Fast trades some retention for speed.
AI.conclusion: FTN provides structural guarantees against catastrophic forgetting through disjoint masks and gradient updates, enables unsupervised task segmentation, and reduces mask search complexity from combinatorial O(C(H,K)) to near-linear O(H) over compact cortical neighborhoods.

title: The Last Human-Written Paper: Agent-Native Research Artifacts
summary: Scientific publication compresses a branching, iterative research process into a linear narrative, discarding the majority of what was discovered along the way. This compilation imposes two structural costs: a Storytelling Tax, where failed experiments, rejected hypotheses, and the branching exploration process are discarded to fit a linear narrative; and an Engineering Tax, where the gap between reviewer-sufficient prose and agent-sufficient specification leaves critical implementation details unwritten. Tolerable for human readers, these costs become critical when AI agents must understand, reproduce, and extend published work. We introduce the Agent-Native Research Artifact (Ara), a protocol that replaces the narrative paper with a machine-executable research package structured around four layers: scientific logic, executable code with full specifications, an exploration graph that preserves the failures compilation discards, and evidence grounding every claim in raw outputs. Three mechanisms support the ecosystem: a Live Research Manager that captures decisions and dead ends during ordinary development; an Ara Compiler that translates legacy PDFs and repos into Aras; and an Ara-native review system that automates objective checks so human reviewers can focus on significance, novelty, and taste. On PaperBench and RE-Bench, Ara raises question-answering accuracy from 72.4% to 93.7% and reproduction success from 57.4% to 64.4%. On RE-Bench's five open-ended extension tasks, preserved failure traces in Ara accelerate progress, but can also constrain a capable agent from stepping outside the prior-run box depending on the agent's capabilities.

AI.tldr: Ara is a machine-executable research artifact protocol that replaces narrative papers with structured packages to overcome the storytelling and engineering costs that hinder AI agents from reproducing and extending scientific work.
AI.motivation: Traditional scientific papers impose two structural costs: Storytelling Tax (discarding failed experiments and branching exploration) and Engineering Tax (leaving implementation details unwritten). These are tolerable for human readers but critical when AI agents need to understand, reproduce, and extend published research.
AI.method: Introduces Agent-Native Research Artifact (Ara) protocol with four layers: scientific logic, executable code with full specifications, exploration graph preserving failures, and evidence grounding claims in raw outputs. Supported by Live Research Manager for capturing decisions/dead ends during development, Ara Compiler for converting legacy PDFs/repos, and Ara-native review system.
AI.result: On PaperBench and RE-Bench, Ara raises question-answering accuracy from 72.4% to 93.7% and reproduction success from 57.4% to 64.4%. On RE-Bench's extension tasks, preserved failure traces accelerate progress but can also constrain capable agents from exploring beyond prior-run paths.
AI.conclusion: Ara represents a fundamental shift from narrative papers to machine-executable research artifacts that preserve the full research process, enabling better AI understanding and reproduction while providing mixed results on whether this structured approach helps or hinders open-ended exploration by capable agents.

title: A Functorial Formulation of Neighborhood Aggregating Deep Learning
summary: We provide a mathematical interpretation of convolutional (or message passing) neural networks by using presheaves and copresheaves of the set of continuous functions over a topological space. Based on this interpretation, we formulate a theoretical heuristic which elaborates a number of empirical limitations of these neural networks by using obstructions on such sets of continuous functions over a topological space to be sheaves or copresheaves.

AI.tldr: The paper interprets convolutional/message passing neural networks using presheaves and copresheaves of continuous functions over topological spaces, then uses obstructions to these structures being sheaves/copresheaves to explain empirical limitations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a rigorous mathematical interpretation of convolutional and message passing neural networks, which are widely used but lack deep theoretical understanding. The authors aim to explain empirical limitations of these networks through mathematical obstructions.
AI.method: The method involves using concepts from algebraic topology and category theory: representing neural networks as presheaves and copresheaves of continuous functions over topological spaces. The theoretical heuristic identifies obstructions preventing these structures from being proper sheaves or copresheaves.
AI.result: The results likely include a formal mathematical framework that explains why certain neural network architectures have limitations in practice, particularly when it comes to generalization, stability, or representational capacity.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion would suggest that understanding neural networks through the lens of sheaf theory provides insight into their empirical behavior, potentially guiding architectural improvements or theoretical understanding of their limitations.

title: Diffusion-Guided Feature Selection via Nishimori Temperature: Noise-Based Spectral Embedding
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, extended version (with noise shift proof) of DSPA2026 article
summary: We propose Noise-Based Spectral Embedding (NBSE), a physics-informed framework for selecting informative features from high-dimensional data without greedy search. NBSE constructs a sparse similarity graph on the samples and identifies the Nishimori temperature $β_N$ the critical inverse temperature at which the Bethe Hessian becomes singular. The corresponding smallest eigenvector captures the dominant mode of an intrinsically degree-corrected diffusion process, naturally reweighting nodes to prevent hub dominance. By transposing the data matrix and applying NBSE in feature space, we obtain a one-dimensional spectral embedding that reveals groups of redundant or semantically related dimensions; balanced binning then selects one representative per group. We prove that coloured Gaussian perturbations shift $β_N$ by at most $O(\barσ^2)$, guaranteeing robustness to measurement noise. Experiments on ImageNet embeddings from MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4 show that NBSE preserves classification accuracy even under aggressive compression: on EfficientNet-B4 the accuracy drop is below $1\%$ when retaining only $30\%$ of features, outperforming ANOVA $F$-test and random selection by up to $6.8\%$.

AI.tldr: NBSE is a physics-inspired spectral method for automatic feature selection that identifies informative features without greedy search, preserving model accuracy even with aggressive compression.
AI.motivation: Traditional feature selection methods often rely on greedy search and can be computationally expensive for high-dimensional data. There's a need for principled, physics-informed approaches that can identify informative feature groups while maintaining robustness to noise.
AI.method: NBSE constructs a sparse similarity graph on samples and identifies the Nishimori temperature β_N where the Bethe Hessian becomes singular. The smallest eigenvector captures the dominant mode of degree-corrected diffusion. By transposing the data matrix and applying NBSE in feature space, it obtains a 1D spectral embedding that reveals groups of related dimensions, then uses balanced binning to select one representative per group.
AI.result: NBSE proves robust to colored Gaussian noise (shifts β_N by O(σ̄²)). On ImageNet embeddings from MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B4, it preserves classification accuracy under aggressive compression: on EfficientNet-B4, accuracy drops <1% when retaining only 30% of features, outperforming ANOVA F-test and random selection by up to 6.8%.
AI.conclusion: NBSE provides a principled, physics-informed framework for feature selection that achieves effective compression while maintaining model performance, demonstrating superior performance over traditional statistical methods.

title: Scalable Hyperparameter-Divergent Ensemble Training with Automatic Learning Rate Exploration for Large Models
summary:
Training large neural networks with data-parallel stochastic gradient descent allocates N GPU replicas to compute effectively identical updates -- a practice that leaves the rich space of learning rate configurations entirely unexplored during training. We propose Hyperparameter-Divergent Ensemble Training (HDET), a method that repurposes these replicas for simultaneous learning rate exploration at negligible communication overhead. HDET operates in alternating phases: a fan-out stage in which replicas train independently under a structured, symmetric spread of learning rates, and a converge stage in which parameters are averaged across all replicas via AllReduce every T steps. Building on this ensemble substrate, we further propose an automatic learning rate (auto-LR) controller that treats the relative training loss across replicas as a performance signal, updating the shared base schedule toward higher-performing configurations via a momentum-based gradient-free meta-update. The combined method produces a self-adapting learning rate schedule that improves both optimization quality and generalization without additional hyperparameter sweeps or training budget.
  Crucially, the framework generalizes beyond learning rate: any scalar hyperparameter that does not alter model architecture -- such as dropout rate, attention scale temperature, or weight-decay coefficient -- can be explored across replicas using the same fan-out/converge protocol, with inter-replica loss differences serving as zero-order hypergradients that guide the search direction. HDET is implemented as a drop-in replacement for PyTorch's OneCycleLR scheduler, requiring no changes to model architecture, optimizer, or data pipeline.

AI.tldr: HDET enables parallel hyperparameter exploration across data-parallel replicas with negligible overhead by alternating between independent training phases and parameter averaging, with an auto-LR controller that meta-learns optimal schedules.
AI.motivation: Current data-parallel training wastes computational resources by having all GPU replicas compute identical updates, missing opportunities to explore learning rate configurations during training rather than through separate hyperparameter sweeps.
AI.method: HDET alternates between fan-out phases (replicas train independently with structured learning rate variations) and converge phases (parameter averaging via AllReduce). Includes auto-LR controller that uses relative loss differences as performance signals for meta-updates.
AI.result: Enables self-adapting learning rate schedules that improve optimization quality and generalization without additional hyperparameter tuning or training budget, generalizes to other scalar hyperparameters, and works as drop-in PyTorch replacement.
AI.conclusion: HDET efficiently repurposes data-parallel resources for simultaneous hyperparameter exploration, creating a practical framework for adaptive training that improves performance without extra computational cost.

title: SpecRLBench: A Benchmark for Generalization in Specification-Guided Reinforcement Learning
summary: Specification-guided reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled framework for encoding complex, temporally extended tasks using formal specifications such as linear temporal logic (LTL). While recent methods have shown promising results, their ability to generalize across unseen specifications and diverse environments remains insufficiently understood. In this work, we introduce SpecRLBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate the generalization capabilities of LTL-based specification-guided RL methods. The benchmark spans multiple difficulty levels across navigation and manipulation domains, incorporating both static and dynamic environments, diverse robot dynamics, and varied observation modalities. Through extensive empirical evaluation, we characterize the strengths and limitations of existing approaches and reveal the challenges that emerge as specification and environment complexity increase. SpecRLBench provides a structured platform for systematic comparison and supports the development of more generalizable specification-guided RL methods. Code is available at https://github.com/BU-DEPEND-Lab/SpecRLBench.

AI.tldr: A benchmark (SpecRLBench) for evaluating generalization of LTL-based specification-guided RL methods across diverse environments and specifications.
AI.motivation: Specification-guided RL using formal specifications like LTL shows promise, but generalization capabilities across unseen specifications and diverse environments are not well understood.
AI.method: Introduces SpecRLBench benchmark spanning multiple difficulty levels across navigation and manipulation domains with static/dynamic environments, diverse robot dynamics, and varied observation modalities.
AI.result: Through extensive evaluation, characterizes strengths/limitations of existing approaches and reveals challenges that emerge with increasing specification and environment complexity.
AI.conclusion: SpecRLBench provides a structured platform for systematic comparison and supports development of more generalizable specification-guided RL methods.

title: Learning to Think from Multiple Thinkers
comment: Comments are welcome. There are 78 pages and 5 Figures
summary:
We study learning with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) supervision from multiple thinkers, all of whom provide correct but possibly systematically different solutions, e.g., step-by-step solutions to math problems written by different thinkers, or step-by-step execution traces of different programs solving the same problem.
  We consider classes that are computationally easy to learn using CoT supervision from a single thinker, but hard to learn with only end-result supervision, i.e., without CoT (Joshi et al. 2025). We establish that, under cryptographic assumptions, learning can be hard from CoT supervision provided by two or a few different thinkers, in passive data-collection settings.
  On the other hand, we provide a generic computationally efficient active learning algorithm that learns with a small amount of CoT data per thinker that is completely independent of the target accuracy $\varepsilon$, a moderate number of thinkers that scales as $\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\log \log \frac{1}{\varepsilon}$, and sufficient passive end-result data that scales as $\frac{1}{\varepsilon}\cdot poly\log\frac{1}{\varepsilon}$.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that learning from multiple thinkers' Chain-of-Thought supervision can be computationally hard in passive settings, but efficient in active learning with moderate numbers of thinkers.
AI.motivation: While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) supervision from a single thinker enables learning of classes that are hard with only end-result supervision, it's unclear how learning behaves when CoT data comes from multiple different thinkers, each providing correct but systematically different solutions to the same problem.
AI.method: The paper uses computational learning theory and cryptographic assumptions to analyze both passive and active learning scenarios. It establishes hardness results under cryptographic assumptions for passive learning with few thinkers, and provides a generic active learning algorithm that efficiently learns from multiple thinkers.
AI.result:
1. Under cryptographic assumptions, learning can be computationally hard from CoT supervision by just 2 or a few different thinkers in passive data collection.
2. In active learning, the paper provides an efficient algorithm requiring only a small amount of CoT data per thinker (independent of target accuracy ε), a moderate number of thinkers (log(1/ε)log log(1/ε)), and sufficient passive end-result data (1/ε·polylog(1/ε)).
AI.conclusion: The gap between passive and active learning persists even when leveraging CoT supervision from multiple thinkers. While passive learning with multiple thinkers can be computationally hard, active learning can efficiently leverage moderate numbers of thinkers to learn classes that are hard with only end-result supervision.

title: Conflict-Aware Harmonized Rotational Gradient for Multiscale Kinetic Regimes
summary:
In this paper, we propose a harmonized rotational gradient method, termed HRGrad, for simultaneously tackling multiscale time-dependent kinetic problems with varying small parameters.
  These parameters exhibit asymptotic transitions from microscopic to macroscopic physics, making it a challenging multi-task problem to solve over all ranges simultaneously.
  Solving tasks in different asymptotic regions often encounter gradient conflicts, which can lead to the failure of multi-task learning.
  To address this challenge, we explicitly encode a hidden representation of these parameters, ensuring that the corresponding solving tasks are serialized for simultaneous training.
  Furthermore, to mitigate gradient conflicts, we segment the prediction results to construct task losses and introduce a novel gradient alignment metric to ensure a positive dot product between the final update and each loss-specific gradient.
  This metric maintains consistent optimization rates for all task losses and dynamically adjusts gradient magnitudes based on conflict levels.
  Moreover, we provide a mathematical proof demonstrating the convergence of the HRGrad method, which is evaluated across a range of challenging asymptotic-preserving neural networks (APNNs) scenarios.
  We conduct an extensive set of experiments encompassing the Bhatnagar-Gross-Krook (BGK) equation and the linear transport equation in all ranges of Knudsen number.
  Our results indicate that HRGrad effectively overcomes the `failure modes' of APNNs in these problems.

AI.tldr: HRGrad is a harmonized rotational gradient method that solves multi-scale kinetic problems by serializing tasks and aligning gradients to prevent conflicts, enabling effective simultaneous training across all asymptotic regions.
AI.motivation: Multi-scale time-dependent kinetic problems with varying small parameters exhibit asymptotic transitions from microscopic to macroscopic physics, creating challenging multi-task learning problems. Solving tasks across different asymptotic regions often encounters gradient conflicts that can lead to multi-task learning failure.
AI.method: HRGrad explicitly encodes hidden representations of small parameters to serialize solving tasks for simultaneous training. It segments prediction results to construct task losses and introduces a novel gradient alignment metric that ensures positive dot products between final updates and loss-specific gradients, maintaining consistent optimization rates and dynamically adjusting gradient magnitudes based on conflict levels.
AI.result: The method was evaluated across challenging asymptotic-preserving neural networks (APNNs) scenarios, including the Bhatnagar-Gross-Krook (BGK) equation and linear transport equation in all ranges of Knudsen number. Results show HRGrad effectively overcomes the 'failure modes' of APNNs in these problems.
AI.conclusion: HRGrad successfully addresses gradient conflicts in multi-task learning for multi-scale kinetic problems, with mathematical proof of convergence provided. The method enables effective simultaneous training across all asymptotic regions where traditional APNNs fail.

title: The Optimal Sample Complexity of Multiclass and List Learning
summary:
While the optimal sample complexity of binary classification in terms of the VC dimension is well-established, determining the optimal sample complexity of multiclass classification has remained open. The appropriate complexity parameter for multiclass classification is the DS dimension, and despite significant efforts, a gap of $\sqrt{\text{DS}}$ has persisted between the upper and lower bounds on sample complexity.
  Recent work by Hanneke et al. (2026) shows a novel algebraic characterization of multiclass hypothesis classes in terms of their DS dimension. Building up on this, we show that the maximum hypergraph density of any multiclass hypothesis class is upper-bounded by its DS dimension. This proves a longstanding conjecture of Daniely and Shalev-Shwartz (2014). As a consequence, we determine the optimal dependence of the sample complexity on the DS dimension for multiclass as well as list learning.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves a longstanding open problem in multiclass classification by proving the Daniely-Shalev-Shwartz conjecture that the maximum hypergraph density of any multiclass hypothesis class is bounded by its DS dimension, leading to optimal sample complexity bounds.
AI.motivation: While binary classification has well-established optimal sample complexity bounds in terms of VC dimension, the optimal sample complexity for multiclass classification has remained an open problem. Despite significant efforts, there has been a persistent gap of √DS between upper and lower bounds on sample complexity, with DS dimension being the appropriate complexity parameter for multiclass classification.
AI.method: Building on recent work by Hanneke et al. (2026) that provides a novel algebraic characterization of multiclass hypothesis classes in terms of their DS dimension, the authors show that the maximum hypergraph density of any multiclass hypothesis class is upper-bounded by its DS dimension.
AI.result: The main result is the proof of the longstanding conjecture of Daniely and Shalev-Shwartz (2014) about the relationship between maximum hypergraph density and DS dimension. This resolves the open problem and determines the optimal dependence of sample complexity on DS dimension for both multiclass and list learning.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a complete solution to the multiclass sample complexity problem that has been open for over a decade, closing the √DS gap between bounds and establishing optimal sample complexity results that parallel the well-known VC dimension results for binary classification.
